Each condition is independently pinned at boundaries: CA notice warranted at day 14 (yes) and day 13 (no); CA notice not warranted without indicia even at day 14; CA notice period satisfied at 14d (yes) and 13d (no); CA belongings disposal at 18d (yes) and 17d (no).

**Additional indicia flag is gatekeeper** for statutory and case-by-case regimes. Without other indicia (utilities terminated, mail accumulating, neighbor reports of absence, keys returned), the rent-unpaid threshold alone does NOT warrant the notice. Pinned by `ca_notice_not_warranted_without_indicia` (14d unpaid but no indicia = not warranted).

**Statutory state data integrity**: all statutory-abandonment states have all three day thresholds (rent unpaid + notice period + belongings disposal). Pinned by `statutory_states_have_all_three_day_thresholds` (sweep verifying no Some/None mismatches).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/abandonment-check`. Twenty-five tests pin: 51-row coverage; CA boundaries at 14/13d rent unpaid; CA without indicia not warranted (gatekeeper regression target); CA notice period at 14/13d boundaries; CA belongings disposal at 18/17d boundaries; **WA 45-day strictest belongings window** + sweep; TX 30-day belongings; NY court-process-only + CO/NJ mirror; **4-state court-process sweep** (NY/CO/DC/NJ); FL/MA case-by-case; no-statute 9-state sweep; unknown state handled; case-insensitive lookup; sorted all_states; non-empty citations; statutory states have all three day thresholds (integrity sweep); case-by-case states have no day thresholds (integrity sweep); **CA complete-workflow sweep** through all three compliance steps (notice warranted вҶ’ period satisfied вҶ’ disposal allowed).

`traderview-expense::tenant_accessible_parking` is the **rental property tenant accessible parking accommodation right module** вҖ” when must a trader-landlord (1) provide accessible parking spaces as a matter of design and construction requirements, AND (2) grant a reasonable accommodation request from a disabled tenant for reserved or accessible parking? Trader-landlord critical for any multifamily property owner: refusal to grant reasonable parking accommodation exposes owner to FHA enforcement + HUD complaints + private right of action + significant civil penalties. Distinct from siblings `emotional_support_animal_documentation` (ESA accommodation framework), `service_animal` (ADA service animal rules), `fha_design_construction` (FHA В§ 3604(f)(3)(C) generally), and `fair_chance_housing` (criminal-background screening).

**Three regimes** вҖ” Federal FHA universal vs California FEHA extension vs Default:

| Regime | Authority | State extension |
|--------|-----------|------------------|
| Federal FHA only | 42 USC В§ 3604(f) + 24 CFR В§В§ 100.204, 100.205(c) | No |
| California | + Cal. Gov. Code В§В§ 12955(c) + 12927(c) FEHA + Cal. Civ. Code В§ 54.1 Disabled Persons Act | **YES** ($4K+ statutory damages) |
| Default | Federal FHA only (ADA Title III not applicable to private residential) | No |

**В§ 100.204 reasonable accommodation three-prong test** вҖ” applies to ALL multifamily rental housing (no covered-dwelling exception). Landlord must grant request from disabled tenant if:
1. Tenant has FHA-recognized disability
2. Accommodation necessary for equal opportunity to use and enjoy dwelling
3. Accommodation is reasonable (not unduly burdensome + no fundamental alteration of services)

Pinned by `ra_three_prong_satisfied_grant_compliant`, `ra_three_prong_satisfied_refusal_violates`, `ra_no_disability_no_obligation`, `ra_accommodation_not_necessary_no_obligation`, `ra_unreasonable_request_no_obligation`, `ra_three_prong_truth_table` (6-cell sweep вҖ” only all-three-true triggers grant requirement), and `note_pins_three_prong_test`.

**В§ 100.205(c) FHA design and construction вҖ” 2% minimum + accessible route**:
- Applies only to "covered multifamily dwellings" first occupied AFTER March 13, 1991
- Minimum 2% of parking spaces serving covered dwellings must be accessible (rounded UP, minimum 1)
- Accessible spaces must be located on accessible route
- Sufficient number of EACH TYPE (surface, garage, covered) must be accessible

Exemptions:
- Multifamily dwellings with fewer than 4 units
- Multifamily townhouses without elevator

Pinned by `dc_50_spaces_1_accessible_compliant` (2% of 50 = 1), `dc_100_spaces_2_accessible_compliant`, `dc_100_spaces_1_accessible_violates`, `dc_under_4_units_not_covered`, `dc_pre_march_13_1991_not_covered`, `dc_townhouse_without_elevator_not_covered`, `dc_not_on_accessible_route_violates`, `dc_two_violations_stack_low_count_no_route`, `dc_minimum_calculation_truth_table` (8-cell sweep), `dc_design_construction_engagement_truth_table` (6-cell sweep вҖ” only 4+ units + post-1991 + not-townhouse triggers engagement), and `note_pins_2_percent_design_construction`.

**California FEHA + Disabled Persons Act extension** вҖ” Cal. Gov. Code В§В§ 12955(c) + 12927(c) FEHA extends FHA reasonable accommodation requirements with broader coverage; Cal. Civ. Code В§ 54.1 Disabled Persons Act establishes equal right with statutory damages of at least $4,000 + attorney's fees. Pinned by `ca_state_law_extension_engaged`, `ca_citation_pins_feha_dpa`, `ca_uniquely_engages_state_extension_invariant` (CA alone engages state extension; FHA + Default do not), `fha_only_no_state_extension`, and `default_no_state_extension`.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/tenant-accessible-parking`. Twenty-six tests pin: **RA three-prong satisfied grant compliant**; **RA three-prong satisfied refusal violates** (В§ 3604(f) + В§ 100.204); **RA no disability no obligation**; **RA accommodation not necessary no obligation**; **RA unreasonable request no obligation**; **RA three-prong truth table** (6-cell sweep); **DC 50 spaces 1 accessible compliant** (2% of 50 = 1); **DC 100 spaces 2 accessible compliant**; **DC 100 spaces 1 accessible violates**; **DC under 4 units not covered**; **DC pre-March 13 1991 not covered**; **DC townhouse without elevator not covered**; **DC not on accessible route violates**; **DC two violations stack low count + no route**; **DC minimum calculation truth table** (8-cell sweep: 0/1/49/50 вҶ’ 1; 51-100 вҶ’ 2; 101-200 вҶ’ 3+); **DC design and construction engagement truth table** (6-cell sweep); **CA state law extension engaged** (FEHA + В§ 12955(c) + $4,000 + Disabled Persons Act); **CA citation pins FEHA + DPA** (В§В§ 12955(c), 12927(c) + В§ 54.1); **FHA only no state extension**; **default no state extension**; **note pins FHA reasonable accommodation** (В§ 3604(f) + В§ 100.204); **note pins 2% design and construction** (В§ 100.205(c) + accessible route); **note pins exemptions** (4 units + townhouses without elevator); **note pins three-prong test**; **three regimes routed correctly**; **CA uniquely engages state extension invariant**.

`traderview-expense::tenant_assistance_animal_accommodation` is the **multi-jurisdictional tenant assistance animal accommodation framework module** вҖ” among the highest-stakes landlord exposure regimes in residential landlord-tenant law. Misclassifying an emotional support animal as a "pet" and applying pet fees, weight restrictions, breed restrictions, or refusing the accommodation is the single most common Fair Housing Act discrimination complaint received by HUD and state fair-housing agencies. Trader-landlord critical because complaints route to HUD administrative penalties (2026: $25,597 first / $63,993 second / $127,985 third+ per 24 CFR В§ 30.65, annually inflation-adjusted) PLUS DOJ Pattern-or-Practice exposure PLUS private В§ 3613 actions with actual + PUNITIVE damages + attorney fees + costs. Distinct from siblings `rental_pet_deposit_separate_security` (general pet deposit rules), `tenant_data_privacy` (HIPAA-adjacent documentation rules), `fair_chance_housing` (criminal-background screening), `landlord_self_help_eviction_prohibition`.

**Federal framework вҖ” Fair Housing Act В§ 3604(f)(3)(B) + HUD FHEO Notice 2020-01**:

| Authority | Rule |
|-----------|------|
| 42 USC В§ 3604(f)(3)(B) | Unlawful housing discrimination to refuse to make reasonable accommodations in rules, policies, practices, or services when necessary to afford disabled tenant equal opportunity to enjoy and use a dwelling |
| HUD FHEO Notice 2020-01 (January 28, 2020) | Replaced HUD 2013 guidance; defines two animal types |
| 42 USC В§ 3604(f)(9) | Direct-threat OR substantial-property-damage defense (INDIVIDUALIZED finding required) |
| 42 USC В§ 3613 | Private enforcement: actual + PUNITIVE damages + attorney fees + costs + injunctive relief |
| 24 CFR В§ 30.65 (2026) | HUD administrative penalties: $25,597 / $63,993 / $127,985 (first / second / third+) |

**Two animal types under HUD FHEO 2020-01**:

| Type | Source | Scope |
|------|--------|-------|
| **Service Animal** | ADA Title III + 28 CFR В§ 36.302(c) | Dogs (and sometimes miniature horses) individually trained to perform tasks for individual with disability |
| **Support Animal** | HUD FHEO 2020-01 | Any species providing emotional support, comfort, well-being, therapeutic benefit; need NOT be individually trained but must be necessary to ameliorate a disability symptom |

Pinned by `service_animal_engages_accommodation`, `baseline_esa_compliant`, `pet_no_accommodation_required`, `animal_type_truth_table_three_cells` (ServiceAnimal + SupportAnimal + Pet sweep).

**HUD FHEO 2020-01 вҖ” pet fee and deposit PROHIBITION**: landlords may NOT charge a pet fee or deposit for assistance animals as a condition of granting a reasonable accommodation; assistance animals are NOT pets. Tenant remains responsible for actual damage caused by the animal (general security deposit recovery). Pinned by `pet_fee_charged_for_esa_violation`.

**HUD FHEO 2020-01 вҖ” NO breed, weight, or species restrictions** for assistance animals. Pinned by `breed_or_weight_restriction_for_esa_violation`.

**HUD FHEO 2020-01 вҖ” documentation standards** вҖ” five prohibitions on landlord requests:

1. NO specific form requirement
2. NO notarized statements
3. NO statements under penalty of perjury
4. NO individual's diagnosis
5. NO detailed physical or mental impairment information

Reliable documentation may be requested ONLY when disability OR disability-related need is NON-OBVIOUS. Pinned by `excessive_documentation_requirement_violation` and `non_obvious_disability_no_documentation_violation` and `obvious_disability_no_documentation_required`.

**В§ 3604(f)(9) Direct-threat / substantial-property-damage defense** вҖ” refusal valid IF:

1. Finding is INDIVIDUALIZED (not based on generic breed reputation, stereotypes, or fears); AND
2. Cannot be eliminated by another reasonable accommodation

Pinned by `direct_threat_defense_engages_with_individualized_finding`, `substantial_property_damage_defense_engages`, `direct_threat_with_finding_engages_defense_unique_invariant`.

**California overlay вҖ” Cal. Gov. Code В§ 12955 + Cal. Civ. Code В§ 54.1 + AB 468 of 2021** вҖ” California-specific ESA documentation requires licensed mental health professional to have ESTABLISHED CLIENT RELATIONSHIP of at least 30 DAYS before issuing ESA documentation (effective January 1, 2022); must complete clinical evaluation; must comply with California professional standards. Pinned by `california_ab468_30_day_relationship_required` and `california_ab468_only_applies_to_support_animals` (service animals exempt).

**HUD administrative penalty tier progression (24 CFR В§ 30.65, 2026)**:

| Offense | Maximum |
|---------|---------|
| First offense | **$25,597** |
| Second offense (within 5 years) | **$63,993** |
| Third+ offense (within 7 years) | **$127,985** |

Pinned by `hud_first_offense_2026_penalty_25597`, `hud_second_offense_2026_penalty_63993`, `hud_third_offense_2026_penalty_127985`, `offense_history_progressive_increase_invariant`.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/tenant-assistance-animal-accommodation`. Thirty-four tests pin: **baseline ESA compliant**; **service animal engages accommodation**; **pet no accommodation required**; **no disability no accommodation**; **pet fee charged for ESA violation**; **breed or weight restriction for ESA violation**; **accommodation refusal without direct threat violation**; **excessive documentation requirement violation**; **non-obvious disability no documentation violation**; **obvious disability no documentation required**; **direct threat defense engages with individualized finding**; **substantial property damage defense engages**; **California AB 468 30-day relationship required**; **California AB 468 only applies to support animals** (service animal carveout); **HUD first offense 2026 penalty $25,597**; **HUD second offense 2026 penalty $63,993**; **HUD third offense 2026 penalty $127,985**; **offense history progressive increase invariant**; **animal type truth table three cells** (ServiceAnimal + SupportAnimal + Pet); **citation pins all authorities** (В§ 3604(f)(3)(B) + В§ 3604(f)(9) + HUD FHEO Notice 2020-01 + В§ 3613 + 24 CFR В§ 30.65 + ADA Title III + В§ 504 Rehabilitation Act + В§ 12955 + В§ 54.1 + AB 468); **note pins FHA В§ 3604(f)(3)(B) reasonable accommodation**; **note pins HUD 2020-01 two animal types**; **note pins HUD 2020-01 pet fee prohibition**; **note pins HUD 2020-01 no breed/weight/species**; **note pins documentation standards five prohibitions**; **note pins В§ 3604(f)(9) direct threat substantial damage**; **note pins ADA Title III dogs horses**; **note pins California FEHA**; **note pins California AB 468 30-day relationship**; **note pins В§ 504 Rehabilitation Act**; **note pins В§ 3613 private enforcement punitive**; **note pins HUD 2026 penalty tiers**; **multiple failures stack**; **direct threat with finding engages defense unique invariant**.

`traderview-expense::str_regulation` is the **state short-term rental (Airbnb/VRBO) regulation compliance table** вҖ” directly affects trader-landlords using Airbnb/VRBO/Booking.com. Recent legislative wave (2018-2024) reshaped the STR landscape with state tax-and-register regimes, primary-residence requirements, and major-city outright bans.

**Four regimes** across 51 jurisdictions:

| Regime                                    | States                                                             |
|-------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------|
| **State preemption**                      | FL В§ 509.032 (uniquely) вҖ” preempts local STR regulation, only pre-June-2011 ordinances grandfathered |
| **State tax + registration**              | MA M.G.L. c. 64G (5.7% state excise eff. 2019), VT 32 V.S.A. В§ 9301 (3% surcharge eff. Aug 2024 + 270-day primary residence) |
| **Local authority with major-city rules** | CA / DC / HI / IL / LA / NJ / NV / NY / OR / WA вҖ” state leaves authority to localities; major cities have notable restrictions |
| **Local authority, no state rule**        | 38 other states вҖ” localities may or may not regulate independently |

**FL В§ 509.032 is the only state preemption regime.** Cities and counties may not prohibit STRs or regulate duration/frequency, unless pre-June-1, 2011 ordinances are grandfathered. Pinned by `fl_state_preemption_complies_without_registration` (no registration required, complies) + `fl_only_state_with_preemption_regime` (sweep verifying no other state has StatePreemption).

**MA M.G.L. c. 64G 5.7% state excise** is load-bearing. State excise of 570 basis points required on top of local up to 6% (6.5% Boston) plus community impact fee for professional operators. Pinned by `ma_state_excise_5_7_required` (570 bp threshold) + `ma_under_remittance_violates` (500 bp insufficient).

**VT Act 183 of 2024 (eff. Aug 1, 2024) is strictest residency in country.** 270-day primary residence requirement plus 3% STR surcharge. Pinned by `vt_270_day_residency_required` (269 days fails) + `vt_270_day_exact_boundary_satisfies` (270 exact = OK) + `vt_3_percent_surcharge_required` (300 bp) + `vt_strictest_residency_requirement_270_days` (sweep verifying no state exceeds 270).

**NYC LL 18 (passed Jan 2022, enforced Sept 2023) requires 183-day primary residence + host presence + OSE registration.** Platforms (Airbnb / VRBO / Booking) cannot process unregistered listings. Pinned by `ny_183_day_primary_residence_required` (182 days fails) + `ny_183_day_exact_satisfies` (183 = OK) + `ny_ll18_registration_required`.

**DC mirrors the 183-day primary residence threshold** under D.C. Code В§ 47-2829. Pinned by `dc_183_day_residency`.

**Honolulu Bill 41 (CO 22-7, 2022)** вҖ” 30-day minimum residential (court injunction blocked 30вҶ’90 expansion); $1,000 registration + $500 annual. Major-city regime. Pinned by `hi_honolulu_registration_required_for_major_city_regime`.

**LocalAuthorityWithMajorCityRules vs LocalAuthorityNoStateRule distinction**:

- **WithMajorCityRules** triggers registration requirement at the state-table level (city-specific compliance assumed)
- **NoStateRule** doesn't require registration at the state level (compliance defaults to whatever local ordinance exists)

Pinned by `ca_local_authority_with_major_city_no_state_residency` (CA has no state residency rule but registration is required for the major-city regime) + `tx_local_authority_no_state_rule_lenient` (TX default input complies even without registration).

**Major city carve-outs surfaced in citation**: CA (SF 90-day unhosted cap + LA HSO), CO (Denver primary residence + license), HI (Honolulu Bill 41), IL (Chicago SHO + Cook County), LA (New Orleans 2023 ban), NJ (Jersey City + Hoboken), NV (Las Vegas + Clark County), NY (NYC LL 18), OR (Portland TLT), WA (Seattle).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/str-regulation-check`. Twenty-two tests pin: 51-row coverage; **FL state preemption** complies without registration + uniqueness sweep; **MA 5.7% state excise** required + under-remittance violates + registration required; **VT 270-day primary residence** at 269/270 boundary + 3% surcharge required + sweep verifying VT is strictest (no state > 270); **NY 183-day residency** at 182/183 boundary; NY LL 18 registration required; DC 183-day mirror; HI Honolulu registration; CA major-city regime has no state residency but still requires registration; TX no-state-rule lenient default; unknown state errors; case-insensitive lookup; sorted all_states; non-empty citations; **FL-only StatePreemption regime sweep**; **MA + VT StateTaxAndRegistration regime sweep**.

`traderview-expense::squatter_unauthorized_occupant_removal` is the **squatter / unauthorized-occupant removal procedures** module вҖ” state-by-state pathway for evicting a person occupying property without any lease right. Critical for absentee trader-landlords whose property is occupied during periods of non-occupancy or after a tenancy ends with a holdover that lacks any colorable claim. Distinct from `adverse_possession_claim` (statutory title acquisition via 5-25 year continuous occupation), `eviction_notices` (formal eviction process for actual tenants), `holdover_tenant_damages` (damages against tenants holding over past lease term), and `landlord_harassment` (anti-retaliation framework).

**Five regimes**:

| Regime | Authority | Removal pathway | Timeline |
|--------|-----------|-------------------|----------|
| **Florida** | Fla. Stat. В§ 82.036 (HB 621, eff. July 1, 2024) | **EXPEDITED 24-HOUR SHERIFF REMOVAL** on verified owner affidavit + valid ID; no court order required | ~24 hours after sheriff posts notice |
| **New York** | RPAPL В§В§ 711(1), 713 (April 22, 2024 amendment) | В§ 713 summary holdover proceeding + 10-day notice to quit; squatters now excluded from "tenant" definition under В§ 711(1) so police may arrest as trespassers | 10-day notice + summary proceeding |
| **California** | Cal. Civ. Proc. В§ 1161 | Unlawful detainer with 3-day notice + UD complaint + judgment + writ of possession; no expedited squatter pathway | 3-6 weeks uncontested |
| **Texas** | Tex. Prop. Code В§В§ 24.005, 24.005(c), 24.002 | Forcible Entry and Detainer (FED) in Justice of the Peace court with 3-day notice to vacate; В§ 24.005(c) expressly authorizes 3-day notice for tenant-at-sufferance / squatter | 2-4 weeks uncontested |
| **Default** | common-law ejectment + state-specific summary procedure | Common-law ejectment action; self-help universally prohibited (exposes landlord to `lockout_penalties` damages) | varies |

**Florida HB 621 (signed March 27, 2024, effective July 1, 2024) is the strongest pro-owner squatter law in the US.** Four eligibility prerequisites must all be satisfied: (a) individual unlawfully entered and remains; (b) owner directed individual to leave; (c) individual is neither current/former tenant in legal dispute nor family member; (d) no pending occupancy litigation. Sheriff posts a 24-hour notice to vacate upon owner's verified affidavit + valid ID; physical removal follows if non-compliant. Pinned by `fl_full_eligibility_yields_24_hour_sheriff_removal`, `fl_current_former_tenant_disqualifies_expedited_removal`, `fl_pending_litigation_disqualifies`, `fl_owner_must_direct_to_leave`, `fl_no_verified_affidavit_no_pathway`, `fl_unique_24_hour_pathway_invariant` (5-regime sweep confirming only FL has 24-hour sheriff pathway), `fl_eligibility_combined_failures_yields_multiple_violations` (3 simultaneous gaps = 3 eligibility-violation notes).

**В§ 82.036(8) felony criminal exposure for fraudulent documents or property damage.** False legal authority claims, fraudulent documents (forged lease), or property damage during unauthorized occupancy trigger felony charges. Florida UNIQUELY carries criminal exposure flag вҖ” pinned by `florida_uniquely_carries_criminal_exposure_invariant` (5-regime sweep confirms only FL surfaces criminal exposure) and `fl_criminal_exposure_persists_even_when_eligibility_fails` (criminal flag survives even when civil removal pathway is unavailable).

**New York's April 22, 2024 RPAPL В§ 711(1) amendment abolished the 30-day occupancy threshold.** Pre-amendment NY law gave squatters tenant-like protections after 30 days of occupancy. The 2024 amendment explicitly defines "tenant" to EXCLUDE squatters and clarifies that squatters may be arrested as trespassers without an eviction order. The В§ 713 summary holdover proceeding (10-day notice to quit) remains available for procedural certainty. Pinned by `ny_post_april_2024_with_notice_yields_section_713_holdover`, `ny_pre_april_2024_warning_note_present`, `ny_amendment_abolishes_30_day_threshold_note`, `ny_post_amendment_note_describes_police_arrest_authority`.

**Self-help removal universally prohibited.** California and Default regime notes both surface the self-help warning вҖ” exposing the landlord to `lockout_penalties` damages framework. Pinned by `ca_self_help_removal_warning_in_notes`, `default_self_help_warning_in_notes`.

**Universal applicability gate вҖ” unauthorized entry required.** When the individual entered the property with permission (former tenant holding over, family member, etc.), the squatter removal pathway does not engage and standard eviction procedures apply. Pinned by `no_unauthorized_entry_no_pathway_all_regimes` (5-regime sweep).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/squatter-unauthorized-occupant-removal`. Twenty-nine tests pin: **FL full eligibility yields 24-hour sheriff removal**; **FL current/former tenant disqualifies expedited removal**; **FL pending litigation disqualifies**; **FL owner must direct to leave**; **FL no verified affidavit no pathway**; **FL fraudulent documents trigger felony charges** (В§ 82.036(8)); **NY post-April-2024 with notice yields В§ 713 holdover**; **NY pre-April-2024 warning note present** (30-day threshold); **NY no 10-day notice no pathway**; **CA with 3-day notice yields unlawful detainer**; **CA no 3-day notice no pathway**; **TX with 3-day notice yields FED action** (Justice of the Peace court); **TX no 3-day notice no pathway**; **default yields common-law ejectment**; **no unauthorized entry no pathway all regimes** (5-regime sweep); **FL unique 24-hour pathway invariant** (5-regime sweep); **citation FL pins HB 621 + В§ 82.036 + В§ 82.036(8) + July 1, 2024**; **citation NY pins В§В§ 711(1), 713 + April 22, 2024 amendment**; **citation CA pins В§ 1161**; **citation TX pins В§В§ 24.005, 24.005(c), 24.002**; **citation default pins common-law ejectment**; **FL criminal exposure persists even when eligibility fails**; **NY post-amendment note describes police arrest authority**; **CA self-help removal warning in notes**; **default self-help warning in notes**; **FL eligibility fails when owner did not direct to leave**; **FL eligibility combined failures yields multiple violations** (3 simultaneous gaps); **NY amendment abolishes 30-day threshold note**; **Florida uniquely carries criminal exposure invariant** (5-regime sweep).

`traderview-expense::pet_fees` is the **state pet deposit / pet rent / pet fee compliance table** вҖ” directly relevant to trader-landlords screening pet-owning applicants. Four regimes classify how each state regulates the money a landlord may charge for a tenant's pet:

| Regime                                  | States | Source                                                                |
|-----------------------------------------|--------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------|
| **SpecificPetDepositAndRentCap**        | CO     | CRS В§ 38-12-106 (HB 23-1068, eff. 2024-01-01) вҖ” pet deposit вүӨ $300 refundable + pet rent вүӨ max($35, 1.5% Г— monthly rent) ([Colorado General Assembly HB23-1068](http://leg.colorado.gov/bills/hb23-1068)) |
| **TotalDepositCapAbsorbsPet**           | CA, WA | Cal. Civ. Code В§ 1950.5 (AB 12, eff. 2024-07-01); WA RCW 59.18.260 вҖ” no SEPARATE pet deposit cap, but overall security deposit (1 month rent in CA) absorbs any pet portion ([Long Beach PM, AB 12](https://www.longbeachpropertymgmt.com/blog/ab12-maximum-security-deposit-in-california-changes-july-2024)) |
| **NoSeparatePetDepositAllowed**         | MA     | MGL c.186 В§ 15B вҖ” pet deposit BANNED; monthly pet rent permitted by case law ([MassLandlords](https://masslandlords.net/is-it-legal-to-charge-pet-rent-or-a-pet-deposit-for-tenants-with-animals/)) |
| **NoStateRule**                         | 47 other states + DC | Overall security deposit framework applies; no pet-specific cap |

**Federal-floor preemption (FHA + ADA)**: under 42 U.S.C. В§ 3604(f) and 42 U.S.C. В§ 12101, service animals AND emotional support animals are NOT pets вҖ” no pet deposit, no pet rent, no pet fee may be charged for an ESA / service animal in any state regardless of state regime. The module surfaces this as `federal_fha_ada_exemption_applies` and overrides the state-level caps with zero-charge enforcement.

**Colorado HB 23-1068 specifics pinned in the rule table**: pet deposit вүӨ $300 refundable, pet rent вүӨ max($35, 1.5% Г— monthly rent), breed-based insurance discrimination banned, landlord pet lien banned. As an example calculation: at $1,000 rent, 1.5% Г— $1,000 = $15 < $35 вҶ’ cap = $35; at $5,000 rent, 1.5% Г— $5,000 = $75 > $35 вҶ’ cap = $75 (the percentage takes over).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/pet-fees-check`. Twenty-two tests pin: **CO pet deposit at $300 exact complies / $301 violates**; **CO pet rent flat-$35 dominates at low rent** ($1k rent вҶ’ cap $35) + **percentage-of-rent dominates at high rent** ($5k rent вҶ’ cap $75) + **1.5% exact boundary at $4k rent вҶ’ cap = $60 exactly**; CO percentage rounds down at low rent (1.5% Г— $1 = 0 вҶ’ cap falls back to $35 flat); CO pinned breed-insurance + pet-lien bans on the rule table; **CA no separate pet deposit cap вҖ” TotalDepositCapAbsorbsPet regime** surfaces correctly; **MA separate pet deposit banned** (any non-zero deposit violates); MA zero-deposit + monthly pet rent complies; **NoStateRule 5-state sweep** (FL/TX/NY/NJ/IL accept any amount); **federal FHA + ADA service animal / ESA preemption** вҖ” zero-charge override in CO + TX (no-rule state) + CA with state-rule states; service animal with zero charges no violation; unknown state falls back to NoStateRule; lowercase state code normalizes; **51-state coverage assertion**; all citations non-empty; CO full-violation compound path ($400 deposit + $50/mo rent at $1k rent вҶ’ $100 deposit + $15 rent violations); MA zero deposit + pet rent complies; note describes CO explicit-cap path; note describes MA ban path.

`traderview-expense::non_refundable_cleaning_fees` is the **non-refundable cleaning / move-in fee enforceability check** вҖ” when a landlord labels a fee as "non-refundable" in the lease, is it actually enforceable as such, or does state law convert it into a refundable security deposit subject to deposit-cap and itemized-deduction rules? Distinct from `pet_fees` (pet-specific deposits and fees), `application_fees` (pre-tenancy screening fee caps), `damage_deduction_itemization` (post-tenancy deduction requirements), and `security_deposit_caps` (statutory caps on deposit amount) вҖ” this module addresses ONLY the threshold question of whether the "non-refundable" label survives.

**Five regimes** with sharp divergence:

| Regime | Authority | Default enforceability | Consequence of non-compliance |
|--------|-----------|--------------------------|--------------------------------|
| **California** | Cal. Civ. Code В§ 1950.5(n)/(b)/(m) | STRICT PROHIBITION (regardless of disclosure) | Fee treated as refundable security deposit; В§ 1950.5(m) limits cleaning deduction to inception cleanliness level |
| **Texas** | Tex. Prop. Code Ch. 92 + В§ 92.103 | PERMITTED with lease disclosure | Undisclosed fees treated as security deposit subject to В§ 92.103 refund obligation |
| **Washington** | RCW 59.18.285 + RCW 59.18.260/.270/.280 | PERMITTED with written lease + clear non-refundable designation | No written lease вҶ’ landlord LIABLE for full fee; written lease without designation вҶ’ treated as refundable deposit |
| **New York** | N.Y. Gen. Oblig. Law В§ 7-108(1-a) (HSTPA 2019) | IMPLICIT PROHIBITION (1-month security cap + advance-payment limit) | Fee treated as part of voided advance payment; statute makes no carve-out for "non-refundable" label |
| **Default** | common-law contract | PERMITTED with written lease disclosure | Treated as refundable deposit if oral or undisclosed |

**California is UNIQUELY STRICT** вҖ” the В§ 1950.5(b) "security" definition is broad enough to capture cleaning fees, key deposits, move-in fees, pet deposits, last month's rent, and "closing costs." All of these are refundable regardless of lease label. The only exceptions are application screening fees, holding deposits, and total destruction of property. Pinned by `ca_always_treats_nonrefundable_as_refundable_violation`, `ca_violation_persists_even_with_lease_disclosure`, `california_uniquely_strict_prohibition_invariant` (4-regime sweep confirms CA blocks while TX/WA/Default permit), and `ca_notes_security_breadth_and_cleaning_deduction_rule`.

**Washington's RCW 59.18.285 is uniquely two-tiered.** Tier 1 (no written lease) вҶ’ landlord LIABLE TO TENANT for the full amount of fees collected. Tier 2 (written lease lacking non-refundable specification) вҶ’ fee treated as refundable deposit. Tier 3 (fully compliant) вҶ’ fee enforceable. Pinned by `wa_no_written_lease_landlord_liable_for_full_fee`, `wa_written_lease_no_nonrefundable_clause_treated_as_refundable`, `wa_full_compliance_fee_enforceable`, and `washington_uniquely_imposes_landlord_liability_for_no_written_lease` (4-regime sweep confirms only WA imposes full-fee liability).

**New York's HSTPA 2019 implicit prohibition persists regardless of advance-payment math.** Even when total advance (first month rent + security deposit + cleaning fee) fits within the cap, the statute provides no carve-out for fees labeled "non-refundable" вҖ” the implicit prohibition voids them outright. Pinned by `newyork_advance_payment_within_cap_still_void_due_to_implicit_prohibition`.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/non-refundable-cleaning-fees`. Twenty-two tests pin: **CA always treats nonrefundable as refundable violation**; **CA violation persists even with lease disclosure** (strict prohibition regardless of compliance); **CA notes security breadth and cleaning deduction rule** (В§ 1950.5(b) + В§ 1950.5(m)); **CA notes exception list** (application screening + holding deposits); **TX disclosed nonrefundable fee enforceable**; **TX undisclosed fee treated as refundable** (В§ 92.103); **WA no written lease landlord liable for full fee** (RCW 59.18.285 liability tier); **WA written lease no nonrefundable clause treated as refundable** (RCW 59.18.260/.270/.280 cascade); **WA full compliance fee enforceable**; **WA notes no-deposit-designation rule**; **NY advance payment cap violation when total exceeds**; **NY implicit prohibition persists even with disclosure**; **default disclosed in written lease enforceable**; **default undisclosed treated as refundable**; **default oral lease treated as refundable**; **citation CA pins subsections** (В§ 1950.5(n)/(b)/(m)); **citation TX pins Chapter 92 + В§ 92.103**; **citation WA pins RCW 59.18.285 + deposit framework**; **citation NY pins В§ 7-108(1-a) HSTPA 2019**; **California uniquely strict prohibition invariant** (4-regime sweep CA blocks, TX/WA/Default permit); **Washington uniquely imposes landlord liability for no written lease** (4-regime sweep); **New York advance payment within cap still void due to implicit prohibition**.

`traderview-expense::eviction_record_sealing` is the **state eviction record sealing / "clean slate" compliance table** вҖ” directly relevant to trader-landlords running tenant screening reports. Since 2020 a wave of state laws either auto-seal eviction court records, restrict landlords from using them for screening, or both. Per [PolicyLink, 2024](https://www.policylink.org/resources/tools/housing-anti-displacement/eviction-records-tenant-screening-protections), 17 states + DC now have some form of sealing law; this module pins the five with the strongest current legal frameworks plus the petition states. Four regimes:

| Regime                       | States                     | Source                                                                |
|------------------------------|----------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------|
| **AutomaticSealing**         | CA, CT, NV, MD, MN         | CA Civ. Proc. В§ 1161.2 (AB 2819, eff. 2017); CT Public Act 23-207 (SB 998, eff. 2024-07-01); NRS 40.2545 (AB 107 of 2017 + AB 161 of 2021); MD Real Prop. В§ 8-401 (SB 19 of 2024); Minn. Stat. В§ 504B.345 (SF 3492 of 2024) |
| **TenantPetitionOnly**       | WA, OR, IL, DC             | WA RCW 59.18.367; Or. SB 282 of 2021; Ill. 735 ILCS 5/9-121; D.C. Eviction Record Sealing Authority Act of 2022 |
| **PandemicPeriodOnly**       | NJ                         | N.J. P.L. 2021 c. 188 (A 4463) вҖ” pandemic relief; no permanent law    |
| **NoStateRule**              | 41 other states            | FCRA 15 U.S.C. В§ 1681c 7-year limit on civil judgments applies        |

**California AB 2819 (CCP В§ 1161.2)** is the prototype: from the moment an unlawful detainer is FILED, the case is masked from public view for **60 days**; if the landlord wins a judgment within that window, the case becomes public, otherwise it stays sealed forever ([Bornstein Law](https://bornstein.law/california-eviction-records-sealed/)). Module models this as the 60-day window with all four outcomes eligible.

**Connecticut SB 998 (Public Act 23-207, eff. 2024-07-01)** removes a tenant's eviction record from the court system's public view within **30 days** for cases where (a) the eviction was withdrawn by the landlord, (b) the tenant won their case, or (c) the case was dismissed by the judge. The law also makes it ILLEGAL to sell the record to any third-party tenant-screening company ([WFSB News, 2023](https://www.wfsb.com/2023/07/05/new-ct-law-expands-renters-rights-erases-some-eviction-records-limits-application-fees/)). Module models the 30-day window with only the three tenant-favorable outcomes triggering auto-seal; landlord-won judgments REMAIN public.

**Minnesota SF 3492 (eff. 2024)** is the strictest: **same-day** mandatory expungement on dismissed/withdrawn cases, tenant-won cases, both-parties-agree expungement, OR three years after the eviction was ordered against the tenant ([Loebsack & Brownlee](https://loebsackbrownlee.com/new-bills-seek-to-seal-and-expunge-eviction-records-for-tenants/)). Modeled with `auto_seal_window_days: Some(0)`.

**Maryland SB 19 of 2024** auto-shields eviction records within 60 days where the case did NOT result in physical removal of the tenant within 60 days of judgment ([Maryland Matters, 2024](https://marylandmatters.org/2024/08/26/landlords-cry-foul-as-more-states-seal-eviction-records/)).

**Nevada NRS 40.2545 (AB 107 of 2017 + AB 161 of 2021)** auto-seals summary eviction records 31 days after dismissed/withdrawn/tenant-won outcomes; landlord-won judgments require petition.

**Federal floor (FCRA 15 U.S.C. В§ 1681c(a)(2))**: civil judgments more than 7 years old cannot appear on tenant screening consumer reports regardless of state sealing law. State sealing laws layer ON TOP of this вҖ” most push the timeline far earlier than the FCRA window.

**Screening-use restriction flag** (`screening_use_restricted_by_state`) is set for CA, CT, MD (auto-seal + screening ban) AND for all four petition states (WA, IL, OR, DC). NV and MN currently do not have an explicit screening-side ban. This distinguishes "court hides the record" (sealing) from "landlord can't use the record even if visible" (screening restriction) вҖ” different liability surfaces.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/eviction-sealing-check`. Twenty-eight tests pin: **CA 60-day auto-mask covers all outcomes** (landlord-won @ 60d eligible; @ 59d not yet); **CT 30-day dismissed/tenant-won qualifies** (day 29 not yet / day 30 exact boundary); **CT landlord-won does NOT auto-seal** + note explains "does NOT qualify"; **MN same-day expungement** on dismissed; **NV 31-day window** dismissed qualifies / landlord-won doesn't; **MD 60-day settlement qualifies**; **WA petition required вҖ” without petition no sealing, with petition eligible**; OR + IL + DC petition-only regime sweep; **NJ pandemic-only** non-pandemic case ineligible, pandemic case eligible + COVID-era note; **5-state NoStateRule sweep** (TX/FL/NY/PA/OH never eligible); NoStateRule note cites FCRA floor; **screening_use_restricted flag for all 3 auto-seal-plus-screening states** (CA/CT/MD) and all 4 petition states; unknown state fallback; lowercase state code normalizes; **51-state coverage**; non-empty citations; **AutomaticSealing regime invariant** (every AutoSeal state has window set); **non-AutoSeal regime invariant** (no other regime has window); note describes partial window progress ("15 of 30 days").

`traderview-expense::lease_termination_notice` is the **state landlord-side lease termination / non-renewal notice period compliance table** вҖ” every landlord-trader ending a month-to-month tenancy, declining renewal, or raising rent must hit statutory minimum advance notice that varies sharply (20-180 days) across states by tenancy length, intent, and just-cause regime. Four regimes:

| Regime                              | States                | Source                                                                |
|-------------------------------------|-----------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------|
| **TieredByTenancyLength**           | NY (30/60/90 by tenancy < 1yr / 1-2yr / 2+yr); DE (60-day landlord) | N.Y. RPL В§ 226-c (HSTPA 2019); Del. Code tit. 25 В§ 5106 |
| **JustCauseAfterTwelveMonths**      | CA, OR                | Cal. Civ. Proc. В§ 1946.1 + Tenant Protection Act 2019 (AB 1482); Or. SB 608 of 2019 (ORS 90.427) |
| **StatewideJustCauseAlways**        | WA, NJ                | Wash. RCW 59.18.650 (Engrossed SSB 5160 of 2021); N.J.S.A. 2A:18-61.1 (Anti-Eviction Act 1974) |
| **StandardThirtyDay**               | 46 other states + DC  | Default 30-day month-to-month termination; GA 60-day landlord variant (В§ 44-7-7) |

**NY HSTPA RPL В§ 226-c (2019)** is the prototype tiered framework: landlords must give **30 days' notice** if the tenant has occupied for less than 1 year, **60 days** for 1-2 years, and **90 days** for 2+ years, regardless of whether the lease is expiring or the tenancy is month-to-month. Failure to give the proper notice means the tenancy continues on the same terms until proper notice is given and the required time passes ([Dibbini Law вҖ” HSTPA Termination Notices](https://dibbinilaw.com/understanding-the-new-notice-requirements-under-hstpa-for-lease-termination-non-renewal/), [N.Y. RPL В§ 226-c](https://www.nysenate.gov/legislation/laws/RPP/226-C)).

**Oregon SB 608 (2019, ORS 90.427)** is the prototype just-cause-after-12-months framework: during the first year, landlord can terminate month-to-month for ANY reason with **30 days' notice**; after 12 months, termination requires one of the enumerated just-cause reasons AND **90 days' written notice** describing the qualifying reason (demolition, owner move-in, immediate family residence, substantial repairs, etc.) ([Oregon Realtors SB 608 Summary](https://oregonrealtors.org/sites/default/files/SB%20608%20.%202019%20.%20FAQs.pdf)).

**Washington RCW 59.18.650 (Engrossed SSB 5160 of 2021)** is the strictest framework: just cause required from **day 1** of tenancy. 20-day no-cause termination by landlord is now ILLEGAL with very few exceptions; tenants are protected under statewide just-cause at every tenancy length.

**Rent increase notice** is a separate axis. CA: **30 days for increases вүӨ 10%, 90 days for > 10%**; OR: **90 days for вүӨ 10%, 180 days for > 10%**. The module pins these separately under `rent_increase_notice_days_under_10_pct` / `_over_10_pct` and dispatches on `TerminationIntent::RentIncrease` with `rent_increase_pct_bp` (basis points; 1000 = 10.00%).

**Just-cause-required regimes set `no_cause_termination_unavailable: true`** when the tenant has been in occupancy past the statewide threshold (12 months for CA/OR, 0 months for WA/NJ) AND the intent is `NoCauseTermination` AND `has_qualifying_just_cause: false`. Landlords with a qualifying cause path must check via `JustCauseTermination` intent with `has_qualifying_just_cause: true`.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/termination-notice-check`. Twenty-nine tests pin: **NY tiered** under-1yr/30d / 1-2yr/60d / 2yr+/90d complete with 1-2yr 30d insufficient + 24mo exact-boundary uses 2yr+ tier; **CA TPA path** вҖ” under-12mo no-cause complies / over-12mo no-cause UNAVAILABLE / over-12mo just-cause with 60d complies; **OR SB 608** вҖ” under-12mo no-cause 30d complies / after-12mo no-cause UNAVAILABLE / after-12mo just-cause 90d complies; **WA day-one just-cause required** + just-cause termination complies with 20d; **NJ Anti-Eviction Act no-cause unavailable always**; **TX default-30 complies / 29d insufficient**; **DE + GA 60d landlord notice required** (sweep); **rent increase pathway**: CA under-10% 30d complies, CA over-10% 90d required, OR over-10% 180d required; rent-increase intent bypasses no-cause-unavailable (raising rent is not termination); **51-state coverage**; non-empty citations; unknown state falls back to 30-day; lowercase normalizes; **regime invariant**: JustCauseAfterTwelveMonths вҶ’ threshold Some(12), StatewideJustCauseAlways вҶ’ Some(0), others вҶ’ None; note describes tiered path including the "30/60/90" tier shorthand; note describes the no-cause-unavailable path.

`traderview-expense::lease_termination_catastrophic_damage` is the **tenant lease termination right for catastrophic property damage** (fire, flood, hurricane, earthquake, explosion, similar casualty) вҖ” trader-landlord operational concern after natural disaster or casualty loss. Distinct from `dv_termination` (DV survivor termination), `military_termination` (SCRA-based PCS termination), `crime_victim_termination` (state-broader crime-victim termination), and `habitability_remedies` (ongoing repair / withholding / rent escrow) вҖ” this module addresses ONLY the CATASTROPHIC-DAMAGE TERMINATION pathway after a casualty loss.

**Five regimes**:

| Regime | Authority | Total destruction | Partial destruction | Tenant-fault gate |
|--------|-----------|---------------------|----------------------|---------------------|
| **California** | Cal. Civ. Code В§В§ 1932(2), 1933(4), 1950.5(g) | В§ 1933(4) AUTOMATIC termination if no landlord/tenant fault | В§ 1932(2) tenant election on "greater part" destruction | yes вҖ” defeats termination |
| **Texas** | Tex. Prop. Code В§В§ 92.054(a)/(b)/(c), 92.052(b) | В§ 92.054(b) "totally unusable" + written notice before repairs вҶ’ mutual termination right | partial damage does NOT satisfy В§ 92.054 standard | yes |
| **New York** | N.Y. Real Prop. Law В§ 227 | tenant SURRENDER POSSESSION right (affirmative election) | В§ 227 not triggered by partial damage | yes |
| **New Jersey** | N.J.S.A. 46:8-6, 46:8-7, 46:8-8 | В§ 46:8-6 destruction-of-premises termination | В§ 46:8-7 partial вҶ’ terminate OR PROPORTIONALLY REDUCED RENT | В§ 46:8-8 fault attribution |
| **Default** | Restatement (Second) of Contracts В§ 261 | impossibility of performance вҶ’ lease terminates | continues with rent abatement | universal common-law principle |

**Texas uniquely requires written notice + insurance-proceeds-triggered repair period.** В§ 92.054(c) requires the terminating party (landlord OR tenant) to give written notice BEFORE repairs are completed. В§ 92.052(b) provides the uniquely Texas procedural rule that the landlord's repair period does NOT begin until insurance proceeds are received. Pinned by `texas_uniquely_requires_written_notice_invariant` (4-regime sweep confirms only TX requires written notice formally; CA/NY/NJ/Default permit termination on facts alone), `tx_no_written_notice_termination_unavailable`, `tx_repairs_completed_termination_unavailable`, `tx_insurance_proceeds_trigger_repair_period_note`.

**California В§ 1933(4) automatic termination vs В§ 1932(2) tenant election distinction.** В§ 1933(4) automatically terminates the lease on entire destruction WITHOUT landlord or tenant fault. В§ 1932(2) permits tenant election on greater-part destruction. If landlord fault is present, В§ 1933(4) automatic path is unavailable but tenant may still elect В§ 1932(2). Pinned by `ca_totally_destroyed_automatic_termination`, `ca_greater_part_destroyed_tenant_election_termination`, `ca_landlord_fault_blocks_automatic_termination_under_1933` (tenant may still elect), `ca_21_day_deposit_refund_note_always_present` (В§ 1950.5(g) 21-day refund).

**New Jersey В§ 46:8-7 partial destruction permits proportional rent reduction in lieu of termination.** Unique among the regimes вҖ” tenant may elect to terminate OR continue at proportionally reduced rent for the unusable portion. Pinned by `nj_partial_destruction_choice_terminate_or_proportional_rent`, `nj_minor_partial_damage_continued_tenancy_with_reduction`, `nj_proportional_rent_reduction_distinct_from_ca` (cross-regime distinction at GreaterPart level).

**Tenant fault universally defeats catastrophic-damage termination.** All five regimes block the termination right when the tenant (or family member, guest, or invitee) caused the damage. Pinned by `tenant_fault_blocks_termination_all_regimes` (5-regime sweep confirms all block termination on tenant fault).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/lease-termination-catastrophic-damage`. Twenty-seven tests pin: **CA totally destroyed automatic termination** (В§ 1933(4) without fault); **CA greater part destroyed tenant election termination** (В§ 1932(2)); **CA partial damage no termination** (lease continues with abatement); **CA landlord fault blocks automatic termination under В§ 1933(4)** but tenant may elect В§ 1932(2); **CA В§ 1950.5(g) 21-day deposit refund note always present**; **TX totally unusable with written notice before repairs termination** (В§ 92.054(b)); **TX partial damage no termination right under В§ 92.054**; **TX no written notice termination unavailable** (В§ 92.054(c)); **TX repairs completed termination unavailable**; **TX insurance proceeds trigger repair period note** (В§ 92.052(b) uniquely Texas); **NY totally destroyed surrender termination** (RPL В§ 227); **NY greater part also surrender termination**; **NY partial damage no termination continued tenancy**; **NY tenant must affirmatively elect surrender note**; **NJ totally destroyed termination under В§ 46:8-6**; **NJ partial destruction choice terminate or proportional rent** (В§ 46:8-7); **NJ minor partial damage continued tenancy with reduction**; **default total destruction terminates under impossibility** (Restatement (Second) of Contracts В§ 261); **default partial no termination with abatement**; **tenant fault blocks termination all regimes** (5-regime sweep); **citation CA pins В§В§ 1932(2), 1933(4), 1950.5(g)**; **citation TX pins В§ 92.054(a)/(b)/(c) + В§ 92.052(b)**; **citation NY pins В§ 227**; **citation NJ pins В§ 46:8-6/7/8**; **citation default pins Restatement В§ 261**; **Texas uniquely requires written notice invariant** (4-regime sweep); **NJ proportional rent reduction distinct from CA** (cross-regime distinction at GreaterPart level).

`traderview-expense::occupancy_standards` is the **federal HUD + state occupancy standards compliance table** вҖ” foundational rule for tenant screening that traps unwary landlord-traders into Fair Housing Act В§ 3604 familial-status discrimination claims when the limit is set too low. The federal floor is the **HUD Keating Memorandum** (1991 internal, formalized in 63 FR 70982 on 1998-12-18): a presumption that **2 persons per bedroom is reasonable**, but REBUTTABLE based on bedroom size, septic / fire code, configuration, and AGE of children. The memo explicitly prohibits using occupancy limits as a PRETEXT for excluding families with children ([HUD Exchange вҖ” Occupancy Standards (Keating Memorandum)](https://www.hudexchange.info/resource/6632/occupancy-standards-keating-memorandum-63-fr-70982/), [Fair Housing Forum primer](https://fairhousingforum.org/uncategorized/keating-memo/)).

Four regime classification:

| Regime                                       | States   | Source                                                                |
|----------------------------------------------|----------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------|
| **SqftPerOccupantFormula**                   | NY, MA   | NYC Admin Code В§ 27-2075 (80 sqft livable/person); Mass. 105 CMR 410 (150 sqft first occupant + 100 sqft each additional) |
| **TwoPlusOneStatutory**                      | CA       | Smith v. FEHC line of cases + CA Health & Safety Code В§ 17920.3 + Uniform Housing Code (120 sqft first room + 50 sqft each occupant over 2 in a bedroom) |
| **NoMoreRestrictiveThanTwoPerBedroom**       | OR       | ORS 90.262 вҖ” landlord-adopted rule CAPPED at 2 per bedroom + FHCO 2+1 guidance |
| **HudKeatingDefault**                        | 47 other states + DC | HUD Keating Memo 2-per-bedroom presumption + state sanitary code overlay (WA additionally: RCW 35A.21.314 / SB 5235 of 2021 preempts city restrictions on number of unrelated occupants) |

**California's "2+1" framework** is the strictest pro-occupant standard: **2 persons per bedroom + 1 additional in the unit**. So a 2BR allows 5; a 3BR allows 7. State formula EXCEEDS the Keating presumption in CA (the Keating 2-per-BR would only allow 4 in a 2BR), and CA explicitly overrides Keating with its own statutory 2+1 floor.

**Oregon ORS 90.262** is the strictest pro-tenant overlay: a landlord's occupancy rule **cannot be more restrictive than 2 per bedroom**. The state statutorily caps landlord discretion to set tighter limits than Keating allows.

**New York City HMC В§ 27-2075** uses a pure sqft formula: total occupancy = livable floor area / 80 sqft. So a 240 sqft studio allows 3 occupants by formula but is capped by the Keating 2-per-BR overlay (0 BR вҶ’ 0 effective max under the model's literal Keating reading; in practice studios get 1-2 person allowance via local rule outside the federal-floor model). Plus a NYC-specific child-under-4 carveout: one child under 4 per pair of lawful adults.

**Massachusetts 105 CMR 410 State Sanitary Code** uses a hybrid sqft formula: **150 sqft for the first dwelling-unit occupant + 100 sqft for each additional** at the unit level, plus **70 sqft for the first sleeping-room occupant + 100 sqft combined for two + 50 sqft each additional** at the sleeping-room level. Below 150 sqft total вҶ’ zero occupants permitted.

**Federal floor (FHA В§ 3604 familial-status pretext) ALWAYS applies**: even where the state-formula and Keating presumption are met, selectively enforcing the occupancy rule against families with children but NOT against adult roommate groups is a per-se Fair Housing Act violation. The module surfaces `fha_familial_status_pretext_violation` as a separate flag that overrides `proposed_within_limit` to `false` regardless of any other compliance.

**Building code rebuttal** (Keating Memo): if septic capacity, fire code, or another building code provision caps occupancy BELOW the 2-per-bedroom presumption, the landlord may invoke that as a Keating rebuttal. Module accepts this via `building_code_caps_below_keating: true` + `building_code_max_occupants`.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/occupancy-check`. Twenty-six tests pin: **NY 80-sqft-per-person formula** (800 sqft / 80 = 10 max, but Keating 2BR = 4 binds вҶ’ effective 4); NY 5 occupants in 2BR/800sqft violates; **NY small-sqft binds below Keating** (240 sqft / 80 = 3 вҶ’ effective 3 < Keating 4); **MA first-occupant floor then per-additional** (150 + 100Г—4 = 550 sqft вҶ’ formula 5, Keating 4 binds); MA below 150 sqft zero occupants; **CA 2+1 explicit** (2BR вҶ’ 5 max), CA 3BR вҶ’ 7 max + 8 violates, CA state formula overrides Keating; **OR landlord capped at 2 per bedroom** + flag set + only-state-with-this-regime invariant; **HudKeatingDefault 2 per bedroom** for TX; WA Keating with SB 5235 / RCW 35A.21.314 citation; **building code caps below Keating** (septic cap 3 < Keating 4); **FHA familial-status pretext overrides compliance** even when within Keating + pretext violation blocks within_limit even at zero proposed; CA state familial-status pretext flag set; studio 0BR Keating zero caps at state floor; **CA studio 2+1 yields 1** (sensible 2+1 floor for studios); **51-state coverage**; non-empty citations; unknown state falls back to Keating; lowercase normalizes; **NY + MA only on SqftPerOccupantFormula** invariant; OR unique on NoMoreRestrictive invariant; note describes within-limit path; note describes exceeds-limit path.

`traderview-expense::move_in_fee_cap` is the **move-in fee cap and disclosure obligations module** вҖ” when a landlord charges a one-time non-refundable move-in fee (cleaning + screening + administrative + lease-prep), how much may that fee be, what purposes may it cover, and what disclosure must accompany it? Trader-landlord critical: misclassifying a refundable security deposit as a non-refundable fee in a regulated jurisdiction reclassifies the entire amount as a refundable deposit AND can void enforcement. Distinct from siblings `application_fees` (pre-tenancy screening cost), `late_fee_caps` (post-tenancy delinquency penalty), `advance_rent_limit` (advance rent payments), and `move_in_inspection` (procedural walk-through).

**Four regimes** вҖ” Seattle's three-layer maximum versus Washington's disclosure-only versus Chicago's no-cap-but-itemize versus Default:

| Regime | Authority | Amount cap | Permitted purposes | Combined deposit + fee cap | Disclosure required |
|--------|-----------|------------|--------------------|-----------------------------|----------------------|
| Seattle | SMC В§ 7.24.030 + RCW 59.18.285 + RCW 59.18.610 | **10% of one month's rent** | **Cleaning + screening ONLY** | **Yes вҖ” one month's rent** | Yes вҖ” non-refundable label |
| Washington | RCW 59.18.285 | None | None | None | **Yes вҖ” undisclosed reclassified as deposit** |
| Chicago | Chicago Mun. Code В§ 5-12-080 + В§ 5-12-081 (RLTO) | None | None | None | **Yes вҖ” itemized purpose list** |
| Default | Common-law unconscionability | None | None | None | None |

**Seattle SMC В§ 7.24.030 вҖ” three-layer maximum** вҖ” non-refundable fee capped at 10% of one month's rent + permitted purposes restricted to cleaning + screening only + security deposit + fees combined cannot exceed one month's rent. Pinned by `seattle_compliant_passes`, `seattle_fee_at_10_percent_boundary_compliant`, `seattle_fee_exceeds_10_percent_violates`, `seattle_pet_fee_purpose_violates`, `seattle_screening_purpose_compliant`, `seattle_combined_cap_violation`, `seattle_at_1_month_combined_boundary_compliant`, `seattle_1_cent_over_combined_violates` (precision boundary), `seattle_uniquely_engages_combined_cap_invariant` (4-regime sweep: only Seattle engages combined cap), `seattle_stacks_3_violations_when_high_fee_wrong_purpose_undisclosed`, and `fee_purpose_truth_table_seattle` (6-purpose sweep).

**Washington RCW 59.18.285 reclassification rule** вҖ” if the lease does not specify a fee as non-refundable, the fee MUST be treated as a refundable security deposit. State-wide disclosure-only obligation with no amount cap (Seattle layers caps on top). Pinned by `washington_compliant_when_disclosed`, `washington_undisclosed_reclassifies_as_deposit`, `washington_no_amount_cap_high_fee_ok`, `washington_citation_pins_rcw`, `washington_note_pins_reclassification_rule`, and `reclassification_uniquely_in_seattle_and_washington_invariant` (Seattle + Washington reclassify; Chicago + Default do not).

**Chicago RLTO Mun. Code В§ 5-12-080 вҖ” itemization-only obligation** вҖ” no amount cap on non-refundable move-in fees; landlord MUST provide itemized list detailing fee purposes. Effectively shifted Chicago landlords from regulated security deposits (under В§ 5-12-081 with interest obligations) to unregulated non-refundable move-in fees. Pinned by `chicago_compliant_with_itemized_purpose`, `chicago_no_amount_cap_high_fee_ok`, `chicago_missing_itemization_violates`, `chicago_citation_pins_rlto_sections`, `chicago_note_pins_landlords_property_rule`, and `chicago_uniquely_requires_itemized_purpose_invariant` (Chicago requires itemized list; Default does not).

**Default вҖ” no cap or disclosure obligation** вҖ” local protections may apply (Bellingham WA Aug 2025 fee laws, Portland OR, Minneapolis MN, etc.); verify jurisdiction-specific ordinances before relying on default rule. Pinned by `default_compliant_always`, `default_no_cap_or_disclosure_obligation`, and `default_note_pins_local_protections_warning` (Bellingham + Portland).

**Defensive arithmetic clamps** вҖ” negative rent / negative fee / overflow combined-sum all clamped via `.max(0)` and `.saturating_add()`. Pinned by `defensive_negative_rent_clamped`, `defensive_negative_fee_clamped`, and `defensive_overflow_combined_saturating`.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/move-in-fee-cap`. Thirty-three tests pin: **Seattle compliant passes**; **Seattle fee at 10% boundary compliant**; **Seattle fee exceeds 10% violates**; **Seattle pet fee purpose violates**; **Seattle screening purpose compliant**; **Seattle combined cap violation**; **Seattle undisclosed reclassifies as deposit**; **Seattle citation pins all three statutes**; **Washington compliant when disclosed**; **Washington undisclosed reclassifies as deposit**; **Washington no amount cap high fee OK**; **Washington citation pins RCW**; **Chicago compliant with itemized purpose**; **Chicago no amount cap high fee OK**; **Chicago missing itemization violates**; **Chicago citation pins RLTO sections**; **default compliant always**; **default no cap or disclosure obligation**; **four regimes routed correctly**; **Seattle uniquely engages combined cap invariant** (4-regime sweep); **defensive negative rent clamped**; **defensive negative fee clamped**; **fee purpose truth table Seattle** (6-purpose sweep); **Seattle stacks 3 violations when high fee wrong purpose undisclosed**; **defensive overflow combined saturating**; **Seattle note pins 10% cap and combined cap**; **Washington note pins reclassification rule**; **Chicago note pins landlords property rule**; **default note pins local protections warning**; **Seattle at 1-month combined boundary compliant**; **Seattle 1-cent over combined violates** (precision boundary); **reclassification uniquely in Seattle and Washington invariant**; **Chicago uniquely requires itemized purpose invariant**.

`traderview-expense::move_in_inspection` is the **state move-in / move-out inspection requirement compliance table** вҖ” directly determines deposit-return liability. Some states impose statutory walkthrough mandates with severe penalties for landlord non-compliance; the strictest is **Washington RCW 59.18.260** which forfeits the **ENTIRE security deposit plus attorney's fees and court costs** if the landlord collects a deposit without first providing a written checklist of damages signed by both parties ([Washington RCW 59.18.260 official text](https://app.leg.wa.gov/rcw/default.aspx?cite=59.18.260)). Four regimes:

| Regime                                  | States       | Source                                                                |
|-----------------------------------------|--------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------|
| **MandatoryMoveInChecklist**            | WA, AZ, MI, KY | Wash. RCW 59.18.260 (full deposit + atty fees forfeit); Ariz. ARS В§ 33-1321; Mich. MCL 554.608 (7-day window for tenant to return signed); Ky. KRS 383.580(2) |
| **TenantRequestedMoveInChecklist**      | MD           | Md. Real Prop. В§ 8-203.1 вҖ” landlord must provide written damage list only if tenant requests within 15 days of occupancy |
| **PreMoveOutInspectionOffer**           | CA           | Cal. Civ. Code В§ 1950.5(f) вҖ” landlord must OFFER pre-move-out walk-through, giving tenant opportunity to cure curable deficiencies before final deduction |
| **NoStateRequirement**                  | 45 other states + DC | Common-law walk-through by agreement only; no specific statutory penalty for omission |

**Washington's penalty** (`FullDepositPlusAttorneysFees`) is uniquely severe вҖ” modeled as the only state with this `DepositForfeitPenalty` variant (pinned by `wa_unique_full_deposit_penalty_invariant`). Other MandatoryMoveInChecklist states (AZ/MI/KY) trigger the lighter `PreExistingDamageDeductionsBarred` penalty вҖ” landlord can't deduct for pre-existing damages not documented but otherwise keeps the deposit framework intact.

**Michigan's 7-day commencement-inventory window** (MCL 554.608) is unique: the tenant has 7 days from receipt of the checklist to return it signed; if the tenant fails to return, the landlord's version controls. Pinned by `mi_7_day_window_day_7_complies` + `mi_7_day_window_day_8_violates`.

**Maryland's tenant-request 15-day window** (Real Prop. В§ 8-203.1) is a passive-duty regime: no automatic obligation unless the tenant requests in writing within 15 days of taking occupancy. Pinned by the 4-test boundary sweep вҖ” day-15 exact complies, day-16 untimely вҶ’ no duty.

**California's pre-move-out walk-through** (Civ. Code В§ 1950.5(f)) is structurally different вҖ” landlord doesn't owe a move-in checklist at all, but must offer the tenant a pre-move-out inspection upon proper request, listing items the landlord intends to deduct from the deposit so the tenant has a chance to remediate. Failure to offer is itself a violation regardless of whether a move-in checklist was provided.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/move-in-inspection-check`. Twenty-four tests pin: **WA checklist at commencement complies**; **WA no-checklist triggers FullDepositPlusAttorneysFees** penalty + VIOLATION note; **WA late checklist (day 1) violates**; WA pre-commencement (day вҲ’2) complies; **MI 7-day window day-7 complies / day-8 violates**; AZ at-commencement requirement; KY no-checklist triggers PreExistingDamageDeductionsBarred penalty; **MD tenant-did-not-request no duty** (compliant by default); MD tenant requested within 15 days landlord must provide; **MD day-15 exact boundary complies** + day-16 late no duty; **CA pre-move-out offer complies**; CA failure-to-offer pre-move-out violation; CA no move-in checklist required regardless (move-in flag does not save CA from pre-move-out violation); **NoStateRequirement 7-state sweep** (TX/FL/NY/PA/OH/MA/OR вҖ” compliant by default); **51-state coverage**; non-empty citations; unknown state fallback; lowercase normalizes; **WA-unique full-deposit penalty invariant** (only state with FullDepositPlusAttorneysFees); **4-state MandatoryMoveInChecklist invariant** (WA/AZ/MI/KY only); note describes WA full-penalty path; note describes MD no-request path.

`traderview-expense::renters_insurance` is the **state landlord-required renters insurance compliance table** вҖ” directly determines whether a landlord-trader's lease provision requiring tenants to carry renters insurance is enforceable, and at what coverage levels. **No US state currently prohibits the requirement entirely**; the variation is in statutory ceilings on coverage amounts the landlord may demand, restrictions on naming the landlord as an additional insured, and low-income tenant carveouts. Two regimes:

| Regime                                  | States                    | Source                                                                |
|-----------------------------------------|---------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------|
| **StatutoryCapWithLowIncomeExemption**  | OR (uniquely)             | Or. ORS 90.222 вҖ” $100,000 per-occurrence liability cap + landlord-additional-insured prohibition + low-income exemption when tenant household income вүӨ 50% Area Median Income (AMI) |
| **GenerallyAllowedNoStateCap**          | 49 other states + DC      | Common-law reasonableness applies; no specific statutory cap; includes Oklahoma (Okla. Stat. tit. 41 В§ 113 permits вҖ” pinned against the common online misconception that OK forbids requiring it) |

**Oregon ORS 90.222** is the only state with a specific statutory framework:

- **Coverage cap**: $100,000 per occurrence OR the customary amount required by landlords for similar properties in the same rental market, whichever is greater. Pinned by `or_at_100k_cap_exact_complies` + `or_101k_coverage_exceeds_cap`.
- **Landlord cannot be named as additional insured** on the policy. Landlord MAY be named as having an interest in the policy for lapse-notice purposes only ([Oregon DFR вҖ” Renter insurance](https://dfr.oregon.gov/insure/home/Pages/renter-insurance.aspx)). Pinned by `or_additional_insured_named_violation` + `or_landlord_as_interest_only_complies`.
- **Low-income exemption**: landlord may NOT require insurance from a tenant whose household income is вүӨ 50% of Area Median Income (adjusted for family size up to 5 persons). Pinned by `or_low_income_50_pct_exemption_blocks_requirement` + 49% boundary + 51% no-exemption.

**Oklahoma misconception pinned**: a widely-circulated online claim says Oklahoma "forbids landlord renters-insurance requirements" вҖ” this is INCORRECT. Okla. Stat. tit. 41 В§ 113 permits landlords to require renters insurance as a lease condition. The OK rule is modeled identically to other GenerallyAllowedNoStateCap states; pinned by `ok_no_state_cap_despite_renters_insurance_misconception`.

**Module invariants** (3 separately pinned):
- Only OR has the `StatutoryCapWithLowIncomeExemption` regime (`or_unique_statutory_cap_regime_invariant`).
- Only OR has `landlord_additional_insured_prohibited: true` (`only_or_has_additional_insured_prohibition`).
- Only OR has `low_income_exemption_pct_ami: Some(_)` (`only_or_has_low_income_exemption`).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/renters-insurance-check`. Twenty-four tests pin: **OR $100k exact cap complies / $100,001 exceeds cap**; **OR additional-insured naming violation** + landlord-as-interest-only complies; **OR low-income exemption boundary** at 49% / 50% / 51% AMI (вүӨ50% blocks requirement); **OR low-income exemption does not fire if landlord does not require** (no compliance impact when nothing required); **OR all three violations stack** (coverage + additional-insured + low-income); **CA no-state-cap accepts $500k coverage**; **TX additional-insured naming complies** (no prohibition); **NY low-income tenant no exemption** (no statutory carveout); FL $1M coverage complies; **OK misconception pinned** (GenerallyAllowedNoStateCap, compliant); **51-state coverage**; non-empty citations; **3 OR-unique invariants** (statutory cap regime / additional-insured prohibition / low-income exemption flag); unknown state falls back to no-cap; lowercase normalizes; OR compliant note describes state path; OR violation note lists each issue (exceeds cap + additional insured + AMI); general-allowed note describes no-cap.

`traderview-expense::mandatory_renters_insurance_provider_choice` is the **tenant right to choose renters insurance provider when landlord mandates coverage вҖ” anti-tying framework module**. Distinct from sibling `renters_insurance` (general renters insurance framework + coverage minimums + OR low-income exemption + additional-insured prohibition) and `rental_junk_fee_transparency` (non-rent fee transparency). When a landlord requires tenant to carry renters insurance, may the landlord also dictate WHICH insurer the tenant must use? In virtually every state the answer is NO вҖ” the landlord may require coverage at specified minimum levels but may not mandate a specific insurer (especially a landlord-affiliated provider).

**Three regimes**:

| Regime | Anti-tying authority | Affiliate scrutiny | Treble damages |
|--------|------------------------|----------------------|------------------|
| California | Cal. Ins. Code + В§ 1942.6 + В§ 17200 UDAP + В§ 1750 CLRA | Heightened under В§ 1750 CLRA | YES under CLRA |
| New York | N.Y. Gen. Bus. Law В§ 349 + N.Y. Ins. Law В§ 2502 | De facto unlicensed agent | YES on willful (В§ 349) |
| Default | Common-law + state UDAP + 15 U.S.C. В§ 45 FTC Act В§ 5 | State-specific | Varies by state |

**California вҖ” Cal. Ins. Code + Cal. Civ. Code В§ 1942.6 + В§ 1750 et seq. + Cal. Bus. & Prof. Code В§ 17200**. Landlord MAY require renters insurance and MAY specify minimum coverage levels in the lease, but MAY NOT mandate a specific insurer. Tenant retains right to choose any licensed insurer whose policy meets the lease's coverage requirements. **Landlord MAY recommend a list of providers without violating anti-tying** вҖ” recommendation is permitted, mandate is not. Anti-tying violations void the mandate and expose landlord to claims under California UDAP (В§ 17200 Unfair Competition Law вҖ” injunctive + restitution remedies) and Cal. Civ. Code В§ 1750 et seq. (Consumers Legal Remedies Act вҖ” treble damages + attorney fees + injunctive relief when landlord or affiliate earns commissions).

**New York вҖ” N.Y. Gen. Bus. Law В§ 349 + N.Y. Ins. Law В§ 2502**. Anti-tying violations are deceptive practices actionable under GBL В§ 349 with **$50 minimum statutory damages per violation + $1,000 cap on willful + treble damages + attorney fees**. В§ 2502 separately reaches landlord acting as de facto insurance agent without limited license / proper licensing.

**Default вҖ” common-law anti-tying + state UDAP + FTC В§ 5**. Most states follow common-law anti-tying doctrine + state UDAP framework. Federal **15 U.S.C. В§ 45 (FTC Act В§ 5 UDAP)** applies to landlord-as-insurance-agent arrangements. State UDAP statutes (47 states + DC) provide private right of action with varying penalty structures.

**Anti-tying violation across all regimes** вҖ” invariant. Mandate of specific provider triggers violation in CA, NY, and Default. Pinned by `anti_tying_violation_invariant_across_regimes` (3-regime sweep). **Clean compliance across all regimes** вҖ” when landlord requires insurance but does NOT mandate specific provider, requirement is enforceable in all three regimes. Pinned by `clean_compliance_invariant_across_regimes`.

**Affiliate financial interest heightens scrutiny** вҖ” when landlord or affiliate earns commissions on tenant-purchased insurance, CA В§ 1750 CLRA and NY В§ 2502 impose heightened standards. Pinned by `affiliate_scrutiny_only_when_mandate_and_affiliate` (4-cell truth table mandate Г— affiliate).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/mandatory-renters-insurance-provider-choice`. Twenty-seven tests pin: **CA clean requirement enforceable**; **CA provider mandate triggers anti-tying violation** (В§ 1942.6 + В§ 17200 UDAP); **CA affiliate provider heightens scrutiny** (В§ 1750 CLRA); **CA affiliate without mandate no heightened scrutiny**; **CA recommendation note describes permitted path**; **CA citation pins В§В§ 1942.6 + 1750 et seq. + В§ 17200**; **NY clean requirement enforceable**; **NY provider mandate triggers GBL В§ 349 violation** (treble damages + attorney fees); **NY provider mandate also triggers Ins. В§ 2502** (de facto insurance agent); **NY citation pins GBL В§ 349 + Ins. В§ 2502**; **default clean requirement enforceable**; **default provider mandate triggers UDAP violation** (common-law anti-tying + state UDAP); **default FTC Act В§ 5 note present** (15 U.S.C. В§ 45 + 47 states + DC); **default citation references FTC Act В§ 5**; **no requirement no enforceability engagement**; **coverage minimum specification permitted invariant**; **coverage minimum independent of provider mandate**; **three regimes routed correctly**; **anti-tying violation invariant across regimes** (3-regime sweep); **clean compliance invariant across regimes**; **affiliate scrutiny only when mandate and affiliate** (4-cell truth table); **CA clean no violations**; **NY clean no violations**; **default clean no violations**; **CA multiple violations simultaneous with affiliate** (3 violations); **NY provider mandate two violations simultaneous** (GBL В§ 349 + Ins. В§ 2502); **CA recommendation path does not trigger violation**.

`traderview-expense::utility_shutoff` is the **state landlord-caused utility shutoff prohibition compliance table** вҖ” directly addresses the most common "self-help eviction" tactic where landlords cut electricity, water, gas, or heat to pressure tenants out. Every US state prohibits this, but the **statutory penalties vary by orders of magnitude**: from $100/day (CA, WA) to $1k flat + one month rent (TX) to 3 months rent or actual damages whichever is higher (FL) to punitive + treble damages + criminal exposure (NY). Five regimes:

| Regime                                      | States | Source                                                                |
|---------------------------------------------|--------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------|
| **PerDayStatutoryPenalty**                  | CA, WA | Cal. Civ. Code В§ 789.3 ($100/day + $250 minimum + actual damages + attorney's fees); Wash. RCW 59.18.300 (up to $100/day + actual damages + prevailing-party attorney's fees and costs) |
| **FlatPlusOneMonthRentPenalty**             | TX     | Tex. Prop. Code В§ 92.008 ($1,000 + one month's rent + actual damages + attorney's fees + court costs) |
| **MonthlyRentMultiplePenalty**              | FL     | Fla. Stat. В§ 83.67 (3 months' rent OR actual damages, whichever higher, + costs + attorney's fees) |
| **PunitiveDamagesFramework**                | NY     | N.Y. RPL В§ 235-a + RPAPL 853 (compensatory + punitive + treble damages for unlawful eviction + criminal exposure) |
| **GeneralProhibitionStandardRemedies**      | 46 other states + DC | Self-help eviction prohibited; tenant entitled to actual damages + injunctive relief; no statute-specific enhanced penalty |

**California's per-day formula** is the most-known: $100 per day of violation, $250 minimum floor (so even a 1-day shutoff yields a $250 base penalty), plus actual damages and attorney's fees. After 10 days the per-day amount ($1,000) exceeds the floor and dominates. Pinned by `ca_10_days_yields_1000_dollar_penalty` + `ca_1_day_minimum_floor_applies_250` + `ca_3_days_above_minimum_applies_per_day` boundary ([Tobener Law CA Shutoff guide](https://www.tobenerlaw.com/shut-offs/)).

**Washington has the same $100/day rate but NO minimum floor** вҖ” a 1-day shutoff is $100, not $250. Pinned by `wa_no_minimum_floor`.

**Texas's flat penalty is duration-independent** вҖ” a 1-day shutoff and a 100-day shutoff yield the same $1,000 + 1 month rent statutory amount (separately from actual damages). Pinned by `tx_duration_does_not_change_penalty`.

**Florida picks the higher of (3 months rent) or (actual damages)** вҖ” this is uniquely beneficial to tenants with low rent but high actual damages (food spoilage, hotel costs, lost wages). Pinned by both directions: `fl_3_months_rent_when_higher_than_actual` + `fl_actual_damages_when_higher_than_rent_multiple` ([Fla. Stat. В§ 83.67](https://www.frls.org/blog/utilitiesprocess)).

**New York alone supports criminal exposure** plus civil compensatory + punitive + treble damages for unlawful eviction under RPL В§ 235-a and RPAPL 853 ([N.Y. RPL В§ 235-a official text](https://newyork.public.law/laws/n.y._real_property_law_section_235-a)).

**Bona-fide repair / emergency exception** is universal: every regime includes a carve-out for utility interruption required for genuine repair, construction, or emergency (water main break, gas leak, structural integrity issue). Module bars any violation when `interruption_due_to_bona_fide_repair_or_emergency: true`. Pinned by `bona_fide_repair_exception_blocks_violation_ca`.

**Module invariants** (4 separately pinned):
- CA + WA only on PerDayStatutoryPenalty (`per_day_regime_only_ca_and_wa`).
- TX only on FlatPlusOneMonthRentPenalty (`flat_plus_month_regime_only_tx`).
- FL only on MonthlyRentMultiplePenalty (`monthly_rent_multiple_regime_only_fl`).
- NY only on PunitiveDamagesFramework (`punitive_regime_only_ny`).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/utility-shutoff-check`. Twenty-six tests pin: **CA 10 days Г— $100 = $1,000 penalty**; **CA 1-day minimum floor $250**; CA 3-day boundary ($300 > $250 minimum); CA attorney fees recoverable; **WA 30 days Г— $100 = $3,000**; WA no minimum floor (1 day = $100); **TX flat $1k + 1 month rent**; TX duration-independent flat penalty; **FL 3 months rent when higher than actual** ($6k > $500 actual); **FL actual damages when higher than rent multiple** ($10k > $3k); **NY punitive + treble + criminal all available**; NY actual damages form statutory base; **OR general-prohibition no specific penalty** (only actual damages, no attorney fees, no punitives); **bona-fide repair exception blocks violation** (CA); no-shutoff no-violation; **51-state coverage**; non-empty citations; **4 regime-uniqueness invariants** (CA+WA / TX / FL / NY); unknown state falls back to general; lowercase normalizes; **CA note describes per-day math** ("10 day", "$100/day"); TX note describes flat + rent path; **NY note describes PUNITIVE + TREBLE + criminal path**.

`traderview-expense::vehicle_towing_from_rental_property` is the **landlord vehicle-towing compliance check** вҖ” when may a landlord lawfully remove an unauthorized vehicle from rental-property parking? Operational concern for any landlord managing multifamily / multitenant parking facilities.
