`traderview-expense::submetering_rules` is the **state utility submetering / RUBS (Ratio Utility Billing System) compliance table** вҖ” practical for trader-landlords with multifamily portfolios who bill tenants for water, sewer, electricity, or gas via individual unit submeters or RUBS (mathematical-formula allocation by square footage / occupancy / bedrooms). Three regimes:

| Regime                                       | States  | Source                                                                |
|----------------------------------------------|---------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------|
| **DisclosureAndTestingRequired**             | CA, VA  | Cal. Civ. Code В§ 1954.201 (SB 7 of 2016, eff. 2018-01-01); Va. Code В§ 55.1-1212 вҖ” lease disclosure required + free tenant-requested meter testing (VA max once per 24 months) |
| **PSCRegisteredCappedFees**                  | TX      | Tex. Water Code Ch. 13 + TCEQ 16 TAC В§ 24.275 вҖ” PSC registration + late fee cap **5%** + service charge cap **9%** of allocated utility cost + specific billing-format disclosure |
| **NoStateRegulation**                        | 48 other states + DC | Common-law and contract terms govern; federal PURPA does not regulate landlord-tenant submetering |

**California SB 7 of 2016 (Cal. Civ. Code В§ 1954.201)** added Chapter 2.5 to the Civil Code to encourage water conservation in multifamily residential rentals. Before executing a rental agreement, a landlord charging separately for water service via submeters MUST clearly disclose this in writing. The disclosure must include the contact information for the local county sealer. Tenants who suspect submeter inaccuracy can request testing through the county sealer ([CA SB 7 enrolled text](https://www.leginfo.ca.gov/pub/15-16/bill/sen/sb_0001-0050/sb_7_bill_20160906_enrolled.html), [Guardian Water & Power вҖ” CA SB 7](https://www.guardianwp.com/post/2018/11/03/Submetering-in-California-Understanding-Senate-Bill-7)). Pinned by `ca_with_lease_disclosure_complies` + `ca_without_lease_disclosure_violates` + `ca_tenant_test_request_must_be_free` + `ca_tenant_test_provided_complies`.

**Virginia Va. Code В§ 55.1-1212** вҖ” submetering or RUBS allowed ONLY if clearly stated in the lease. Energy allocation equipment shall be tested periodically by the owner. Upon tenant request, a free meter test must be provided, though this free test cannot be conducted more frequently than once in a **24-month period** for the same tenant ([Va. Code В§ 55.1-1212](https://law.lis.virginia.gov/vacode/title55.1/chapter12/section55.1-1212/)). Pinned by `va_without_lease_disclosure_violates` + `va_24_month_test_window_pinned_in_citation`.

**Texas TCEQ 16 TAC В§ 24.275 caps**: landlord-operators must register with the Public Service Commission. Charges are capped at:
- **Late fee** вүӨ **5%** of the late bill
- **Service charge** вүӨ **9%** of utility costs allocated to the submetered unit
- Plus cost per gallon + applicable taxes + surcharges from the utility company

Pinned by both rate-cap boundaries: `tx_late_fee_5_pct_exact_complies` + `tx_late_fee_6_pct_violates` (1-point boundary); `tx_service_charge_9_pct_exact_complies` + `tx_service_charge_10_pct_violates`. PSC registration required: `tx_with_psc_registration_complies` + `tx_without_psc_registration_violates`. Multiple violations stack and are all listed: `tx_multiple_violations_all_listed` (3 issues).

**No-submetering carveout**: when the landlord doesn't use submetering or RUBS, no state regulation triggers regardless of state. Pinned by `no_submetering_no_state_check_triggered`.

**Module invariants** (3 separately pinned):
- CA + VA only on DisclosureAndTestingRequired regime (`ca_va_only_disclosure_and_testing_regime`)
- TX only on PSCRegisteredCappedFees regime (`tx_only_psc_registered_regime`)
- TX only with fee caps (`only_tx_has_fee_caps`)

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/submetering-check`. Twenty-four tests pin: **CA with lease disclosure complies** + without violates with "not disclosed" violation; **CA tenant test request must be free** + tenant test provided complies; VA same regime as CA; **VA 24-month test window pinned in citation**; **TX with PSC registration complies** + without violates; **TX late fee 5% exact complies / 6% violates** (boundary); **TX service charge 9% exact complies / 10% violates** (boundary); TX all 3 fee caps individually pinned at 5%/9%; **no-regulation state (FL) no compliance issues** even with 50% late fee; no-submetering-in-use no check triggered; **51-state coverage**; non-empty citations; **3 regime-uniqueness invariants** (CA+VA / TX only / TX only with fee caps); unknown state falls back to no regulation; lowercase normalizes; TX multiple-violations all listed (3 stack); CA violation note lists issues.

`traderview-expense::smoke_free_housing` is the **federal HUD + state smoke-free housing compliance table** вҖ” every trader-landlord operating multifamily, especially public housing or properties accepting В§ 8 vouchers, must comply with the federal HUD smoke-free rule plus any applicable state additions. Two regimes:

| Regime                              | States | Source                                                                |
|-------------------------------------|--------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------|
| **HudFloorPlusStateAdditions**      | CA, MN, OR | HUD 24 CFR В§ 965.653 + Cal. Labor Code В§ 6404.5 + AB 1316 + Berkeley 2014 local; Minn. Stat. В§ 144.414 + 2024 cannabis amendment for MUH common-interest communities (eff. 2024-07-01); Or. ORS Ch. 90 90-day existing-tenant conversion notice |
| **HudFloorOnly**                    | 47 other states + DC | HUD 24 CFR В§ 965.653 federal floor for public housing only; private market governed by lease + local ordinances |

**HUD federal floor (24 CFR В§ 965.653, eff. 2018-07-30)** вҖ” every Public Housing Authority (PHA) in the country MUST enforce a smoke-free policy covering:

- **Indoor**: all living units, common areas, electrical closets, storage units, PHA administrative offices
- **Outdoor**: **25-foot buffer** around housing and admin buildings
- **All prohibited tobacco products** вҖ” cigarettes, cigars, pipes, hookahs; some PHAs include e-cigarettes ([HUD eCFR 24 CFR В§ 965.653](https://www.ecfr.gov/current/title-24/subtitle-B/chapter-IX/part-965/subpart-G/section-965.653), [Federal Register Final Rule on Smoke-Free Public Housing](https://www.federalregister.gov/documents/2016/12/05/2016-28986/instituting-smoke-free-public-housing)).

The federal rule does NOT apply to private market multifamily вҖ” pinned by `tx_private_multifamily_no_state_requirement` + `ny_private_multifamily_no_state_requirement` (TX and NY private multifamily compliant by default even with no smoke-free policy).

**California state addition** вҖ” Cal. Labor Code В§ 6404.5 + AB 1316 + local ordinances (Berkeley was first CA rent-control city to ban smoking in MUH in 2014). Requires common-area smoke-free for private multifamily. Pinned by `ca_private_multifamily_requires_common_area_smoke_free` (without common-area policy violates) + `ca_private_multifamily_with_common_area_complies`. Single-family rentals NOT covered: `ca_private_single_family_no_common_area_requirement`.

**Minnesota state addition** вҖ” Minn. Stat. В§ 144.414 + 2024 cannabis amendment. Common-area smoke-free required. 2024 amendment explicitly added cannabis to the prohibition for MUH common-interest communities (HOAs), effective 2024-07-01. Pinned by `mn_citation_mentions_2024_cannabis_amendment`.

**Oregon state addition** вҖ” ORS Ch. 90 allows the landlord to convert an EXISTING tenancy to non-smoking with **90 days' written notice**. Distinct from the typical 90-day rent-increase notice. New tenancies don't trigger the conversion-notice rule (no existing tenant to give notice to). Pinned by `or_conversion_with_90_day_notice_complies` + `or_conversion_with_89_day_notice_violates` (1-day boundary) + `or_new_tenancy_no_conversion_notice_needed`.

**Module invariants** (3 separately pinned):
- CA + MN + OR only on HudFloorPlusStateAdditions regime (`state_additions_only_ca_mn_or`)
- OR only with conversion-notice requirement (`only_or_has_conversion_notice`)
- CA + MN only with common-area requirement (`only_ca_mn_have_common_area_requirement`)

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/smoke-free-check`. Twenty-three tests pin: **PHA with full compliance passes**; **PHA without indoor policy violates**; **PHA without 25-ft buffer violates**; PHA both violations listed (count = 2); **CA private multifamily requires common-area smoke-free** + with common-area complies; **MN private multifamily common-area required**; **CA private single-family NO common-area requirement** (single-family carveout); **OR conversion with 90-day notice complies / 89-day violates** (boundary); **OR new tenancy NO conversion notice needed** (regression); **TX + NY private multifamily NO state requirement** (HudFloorOnly compliant by default); **51-state coverage**; non-empty citations; **3 regime-uniqueness invariants** (CA+MN+OR state additions / OR only conversion notice / CA+MN only common-area requirement); OR 90-day window pinned; unknown state falls back to HUD-only; lowercase normalizes; PHA violation note describes HUD federal floor VIOLATION; MN citation mentions 2024 cannabis amendment.

`traderview-expense::tenant_cannabis_use_protection` is the **state tenant-cannabis-use protection landlord compliance check** вҖ” addresses the emerging area of cannabis legalization's tenant/landlord power-balance interaction. State approaches diverge into two opposing camps: New York affirmatively protects tenants (landlord cannot refuse to rent based on cannabis use and must permit registered medical cannabis use); Illinois takes the opposite default (landlord may prohibit smoking + vaporizing + cultivation including medical via lease).

**Three regimes**:

| Regime | Statute | Refuse to rent | Ban smoking | Medical patient protection | Federal-benefits exception |
|--------|---------|------------------|---------------|------------------------------|------------------------------|
| `NewYorkCannabisLaw134` | NY Cannabis Law В§ 134 | **Prohibited** | Allowed (general) | **Yes вҖ” must permit medical smoking** | Yes (Section 8/HUD risk excuses restriction) |
| `IllinoisCrta` | 410 ILCS 705/ (eff. 2020-01-01) | Permitted under CRTA (FHA may apply) | Permitted | No (CRTA does not protect; FHA may) | N/A |
| `Default` | вҖ” | Permitted (subject to FHA) | Permitted | No statewide; FHA may apply | N/A |

**Three regression-critical distinguishers**:
- **NY-only refuse-to-rent prohibition**: only NY makes refusing to rent based on cannabis use itself a В§ 134 violation. IL + Default rely on Fair Housing Act analysis (which doesn't apply to cannabis use directly).
- **NY-only medical-patient smoking-ban exception**: only NY's В§ 134 affirmatively bars landlord enforcement of smoking ban against a registered medical cannabis patient (other states leave medical patients to FHA reasonable-accommodation arguments).
- **NY-only federal-benefits exception**: only NY's В§ 134 has a built-in carve-out вҖ” when the landlord receives federal benefits (Section 8 / HUD subsidies), the medical-patient protection yields to preserve federal compliance.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/tenant-cannabis-use-protection`. Twenty tests pin: **NY refuse-to-rent violation** + В§ 134 citation; **NY ban-smoking-in-lease generally permitted** + "medical cannabis exception preserved" citation; **NY ban-cultivation permitted**; **NY enforce-against-medical-patient no-federal-benefits violation** (regression-critical); **NY enforce-against-medical-patient WITH federal benefits permitted** (regression вҖ” Section 8/HUD landlord exception fires); **NY enforce against NON-medical patient permitted**; **NY evict medical patient no-federal-benefits violation**; **NY evict non-medical-user permitted**; **IL landlord may ban smoking in lease** + 410 ILCS 705/ + "CRTA" citation; **IL landlord may ban cultivation**; **IL landlord may enforce against medical patient** + "FHA reasonable-accommodation" note (regression-critical вҖ” CRTA does not protect; FHA is the path); **IL landlord may evict for cannabis use**; **default landlord may prohibit cannabis use**; **default evict for cannabis use permitted**; **state routing NY/IL/Default**; **case-insensitive routing**; **only-NY-protects-refusal-to-rent** (single-test 3-regime invariant); **only-NY-protects-medical-patients-from-smoking-ban** (single-test 3-regime invariant); **only-NY-has-federal-benefits-exception** (single-test 2-regime invariant вҖ” IL ignores federal-benefits flag); **citations pin authorities**.

`traderview-expense::tenant_clothesline_drying_right` is the **California Civil Code В§ 1940.20 tenant clothesline / drying-rack right module** вҖ” under what conditions may a landlord lawfully prohibit a tenant from using a clothesline or drying rack in the tenant's private area? Trader-landlord critical for CA rental owners managing multifamily properties: an outright ban (lease clause or eviction notice) violates В§ 1940.20 effective January 1, 2016 per AB 1448. Distinct from siblings `tenant_solar_installation` (rooftop solar panels), `ev_charger_installation` (vehicle charging), `tenant_organizing` (tenant association formation), and `flag_display_right` (federal Freedom to Display the American Flag Act).

**Two regimes** вҖ” California В§ 1940.20 protection vs Default no-protection:

| Regime | Authority | Definitions | Private area | Conditions |
|--------|-----------|-------------|--------------|-------------|
| California | Cal. Civ. Code В§В§ 1940.20 + 4750.10 + AB 1448 Stats. 2015 ch. 415 | Clothesline = cord/rope/wire; drying rack = apparatus; balcony/railing/awning EXCLUDED | Outdoor area OR enclosed with door access; common areas EXCLUDED | Six conditions under В§ 1940.20(b) |
| Default | Lease provisions + common-law quiet enjoyment | None statutory | None statutory | None |

**В§ 1940.20(a)(1)+(a)(2) device-definition exclusions** вҖ” "clothesline" requires cord / rope / wire; "drying rack" requires an apparatus. Balcony railings, awnings, and other parts of structures or buildings explicitly do NOT qualify. A tenant using a balcony railing as a drying surface gets no statutory protection. Pinned by `ca_balcony_railing_does_not_qualify`, `ca_awning_does_not_qualify`, `ca_balcony_railing_in_private_area_still_does_not_qualify` (location ok, device disqualifies), `device_truth_table` (4-cell sweep: Clothesline/DryingRack qualify; BalconyRailing/Awning do not), and `ca_note_pins_definitions`.

**В§ 1940.20(a)(3) private-area gate** вҖ” outdoor area OR area in tenant's premises enclosed by wall or fence with access from a door of the premises. Common areas shared with other tenants do NOT qualify. Pinned by `ca_common_area_not_private`, `ca_enclosed_private_area_protected`, `ca_clothesline_in_common_area_violates_private_area_gate` (device ok, location disqualifies), `location_truth_table` (4-cell sweep: PrivateOutdoor/EnclosedPrivate/AffixedToBuilding are private; CommonArea is not).

**В§ 1940.20(b) six conditions for tenant use** вҖ” (1) no interference with property maintenance; (2) no health or safety hazard; (3) no blocking doorways or interfering with walkways or utility service equipment; (4) tenant consent if affixed to building; (5) no violation of reasonable time or location restrictions imposed by landlord; (6) effective January 1, 2016 (AB 1448 Stats. 2015 ch. 415). Pinned by `ca_property_maintenance_interference_violates`, `ca_health_safety_hazard_violates`, `ca_blocks_doorway_violates`, `ca_reasonable_restriction_violation`, and `ca_five_violation_stack_when_all_5_conditions_failed` (exactly 5 simultaneous failures).

**В§ 1940.20(b)(4) building-affix consent gate** вҖ” tenant must seek landlord's consent before affixing clothesline to building. Consent requirement engages ONLY when location is AffixedToBuilding; not required for free-standing private-area installations. Pinned by `ca_affixed_to_building_requires_consent`, `ca_affixed_to_building_with_consent_protected`, and `consent_only_required_when_affixed_to_building_invariant` (4-cell sweep: only AffixedToBuilding triggers consent requirement).

**В§ 4750.10 HOA companion right-to-dry** вҖ” same right protected against HOAs under Davis-Stirling Common Interest Development Act. Pinned by `ca_note_pins_hoa_companion`.

**Default fallback** вҖ” no specific tenant clothesline / drying-rack right under state statute; lease provisions controlling; common-law quiet-enjoyment review may apply. Solar-access / HOA right-to-dry laws exist in 20+ states (FL, CO, MD, ME, NV, etc.) but specifically for HOMEOWNERS, not rental tenants. Pinned by `default_no_protection`, `default_no_consent_required`, `default_citation_pins_lease_and_common_law`, and `default_note_pins_homeowner_state_laws` (FL + CO + MD + ME + NV + HOMEOWNERS).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/tenant-clothesline-drying-right`. Twenty-eight tests pin: **CA clothesline private outdoor protected**; **CA drying rack protected**; **CA balcony railing does not qualify**; **CA awning does not qualify**; **CA common area not private**; **CA enclosed private area protected**; **CA affixed to building requires consent**; **CA affixed to building with consent protected**; **CA property maintenance interference violates**; **CA health safety hazard violates**; **CA blocks doorway violates**; **CA reasonable restriction violation**; **CA citation pins authorities** (В§В§ 1940.20 + 4750.10 + AB 1448 Stats. 2015 ch. 415); **CA note pins definitions** (cord + outdoor area); **CA note pins six conditions** (January 1, 2016 + AB 1448); **CA note pins HOA companion** (В§ 4750.10); **default no protection**; **default citation pins lease and common law**; **default note pins homeowner state laws**; **two regimes routed correctly**; **CA uniquely protects clothesline invariant**; **CA five-violation stack when all 5 conditions failed**; **device truth table** (4-cell sweep); **location truth table** (4-cell sweep); **consent only required when affixed to building invariant** (4-cell sweep); **CA balcony railing in private area still does not qualify** (location ok device disqualifies); **CA clothesline in common area violates private area gate** (device ok location disqualifies); **default no consent required**.

`traderview-expense::tenant_data_privacy` is the **state tenant data privacy / records access compliance table** вҖ” emerging area as landlord-traders adopt digital tenant screening, smart-building tech (smart locks, doorbell cameras, facial recognition / biometric entry), and tenant records databases. Three regimes:

| Regime                                            | States                  | Source                                                                |
|---------------------------------------------------|-------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------|
| **BiometricStrictWrittenConsent**                 | IL                      | 740 ILCS 14/ Biometric Information Privacy Act (BIPA, 2008) вҖ” applies to ALL entities with NO revenue threshold; written informed consent required BEFORE biometric collection; **$1,000 per negligent / $5,000 per intentional violation**; private right of action |
| **ComprehensivePrivacyLawRevenueThreshold**       | CA, VA, CO, CT, OR, DE, MD, MN | Cal. Civ. Code В§ 1798.100 (CCPA/CPRA); Va. Code В§ 59.1-575 (VCDPA); Colo. Rev. Stat. В§ 6-1-1301 (CPA); Conn. Gen. Stat. В§ 42-515 (CTDPA); Or. Consumer Privacy Act (eff. 2024-07-01); Del. PDPA (eff. 2025-01-01); Md. ODPA (eff. 2025-10-01); Minn. CDPA (eff. 2025-07-31) вҖ” revenue / consumer thresholds + 45-day DSAR response window |
| **NoStatePrivacyLaw**                             | 42 other states + DC    | No comprehensive state privacy law; common-law and contract terms govern |

**Illinois BIPA is uniquely dangerous for landlord-traders using biometric building entry** вҖ” facial recognition cameras, fingerprint scanners, retina/iris scans all trigger BIPA regardless of building size or landlord revenue. Statutory damages stack PER TENANT PER VIOLATION: a 10-unit IL building using facial recognition without proper BIPA-compliant written consent faces **$10,000 negligent or $50,000 intentional** exposure ([740 ILCS 14/ statutory text](https://www.ilga.gov/legislation/ilcs/ilcs3.asp?ActID=3004) ). Pinned by `il_biometric_use_without_consent_violates` (10 tenants Г— $1k = $10k) + `il_intentional_violation_5x_damages` (10 Г— $5k = $50k) + `il_bipa_has_no_revenue_threshold` ($100k revenue landlord still subject).

**CCPA / CPRA thresholds in CA** (any ONE triggers the law):
- **$25M+** annual gross revenue
- **100K+** CA consumers/households processed annually
- **50%+** annual revenue from selling/sharing CA personal info

Pinned by `ca_under_25m_revenue_under_100k_consumers_not_subject` (small landlord exempt) + `ca_over_25m_revenue_triggers_law` (revenue test) + `ca_100k_consumers_triggers_law` (consumer test).

**VA / CO / CT / OR / MN follow the 100k threshold pattern**: 100k+ residents processed annually OR 25k residents + revenue from data sale. Pinned by `va_100k_residents_triggers_vcdpa` + `co_under_100k_residents_not_subject`.

**DE / MD lowered the threshold to 35k residents** (newer 2025 statutes) вҖ” exposing more mid-size landlords. Pinned by `de_35k_residents_triggers_law` + `md_lower_threshold_than_ca`.

**45-day DSAR (Data Subject Access Request) response window** applies in all 8 comprehensive-law states. A tenant who requests their personal data must receive a response within 45 days or the landlord violates the state privacy law. Pinned by `ca_dsar_response_within_45_days_complies` + `ca_dsar_response_46_days_violates` (boundary).

**Module invariants** (3 separately pinned):
- IL only on BiometricStrictWrittenConsent regime (`only_il_uses_bipa_regime`)
- Exactly **8 states** on ComprehensivePrivacyLawRevenueThreshold regime (`comprehensive_privacy_law_8_states`)
- IL only with BIPA per-violation damages > 0 (`only_il_has_bipa_damages`)

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/tenant-privacy-check`. Twenty-six tests pin: **IL biometric use without consent violates** ($10k exposure for 10 tenants); **IL biometric with consent complies**; **IL intentional violation 5Г— damages** ($50k); IL no biometric no violation; **IL BIPA has no revenue threshold** (even $100k revenue landlord subject); **CA under $25M revenue + under 100k consumers NOT subject**; **CA over $25M revenue triggers**; **CA 100k consumers triggers**; **CA DSAR within 45 days complies / 46 days violates** (boundary); CA DSAR not responded violates; **VA 100k residents triggers VCDPA**; **CO under 100k residents not subject**; **DE 35k residents triggers** (lower threshold); MD lower threshold than CA; **TX no state privacy law no compliance required** (even with biometric in use); NY no comprehensive state law; **51-state coverage**; non-empty citations; **3 regime-uniqueness invariants** (IL only BIPA / 8 states comprehensive / IL only damages); unknown state falls back to no law; lowercase normalizes; IL violation note mentions BIPA + dollar amount; CA subject note describes DSAR response.

`traderview-expense::tenant_domestic_violence_lease_termination` is the **tenant domestic violence (DV) early lease termination compliance module** вҖ” when a tenant or household member is a victim of domestic violence, sexual assault, stalking, or human trafficking and seeks to terminate the rental agreement early without penalty. Trader-landlord operational concern: an improperly handled DV termination request creates anti-discrimination liability (federal VAWA), state-law statutory damages, and per-incident retaliation claims. Distinct from siblings `tenant_accessible_parking` (ADA), `rental_application_denial_disclosure` (screening), `rental_bed_bug_disclosure` (lease disclosure), `tenant_data_privacy` (general data handling).

**Four regimes** вҖ” Federal VAWA 2022 vs California vs Illinois Safe Homes Act vs Washington:

**Federal вҖ” Violence Against Women Act Reauthorization Act of 2022 (34 USC В§ 12491 + 24 CFR В§ 5.2005)** вҖ” applies to all HUD-covered housing programs (Section 8, public housing, LIHTC В§ 42, HOME, Section 202/811). Landlord SHALL NOT terminate or refuse to renew a lease solely because tenant is DV victim. Form HUD-91066 self-certification accepted as documentation; landlord MAY request within 14 business days. Lease provisions purporting to terminate on **police calls in DV situations are VOID**. Lease provisions requiring **waiver of VAWA rights are VOID**. VAWA emergency transfer plan required.

**California вҖ” Cal. Civ. Code В§ 1946.7** вҖ” tenant may terminate lease **14 days after written notice**. Acceptable documentation (issued within prior **180 days**): (1) restraining/protective order; (2) police report; (3) qualified third-party statement from doctor, nurse, therapist, counselor, or caseworker. Tenant liable for rent only up to 14 calendar days after notice; if landlord re-rents sooner, rent prorated. Documentation confidential вҖ” landlord may disclose only with tenant written consent OR if required by law/court order. Landlord retaliation prohibited.

**Illinois вҖ” Safe Homes Act (765 ILCS 750)** вҖ” В§ 750/15(a)(1) survivor may terminate if DV occurred at the leased premises; written notice required. Documentation: protective order OR qualified third-party statement (police report alone insufficient вҖ” distinct from CA). В§ 750/30 eviction defense вҖ” landlord cannot evict based on tenant or household member being DV victim. В§ 750/25 confidentiality of documentation.

**Washington вҖ” RCW 59.18.575** вҖ” termination request must be made **within 90 days** of the reported DV act, event, or circumstance giving rise to a protective order or report to qualified third party. Tenant remains liable for rent for month in which termination occurs; discharged from rent for any period following last day of month of quitting date. Documentation: protective order OR qualified third-party report.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/tenant-domestic-violence-lease-termination`. Thirty-six tests pin: **CA clean termination compliant**; **CA no written notice violation**; **CA 14-day notice boundary compliant**; **CA 13-day notice violation**; **CA 180-day documentation boundary compliant**; **CA 181-day documentation violation**; **CA police report acceptable**; **CA qualified third-party acceptable**; **CA no documentation violation**; **CA landlord disclosure without consent violation**; **CA landlord retaliation violation**; **IL clean termination compliant**; **IL no documentation violation** (765 ILCS 750/15); **IL police report NOT acceptable** (distinct from CA вҖ” IL accepts only protective order or qualified third party); **IL landlord retaliation violation** (В§ 750/30); **WA clean termination compliant**; **WA within 90-day compliant** (boundary); **WA 91-day violation**; **VAWA clean termination compliant**; **VAWA lease void clauses violation** (police-call termination + VAWA-rights-waiver VOID); **VAWA landlord retaliation violation** (34 USC В§ 12491); **VAWA HUD Form 91066 accepted**; **VAWA not HUD-covered no federal engagement**; **citation pins CA / IL / WA / VAWA authorities** (all four); **note pins CA 180-day lookback**; **note pins IL Safe Homes Act**; **note pins WA 90-day lookback**; **note pins VAWA void clauses**; **CA strictest 180-day vs WA 90-day invariant** (150 days OK CA but violates WA); **VAWA uniquely accepts HUD-91066 invariant** (CA rejects HUD-91066 form); **VAWA uniquely engages void-clause doctrine invariant** (state regimes don't); **documentation type truth table for CA** (5-cell sweep); **multiple CA violations stack** (4 violations).

`traderview-expense::tenant_emotional_distress_damages` is the **multi-jurisdictional tenant emotional distress damages framework module** вҖ” when may a tenant recover emotional distress damages from landlord misconduct (lockouts, harassment, retaliation, habitability breach severe enough to cause psychological injury)? Distinct from sibling `landlord_harassment` (civil-penalty exposure for harassment), `landlord_retaliation_damages` (retaliation damages framework), `lockout_penalties` (lockout-specific statute), `retaliation_windows` (timing presumptions), `habitability_remedies` (general repair-and-deduct + rent abatement). Trader-landlord critical because severe misconduct exposes landlord to UNCAPPED emotional distress damages in addition to statutory penalties; modern cases include $500,000+ verdicts for systematic harassment campaigns.

**Four-jurisdiction framework**:

| Jurisdiction | IIED Test | NIED Rule | Punitive Cap |
|--------------|-----------|-----------|--------------|
| **California** | Hughes v. Pair, 46 Cal. 4th 1035 (2009) | Bystander / Dillon v. Legg 68 Cal. 2d 728 (1968) | Cal. Civ. Code В§ 3294 clear-and-convincing malice + Campbell 1-9Г— ratio |
| **New York** | Howell v. NYP Holdings, 21 NY 3d 333 (2013) | Bystander / Zone of Danger | Common-law preponderance + no cap + Campbell ratio |
| **Texas** | Twyman v. Twyman, 855 SW 2d 619 (Tex. 1993) | **IMPACT RULE** вҖ” Boyles v. Kerr, 855 SW 2d 593 (Tex. 1993) | Tex. Civ. Prac. & Rem. Code В§ 41.008 greater of 2Г— economic + non-economic up to $750K OR $200K |
| **Default** | Restatement (Second) of Torts В§ 46 | Zone of Danger / Restatement (Second) В§ 313 | Restatement (Second) of Torts В§ 908 |

**Restatement (Second) of Torts В§ 46 IIED four-element test**:
1. **EXTREME AND OUTRAGEOUS CONDUCT** вҖ” beyond all bounds of decency; intolerable in a civilized community
2. **INTENT OR RECKLESSNESS** вҖ” intentional causation of severe emotional distress OR reckless disregard of high probability of causing such distress
3. **CAUSATION** вҖ” defendant's conduct actually and proximately caused plaintiff's emotional distress
4. **SEVERE EMOTIONAL DISTRESS** вҖ” so severe no reasonable person could be expected to endure it; documented psychological or physical manifestations strongly recommended

**NIED jurisdictional split вҖ” three approaches**:
- **IMPACT RULE** (Texas, Florida, Georgia) вҖ” physical impact required; psychological injury alone NOT compensable; preserved by Boyles v. Kerr (Tex. 1993)
- **ZONE OF DANGER** (Illinois, Pennsylvania, New Jersey) вҖ” plaintiff in zone of physical danger from defendant's negligent conduct
- **BYSTANDER / Dillon v. Legg majority** (California, New York, most states) вҖ” observe physical injury to close relative

**Landlord conduct categories meeting IIED threshold (8 categories from case law synthesis)**: (1) SYSTEMATIC HARASSMENT CAMPAIGN to force tenant out of rent-controlled unit (SF cases awarding $500K+); (2) DELIBERATE UTILITY SHUTOFF (Cal. Civ. Code В§ 789.3 $100/day civil penalty plus IIED); (3) THREATS OF VIOLENCE or physical intimidation; (4) FRIVOLOUS EVICTION LAWSUITS to retaliate for protected activity; (5) UNAUTHORIZED ENTRY repeated or with malicious intent; (6) EXTORTIONATE DEMANDS (sexual harassment quid pro quo); (7) DELIBERATE HABITABILITY DESTRUCTION (cutting heat in winter; cutting hot water); (8) RACIAL OR DISCRIMINATORY ANIMUS based on protected class status.

**State Farm Mut. Auto. Ins. Co. v. Campbell, 538 U.S. 408 (2003) due process ratio analysis** вҖ” federal constitutional due process typically requires SINGLE-DIGIT RATIO between punitive and compensatory damages; ratios exceeding 9Г— receive heightened scrutiny and risk judicial reduction; ratio not absolute but presumptive constitutional limit.

**Punitive damages availability and standards**:
- **California** вҖ” Cal. Civ. Code В§ 3294 malice/oppression/fraud by CLEAR AND CONVINCING EVIDENCE; NO STATUTORY CAP; Campbell ratio 1-9Г—
- **New York** вҖ” common-law preponderance of evidence; no cap; Campbell ratio applies
- **Texas** вҖ” Tex. Civ. Prac. & Rem. Code В§ 41.008 capped at greater of (a) 2Г— economic + non-economic up to $750K; OR (b) $200K (no economic); clear-and-convincing malice required
- **Default** вҖ” Restatement (Second) of Torts В§ 908 malicious or reckless conduct

**Trader-landlord critical fact patterns**: (1) SF rent-controlled building utility shutoff 30 days вҖ” systematic harassment $500K IIED + $250K punitive + civil penalties; (2) NY brownstone three frivolous holdovers 18 months retaliation вҖ” $150K emotional distress + NY RPL В§ 234 attorney's fees; (3) TX duplex lockout with tenant hospitalization вҖ” В§ 92.0081 $1K + one month rent + IIED damages + actual + fees; (4) default jurisdiction quid pro quo extortion вҖ” IIED + В§ 3294 punitive capped by Campbell 9Г—; (5) NYC rent-stabilized portfolio class action вҖ” Tenant Anti-Harassment Act $1K-$10K per violation per tenant.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/tenant-emotional-distress-damages`. Thirty-six tests pin: **CA IIED all four elements satisfied**; **IIED missing extreme outrageous**; **IIED missing intent recklessness**; **IIED missing causation**; **IIED missing severe distress**; **NY IIED satisfied** (Howell + В§ 235-d + Tenant Anti-Harassment Act); **TX IIED satisfied** (Twyman + В§ 92.0081); **default IIED satisfied Restatement В§ 46** (Restatement Third also cited); **TX NIED requires physical impact** (Boyles v.
