**Oregon's first-year prohibition is regression-critical**: ORS 90.323(3) blocks ANY landlord-cause termination during the first 12 months of a non-week-to-week tenancy, regardless of notice вҖ” demolition is no exception. WA and CA have no equivalent floor.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/demolition-tenant-notice`. Twenty-six tests pin: **CA standard tenant 120-day compliant** + В§ 7060.4(a) citation; **CA 119-day insufficient** + note regression; **CA senior 62+ with 1-yr residency 365-day extension** + В§ 7060.4(b) citation; **CA disabled tenant with 1-yr residency 365-day extension**; **CA senior without 1-yr residency only 120 days** (regression вҖ” both prongs required); **CA under-62 not-disabled only 120 days**; **CA at 62 boundary qualifies** (вүҘ strict); **CA at 12-month residency boundary qualifies**; **CA senior with 365-day notice compliant**; **CA senior with only 120 days insufficient** (regression target вҖ” must give 365); **OR first-year prohibition blocks demolition regardless of notice** + ORS 90.323(3) citation; **OR at 12-month boundary demolition allowed**; **OR 90-day compliant**; **OR 89-day insufficient**; **OR first-year takes precedence over insufficient notice** (regression вҖ” same input both violations would fire, first-year wins); **WA 120-day compliant** + RCW 59.18.650 citation; **WA 119-day insufficient** + "demo / substantial-rehab" note; **WA no age extension** (regression вҖ” only CA has the extension); **WA no first-year prohibition** (regression вҖ” only OR has it); **default no obligation**; **state routing CA/OR/WA/Default**; **case-insensitive lookup**; **only CA has age-disability extension** (single-test 3-regime invariant вҖ” same senior + disabled + long-tenancy input only triggers CA extension); **only OR has first-year prohibition** (single-test 3-regime invariant); **notice ordering OR < CA = WA for standard** (3-regime ordering invariant); **citations pin authorities** (В§ 7060.4(a) + В§ 7060.4(b) + ORS 90.427 + RCW 59.18.650).

`traderview-expense::death_in_unit_disclosure` is the **state death-in-unit landlord disclosure compliance check** вҖ” addresses the narrow but litigated question of whether a landlord must affirmatively tell prospective tenants that someone died on the premises. The default U.S. position is caveat emptor (no statutory duty), but three jurisdictions carve out distinct affirmative-disclosure regimes with sharply different scope: California has the broadest (3 years, all causes including natural) while South Dakota and Alaska cover only homicides/suicides over 12 months.

**Four regimes** with distinct lookback windows and cause coverage:

| Regime | Statute | Lookback | Covered causes | Direct-inquiry override? |
|--------|---------|----------|----------------|---------------------------|
| `California1710_2` | Cal. Civ. Code В§ 1710.2(a) | **36 months** | ALL deaths (natural + homicide + suicide + other) | **YES** under В§ 1710.2(b) вҖ” even 50-year-old death must be truthfully disclosed if directly asked |
| `SouthDakota` | S.D. Codified Laws В§ 43-4-44 | 12 months | Homicide / suicide / felony only вҖ” natural deaths NOT covered | No |
| `Alaska` | AS 08.88.615 | 12 months | Homicide / suicide only вҖ” agent disclosure not owner disclosure | No |
| `Default` | вҖ” | 0 | None statutorily | Common-law fraud may apply |

**California's two unique features**:
1. **В§ 1710.2(a)(1) HIV/AIDS carve-out** вҖ” landlord may NOT be required to disclose that an occupant was living with HIV or died from AIDS-related complications. Written into law to prevent discrimination.
2. **В§ 1710.2(b) intentional-misrepresentation override** вҖ” the 3-year window does NOT immunize a landlord who LIES in response to a direct inquiry. The HIV/AIDS carve-out also yields to В§ 1710.2(b) вҖ” affirmative-disclosure-not-required is one thing; lying in response to a direct question is still actionable fraud.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/death-in-unit-disclosure`. Twenty-three tests pin: **CA natural-death within 3 years must disclose** (CA-only вҖ” natural causes are covered); **CA at 36-month boundary still within window** (regression вҖ” вүӨ strict); **CA at 37 months outside window** + "37 months ago" note regression; **CA HIV/AIDS carve-out not disclosable**; **CA direct-inquiry misrep overrides 3-year window** (50-year-old death + lie = actionable under В§ 1710.2(b)); **CA direct-inquiry truthful response compliant**; **CA HIV/AIDS direct-inquiry lie STILL actionable** (regression вҖ” В§ 1710.2(b) override on top of В§ 1710.2(a)(1) carve-out); **SD homicide within 12 months must disclose**; **SD natural-death not disclosable** (regression вҖ” SD's narrower cause coverage); **SD homicide outside 12 months not disclosable**; **SD at 12-month boundary within window**; **SD no direct-inquiry override** (regression вҖ” В§ 1710.2(b) is CA-only); **AK homicide within 12 months must disclose** + AS 08.88.615 citation; **AK natural-cause not disclosable**; **default regime no obligation** + "caveat emptor" citation; **no-death no-obligation across all 4 regimes** (1-test 4-regime invariant); **CA longest lookback window** (CA 36 > SD 12 = AK 12); **CA broadest cause coverage** (natural causes only covered by CA); **state routing CA/SD/AK/Default** (NY вҶ’ Default, TX вҶ’ Default); **state routing case-insensitive**; **CA-only direct-inquiry-misrep override** (single-test 4-regime invariant: only CA returns `direct_inquiry_misrep_exposure: true`); **citations pin authorities** (В§ 1710.2(a) + HIV/AIDS + В§ 43-4-44 + 12-month + 08.88.615 + agent disclosure); **CA required-disclosure compliant when no direct inquiry**.

`traderview-expense::dog_breed_restriction_ban` is the **dog breed-specific restriction (BSL) ban in residential rental housing module** вҖ” when may a landlord lawfully refuse to rent to a tenant purely because of the breed of the tenant's dog? Trader-landlord critical for any multifamily rental property considering breed restrictions: Nevada SB 166 (effective October 1, 2025) prohibits insurance underwriting using breed as a factor for landlord liability policies for multifamily residential properties; combined with Nevada's 2013 statewide BSL preemption, landlords generally cannot refuse to rent based on breed alone. Distinct from siblings `pet_fees` (pet deposit caps), `emotional_support_animal_documentation` (ESA framework), `service_animal` (ADA / FHA service-animal protection), `tenant_organizing`, and `fair_chance_housing` (criminal-background screening).

**Two regimes** вҖ” Nevada (3-layer protection) vs Default (no protection):

| Regime | Authority | Insurance ban | BSL preemption | Breed-alone denial |
|--------|-----------|---------------|----------------|---------------------|
| Nevada | NV SB 166 (eff. Oct 1, 2025) + NV SB 103 (2021) + NV Rev. Stat. В§ 202.500 (2013) | **YES** (multifamily) + general (single-family) | **YES** statewide | **UNLAWFUL** |
| Default | No statewide statute; ~22 states have BSL preemption laws varying | NO | NO statewide | Lawful (subject to local) |

**Nevada three-layer protection**:
1. **SB 166 (October 1, 2025)** вҖ” insurers MAY NOT use dog breed as a factor when underwriting landlord liability policies for **multi-family residential properties**.
2. **SB 103 (2021)** вҖ” insurers MAY NOT deny homeowners or renters coverage based SOLELY on dog breed (broader scope).
3. **NV Rev. Stat. В§ 202.500 (2013)** вҖ” statewide BSL preemption; local governments CANNOT pass laws banning specific breeds (e.g., pit bulls). Nevada was the **14th state** to prohibit BSL by local governments.

Pinned by `nv_breed_alone_denial_unlawful`, `nv_insurance_breed_underwriting_multifamily_violates_sb166`, `nv_insurance_breed_underwriting_single_family_violates_sb103` (single-family falls under SB 103 instead of SB 166), `nv_local_government_bsl_preempted`, `nv_citation_pins_three_authorities`, `nv_note_pins_sb166`, `nv_note_pins_sb103`, `nv_note_pins_2013_bsl_preemption` (14th state + pit bulls), `nv_sb166_multifamily_specific_vs_sb103_general_distinguished` (multifamily вҶ’ SB 166; single-family вҶ’ SB 103), and `nv_uniquely_engages_statewide_protection_invariant`.

**Three lawful pathways under Nevada (landlords may still require)**:
1. Pet deposits within security-deposit cap
2. Liability coverage
3. Behavior screenings (history of bites, training, dog temperament evaluation)

But landlords MAY NOT refuse to rent purely because of breed. Pinned by `nv_behavior_screening_lawful`, `nv_declined_liability_coverage_lawful`, `nv_declined_pet_deposit_lawful`, `nv_three_lawful_pathways_unaffected_by_multifamily_status` (3-basis Г— 2-property-type sweep = all 6 lawful), and `nv_note_pins_lawful_requirements`.

**Default fallback** вҖ” no statewide breed discrimination protection; ~22 states have BSL preemption laws at local-government level (varies by state); many states still allow breed-specific bans at city/county level; insurance may still consider breed in most states. Maryland counties and Massachusetts municipalities have enacted breed discrimination bans. FHA does NOT cover breed unless tied to disability service-animal accommodation. Pinned by `default_no_protection_breed_alone_lawful`, `denial_basis_truth_table_default` (all 6 bases lawful at default), `default_citation_pins_no_statewide_statute`, `default_note_pins_22_state_landscape`, and `default_note_pins_fha_carveout`.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/dog-breed-restriction-ban`. Twenty-two tests pin: **NV breed alone denial unlawful** (SB 166); **NV behavior screening lawful**; **NV declined liability coverage lawful**; **NV declined pet deposit lawful**; **NV insurance breed underwriting multifamily violates SB 166**; **NV insurance breed underwriting single-family violates SB 103** (broader SB 103); **NV local government BSL preempted** (В§ 202.500); **NV citation pins three authorities** (SB 166 + October 1, 2025 + SB 103 + В§ 202.500 + 2013 BSL preemption); **NV note pins SB 166** (multi-family residential); **NV note pins SB 103** (SOLELY); **NV note pins 2013 BSL preemption** (14th state + pit bulls); **NV note pins lawful requirements** (pet deposits + liability coverage + behavior screenings); **default no protection breed alone lawful**; **default citation pins no statewide statute**; **default note pins 22-state landscape**; **default note pins FHA carve-out** (disability service-animal accommodation); **two regimes routed correctly**; **denial basis truth table NV** (6-cell sweep: 3 unlawful + 3 lawful); **denial basis truth table default** (6-cell sweep: all 6 lawful); **NV uniquely engages statewide protection invariant**; **NV SB 166 multifamily-specific vs SB 103 general distinguished**; **NV three lawful pathways unaffected by multifamily status** (3-basis Г— 2-property-type sweep).

`traderview-expense::sex_offender_database_notice` is the **state landlord sex-offender-database notice disclosure compliance check (Megan's Law database notice in residential leases)** вҖ” California is the ONLY state with a statutory mandate that every residential lease include specific verbatim notice language pointing tenants to the state's Megan's Law database. Other states rely on community notification by law enforcement (the registry itself + AG/prosecutor-driven community alerts) rather than a lease-side disclosure. Rounds out the hazard-disclosure / community-information cluster alongside `military_ordnance_disclosure`, `asbestos_disclosure`, `lead_disclosure`, `mold_disclosure`, `radon_disclosure`, `flood_disclosure`, `meth_contamination_disclosure`, `bedbug_disclosure`, `fire_sprinkler_disclosure`, and `death_in_unit_disclosure`.

**Three regimes**:

| Regime | Authority | Lease disclosure mandate | Verbatim language required |
|--------|-----------|---------------------------|------------------------------|
| `California` | Cal. Civ. Code В§ 2079.10a + Pen. Code В§ 290.46 | **YES** | **YES** (no paraphrasing) |
| `NewJersey` | N.J.S.A. 2C:7-1 et seq. (RCNL) | No (community notification only) | n/a |
| `Default` | 34 U.S.C. В§ 20911 et seq. (federal Megan's Law) | No | n/a |

**California В§ 2079.10a вҖ” three independent compliance gates**:

| Gate | Statute | Rule |
|------|---------|------|
| В§ 2079.10a(a) | Cal. Civ. Code В§ 2079.10a(a) | Lease must contain Megan's Law database notice вҖ” VERBATIM statutory language, no paraphrasing |
| В§ 2079.10a(b) | Cal. Civ. Code В§ 2079.10a(b) | Notice must be provided at lease execution / when rental period begins |
| В§ 2079.10a(c) | Cal. Civ. Code В§ 2079.10a(c) | Landlord NOT required to provide specific offender names or addresses; tenant searches database directly at www.meganslaw.ca.gov |

The verbatim notice reads: "Notice: Pursuant to Section 290.46 of the Penal Code, information about specified registered sex offenders is made available to the public via an Internet Web site maintained by the Department of Justice at www.meganslaw.ca.gov. Depending on an offender's criminal history, this information will include either the address at which the offender resides or the community of residence and ZIP code in which he or she resides."

**Three independent California violations** вҖ” each is a standalone failure:
- Missing notice (В§ 2079.10a(a))
- Paraphrased instead of verbatim language (В§ 2079.10a(a))
- Notice not at lease execution (В§ 2079.10a(b))

Pinned by `california_missing_notice_violation`, `california_paraphrased_notice_violation`, `california_notice_after_execution_violation`, and the 3-cell truth-table invariant `ca_three_independent_violations_invariant`.

**В§ 2079.10a(c) вҖ” landlord has NO statutory duty to investigate the registry or affirmatively warn tenants about specific registered offenders.** Affirmative denial or cancellation of tenancy based on registry status alone may trigger FHA + state anti-discrimination scrutiny. Pinned by `california_landlord_no_specific_offender_info_required_note` and `california_no_duty_to_investigate_note` (UX-text regressions).

**Common-law fraudulent-concealment liability applies universally across regimes.** Where the landlord ACTIVELY misrepresents the absence of registered offenders (e.g., affirmatively states "there are no offenders in this neighborhood" when the landlord knows otherwise), common-law fraud liability attaches regardless of state lease-disclosure regime. Pinned by `active_misrepresentation_triggers_common_law_fraud_default`, `active_misrepresentation_triggers_fraud_even_in_california`, `active_misrepresentation_triggers_fraud_even_in_new_jersey`, and the universal invariant `active_misrepresentation_triggers_fraud_universally_invariant`.

**Community notification framework exists across ALL regimes.** Every regime sits within at least the federal Megan's Law framework (34 U.S.C. В§ 20911 et seq., formerly 42 U.S.C. В§ 14071). The federal framework requires states to maintain public registries; what differs is whether the state ALSO imposes a lease-side disclosure mandate. Pinned by `community_notification_framework_exists_across_all_regimes_invariant`.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/sex-offender-database-notice`. Twenty-one tests pin: **CA verbatim notice at execution compliant + missing notice violation + paraphrased notice violation + notice after execution violation + no specific offender info required note + no duty to investigate note**; **NJ no lease disclosure mandate + compliant without notice**; **Default no state-specific mandate + compliant without lease notice**; **active misrepresentation triggers common-law fraud (Default + CA + NJ)**; **no misrepresentation no common-law fraud**; **only-California-imposes-lease-disclosure-mandate 3-regime invariant**; **only-California-requires-verbatim-language 3-regime invariant**; **community notification framework exists across all 3 regimes invariant**; **active misrepresentation triggers fraud universally 3-regime invariant**; **CA three independent violations 3-cell invariant** (missing/paraphrased/timing); **citation pins authority per regime** (В§ 2079.10a + В§ 290.46 for CA; N.J.S.A. 2C:7-1 for NJ; 34 U.S.C. В§ 20911 for Default); **sibling-module note across all 3 regimes**.

`traderview-expense::short_term_rental_conversion` is the **short-term rental (STR) conversion restriction compliance module** вҖ” when may a trader-landlord lawfully convert a long-term residential rental unit to a short-term rental (Airbnb / VRBO / Booking.com listing under 30 nights) without violating local registration ordinances? Trader-landlord critical for any rental owner in NYC + SF + LA considering STR conversion: unregistered hosting exposes owner to per-violation penalties + booking-platform verification refusal + ordinance enforcement. Distinct from siblings `rental_property_registration` (general landlord registration), `condominium_conversion_protection` (condo conversion), `tenant_relocation_assistance` (relocation), and `just_cause_eviction` (eviction).

**Four regimes** вҖ” three city-specific frameworks vs Default:

| Regime | Authority | Registration | Primary residence | Unhosted cap | Max penalty |
|--------|-----------|--------------|-------------------|--------------|--------------|
| NYC | Local Law 18 of 2022 (eff. Sep 5, 2023) | OSE | Permanent occupant + **host present during stay** | **0** (no unhosted allowed) | **$5,000** per violation |
| SF | Admin. Code Chapter 41A (Ord. 218-14) | Business Registration Certificate | **270 nights/year occupancy** | **90 nights/year** | **$1,000** per day |
| LA | LAMC В§ 12.22 A.32 (Home-Sharing Ordinance eff. Nov 1, 2019) | LADBS Home-Sharing Program | Yes | **120/year** default; **240/year** with Extended permit | LAMC enforcement + recordation |
| Default | Locality-controlled | None statewide | None | N/A | N/A |

**NYC Local Law 18 host-present model** вҖ” NYC uniquely requires the host to be the permanent occupant AND PRESENT during the stay (host-and-guest model only); unhosted stays under 30 nights are unlawful. Booking platforms (Airbnb, VRBO, Booking.com) must verify valid OSE registration before processing reservations. Penalties up to $5,000 per violation for both hosts AND platforms. Pinned by `nyc_registered_host_present_lawful`, `nyc_unregistered_unlawful`, `nyc_host_not_present_unlawful`, `nyc_stay_over_30_nights_no_registration_required` (вүҘ30 nights = long-term rental, not subject to Local Law 18), `nyc_max_penalty_5000_per_violation`, `nyc_uniquely_requires_host_present_invariant` (NYC host-presence requirement absent in SF/LA), and `nyc_citation_pins_local_law_18`.

**SF Chapter 41A primary-residence 270-night occupancy + 90-night unhosted cap** вҖ” host must register and obtain Business Registration Certificate; primary residence requirement is 270 nights/year occupancy; cap of 90 unhosted nights per year; penalties up to $1,000 per day for unregistered hosting. Pinned by `sf_registered_within_caps_lawful`, `sf_unregistered_unlawful`, `sf_below_270_night_primary_residence_violates` (269 fails), `sf_at_270_night_boundary_compliant` (exactly 270 compliant), `sf_over_90_unhosted_nights_violates` (91 violates), `sf_at_90_unhosted_nights_boundary_compliant` (exactly 90 compliant), `sf_max_penalty_1000_per_day`, `sf_uniquely_requires_270_night_occupancy_invariant`, and `sf_citation_pins_chapter_41a`.

**LA В§ 12.22 A.32 120-day default vs 240-day Extended Home-Sharing permit** вҖ” host must register with LADBS Home-Sharing Program; cap of 120 days/year unhosted by default; cap extended to 240 days/year with Extended Home-Sharing permit. Pinned by `la_registered_within_default_cap_lawful`, `la_unregistered_unlawful`, `la_at_120_default_boundary_compliant` (exactly 120 compliant), `la_121_default_violates`, `la_extended_permit_240_day_cap` (240 days OK with permit), `la_extended_permit_241_violates`, `la_not_primary_residence_violates`, `la_extended_permit_doubles_unhosted_cap_invariant` (default 120; extended 240), and `la_citation_pins_lamc_12_22_a_32`.

**Default locality-controlled fallback** вҖ” most states do not regulate STR conversion at state level; some states have preempted local STR regulation (FL + AZ + partial TX); some have permitted it (NY + CA + CO). Pinned by `default_no_regulation_lawful`, `default_unregistered_still_lawful`, and `default_citation_pins_locality_preemption`.

**Regime unhosted cap truth table** вҖ” NYC: 0 (none allowed unhosted); SF: 90/year; LA: 120/year default + 240/year extended; Default: 0 (no cap). Pinned by `regime_unhosted_cap_truth_table` (4-regime sweep).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/short-term-rental-conversion`. Thirty tests pin: **NYC registered host present lawful**; **NYC unregistered unlawful** (Local Law 18); **NYC host not present unlawful**; **NYC stay over 30 nights no registration required**; **NYC max penalty $5,000 per violation**; **SF registered within caps lawful**; **SF unregistered unlawful** (Chapter 41A); **SF below 270-night primary residence violates** (269); **SF at 270-night boundary compliant** (270); **SF over 90 unhosted nights violates** (91); **SF at 90 unhosted nights boundary compliant** (90); **SF max penalty $1,000 per day**; **LA registered within default cap lawful**; **LA unregistered unlawful** (В§ 12.22 A.32); **LA at 120 default boundary compliant**; **LA 121 default violates**; **LA Extended Permit 240-day cap**; **LA Extended Permit 241 violates**; **LA not primary residence violates**; **default no regulation lawful**; **default unregistered still lawful**; **NYC citation pins Local Law 18**; **SF citation pins Chapter 41A**; **LA citation pins LAMC В§ 12.22 A.32**; **default citation pins locality preemption** (FL/AZ preempt; NY/CA/CO permit); **four regimes routed correctly**; **NYC uniquely requires host present invariant**; **SF uniquely requires 270-night occupancy invariant**; **LA Extended Permit doubles unhosted cap invariant** (120 вҶ’ 240); **regime unhosted cap truth table** (4-regime sweep: NYC=0 / SF=90 / LA=120 / Default=0).

`traderview-expense::military_ordnance_disclosure` is the **state landlord former-federal-or-state-ordnance-location disclosure compliance check** вҖ” specialized hazard-disclosure topic prompted by the December 10, 1983 Tierra Santa tragedy in San Diego where a live munition exploded in a residential area that was formerly a U.S. military ordnance location, killing two residents. California responded with Cal. Civ. Code В§ 1940.7, the only statute of its kind. Rounds out the hazard-disclosure cluster alongside `asbestos_disclosure`, `lead_disclosure`, `mold_disclosure`, `radon_disclosure`, `flood_disclosure`, `meth_contamination_disclosure`, `bedbug_disclosure`, `fire_sprinkler_disclosure`, and `death_in_unit_disclosure`.

**Three regimes**:

| Regime | Authority | Disclosure mandate | Trigger |
|--------|-----------|----------------------|---------|
| `California` | Cal. Civ. Code В§ 1940.7 | **YES** | Actual knowledge + within 1 mile |
| `FederalMMRP` | 10 U.S.C. В§ 2710 + В§ 2701 (Defense Environmental Restoration Program / FUDS database) | No (public information only) | n/a |
| `Default` | None | No (common-law latent-defect may apply) | n/a |

**Cal. Civ. Code В§ 1940.7 вҖ” actual-knowledge AND within-1-mile**:

| Subsection | Statute | Rule |
|------------|---------|------|
| В§ 1940.7(a) | Cal. Civ. Code В§ 1940.7(a) | **Legislative findings** вҖ” Tierra Santa tragedy of December 10, 1983 |
| В§ 1940.7(b) | Cal. Civ. Code В§ 1940.7(b) | **Prospective tenant** вҖ” written notice prior to execution of rental agreement |
| В§ 1940.7(c) | Cal. Civ. Code В§ 1940.7(c) | **Grandfathered tenancies** in existence on January 1, 1990 вҖ” notice as soon as practicable |
| В§ 1940.7(d)(1) | Cal. Civ. Code В§ 1940.7(d)(1) | **"Former federal or state ordnance location"** вҖ” area identified by federal or state government as once used for military training and may contain potentially explosive munitions |
| В§ 1940.7(d)(2) | Cal. Civ. Code В§ 1940.7(d)(2) | **"Neighborhood area"** вҖ” within ONE MILE of the residential dwelling |

**The disclosure trigger requires BOTH actual knowledge AND within-1-mile.** A 4-cell truth table:

| Actual knowledge | Within 1 mile | Trigger fired |
|--------------------|----------------|----------------|
| No | No | No |
| No | Yes | No |
| Yes | No | No |
| **Yes** | **Yes** | **YES** |

Pinned by the invariant `disclosure_trigger_requires_actual_knowledge_AND_within_1_mile_invariant` (4 cells).

**Constructive knowledge is NOT enough.** В§ 1940.7 imposes the disclosure obligation only where the landlord has ACTUAL KNOWLEDGE of the former ordnance location. Constructive knowledge, duty to investigate, or imputed knowledge does not trigger the statute. Pinned by `california_no_actual_knowledge_trigger_not_fired` and the UX-text note `california_constructive_knowledge_not_triggered_note`.

**Locations beyond 1 mile fall outside the neighborhood area.** В§ 1940.7(d)(2) precisely defines "neighborhood area" as within ONE MILE of the residential dwelling. A former ordnance site 1.1 miles away вҖ” even with landlord's actual knowledge вҖ” does not trigger the duty. Pinned by `california_actual_knowledge_but_location_beyond_1_mile_no_trigger`.

**Federal FUDS database is public but creates no landlord disclosure duty.** The DoD/Army Corps of Engineers maintains a Formerly Used Defense Sites inventory under 10 U.S.C. В§ 2710. It's public information that informs the California actual-knowledge analysis but does not impose an independent federal mandate on landlords. Pinned by `federal_mmrp_no_landlord_disclosure_mandate`.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/military-ordnance-disclosure`. Eighteen tests pin: **CA actual knowledge within 1 mile with notice compliant**; **CA actual knowledge no notice violation** (В§ 1940.7(b)); **CA no actual knowledge trigger not fired** (compliant with no duty); **CA actual knowledge but beyond 1 mile no trigger**; **CA grandfathered tenant practicable notice compliant** (В§ 1940.7(c)); **CA grandfathered tenant no practicable notice violation**; **federal MMRP no landlord disclosure mandate**; **Default no statutory mandate**; **Default even with actual knowledge no violation**; **CA definitional notes present** (В§ 1940.7(d)(1) + (d)(2)); **CA constructive knowledge not triggered UX-text note**; **only-California-mandates-disclosure 3-regime invariant**; **disclosure trigger requires actual knowledge AND within 1 mile 4-cell invariant**; **non-California regimes never trigger invariant**; **citation pins authority per regime**; **sibling-module note across all 3 regimes** (UX-text regression for hazard-disclosure cluster); **neighborhood radius constant 1 mile invariant**; **legislative findings cite Tierra Santa in California path** (UX-text regression).

`traderview-expense::meth_contamination_disclosure` is the **state methamphetamine-contamination landlord disclosure compliance check** вҖ” sits adjacent to `lead_disclosure`, `mold_disclosure`, `bedbug_disclosure`, `radon_disclosure`, and `flood_disclosure` in the suite of substance-/hazard-specific disclosure tables. Three states ship statutory regimes with explicit ug/100cmВІ remediation standards; the rest rely on the federal FHA material-defect doctrine. The defining axis between the regulated regimes is **whether remediation extinguishes the disclosure obligation** (yes in CO + AZ; **no** in MT, the regression-critical distinguisher).

**Four regimes** with distinct contamination thresholds and post-remediation obligation extinguishment:

| Regime | Statute | Remediation standard | Post-remediation extinguishes disclosure? | Unique feature |
|--------|---------|----------------------|--------------------------------------------|----------------|
| `Arizona` | A.R.S. В§ 32-1166.04 | **0.1 ug/100cmВІ** (STRICTEST) | Yes | Entry by non-owners barred until state-standard cleaning |
| `Colorado` | Colo. Rev. Stat. В§ 38-35.7-103 + 6 CCR 1014-3 | 0.5 ug/100cmВІ (4.0 attics/crawl + 1.5 encapsulated) | Yes | CDPHE-certified remediation extinguishes disclosure |
| `Montana` | Mont. Code Ann. В§ 75-10-1301 et seq. | 1.5 ug/100cmВІ | **NO** | Landlord MUST disclose knowledge + remediation status even after cleanup |
| `Default` | 42 U.S.C. В§ 3604 (FHA) | вҖ” | NO (general fraud + FHA material-defect doctrine) | No statewide statute identified |

Strict ordering of standards: AZ (0.1) < CO (0.5) < MT (1.5). The integer field `remediation_standard_ug_per_100cm2_x10` is stored Г—10 to avoid float math (1, 5, 15).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/meth-contamination-disclosure`. Twenty tests pin: **CO no-knowledge no-obligation**; **CO knowledge unremediated undisclosed вҶ’ violation** + 0.5 ug/100cmВІ citation regression; **CO knowledge unremediated disclosed вҶ’ compliant**; **CO knowledge remediated вҶ’ disclosure extinguished + "extinguished" note regression**; **AZ strictest standard 0.1 ug/100cmВІ** + "STRICTEST" + "entry by non-owners barred" citation regression; **AZ post-remediation extinguishes**; **MT remediation does NOT extinguish disclosure** (regression-critical distinguisher вҖ” Montana's distinct rule); **MT post-remediation with disclosure compliant**; **MT standard 1.5 ug/100cmВІ**; **MT no-knowledge no-obligation**; **default no-statewide-statute falls back to FHA** + "3604" + "FHA" citation regression; **default no-knowledge no-disclosure**; **state routing CO/AZ/MT/Default** (TX вҶ’ Default, CA вҶ’ Default); **state routing case-insensitive**; **standards strict ordering AZ < CO < MT** (single-test 3-regime invariant); **extinguishment invariants** (CO + AZ yes; MT + Default no); **citations pin authorities** (В§ 38-35.7-103 + 6 CCR 1014-3 + В§ 32-1166.04 + В§ 75-10-1301 + "does NOT extinguish"); **CO remediation note mentions extinguished**; **MT unremediated violation note mentions material defect**; **AZ-only entry-restriction in citation** (regression вҖ” entry-restriction is AZ-only, not in CO or MT).

`traderview-expense::prevailing_party_attorney_fees` is the **state prevailing-party attorney-fees lease-clause reciprocity compliance check** вҖ” addresses a common landlord drafting trap: lease clauses that award attorney fees only to the LANDLORD on default are often unenforceable as written in states with a reciprocity statute. The clause is transformed by operation of law into a bilateral right, so a prevailing TENANT collects the landlord's attorney fees even though the clause never said so. Distinct from `late_fee_caps` (penalty on tenant late payments) and `lockout_penalties` (penalty for landlord self-help).

**Three regimes**:

| Regime | Statute | Reciprocity? | Waiver | Effective date |
|--------|---------|---------------|--------|-----------------|
| `CaliforniaCivCode1717` | Cal. Civ. Code В§ 1717(a) | **Yes** вҖ” one-way fee clauses construed as mutual | В§ 1717 is fundamental CA policy; choice-of-law clauses overridden | All leases |
| `WashingtonRcw484330` | RCW 4.84.330 | **Yes** вҖ” same transformation | **PROHIBITED** вҖ” any waiver clause is VOID | Leases entered AFTER 1977-09-21 |
| `Default` | вҖ” | **No** вҖ” American Rule | вҖ” | One-way clauses enforced as written |

**The reciprocity rule** (CA + WA): a lease that says "Landlord may recover attorney fees if Landlord prevails" is automatically read as "the PREVAILING PARTY may recover attorney fees." Tenant beats landlord at trial вҶ’ tenant collects fees from landlord.

**Washington's 1977 effective date is the regression-critical pin**: a one-way-landlord clause in a 1976 lease that goes to trial today is enforced as written under common law (tenant gets nothing); the same clause in a 1978 lease is reformed by RCW 4.84.330 (tenant gets fees). The post-1977 reciprocity / pre-1977 grandfathered split is a real boundary.

**Default-regime trap**: in states without a reciprocity statute, a landlord who drafts a one-way-landlord fee clause and LOSES at trial pays his own fees but does not get the tenant's. Landlord-favorable drafting backfires only via the lost-case path; if landlord wins the clause works as intended.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/prevailing-party-attorney-fees`. Twenty tests pin: **no clause American Rule** + "American Rule" citation; **CA one-way landlord clause + tenant prevails вҶ’ fees** + В§ 1717(a) + "MUTUAL" citation regression (classic В§ 1717 case); **CA one-way landlord + landlord prevails вҶ’ fees** (clause works as intended); **CA bilateral clause prevailing party fees**; **WA post-1977 one-way landlord + tenant prevails вҶ’ fees** + RCW 4.84.330 + "waiver PROHIBITED" citation; **WA pre-1977 lease no reciprocity** (regression вҖ” 1977 boundary excludes); **WA pre-1977 one-way + landlord prevails still gets fees** (clause as-written enforces under common law); **WA post-1977 one-way-tenant + landlord prevails вҶ’ reciprocity flips to give landlord fees** (symmetric regression); **WA bilateral post-1977 compliant**; **default one-way-landlord + tenant prevails вҶ’ NO fees** + "reciprocity does NOT apply" note (American Rule outcome); **default one-way-landlord + landlord prevails вҶ’ fees** (clause as written); **default bilateral works**; **state routing CA/WA/Default**; **case-insensitive routing**; **CA no pre-1977 carve-out** (regression вҖ” only WA has it, CA applies regardless); **WA pre-1977 vs post-1977 paradox split** (same input вҶ’ opposite results regression-critical); **only CA + WA have reciprocity** (single-test 3-regime invariant); **citations pin authorities**; **negative fees clamped**; **CA one-way-tenant + landlord prevails вҶ’ fees** (symmetric reciprocity test вҖ” flips in both directions).

`traderview-expense::damage_deduction_itemization` is the **state security-deposit damage-deduction itemization, receipt-attachment, photographic-documentation, and depreciation-method compliance check** вҖ” sibling to `security_deposit_caps` (how much), `deposit_return_windows` (when), `deposit_interest` (accrual), `pre_move_out_inspection` (cure-period inspection), and `carpet_replacement_useful_life` (specific finite-life category). This module handles the **ITEMIZATION half** of the move-out accounting: the form, content, and method of the damage-deduction notice that accompanies the security-deposit return.

**Five regimes diverge along five axes**:

| Regime | Statute | Deadline | Receipt threshold | Photographic mandate | Depreciation required | Bad-faith penalty |
|--------|---------|----------|-------------------|-----------------------|-----------------------|--------------------|
| `California` | Cal. Civ. Code В§ 1950.5(g) + AB 2801 (Stats. 2024 ch. 444) | **21 days** | **$125** | **Yes** (eff. 2025-04-01 repair/cleaning; 2025-07-01 pre-tenancy) | Yes | В§ 1950.5(l) up to **2Г— withheld** |
| `Washington` | RCW 59.18.280 | 30 days | None | No | Yes (case law) | Up to **2Г— deposit** intentional refusal (discretionary) |
| `Oregon` | ORS 90.300(13) | 31 days | None | No | **Yes** (statutory) | Up to **2Г— wrongfully withheld** |
| `Florida` | Fla. Stat. В§ 83.49(3) | 30 days | **$200** | No | Yes | Court costs + attorney fees (no statutory multiplier) |
| `Texas` | Tex. Prop. Code В§ 92.103 + В§ 92.104(c) + В§ 92.109 | 30 days | None | No | **No** (statutory) | **$100 + 3Г— retained** + attorney fees вҖ” **steepest** |

**California AB 2801 is the regime-defining 2025 expansion.** Stats. 2024 ch. 444, signed Sept 2024, amended В§ 1950.5 to require landlords to take photographs of the unit (a) within a reasonable time after possession is returned and before any repair/cleaning, (b) within a reasonable time after any repair/cleaning, and (c) immediately before or at the beginning of any tenancy commencing on or after 2025-07-01. Photographs must be retained for at least four years and provided to the tenant on request. Failure to take the required photographs is itself a violation independent of any other defect. Pinned by `california_ab2801_no_photographs_violation`, the `only_california_imposes_ab2801_photographic_mandate_5_regime_invariant`, and `california_ab2801_pretenancy_photograph_note_present` (UX-text regression).

**Texas has the steepest bad-faith penalty.** В§ 92.109(a) imposes $100 + 3Г— the amount of the deposit wrongfully retained + reasonable attorney fees on a landlord who acts in bad faith вҖ” versus California / Washington / Oregon's 2Г— multiplier and Florida's no-multiplier fee-shifting. At $1000 withheld in bad faith, Texas exposure is $3100; CA / WA / OR exposure is $2000; Florida exposure is $0 (statutory) plus court costs and attorney fees. Pinned by `texas_bad_faith_steepest_penalty_100_plus_3x` and the 5-regime invariant `texas_has_steepest_bad_faith_penalty_5_regime_invariant`.

**Texas is the only state in this matrix without a statutory depreciation mandate for finite-useful-life items.** В§ 92.104 prohibits charges for normal wear and tear and requires an itemized list, but does not statutorily compel the landlord to depreciate the replacement cost of a finite-life item like paint or carpet. California, Washington, Oregon, and Florida all require depreciation either by statute or by binding case law. Pinned by `texas_no_depreciation_not_a_statutory_violation` and the 5-regime invariant `only_texas_lacks_statutory_depreciation_requirement_5_regime_invariant`.

**California's 21-day deadline is the shortest in the matrix.** WA + FL + TX are 30 days; OR is 31 days; CA is 21. The compressed timeline means California landlords have nine fewer days than the federal-median state to complete the itemized accounting plus AB 2801 photographic deliverable plus receipt-attachment review for any line item above $125. Pinned by the 5-regime invariant `only_california_has_21_day_shortest_deadline_5_regime_invariant`.

**Ordinary-wear-and-tear exclusion is universal.** All five regimes statutorily prohibit deductions for wear resulting from ordinary use of the premises вҖ” paint fade, minor scuffs, carpet matting in traffic paths, etc. Pinned by `wear_and_tear_excluded_across_all_regimes_invariant` (single failure point covers all 5 regimes).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/damage-deduction-itemization`. Thirty tests pin: **CA full compliance**; **CA past 21-day deadline violation**; **CA at 21-day boundary compliant** (вүӨ); **CA $125 threshold no receipts violation**; **CA at $125 threshold no receipts compliant** (NOT above); **CA AB 2801 no photographs violation**; **CA bad-faith 2Г— withheld amount**; **WA full compliance**; **WA past 30-day deadline violation**; **WA at 30-day boundary compliant**; **WA no receipt threshold no violation on large deduction** (regression вҖ” WA has no statutory threshold); **WA bad-faith 2Г— deposit exposure**; **OR full compliance**; **OR no depreciation violation** (regression вҖ” ORS 90.300(13) statutory mandate); **FL full compliance**; **FL $200 threshold no receipts violation**; **FL bad-faith no statutory multiplier only fees** (regression вҖ” В§ 83.49(3)(c) doesn't multiply); **TX full compliance**; **TX no depreciation not a statutory violation** (regression вҖ” TX is the ONLY regime in this matrix without statutory depreciation mandate); **TX bad-faith steepest penalty $100 + 3Г—**; **wear-and-tear excluded across all 5 regimes invariant**; **only-CA-imposes-AB2801-photographic-mandate 5-regime invariant**; **only-TX-lacks-statutory-depreciation-requirement 5-regime invariant**; **only-CA-has-21-day-shortest-deadline 5-regime invariant**; **only-CA-and-FL-have-receipt-thresholds 5-regime invariant**; **TX-has-steepest-bad-faith-penalty 5-regime invariant** (at $1000 withheld: TX = $3100 vs CA/WA/OR = $2000 vs FL = $0); **no-bad-faith-zero-penalty across all 5 regimes invariant**; **citation pins authority per regime** (В§ 1950.5(g)(2) for CA, RCW 59.18.280 for WA, ORS 90.300(13) for OR, В§ 83.49(3) for FL, В§ 92.109 for TX); **note pairs module with sibling modules** (deposit_return_windows + security_deposit_caps + deposit_interest + pre_move_out_inspection вҖ” UX-text regression); **CA AB 2801 pre-tenancy photograph note present** (UX-text regression with 2025-07-01 effective date).

`traderview-expense::pre_move_out_inspection` is the **state pre-move-out inspection landlord compliance check** вҖ” California is currently the only state with a statutory pre-move-out inspection regime under Cal. Civ. Code В§ 1950.5(f), but it imposes substantial procedural obligations that materially affect security-deposit disputes via a unique waiver mechanic. Distinct from `move_in_inspection` (START-of-tenancy condition documentation) and from `abandoned_property_handling` (belongings left behind after vacating).

**Two regimes**:

| Regime | Statute | Notice required | Earliest inspection date | Itemized statement | В§ 1950.5(f)(4) waiver of undisclosed damages |
|--------|---------|------------------|--------------------------|----------------------|--------------------------------------------------|
| `CaliforniaCivCode19505F` | Cal. Civ. Code В§ 1950.5(f) | **Yes** вҖ” written notice of right to request inspection + be present | **No earlier than 14 days (2 weeks)** before termination | **Required** | **YES** вҖ” if premises were clear at inspection, landlord SHALL NOT deduct for items not on itemized statement |
| `Default` | вҖ” | No | вҖ” | вҖ” | No |

**The В§ 1950.5(f)(4) waiver mechanic is regression-critical**: if (a) landlord conducted the pre-move-out inspection AND (b) tenant possessions did NOT prevent landlord from identifying repairs/cleanings during the inspection, the landlord CANNOT use the security deposit to deduct for items NOT identified in the itemized statement at move-out. This effectively converts the optional pre-move-out inspection into a waiver-of-undisclosed-damages event for the landlord. Result: if premises were clear, landlord must catch all the damages during the pre-move-out walk-through or lose the right to deduct.

**Notice obligation independent of tenant request**: В§ 1950.5(f)(1) requires landlord to NOTIFY tenant of the right to request inspection regardless of whether tenant requests one. Skipping the notice itself is a violation.

**Cure period**: tenant has from inspection date until termination to remedy identified deficiencies. This is the practical purpose of the regime вҖ” tenant gets a chance to fix issues to avoid deposit deductions.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/pre-move-out-inspection`. Twenty tests pin: **CA missing notice of right violation** + В§ 1950.5(f)(1) citation; **CA notice provided no request compliant**; **CA inspection 15 days before termination too early** + В§ 1950.5(f)(2) + "15 days" note; **CA at 14-day boundary compliant** (вүӨ strict); **CA inspection 5 days compliant**; **CA missing itemized statement violation** + В§ 1950.5(f)(3); **CA premises clear with undisclosed deduction waiver violation** + В§ 1950.5(f)(4) (regression-critical waiver mechanic); **CA premises blocked no waiver вҖ” undisclosed deduction allowed** (regression вҖ” blocking possessions defeat the waiver); **CA full compliance**; **default no obligation** + "No statewide pre-move-out inspection statute" citation; **default landlord can deduct undisclosed no waiver** (regression вҖ” default regime has no waiver); **state routing CA/Default + case-insensitive**; **CA notice requirement does NOT depend on tenant request** (regression вҖ” landlord must notify regardless); **CA one day above 14 too early** off-by-one; **CA zero days same-day compliant** (statute only caps EARLIEST not LATEST); **CA waiver only when premises clear + itemized provided** (3-condition gate regression); **only-CA-has-pre-move-out-inspection-obligation** 2-regime invariant; **citations pin authorities** (В§ 1950.5(f)(1) + (f)(2) + (f)(3) + (f)(4)); **CA no inspection no waiver even premises clear** (regression-critical вҖ” if tenant never requested + no inspection conducted, the В§ 1950.5(f)(4) waiver does not apply and landlord retains full back-end deduction rights).

`traderview-expense::written_lease_requirement` is the **state written-vs-oral lease requirement compliance check** вҖ” universal U.S. floor under the Statute of Frauds: leases of real property for a term EXCEEDING ONE YEAR must be in writing and signed by the party to be charged; leases of ONE YEAR OR LESS may be oral and remain enforceable. State variations add consequences for oral leases that exceed the threshold (Illinois year-to-year conversion) and provide part-performance escape valves. Sibling to `plain_language_lease` (content requirement for written leases) and `lease_translation` (non-English-language translation requirement).

**Five regimes**:

| Regime | Authority | Oral lease > 1 year consequence |
|--------|-----------|-----------------------------------|
| `NewYork` | N.Y. Gen. Oblig. Law В§ 5-703 | Unenforceable beyond 1 year; part-performance exception |
| `Illinois` | 740 ILCS 80/2 + case-law conversion | **UNIQUE** вҖ” converts to year-to-year tenancy terminable on 60-day written notice |
| `California` | Cal. Civ. Code В§ 1624(a)(3) + В§ 1971 | Unenforceable beyond 1 year |
| `Washington` | RCW 59.18.230 + RCW 64.04.010 | Unenforceable beyond 1 year |
| `Default` | UCC В§ 2A-201 + state common law | Unenforceable beyond 1 year; part-performance exception varies |

**Universal one-year threshold (365 days) is the boundary.** Statute reads "exceeding 1 year" вҖ” exactly 365 days = oral enforceable; 366 days = writing required. Pinned by `lease_365_days_at_boundary_oral_enforceable`, `lease_366_days_above_boundary_requires_writing`, and the 4-cell boundary invariant `one_year_threshold_strict_boundary_invariant` (1/365/366/730 days).

**Illinois is the only regime that converts oral > 1-year leases into enforceable year-to-year tenancies.** Other regimes leave such leases entirely unenforceable beyond the first year (subject to part-performance exception). The IL conversion preserves the tenancy as a year-to-year arrangement terminable on 60-day written notice вҖ” protecting the tenant from sudden ejectment while preserving the original landlord-tenant common-law framework. Pinned by `illinois_oral_2_year_lease_converts_to_year_to_year`, `illinois_oral_6_month_lease_no_conversion`, `illinois_written_2_year_lease_no_conversion`, and the 5-regime invariant `only_illinois_converts_oral_to_year_to_year_invariant`.

**Part-performance exception engages only when (a) lease oral AND (b) exceeds 1 year.** Part performance + вүӨ 1-year lease = no-op (the lease is already enforceable orally). Part performance + written lease = no-op (the lease is enforceable by writing alone). Only the (oral + > 1 year) intersection invokes the part-performance escape valve. Pinned by `part_performance_engages_for_oral_lease_exceeding_1_year`, `no_part_performance_oral_lease_exceeding_1_year_unenforceable`, `part_performance_irrelevant_when_lease_within_one_year`, and the 4-cell truth-table invariant `part_performance_only_engages_when_oral_and_exceeding_one_year_invariant`.

**Universal invariants pinned across all 5 regimes**:
- `written_lease_always_enforceable_regardless_of_term_invariant` (4 term levels Г— 5 regimes = 20 cells)
- `oral_lease_within_one_year_always_enforceable_invariant` (4 term levels Г— 5 regimes = 20 cells)
- `oral_lease_above_one_year_unenforceable_unless_il_or_part_performance_invariant` (3 term levels Г— 4 non-IL regimes + IL always-enforceable)

**NY GBL В§ 5-702 Plain Language Law is a separate content requirement.** The written-vs-oral threshold (В§ 5-703) is distinct from the plain-language content requirement (В§ 5-702 вҖ” covered by `plain_language_lease` module). A lease can be in writing yet fail plain-language scrutiny. Pinned by `new_york_plain_language_note_present` (UX-text regression).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/written-lease-requirement`. Twenty-four tests pin: **lease 30 days oral enforceable under all regimes**; **lease 365 days at boundary oral enforceable**; **lease 366 days above boundary requires writing**; **lease 2 year written enforceable**; **Illinois oral 2-year lease converts to year-to-year** + 60-day termination notice; **Illinois oral 6-month no conversion**; **Illinois written 2-year no conversion**; **part-performance engages for oral lease exceeding 1 year**; **no part-performance oral > 1 year unenforceable**; **part-performance irrelevant when lease within 1 year**; **NY plain-language note present**; **California 2-year oral unenforceable**; **California oral within year enforceable**; **Washington oral lease within year authorized** (RCW 59.18.230); **Washington oral 2-year unenforceable**; **Default one-year rule**; **one-year threshold strict boundary 4-cell invariant** (1/365/366/730 days); **only-Illinois-converts-oral-to-year-to-year 5-regime invariant**; **written lease always enforceable regardless of term 20-cell invariant** (5 regimes Г— 4 term levels); **oral lease within 1 year always enforceable 20-cell invariant**; **oral lease above 1 year unenforceable unless IL or part performance 12-cell + 3-IL invariant**; **part-performance only engages when oral and exceeding 1 year 4-cell truth-table invariant**; **citation pins authority per regime** (В§ 5-703 for NY; 740 ILCS 80/2 for IL; В§ 1624(a)(3) for CA; RCW 59.18.230 for WA; UCC В§ 2A-201 for Default); **sibling-module note across all 5 regimes** (UX-text regression for plain_language_lease + lease_translation pairing).

`traderview-expense::holdover_tenant_damages` is the **holdover tenant landlord-damages calculation module** вҖ” what a landlord may recover when a tenant remains in possession after lease expiration. Two structurally distinct regimes operate across U.S. landlord-tenant law: **(1) STATUTORY DOUBLE RENT** recovery imposes a multiplier on the holdover damages (Florida вҖ” Fla. Stat. В§ 83.58); **(2) RENT-ACCEPTANCE month-to-month conversion** automatically renews the tenancy on the same terms when the landlord accepts post-expiration rent (California вҖ” Cal. Civ. Code В§ 1945; New York вҖ” N.Y. Real Prop. Law В§ 232-c). Default common-law (Restatement (Second) of Property В§ 14.5) gives the landlord an election between treating the holdover as a new tenancy or suing for use-and-occupancy damages.

**Four regimes**:

| Regime | Authority | Damages multiplier | Rent-acceptance effect |
|--------|-----------|--------------------|------------------------|
| Florida | Fla. Stat. В§ 83.58 | **2Г—** rent for holdover period | Split authority on waiver вҖ” document carefully |
| California | Cal. Civ. Code В§ 1945 | 1Г— rent (actual damages) | Rebuttable presumption of month-to-month renewal on same terms |
| New York | N.Y. Real Prop. Law В§ 232-c | 1Г— rent (use-and-occupancy) | Mandatory month-to-month conversion from day after expiration |
| Default | Restatement (2d) of Property В§ 14.5 | 1Г— rent (actual damages) | Landlord election between new-tenancy and trespass-damages theories |

**Florida В§ 83.58 partial-month rule.** "The landlord may recover DOUBLE the amount of rent due ... for the period during which the tenant refuses to surrender possession" вҖ” the "any part thereof" clause means a partial month counts as a full period. 1 day of holdover triggers a full month's double rent. Pinned by `florida_1_day_partial_month_full_charge_per_any_part_thereof`, `florida_29_days_one_month_boundary`, `florida_30_day_holdover_full_month_double_rent`, `florida_31_day_holdover_rounds_up_to_2_months_double_rent`, `florida_60_day_holdover_2_months_double_rent`, and `florida_365_day_extreme_holdover_double_rent` (13 months Г— 2Г—).

**California В§ 1945 rebuttable presumption.** Rent acceptance creates a rebuttable presumption of month-to-month renewal on the same terms вҖ” landlord must then serve fresh 30/60/90-day notice under Cal. Civ. Code В§ 1946 / В§ 1946.1 to terminate. Attempting to maintain eviction on the original notice after accepting rent is a violation; the acceptance waives the original demand. Pinned by `california_rent_accepted_creates_month_to_month`, `california_no_rent_accepted_no_month_to_month`, and `california_rent_accepted_plus_eviction_attempt_violation`.

**New York В§ 232-c mandatory conversion.** Statute states: "If the landlord shall accept rent for any period subsequent to the expiration of such term, then ... the tenancy created by the acceptance of such rent shall be a tenancy from month to month commencing on the first day after the expiration of such term." Stricter than CA вҖ” conversion is mandatory, not rebuttable. Pinned by `new_york_rent_accepted_creates_month_to_month`, `new_york_no_rent_accepted_no_month_to_month`, and `new_york_rent_accepted_plus_holdover_proceeding_violation`.

**Default common-law landlord election.** Restatement (Second) of Property В§ 14.5 вҖ” landlord may (a) treat the holdover as a new tenancy at the same rent OR (b) sue the tenant for use-and-occupancy / trespass damages equal to the actual rental value. No statutory multiplier and no automatic conversion regardless of rent acceptance. Pinned by `default_regime_single_rent_recovery` and `default_regime_no_month_to_month_auto_creation`.

**Multi-regime invariants** вҖ” `florida_is_the_only_2x_multiplier_regime_invariant` confirms FL is the unique double-rent state (CA/NY/Default all 1Г—); `rent_acceptance_creates_month_to_month_only_for_ca_and_ny_invariant` confirms automatic conversion engages only in CA + NY (4-regime sweep, 2 hits); `same_input_florida_double_vs_others_single_damages_invariant` pins FL at exactly 2Г— the other three for identical input; `zero_days_zero_damages_all_regimes_invariant` confirms no recovery without holdover across all 4 regimes; `single_state_uniqueness_invariant` produces a 4-cell truth table of `(multiplier, mtm_created)` showing each regime is structurally distinct.

**Sibling distinction note** вҖ” every result references the structural split between **statutory-double-rent** (FL) and **rent-acceptance** (CA + NY) regimes, with **default common-law election** (Restatement В§ 14.5) as the residual. Pinned by `sibling_distinction_note_present` (UX-text regression).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/holdover-tenant-damages`. Twenty-five tests pin: **Florida 30-day holdover full month double rent**; **Florida 31-day rounds up to 2 months double rent**; **Florida 1-day partial month full charge** ("any part thereof"); **Florida 60-day 2 months double rent**; **Florida zero days zero damages**; **Florida 365-day extreme holdover** (13 months Г— 2Г—); **Florida rent acceptance does not create month-to-month**; **California rent accepted creates month-to-month**; **California no rent accepted no month-to-month**; **California rent accepted + eviction attempt violation**; **New York rent accepted creates month-to-month**; **New York no rent accepted no month-to-month**; **New York rent accepted + holdover proceeding violation**; **Default regime single rent recovery**; **Default regime no month-to-month auto-creation**; **Florida is the only 2Г— multiplier regime invariant** (4-regime sweep); **rent acceptance creates month-to-month only for CA and NY invariant**; **same input Florida double vs others single damages invariant** (4-regime comparison); **zero days zero damages all regimes invariant**; **Florida 29 days one month boundary**; **citation pins all regime authorities** (В§ 83.58 + В§ 1945 + В§ 1946 + В§ 232-c + RPAPL В§ 711(1) + В§ 1161 + Restatement В§ 14.5); **sibling distinction note present** (UX-text regression for statutory-double-rent vs rent-acceptance vs common-law-election); **defensive negative days clamped to zero**; **defensive negative rent clamped to zero**; **single state uniqueness invariant** (4-cell truth table).

`traderview-expense::lease_assignment_consent` is the **lease assignment consent module** вҖ” landlord consent rules for tenant assignment of a residential lease. Direct sibling to `sublet_consent` but structurally distinct: ASSIGNMENT is a complete transfer of the leasehold interest (tenant discharged); SUBLEASING leaves the tenant on the hook with a subtenant occupying under the tenant's lease. Only New York draws a sharp structural distinction between the two in statute (В§ 226-b); other regimes treat them as variants of the same consent question.

**Four regimes**:

| Regime | Authority | Standard | Distinctive feature |
|--------|-----------|----------|---------------------|
| New York | N.Y. Real Prop. Law В§ 226-b | Sublease: reasonable; Assignment: unconditional | **30-day release valve** вҖ” landlord must release tenant on 30 days notice for unreasonable assignment refusal |
| California | Cal. Civ. Code В§ 1995.260 + В§ 1995.270 | Reasonable if silent on standard | NOT retroactive вҖ” pre-September 23, 1983 silent-standard leases keep unconditional rule |
| Restatement | Restatement (2d) of Property В§ 15.2 | Free assignability default | No clause вҶ’ tenant may freely assign; clause exists вҶ’ reasonableness implied (strict construction) |
| LeaseControls | Modern majority | Clause as written | Texas + Illinois + Massachusetts commercial follow this rule |

**New York В§ 226-b вҖ” the only sharp structural distinction.** Sublease branch (4+ unit buildings): tenant has STATUTORY right with landlord's reasonable consent; landlord's 30-day silent treatment = DEEMED CONSENT under В§ 226-b(2)(b); unreasonable refusal allows tenant to proceed AND recover costs + attorney fees on bad-faith showing. Assignment branch: landlord may UNCONDITIONALLY withhold consent BUT if the refusal is unreasonable, landlord MUST RELEASE THE TENANT from the lease on 30 days written notice вҖ” the **strongest tenant-side exit valve in U.S. landlord-tenant law**. Pinned by `ny_sublease_4plus_units_unreasonable_refusal_tenant_may_proceed`, `ny_sublease_30_day_failure_to_respond_deemed_consent`, `ny_sublease_29_days_pending_no_deemed_consent`, `ny_sublease_less_than_4_units_no_statutory_right` (statute only applies to 4+ unit buildings), `ny_assignment_unreasonable_refusal_triggers_30_day_release`, `ny_assignment_reasonable_refusal_no_release`, `only_ny_distinguishes_sublease_from_assignment_structurally`, `deemed_consent_only_applies_to_ny_sublease` (8-cell sweep over 4 regimes Г— 2 transfer types вҖ” only NY-sublease branch engages), and `ny_release_only_for_unreasonable_assignment_refusal` (4-cell truth table).

**California В§ 1995.260 вҖ” silent-standard implied reasonableness.** Codifies Kendall v. Ernest Pestana, Inc., 40 Cal. 3d 488 (1985) вҖ” if the lease has a consent clause but specifies no standard, an implied reasonableness standard applies. Tenant has burden of proof; satisfied by showing landlord failed to state a written reasonable objection within reasonable time of the tenant's written request. NOT retroactive вҖ” leases executed before September 23, 1983 with silent-standard clauses remain governed by pre-statute common-law rule allowing unreasonable refusal. Express-standard leases (whether reasonable or unconditional) governed by В§ 1995.270. Pinned by `ca_silent_standard_post_1983_implies_reasonableness`, `ca_silent_standard_pre_1983_no_implied_reasonableness` (grandfather clause), `ca_express_standard_governs`, `ca_reasonable_refusal_under_implied_standard_compliant`, and `ca_pre_1983_grandfather_only_when_silent_standard` (express-standard pre-1983 still respects express).

**Restatement В§ 15.2 вҖ” default free assignability.** Common-law default: tenant may freely assign the leasehold absent a lease restriction. When a restriction exists, it is strictly construed against the landlord; many Restatement-following states imply reasonableness on the restriction. Pinned by `restatement_no_consent_clause_free_assignment` and `restatement_with_consent_clause_strict_construction` and `restatement_free_assignability_only_when_no_clause` (free assignability engages ONLY when there is no clause).

**LeaseControls modern majority.** Lease enforced as written. No clause вҶ’ tenant may freely transfer (no restriction to enforce). Clause with no specified standard вҶ’ landlord may withhold for any reason absent state statute imposing reasonableness (Texas, Illinois, Massachusetts commercial context, and others follow this rule). Pinned by `lease_controls_no_clause_free_transfer`, `lease_controls_clause_no_standard_unconditional`, and `lease_controls_clause_with_reasonable_standard`.

**Sibling-distinction note** вҖ” every result references the structural ASSIGNMENT vs. SUBLEASING distinction and points to the sibling `sublet_consent` module. Only New York В§ 226-b draws a sharp structural difference between the two in statute. Pinned by `sibling_distinction_note_present` (UX-text regression).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/lease-assignment-consent`. Twenty-six tests pin: **NY sublease 4+ units unreasonable refusal вҶ’ tenant may proceed**; **NY sublease 4+ units reasonable refusal вҶ’ may not proceed**; **NY sublease 30-day failure to respond вҶ’ deemed consent**; **NY sublease 29 days pending вҶ’ no deemed consent**; **NY sublease less than 4 units вҶ’ no statutory right**; **NY assignment unreasonable refusal вҶ’ 30-day release**; **NY assignment reasonable refusal вҶ’ no release**; **CA silent standard post-1983 вҶ’ implied reasonableness**; **CA silent standard pre-1983 вҶ’ no implied reasonableness** (grandfather); **CA express standard governs**; **CA reasonable refusal under implied standard вҶ’ compliant**; **Restatement no consent clause вҶ’ free assignment**; **Restatement with consent clause вҶ’ strict construction**; **LeaseControls no clause вҶ’ free transfer**; **LeaseControls clause no standard вҶ’ unconditional**; **LeaseControls clause with reasonable standard**; **only NY distinguishes sublease from assignment structurally invariant**; **CA pre-1983 grandfather only when silent standard invariant**; **deemed consent only applies to NY sublease invariant** (8-cell sweep вҖ” 4 regimes Г— 2 transfer types); **NY release only for unreasonable assignment refusal invariant** (4-cell truth table); **citation pins all regime authorities** (В§ 226-b + В§ 1995.260 + В§ 1995.270 + Restatement В§ 15.2 + Kendall + September 23, 1983 effective date); **sibling distinction note present** (UX-text regression for ASSIGNMENT vs. SUBLEASING + В§ 226-b); **defensive negative pending days no deemed consent**; **Restatement free assignability only when no clause**; **NY 30-day response window constant invariant** (both 30-day windows); **single regime uniqueness invariant** (8 combinations).

`traderview-expense::lease_cure_period` is the **tenant cure period for non-rent lease breach module** вҖ” the statutory window during which a tenant may correct a non-rent lease violation (unauthorized pets, parking, occupancy limits, cleanliness, lease-clause violations) and avoid forfeiture. Distinct from rent-payment cure (covered by `eviction_notices`) and grace periods before late fees (covered by `late_payment_grace_period`). Four regimes with sharply different cure-period lengths and weekend-exclusion rules.

**Four regimes** (cure period strictly ordered CA < FL < NY < Default):

| Regime | Authority | Cure period | Weekends excluded | Recurrence bypass |
|--------|-----------|-------------|-------------------|-------------------|
| California | Cal. Code Civ. Proc. В§ 1161(3) | 3 days | **YES вҖ” excludes Saturdays, Sundays, judicial holidays** | No |
| Florida | Fla. Stat. В§ 83.56(2)(b) | 7 days curable; 7-day vacate non-curable | No | **YES вҖ” 12-month recurrence rule** |
| New York | N.Y. RPAPL В§ 753(4) | 10 days | No | No |
| Default | Restatement (2d) of Property В§ 13.1 + В§ 16.1 | 14 days (safe-harbor baseline) | No | No |

**California В§ 1161(3) вҖ” only regime excluding weekends + holidays.** 3-day cure period explicitly excludes Saturdays, Sundays, and judicial holidays вҖ” effectively 3 business days. NOT applicable to non-payment-of-rent cases (use В§ 1161(2) 3-day pay-or-quit) or nuisance (use В§ 1161(4) unlawful detainer for waste). Notice must specifically identify the violation, what must be corrected, and by when. Pinned by `california_3_business_days_cure_curable`, `california_2_business_days_still_within_window`, `california_uses_business_days_not_calendar` (5 calendar days but only 2 business days вҶ’ not expired вҖ” confirms regime uses business-day count), and `california_non_curable_no_cure_right`.

**Florida В§ 83.56(2) вҖ” only regime with 12-month recurrence bypass.** Two statutory tracks: В§ 83.56(2)(b) CURABLE noncompliance gets 7-day cure period (unauthorized pets, guests, vehicles; parking; cleanliness; sanitation); В§ 83.56(2)(a) NON-CURABLE noncompliance (intentional destruction, damage, or continued unreasonable disturbance) gets 7-day notice to VACATE with NO cure right. 12-MONTH RECURRENCE RULE: if the same curable noncompliance recurs within 12 months of initial notice, landlord may commence eviction WITHOUT delivering a subsequent notice (tenant's cure right does not reset on recurrence). Pinned by `florida_7_day_cure_curable`, `florida_at_7_day_boundary_expired`, `florida_non_curable_no_cure_right_7_day_vacate`, `florida_recurrence_within_12_months_no_second_notice`, and `florida_no_recurrence_requires_second_notice`.

**New York RPAPL В§ 753(4) вҖ” longest baseline cure.** 10-day cure period for breach of lease covenant in residential leases. Distinct from HSTPA 2019 30-day cure for chronic late-rent / nuisance defenses (those are evidentiary defenses in housing court, not В§ 753(4) lease-covenant breaches). Pinned by `new_york_10_day_cure_curable`, `new_york_at_10_day_boundary_expired`, and `new_york_9_days_within_window`.

**Default common-law reasonable cure.** Restatement (Second) of Property В§ 13.1 + В§ 16.1 вҖ” reasonable cure period determined case-by-case based on nature of breach + good-faith effort to remedy. Safe-harbor baseline: 14 days. Pinned by `default_14_day_safe_harbor` and `default_at_14_day_boundary_expired`.

**Multi-regime invariants** вҖ” `cure_period_strict_ordering_ca_lt_fl_lt_ny_lt_default_invariant` (3 < 7 < 10 < 14 day ordering); `only_california_excludes_weekends_invariant` (4-regime sweep confirms unique CA weekend-exclusion); `only_florida_has_recurrence_bypass_invariant` (4-regime sweep confirms unique FL recurrence bypass); `non_curable_no_cure_right_all_regimes_invariant` (non-curable violations forfeit cure right uniformly); `cure_window_expired_truth_table_curable` (8-cell sweep вҖ” 4 regimes Г— 2 day-counts each at boundary and one-day-before).

**Sibling distinction note** вҖ” every result references the rent-payment-cure regime distinction (`eviction_notices` covers CA В§ 1161(2) 3-day pay-or-quit, FL В§ 83.56(3) 3-day, NY HSTPA 14-day rent demand) and the late-fee grace-period regime distinction (`late_payment_grace_period`). Pinned by `sibling_distinction_note_present` (UX-text regression).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/lease-cure-period`. Twenty-two tests pin: **California 3 business days cure curable**; **California 2 business days still within window**; **California uses business days not calendar** (5 calendar / 2 business вҶ’ not expired); **California non-curable no cure right**; **Florida 7-day cure curable**; **Florida at 7-day boundary expired**; **Florida non-curable no cure right 7-day vacate**; **Florida recurrence within 12 months no second notice**; **Florida no recurrence requires second notice**; **New York 10-day cure curable**; **New York at 10-day boundary expired**; **New York 9 days within window**; **Default 14-day safe harbor**; **Default at 14-day boundary expired**; **cure period strict ordering CA < FL < NY < Default invariant** (3 < 7 < 10 < 14); **only California excludes weekends invariant** (4-regime sweep); **only Florida has recurrence bypass invariant** (4-regime sweep); **non-curable no cure right all regimes invariant**; **citation pins all authorities** (В§ 1161(2) + В§ 1161(3) + В§ 1161(4) + В§ 83.56(2)(a) + В§ 83.56(2)(b) + В§ 83.56(3) + RPAPL В§ 753(4) + HSTPA + Restatement В§ 13.1 + В§ 16.1); **sibling distinction note present** (UX-text regression for non-rent vs rent-payment cure + late-fee grace period); **defensive negative days no premature expiration**; **cure window expired truth table curable** (8-cell sweep вҖ” 4 regimes Г— 2 day-counts each at boundary and one-day-before).

`traderview-expense::portable_tenant_screening_report` is the **portable / reusable tenant screening report regulation module** вҖ” when must a landlord accept a tenant-prepared pre-pulled screening report in lieu of conducting and charging for their own? Distinct from sibling `application_fees` (general caps on screening fees) and `adverse_action_notice` (FCRA disclosure when application denied based on report). Both Colorado and Washington regimes build on the federal Fair Credit Reporting Act (15 U.S.C. В§ 1681 et seq.) consumer reporting agency framework.

**Three regimes** with sharply different acceptance + fee rules:

| Regime | Authority | Acceptance rule | Penalty |
|--------|-----------|-----------------|---------|
| Colorado | Colo. Rev. Stat. В§ 38-12-902 / В§ 38-12-904 (HB23-1099, 2023) | **MANDATORY** acceptance of compliant report | **$2,500** ($50 if cured within 7 days) |
| Washington | RCW 59.18.257 | **OPT-IN** disclosure вҖ” landlord publishes acceptance status on property website | **$100** max + attorney fees |
| Default | None | No portability requirement; landlord may demand fresh fee-based screening | n/a |

**Colorado В§ 38-12-902 / В§ 38-12-904 вҖ” mandatory acceptance.** Four conditions for a compliant portable report: (1) вүӨ30 days old at submission; (2) prepared directly by consumer reporting agency or third-party website complying with state and federal laws; (3) made available to landlord at NO cost to access/use; (4) includes tenant's STATEMENT OF NO MATERIAL CHANGE in the report information. If conditions met, landlord MUST accept AND MAY NOT charge any rental-application or report-access fee. В§ 38-12-904(4) violation: $2,500 plus court costs + attorney fees, REDUCED to $50 if landlord cures within 7 calendar days of notice. В§ 38-12-904 single-application-at-a-time exception: landlord accepting only one application at a time + refunding fee within 20 days of denial is exempt from mandatory acceptance. Pinned by `colorado_compliant_report_accepted_compliant`, `colorado_compliant_report_refused_2500_penalty`, `colorado_refused_cured_within_7_days_50_penalty`, `colorado_accepted_but_fee_charged_violation`, `colorado_single_application_exception_no_portability_required`, `colorado_report_31_days_old_outside_validity`, and `colorado_missing_material_change_statement_not_compliant_report`.

**Washington RCW 59.18.257 вҖ” opt-in disclosure.** Landlord must publish on the property's online listing AND in pre-screening writing to the prospective tenant whether the landlord will accept a Comprehensive Reusable Tenant Screening Report (CRTSR). CRTSR statutory components: consumer credit report + criminal history + eviction history + employment verification + address and rental history; вүӨ30 days old. If landlord OPTS IN (declares acceptance), MUST accept compliant CRTSR and may NOT charge a screening fee. If landlord OPTS OUT (declares non-acceptance), may conduct own screening at actual cost capped at customary local screening-service rate. В§ 59.18.257(2) violation: up to $100 + court costs + reasonable attorney fees. Pinned by `washington_landlord_accepts_compliant_report_compliant`, `washington_landlord_declared_accepts_then_refused_100_penalty`, `washington_landlord_opt_out_no_portability_required`, `washington_accepts_then_charges_fee_violation`, and `washington_missing_crtsr_component_landlord_may_reject`.

**Default вҖ” no portability requirement.** Landlord may demand a fresh fee-based screening under the general `application_fees` regime and FCRA disclosure obligations. The portable-report mandate originated in Colorado (HB23-1099, 2023) and the opt-in disclosure model in Washington; most other states retain default landlord discretion.

**Multi-regime invariants** вҖ” `colorado_penalty_higher_than_washington_invariant` (CO $2,500 = 25Г— WA $100); `colorado_cure_reduces_penalty_50x_invariant` ($50 cured = 1/50 of $2,500 full); `validity_window_30_days_constant_invariant` (both regimes use 30-day report validity; CO 7-day cure window); `only_colorado_has_mandatory_acceptance_invariant` (CO mandatory default; WA depends on disclosure; Default false); `missing_crtsr_components_truth_table` (5-cell sweep вҖ” any missing component fails completeness in WA).

**Sibling distinction note** вҖ” every result references `application_fees` (general screening fee caps) and `adverse_action_notice` (FCRA disclosure on denial) and the federal FCRA framework underlying both state regimes. Pinned by `sibling_distinction_note_present` (UX-text regression).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/portable-tenant-screening-report`. Twenty-two tests pin: **Colorado compliant report accepted compliant**; **Colorado compliant report refused $2,500 penalty**; **Colorado refused cured within 7 days $50 penalty**; **Colorado accepted but fee charged violation**; **Colorado single-application exception no portability required**; **Colorado report 31 days old outside validity**; **Colorado missing material change statement not compliant**; **Washington landlord accepts compliant report compliant**; **Washington landlord declared accepts then refused $100 penalty**; **Washington landlord opt-out no portability required**; **Washington accepts then charges fee violation**; **Washington missing CRTSR component landlord may reject**; **Default no portability no violation**; **Colorado penalty higher than Washington invariant** (25Г— ratio); **Colorado cure reduces penalty 50Г— invariant** ($2,500 вҶ’ $50); **validity window 30 days constant invariant** (both regimes + Colorado 7-day cure window); **only Colorado has mandatory acceptance invariant** (3-regime sweep with WA opt-in/opt-out branch); **citation pins all regime authorities** (В§ 38-12-902 + В§ 38-12-904 + В§ 38-12-904(4) + RCW 59.18.257 + В§ 59.18.257(1) + В§ 59.18.257(2) + 15 U.S.C. В§ 1681 + HB23-1099); **sibling distinction note present** (UX-text regression for application_fees + adverse_action_notice + FCRA); **defensive negative report age no validity**; **validity boundary 30 days exact within window** (вүӨ comparison); **missing CRTSR components truth table** (5-cell sweep вҖ” each missing component fails).

`traderview-expense::hoa_rental_restriction` is the **HOA rental restriction enforceability module** вҖ” when may a homeowners association enforce restrictions on a unit owner's right to rent their property? Trader-critical for landlord-owners holding property inside common-interest communities, where a single HOA amendment can destroy a building's rental cash flow. Distinct from sibling `str_regulation` (municipal short-term-rental laws вҖ” public regulation enforced by local government, vs. HOA private covenants), `tenant_topa` (tenant opportunity-to-purchase rights), and `condominium_conversion_protection` (tenant-side protection during condo conversions).

**Three regimes**:

| Regime | Authority | Rule |
|--------|-----------|------|
| Florida | Fla. Stat. В§ 720.306(1)(h) (eff. July 1, 2021) | **GRANDFATHER RULE** вҖ” post-July-2021 amendments bind only owners acquiring after amendment OR consenting; two narrow exceptions bind all |
| Arizona | A.R.S. В§ 33-1806.01 | **DECLARATION CONTROLS** + statutory third-party agent designation right under В§ 33-1806.01(B) |
| Default | None | Declaration controls; no statutory grandfather |

**Florida В§ 720.306(1)(h) вҖ” grandfather rule.** Any HOA amendment adopted after July 1, 2021 prohibiting or regulating rentals applies ONLY to (a) owners who acquired title AFTER the amendment took effect; OR (b) owners who AFFIRMATIVELY consented to the amendment. **Silence does NOT count as consent.** TWO NARROW EXCEPTIONS bind ALL owners regardless of grandfather status:

| Exception | Statute | Scope |
|-----------|---------|-------|
| Short-term rental | В§ 720.306(1)(h)1 | Amendments restricting leases вүӨ 6 months |
| Frequency | В§ 720.306(1)(h)2 | Amendments limiting rentals to вүӨ 3 per calendar year |

Grandfather protection SURVIVES transfer to (a) heir acquiring on prior owner's death, or (b) entity affiliated with prior owner. **LOST upon transfer to unrelated third party** under В§ 720.306(1)(h)3. Pinned by `florida_pre_amendment_owner_no_consent_grandfather_engaged`, `florida_pre_amendment_owner_consented_no_grandfather`, `florida_post_amendment_owner_no_grandfather`, `florida_heir_acquires_grandfather_preserved`, `florida_transfer_to_unrelated_party_erodes_grandfather`, `florida_pre_july_2021_amendment_not_subject_to_grandfather` (statute applies only to post-July-2021 amendments), `florida_grandfather_truth_table` (4-cell pre/post Г— consent sweep), `florida_grandfather_eroded_by_transfer_truth_table` (4-cell heir Г— transferred sweep), and `florida_exception_overrides_grandfather_invariant` (both exceptions bind all owners regardless of grandfather-favoring facts).

**Florida short-term + frequency exceptions bind everyone.** Even owners with otherwise-perfect grandfather facts (acquired before amendment, did not consent) are bound by these two narrow exception categories. Pinned by `florida_short_term_rental_restriction_binds_all_owners` and `florida_three_or_fewer_rentals_restriction_binds_all_owners`.

**Arizona В§ 33-1806.01 вҖ” declaration controls + agent designation.** Declaration controls rental restrictions in planned communities; HOA may prohibit rentals or impose time-period restrictions if such restrictions appear in the declaration. Statute does NOT set a percentage cap on rentals. Owner has statutory right under В§ 33-1806.01(B) to designate a third-party agent for HOA matters (except voting in elections and serving on the board) by SIGNED WRITTEN DESIGNATION delivered to the HOA. Pinned by `arizona_declaration_permits_no_enforceable_restriction`, `arizona_declaration_restricts_enforceable`, `arizona_agent_designation_validly_invoked`, and `arizona_no_agent_designation_no_engagement`.

**Default вҖ” declaration controls.** No statutory grandfather protection. HOA amendments bind existing owners under general covenants law subject to declaration's amendment procedure. Most states follow this default absent specific legislation. Pinned by `default_declaration_permits_no_enforceable_restriction` and `default_declaration_restricts_enforceable` (confirms no grandfather engaged in Default regime).

**Multi-regime invariants** вҖ” `only_florida_offers_grandfather_protection_invariant` (3-regime sweep with restriction in declaration; grandfather engages only in FL); `only_arizona_has_statutory_agent_designation_invariant` (3-regime sweep with agent designation signed; engages only in AZ).

**Sibling distinction note** вҖ” every result distinguishes HOA private covenants (enforced by association) from municipal STR public regulation (enforced by local government); references `tenant_topa`, `str_regulation`, and `condominium_conversion_protection`. Pinned by `sibling_distinction_note_present` (UX-text regression).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/hoa-rental-restriction`.
