Trader-landlord critical because В§ 235-e affirmative defense is one of the most-litigated landlord-tenant defenses in NY post-HSTPA вҖ” many nonpayment proceedings have been dismissed when landlords failed to send the certified-mail late notice and tenants raised the affirmative defense at the trial. The HSTPA 2019 certified-mail requirement specifically rejected email and text notice (even with acknowledgment receipts) вҖ” a strict procedural requirement that catches many landlords who rely on contemporary communication methods. Smaller landlords who hand-deliver rent receipts often miss the "immediately" requirement for personal payments, creating cheap В§ 235-e(a) violations. The 3-year recordkeeping requirement under В§ 235-e(b) is the most-overlooked obligation вҖ” many landlords don't maintain organized cash payment records and face В§ 235-e(b) violations during audit or dispute. Sibling cluster: `rental_late_fee_cap` (separate cap on late fees imposed by landlord), `rental_just_cause_eviction` (iter 573 вҖ” NY Good Cause Eviction adds additional eviction restrictions stacked on top of В§ 235-e procedural requirements), `rental_security_deposit_interest` (NY RPL В§ 7-103 вҖ” 1.5% interest on security deposits, distinct landlord financial obligation), `rental_landlord_notice_to_enter` (HSTPA-related procedural notice rule), `rental_eviction_record_sealing_screening` (iter 579 вҖ” NY RPAPL В§ 745(2)(c)(iv) 5-year housing-court records lookback, affects tenant screening downstream of В§ 235-e violations), `rental_application_denial_disclosure` (NY-specific disclosure regime), `rental_just_cause_eviction` (iter 573 вҖ” NY Good Cause Eviction effective April 2024 stacks atop В§ 235-e).

`traderview-expense::rental_local_law_88_lighting_upgrades_sub_metering` is the **NYC Local Law 88 of 2009 Lighting Upgrades & Sub-Metering compliance module** (iter 601) вҖ” NYC Greener, Greater Buildings Plan (GGBP) companion to `rental_local_law_87_energy_audit_retro_commissioning` (iter 599 NYC LL 87 energy audits), `rental_energy_benchmarking` (NYC LL 84 annual benchmarking), `rental_climate_mobilization_act_ll97_emissions` (iter 587 NYC LL 97 emissions cap). Local Law 88 of 2009 is part of the Greener, Greater Buildings Plan (GGBP) along with Local Law 84 (annual benchmarking), Local Law 87 (energy audits), and Local Law 97 (emissions cap). Enacted **December 28, 2009**. Codified at **NYC Admin Code В§ 28-310** (lighting upgrades) and **В§ 28-311** (sub-metering). **Two distinct compliance vectors**: (1) **В§ 28-310 Lighting Upgrades** вҖ” lighting systems in covered buildings must comply with current **NYC Energy Conservation Code (NYCECC)** standards for new systems by **January 1, 2025**; (2) **В§ 28-311 Electrical Sub-Metering** вҖ” electrical sub-meters must be installed in each covered tenant space by January 1, 2025; building owners must provide monthly billing statements to tenants showing electrical consumption and charges. **Coverage**: originally buildings > 50,000 sqft; subsequently amended to include buildings **25,000+ square feet**. Sub-metering specifically applies to non-residential tenant spaces **greater than 5,000 gross square feet**, OR floors > 5,000 sqft with two or more tenants. **Exemptions**: occupancy group R-2 (multifamily residential) and R-3 (one- and two-family) dwelling units; tenant spaces where electrical consumption is already measured by a dedicated utility meter. **Monthly tenant billing statements** under В§ 28-311.4: building owners must provide tenants with monthly statements showing electrical consumption measured by the submeter and the electrical charges billed; for shared submeters covering multiple tenants, statements must include total consumption for the metered area and each tenant's percentage of that area. **Filing requirements** under В§ 28-311.4: owners must file lighting upgrade and submeter installation compliance reports with NYC DOB by **May 1, 2025** along with a **$115 filing fee** (covers both lighting and submetering reports); reports uploaded via BEAM (Building Energy Audit Management) system. **Penalty schedule** under NYC Admin Code В§ 28-311.5: **$500** per covered tenant space without a sub-meter, assessed annually until all required submeters are installed; **$1,500** for failure to file lighting upgrade report, assessed annually until compliance; **$1,500** for failure to file submeter installation report, assessed annually until compliance. **Ten-mode severity ladder**: NotApplicable, ExemptBuildingUnder25000Sqft, ExemptR2OrR3OccupancyOnly, CompliantLightingAndSubMeteringComplete, ViolationLightingNotUpgradedByJan1_2025, ViolationSubMeterNotInstalledByJan1_2025, ViolationLightingReportNotFiledByMay1_2025, ViolationSubMeteringReportNotFiledByMay1_2025, ViolationMonthlyTenantBillingNotProvided, AggravatedViolationMultipleCompoundingFailures. **Five occupancy groups**: R2MultifamilyResidential, R3OneOrTwoFamily, Commercial, MixedUse, Other. Mounted at `POST /api/rental/rental-local-law-88-lighting-upgrades-sub-metering`. **Twenty-three tests pin**: **fully compliant baseline** (100K sqft + 0 violations + $115 filing fee), **building under 25,000 sqft exempt**, **building at exactly 25,000 sqft covered** (statutory вүҘ reading), **R-2 multifamily without commercial tenants exempt**, **R-3 1-2 family exempt**, **mixed-use R-2 with commercial tenants covered**, **lighting not upgraded violation**, **sub-meter not installed 3 spaces $1,500 penalty** (3 Г— $500), **sub-meter not installed 10 spaces $5,000 penalty** (10 Г— $500), **lighting report not filed $1,500 penalty**, **sub-metering report not filed $1,500 penalty**, **monthly tenant billing not provided violation**, **aggravated multiple compounding failures** (lighting + submeter + report all missing = $5,500 aggregate for 5 spaces + 2 reports), **pre-2025 compliance year no penalty assessed** (2024 current year), **citations pin LL 88 + NYC Admin Code В§ 28-310/.311/.311.4/.311.5**, **citations pin NYCECC + GGBP + BEAM**, **constant pins 25,000 sqft building threshold**, **constant pins 5,000 sqft tenant space threshold**, **constant pins $115 filing fee**, **constant pins $500 per tenant space penalty**, **constant pins $1,500 per report penalty**, **constant pins January 1, 2025 deadline and May 1 filing**, **very large tenant space count no overflow** (u32::MAX defense). Trader-landlord critical because NYC LL 88 affects every commercial and mixed-use building > 25,000 sqft and creates compounding annual penalties that can exceed $5,000-$50,000/year for buildings with multiple unsubmetered tenant spaces. The penalty structure is designed for cumulative escalation вҖ” a 50,000 sqft commercial building with 10 unsubmetered tenant spaces and unfiled reports faces $5,000 + $1,500 + $1,500 = $8,000 annually, payable until compliance is achieved. Trader-landlords acquiring NYC commercial inventory inherit any back-penalties; due diligence must include LL 88 compliance review. The submeter installation costs $500-$2,500 per space, but lighting upgrades to NYCECC standards can run $50,000-$500,000+ per building depending on LED retrofit scope. Sibling cluster: `rental_local_law_87_energy_audit_retro_commissioning` (iter 599 вҖ” NYC LL 87 every-10-year audit cycle that identifies retrofits including LL 88 lighting), `rental_energy_benchmarking` (NYC LL 84 вҖ” annual building energy use benchmarking that feeds LL 88 baseline), `rental_climate_mobilization_act_ll97_emissions` (iter 587 вҖ” NYC LL 97 emissions cap whose retrofits often include LL 88 lighting + submetering), `rental_facade_inspection_fisp_local_law_11` (iter 583 вҖ” NYC LL 11 facade, structurally parallel periodic compliance regime), `rental_gas_piping_inspection_local_law_152` (iter 585 вҖ” NYC LL 152 gas), `rental_cooling_tower_inspection_local_law_77` (iter 589 вҖ” NYC LL 77 cooling tower).

`traderview-expense::rental_local_law_87_energy_audit_retro_commissioning` is the **NYC Local Law 87 of 2009 Energy Audits & Retro-Commissioning compliance module** (iter 599) вҖ” NYC Greener, Greater Buildings Plan (GGBP) companion to `rental_energy_benchmarking` (NYC LL 84 annual benchmarking вҖ” the predicate data foundation), `rental_climate_mobilization_act_ll97_emissions` (iter 587 NYC LL 97 emissions cap that builds on LL 87 audit data), `rental_facade_inspection_fisp_local_law_11` (iter 583 NYC LL 11), `rental_gas_piping_inspection_local_law_152` (iter 585 NYC LL 152), `rental_cooling_tower_inspection_local_law_77` (iter 589 NYC LL 77). Local Law 87 of 2009 is part of the Greener, Greater Buildings Plan (GGBP) along with Local Law 84 (annual benchmarking) and Local Law 97 (emissions cap). Codified at **NYC Admin Code В§ 28-308**. Requires owners of covered buildings to undergo an **ASHRAE Level II energy audit** and **retro-commissioning** (RCx) study of base building systems every **10 years** and submit the results in an **Energy Efficiency Report (EER)** to NYC DOB. **Coverage**: buildings with gross floor area **greater than 50,000 square feet** ("covered buildings"). Excludes one- and two-family residential (R-3 occupancy). **Filing schedule based on tax block last digit**: owner must submit an EER in the calendar year in which the **last digit of the year coincides with the last digit of the building's tax block number**. E.g., tax block ending in "5" вҶ’ file in 2015, 2025, 2035, etc. This tax-block-distributed filing schedule effectively staggers the entire NYC commercial building inventory across the 10-year cycle, with roughly 10% of covered buildings filing in any given calendar year. **2025 filing extension**: NYC DOB extended the 2025 EER filing deadline to **March 31, 2026** (one-time extension for 2025 filing year only). Subsequent filing years revert to standard calendar-year deadline. **Qualified Professional requirement**: all individuals performing or supervising Energy Audit and Retro-commissioning studies for inclusion in an EER must be a **registered design professional** (NY-licensed Professional Engineer or Registered Architect) with appropriate qualifications, AND cannot be on the staff of the building being audited or retro-commissioned. The staff-independence rule is the most-litigated procedural requirement вҖ” building owners commonly attempt to use in-house facilities engineers, which fails LL 87's independence test. **ASHRAE Level II Energy Audit**: more detailed than Level I вҖ” includes savings calculations, payback period analysis, and identifies retrofit opportunities at the system/component level. **Retro-Commissioning (RCx)**: process to ensure existing energy systems are installed as per design intentions, functionally tested, and capable of being operated and maintained according to owner's operational needs. **Civil penalties** under NYC Admin Code В§ 28-308.7: **$3,000** for the first year of non-submittal; **$5,000** for each additional year. NYC DOB will NOT accept any outstanding EER submission if outstanding penalties are not paid in full вҖ” creating a compounding compliance trap where a delinquent owner cannot file the current year's EER without first paying back-penalties on prior delinquencies. **Nine-mode severity ladder**: NotApplicable, ExemptBuildingUnder50000Sqft, CompliantEerFiledOnTimeForAssignedYear, CompliantExtensionGrantedAndFiledByMarch31_2026, ViolationEerNotFiledByAssignedDeadline, ViolationQualifiedProfessionalNotLicensedPeOrRa, ViolationQualifiedProfessionalIsBuildingStaffMember, ViolationAuditNotAshraeLevelII, AggravatedViolationCivilPenaltyAccumulatingMultipleYears. Mounted at `POST /api/rental/rental-local-law-87-energy-audit-retro-commissioning`. **Twenty-three tests pin**: **fully compliant baseline** (100K sqft + tax block last digit 5 + 2025 EER filed), **building under 50K sqft exempt**, **building at exactly 50K sqft exempt** (statutory > 50K reading), **building at 50,001 sqft covered**, **audit below ASHRAE Level II violation**, **qualified professional not PE/RA violation**, **qualified professional is building staff violation**, **EER not filed by assigned deadline $3,000 penalty**, **EER 2 years late penalty $8,000** ($3K + $5K), **EER 3 years late penalty $13,000** ($3K + 2 Г— $5K), **extension granted filed by March 31, 2026 compliant**, **tax block 5 assigned 2025 in year 2025**, **tax block 3 assigned 2023 or 2033** (decade-base math), **tax block 5 next filing year 2035**, **citations pin LL 87 + NYC Admin Code В§ 28-308 + В§ 28-308.7**, **citations pin ASHRAE Level II + Greener Greater Buildings Plan + EER2**, **citations pin 2025 Extension Service Notice + March 31, 2026 deadline**, **constant pins 50K sqft threshold**, **constant pins 10-year reporting cycle**, **constant pins $3K first-year penalty**, **constant pins $5K subsequent-year penalty**, **constant pins 2025 extension date** (year/month/day), **very large continuing non-submittal no overflow** (u32::MAX defense). Trader-landlord critical because NYC LL 87 is the single most-comprehensive building-systems audit requirement in NYC and the gateway compliance regime for the GGBP вҖ” LL 87 EERs feed into LL 97 emissions caps as the technical basis for retrofit planning. A trader-landlord acquiring NYC commercial inventory > 50K sqft inherits the building's tax-block-based filing assignment AND any back-penalties from prior owners; due diligence must include EER compliance review before closing. The 10-year cycle means typical landlord operations encounter LL 87 obligations rarely enough to be easily missed but with substantial financial consequences. The EER process itself runs $15K-$75K per building depending on size and complexity (engineering fees + commissioning work), plus the retrofit recommendations identified in the audit can cost $500K-$5M+ to implement (though many are now mandatory under LL 97 emissions caps). Sibling cluster: `rental_energy_benchmarking` (NYC LL 84 вҖ” annual building energy use benchmarking that feeds into LL 87 audit data and LL 97 emissions reporting), `rental_climate_mobilization_act_ll97_emissions` (iter 587 вҖ” NYC LL 97 emissions cap that uses LL 87 audit data as basis for retrofit compliance pathway), `rental_facade_inspection_fisp_local_law_11` (iter 583 вҖ” NYC LL 11 facade, structurally parallel periodic compliance regime), `rental_gas_piping_inspection_local_law_152` (iter 585 вҖ” NYC LL 152 gas), `rental_cooling_tower_inspection_local_law_77` (iter 589 вҖ” NYC LL 77 cooling tower), `rental_nyc_loft_law_article_7c` (iter 597 вҖ” once IMD legalization complete, building becomes subject to LL 87), `rental_nyc_childhood_lead_poisoning_prevention_act` (iter 595 вҖ” different building-class compliance vector).

`traderview-expense::rental_nyc_loft_law_article_7c` is the **NYC Loft Law (NY Multiple Dwelling Law Article 7-C) compliance module** (iter 597) вҖ” distinctive NYC-specific land-use companion to `rental_nyc_childhood_lead_poisoning_prevention_act` (iter 595 NYC LL 1 of 2004), `rental_facade_inspection_fisp_local_law_11` (iter 583 NYC LL 11), `rental_just_cause_eviction` (iter 573 вҖ” NY Good Cause separate tenant-protection regime), `rental_rent_control_stabilization` (NY RSL parallel regime). Article 7-C of the NY Multiple Dwelling Law (MDL В§В§ 280-287) was enacted in **1982** (NY Laws of 1982, c. 349) to address the widespread residential occupation of commercial and manufacturing loft buildings in SoHo, TriBeCa, NoHo, the Lower East Side, Williamsburg, and Long Island City during the 1970s and early 1980s. The statute creates a third building classification вҖ” **Interim Multiple Dwelling (IMD)** вҖ” for commercial/manufacturing buildings residentially occupied during specified eligibility windows, and mandates legalization through code compliance with Article 7-B safety and fire protection standards. **MDL В§ 281 IMD coverage criteria**: building was originally commercial or manufacturing in use; residentially occupied by **3 or more families** living independently from one another for **12 consecutive months** during a specified eligibility window; not previously a legal residential building with Certificate of Occupancy. **Eligibility windows**: **Original (1982 law)** вҖ” residential occupation April 1, 1980 to December 1, 1981; **2010 expansion** (Laws of 2010, c. 135) under MDL В§ 281(5) вҖ” residential occupation 12 consecutive months in 2008-2009; **2013 amendment** (Laws of 2013, c. 4) вҖ” clarified coverage for additional building types; **2019 amendment** вҖ” expanded coverage criteria. **MDL В§ 284 code compliance timetable** (the four-stage statutory schedule): **Stage 1** вҖ” file alteration application with NYC DOB within **12 months** of IMD designation; **Stage 2** вҖ” obtain alteration permit within **18 months**; **Stage 3** вҖ” achieve Article 7-B safety and fire protection compliance within **36 months**; **Stage 4** вҖ” obtain residential Certificate of Occupancy within **48 months**. The NYC Loft Board may grant extensions for cause but generally enforces strict compliance with the statutory schedule. **Narrative Statement Process** under 29 RCNY В§ 2-01.1: document describing in plain language the proposed alterations for each unit (residential and non-residential), work the owner will perform in such unit, and work in common areas. Once Loft Board verifies the Narrative Statement process is complete, DOB plan examiner can issue a building permit. **Protected Occupant** under MDL В§ 286: an occupant qualified for protection under Article 7-C is a "Protected Occupant" and CANNOT be evicted except for good cause; entitled to rent stabilization after building legalization. **Civil penalties** under Loft Board Rules 29 RCNY В§ 2-12: **$500 minimum, $1,000+ maximum per violation per day**; cumulative daily penalties for ongoing non-compliance with statutory timetable. **Thirteen-mode severity ladder**: NotApplicable, ExemptResidentialFromInception, ExemptCommercialFullyVacant, CompliantImdRegisteredAndLegalizationOnSchedule, CompliantArticle7BComplianceMet, CompliantCertificateOfOccupancyObtainedRemovedFromLoftBoardJurisdiction, ViolationImdNotRegisteredWithLoftBoard, ViolationNarrativeStatementNotFiled, ViolationAlterationApplicationNotFiledWithin12Months, ViolationAlterationPermitNotObtainedWithin18Months, ViolationArticle7BComplianceNotMetWithin36Months, ViolationCertificateOfOccupancyNotObtainedWithin48Months, ViolationHarassmentOrIllegalEvictionOfProtectedOccupant. Mounted at `POST /api/rental/rental-nyc-loft-law-article-7c`. **Twenty-six tests pin**: **IMD registered legalization on schedule compliant** (baseline), **residential from inception exempt** (no commercial/manufacturing history), **previously residential CofO exempt**, **under 12 consecutive months not IMD** (11 months falls short), **exactly 12 consecutive months qualifies IMD** (statutory вүҘ reading), **under 3 family units not IMD** (2 falls short), **exactly 3 family units qualifies IMD**, **IMD not registered violation** (60 days Г— $500 = $30,000 aggregate), **narrative statement not filed violation**, **alteration application not filed within 12 months violation** (18 months elapsed), **alteration application at exactly 12 months compliant** (statutory вүӨ reading), **alteration permit not obtained within 18 months violation** (24 months elapsed), **Article 7-B compliance not met within 36 months violation** (40 months elapsed), **Certificate of Occupancy not obtained within 48 months violation** (60 months elapsed), **Article 7-B met pending CofO compliant**, **CofO obtained removed from Loft Board jurisdiction** (subject to NYC Rent Stabilization Law going forward), **harassment of Protected Occupant violation** (30 days Г— $1,000 = $30,000 aggregate), **citations pin MDL В§В§ 280/281/284/286/287**, **citations pin 29 RCNY Loft Board Rules В§ 2-01.1 + В§ 2-12**, **citations pin NY Laws of 1982/2010/2013/2019**, **constant pins 3 family unit threshold**, **constant pins 12-month residential threshold**, **constant pins 12/18/36/48 month statutory timetable**, **constant pins $500 minimum and $1,000 maximum daily civil penalty**, **constant pins 1982 original enactment year**, **very large continuing violation days no overflow** (u32::MAX defense). Trader-landlord critical because Loft Law compliance is the single most consequential building-class determination for any trader-landlord acquiring formerly commercial/manufacturing inventory in NYC вҖ” IMD designation transforms a vacant-commercial purchase into a regulated-residential acquisition with mandatory legalization costs ($500K-$5M+ per building for Article 7-B compliance work) PLUS post-legalization NYC Rent Stabilization Law subjection with Protected Occupants paying below-market rents indefinitely. The 12/18/36/48 month statutory timetable allows essentially no flexibility вҖ” trader-landlords who acquire IMD-designated buildings inherit the original IMD designation date and remaining schedule, often facing only 6-12 months to complete years of pending compliance work. The harassment-of-Protected-Occupant branch is the highest-stakes enforcement vector вҖ” Loft Board harassment findings carry $5,000-$100,000+ per-incident civil penalties plus tenant action for compensatory and punitive damages plus criminal referral for repeated harassment. Sibling cluster: `rental_nyc_childhood_lead_poisoning_prevention_act` (iter 595 вҖ” NYC LL 1 of 2004 lead-paint regime that stacks atop Loft Law IMD legalization), `rental_facade_inspection_fisp_local_law_11` (iter 583 вҖ” NYC LL 11 facade applies to legalized IMD buildings post-CofO), `rental_gas_piping_inspection_local_law_152` (iter 585 вҖ” LL 152 gas inspection applies post-legalization), `rental_climate_mobilization_act_ll97_emissions` (iter 587 вҖ” LL 97 emissions apply to legalized residential), `rental_just_cause_eviction` (iter 573 вҖ” NY Good Cause supplements Article 7-C Protected Occupant protections), `rental_rent_control_stabilization` (NY RSL вҖ” legalized IMD buildings become rent-stabilized).

`traderview-expense::rental_nyc_childhood_lead_poisoning_prevention_act` is the **NYC Local Law 1 of 2004 (Childhood Lead Poisoning Prevention Act) compliance module** (iter 595) вҖ” direct NYC-specific lead-paint companion to `rental_lead_paint_disclosure` (iter 569 вҖ” federal Title X В§ 1018 disclosure floor that NYC LL 1 stacks atop), `rental_lead_pipe_disclosure` (lead service line water вҖ” distinct lead-exposure vector), `rental_post_construction_lead_dust_clearance` (post-renovation lead-dust clearance вҖ” adjacent post-work compliance), `rental_facade_inspection_fisp_local_law_11` (iter 583 вҖ” NYC LL 11 facade), `rental_gas_piping_inspection_local_law_152` (iter 585 вҖ” NYC LL 152 gas), `rental_cooling_tower_inspection_local_law_77` (iter 589 вҖ” NYC LL 77 cooling tower). NYC Local Law 1 of 2004 (also known as the Childhood Lead Poisoning Prevention Act) was enacted **August 2, 2004** as one of the most comprehensive lead-based paint laws in the United States. It replaced the earlier Local Law 38 of 1999 (which had been struck down by NY courts as inadequate). Codified at **NYC Admin Code В§ 27-2056 et seq.** plus **28 RCNY Subchapter K** (HPD implementing regulations). **Coverage** under В§ 27-2056.2: multiple dwellings (3+ units) built BEFORE 1960; OR buildings constructed 1960-1977 if lead-based paint is known to be present; AND child under 6 years old "resides" in unit, where "resides" means living in or routinely spending **10 or more hours per week** in the dwelling unit. **Six core landlord obligations**: (1) **Annual Notice** under 28 RCNY В§ 11-02 вҖ” each year between January 1 and February 15, landlord must distribute notice to all tenants asking whether a child under 6 resides or spends 10+ hours per week in the unit, tenant response due by February 15; (2) **Annual Investigation** under В§ 27-2056.4(d) вҖ” if a child under 6 resides, landlord must perform a visual inspection of the unit and common areas for lead-based paint hazards (peeling paint, friction surfaces, etc.); (3) **Turnover Inspection** under 28 RCNY В§ 11-04 вҖ” when a unit is vacated and a new tenancy commences with a child under 6 residing, landlord must perform a turnover inspection AND have any lead-based paint hazards remediated before occupancy; (4) **Lead Hazard Remediation** under В§ 27-2056.4(g) вҖ” **21 days** to correct identified lead hazards or HPD may complete emergency repair and charge owner under В§ 27-2125 administrative-lien process; (5) **EPA-Certified Renovator** under В§ 27-2056.11 вҖ” EPA Lead Renovation, Repair, and Painting (RRP) certified firm required for any work disturbing more than **100 sqft** of lead-based paint, replacing windows, or fixing HPD lead violations; (6) **Recordkeeping** under В§ 27-2056.14 вҖ” maintain inspection records, remediation records, and tenant notice records for at least **10 years**. **NYC Local Law 31 of 2020 вҖ” XRF Testing Requirement**: all dwelling units where a child under 6 resides must have X-ray fluorescence (XRF) lead-paint testing performed by EPA-certified inspector/risk assessor by **August 9, 2025**. Failure to comply is Class "C" immediately hazardous violation carrying civil penalty up to **$1,500**. **NYC Local Law 66 of 2019 вҖ” Lead-Based Paint Threshold**: NYC lowered the lead-paint threshold from the federal HUD 1.0 mg/cmВІ to **0.5 mg/cmВІ** (stricter NYC standard), effective December 1, 2021. NYC pre-1960 buildings are presumed to contain lead-based paint at the 0.5 mg/cmВІ threshold absent professional XRF testing showing otherwise. **Twelve-mode severity ladder**: NotApplicable, ExemptBuildingPost1960NoKnownLead, ExemptUnder3UnitsNotMultipleDwelling, ExemptNoChildUnder6Residing, CompliantAllObligationsMet, ViolationAnnualNoticeNotSentJanFeb, ViolationAnnualInvestigationNotPerformed, ViolationXrfTestingMissedAug9_2025Deadline, ViolationLeadHazardNotRemediatedWithin21Days, ViolationEpaCertifiedRrpRenovatorNotUsedForDisturbanceOver100Sqft, ViolationRecordkeepingRetentionUnder10Years, AggravatedViolationHpdEmergencyRepairChargeback. Mounted at `POST /api/rental/rental-nyc-childhood-lead-poisoning-prevention-act`. **Twenty-eight tests pin**: **fully compliant baseline** (1925 pre-1960 + 12 units + child under 6 + all six obligations met), **building post-1960 no known lead exempt**, **building 1960-1977 with known lead covered**, **under 3 units not multiple dwelling exempt**, **no child under 6 exempt if notice sent**, **no child under 6 but no notice still violation** (annual notice required regardless of occupancy), **annual notice not sent violation** ($1,500 penalty), **annual investigation not performed violation**, **XRF testing missed August 9, 2025 deadline violation**, **XRF testing NOT required pre-2025** (2024 current year), **lead hazard 22 days unremediated violation** (statutory > 21 reading), **lead hazard at exactly 21 days compliant** (boundary), **EPA RRP not used for 100 sqft disturbance violation**, **recordkeeping under 10 years violation**, **HPD emergency repair aggravated violation** (В§ 27-2125 administrative-lien chargeback), **citations pin LL 1 + Admin Code subsections** (В§ 27-2056 + В§ 27-2056.4 + В§ 27-2056.11 + В§ 27-2056.14 + В§ 27-2125), **citations pin LL 31 of 2020 XRF + LL 66 of 2019 0.5 mg/cmВІ threshold**, **citations pin 28 RCNY Subchapter K + EPA RRP 40 C.F.R. Part 745 Subpart E**, **constant pins pre-1960 threshold**, **constant pins child age 6 threshold**, **constant pins 10 hours per week residing**, **constant pins 21-day remediation**, **constant pins XRF 2025 deadline**, **constant pins $1,500 civil penalty**, **constant pins 0.5 mg/cmВІ threshold Г— 10** (5), **constant pins 3-unit multiple dwelling threshold**, **constant pins 100 sqft EPA RRP threshold**, **constant pins 10-year recordkeeping**. Trader-landlord critical because NYC LL 1 is the highest-frequency lead-paint enforcement regime in the United States вҖ” HPD performs over 30,000 lead-paint visual inspections annually with 5,000+ lead violations issued, and the August 9, 2025 LL 31 XRF testing deadline has triggered a wave of compliance failures across NYC's pre-1960 multifamily inventory. The $1,500 per-violation civil penalty is just the floor: HPD В§ 27-2125 emergency repair chargebacks can exceed $50,000 per unit for full-room paint replacement, the 21-day remediation window leaves no time for sophisticated abatement planning, and the EPA RRP certification requirement adds $200-$500/hour to any covered renovation work. The LL 66 0.5 mg/cmВІ threshold is the stricter-than-federal trap вҖ” landlords compliant with federal Title X В§ 1018 disclosure thinking they have lead-free paint at 0.8 mg/cmВІ are still in violation under the NYC 0.5 mg/cmВІ threshold. Sibling cluster: `rental_lead_paint_disclosure` (iter 569 вҖ” federal Title X В§ 1018 disclosure floor that LL 1 stacks atop), `rental_lead_pipe_disclosure` (lead service line water вҖ” distinct lead-exposure vector), `rental_post_construction_lead_dust_clearance` (post-renovation lead-dust clearance), `rental_facade_inspection_fisp_local_law_11` (iter 583 вҖ” NYC LL 11 facade), `rental_gas_piping_inspection_local_law_152` (iter 585 вҖ” NYC LL 152 gas), `rental_cooling_tower_inspection_local_law_77` (iter 589 вҖ” NYC LL 77 cooling tower), `rental_climate_mobilization_act_ll97_emissions` (iter 587 вҖ” NYC LL 97 emissions), `rental_window_guard_installation` (NYC RHC В§ 131.15 вҖ” adjacent child-safety regime).

`traderview-expense::rental_massachusetts_security_deposit_statute` is the **Massachusetts Security Deposit Statute (MGL c. 186, В§ 15B) compliance module** (iter 593) вҖ” direct state-specific companion to `rental_security_deposit_interest` (NY/CA interest regimes), `rental_security_deposit_return_notice` (parallel state return-notice rules), `rental_just_cause_eviction` (iter 573 вҖ” different tenant-protection vector), `rental_application_denial_disclosure` (state-specific denial regimes). MGL c. 186, В§ 15B is widely regarded as the most stringent security deposit statute in the United States: failure to comply with ANY procedural requirement exposes the landlord to **TRIPLE DAMAGES** (three times the deposit amount) plus court costs and reasonable attorney fees, AND additional liability under MGL c. 93A (the Massachusetts Consumer Protection Act). **Seven core landlord obligations**: (1) **В§ 15B(1)(b) maximum deposit** вҖ” security deposit may not exceed **ONE MONTH'S RENT** at commencement of tenancy; (2) **В§ 15B(2)(b) written receipt** вҖ” lessor must provide written receipt within **30 days** of receipt listing amount received, name and address of lessor, name of person receiving, date of receipt, and description of leased premises; (3) **В§ 15B(2)(c) Statement of Condition** (Apartment Condition Statement) вҖ” within **10 days** after commencement of tenancy OR on receipt of deposit (whichever is later), lessor must furnish a written statement of the present condition of the premises containing a comprehensive listing of any damage then existing; must be signed by lessor or agent, contain a notice in **12-point bold-face type** at the top of the first page, tenant has 15 days to return signed statement; (4) **В§ 15B(3)(a) separate Massachusetts bank account** вҖ” deposit must be held in a separate interest-bearing account in a bank located within Massachusetts, under terms placing the deposit beyond claims of lessor's creditors; (5) **В§ 15B(3)(b) annual interest** вҖ” lessor holding deposit for one year or longer must pay interest at the lesser of **5% per year** or actual bank interest received, paid annually on tenancy anniversary or within 30 days of termination; (6) **В§ 15B(4) 30-day return** вҖ” within **30 days** after termination of occupancy, lessor must return deposit or balance with itemized list of damages signed under penalty of perjury and including written invoices, bills, receipts, or estimates to repair the damage; (7) **В§ 15B(7) triple damages** вҖ” failure to (a) return deposit within 30 days, (b) return interest within 30 days, (c) maintain separate account, (d) disclose bank information, (e) comply with statement of condition procedure results in damages equal to **THREE TIMES** the deposit amount plus court costs and reasonable attorney fees. **Eleven-mode severity ladder**: NotApplicable, CompliantFullStatutoryCompliance, ViolationDepositExceedsOneMonthRent, ViolationDepositReceiptNotProvidedWithin30Days, ViolationDepositNotInSeparateMassBankAccount, ViolationNoStatementOfConditionWithin10Days, ViolationStatementOfConditionMissing12PtBoldNotice, ViolationInterestNotPaidAnnually, ViolationDepositNotReturnedWithin30DaysOfTermination, ViolationItemizedDamagesListMissingOrUnsignedPerjury, AggravatedTripleDamagesUnderSection15B7. Mounted at `POST /api/rental/rental-massachusetts-security-deposit-statute`. **Twenty-six tests pin**: **fully compliant baseline** ($2,500 deposit вүӨ $2,500 rent), **annual interest at 5% baseline $125** ($2,500 Г— 5% Г— 100), **deposit exceeds one month rent violation** ($3,000 > $2,500 вҶ’ triple damages $9,000), **deposit at exactly one month rent compliant** (boundary), **written receipt not provided violation**, **deposit not in MA bank account violation**, **no Statement of Condition within 10 days violation**, **statement missing 12-pt bold-face violation**, **annual interest not paid violation**, **deposit not returned within 30 days violation** (45 days вҶ’ triple damages), **deposit at exactly 30 days still compliant** (statutory вүӨ reading), **deposit at 31 days not returned violation** (boundary), **aggravated triple damages under В§ 15B(7)** (60 days + aggregation flag), **deposit retained without itemized damages violation**, **deposit retained without perjury signature violation**, **deposit retained with proper itemized damages compliant**, **citations pin MGL c. 186, В§ 15B subsections** (В§ 15B(1)(b) + В§ 15B(2)(b) + В§ 15B(2)(c) + В§ 15B(3)(a) + В§ 15B(3)(b) + В§ 15B(4) + В§ 15B(7)), **citations pin MGL c. 93A consumer protection**, **constant pins 1-month rent maximum**, **constant pins 5% interest rate Г— 100** (500), **constant pins 30-day receipt**, **constant pins 10-day Statement of Condition**, **constant pins 30-day return after termination**, **constant pins triple damages multiplier 3Г—**, **constant pins 12-point bold-face font size**, **very large deposit saturating no overflow** (u64::MAX defense). Trader-landlord critical because MA security-deposit violations are the highest-frequency landlord civil-action exposure in the state вҖ” Mass Housing Court dockets see hundreds of triple-damages claims annually for technical statutory violations, often where the landlord has actually returned the deposit but missed one of the procedural requirements (failed to provide 12-pt bold notice, deposited in non-MA bank, etc.). A simple Statement of Condition formatting error converts a $2,500 deposit return into a $7,500 triple-damages judgment plus attorney fees that often equal or exceed the underlying deposit. The c. 93A overlay is particularly punishing вҖ” a finding of unfair or deceptive practice can DOUBLE the triple-damages award (6Г— total) plus reasonable attorney fees, making MA the highest-stakes security-deposit jurisdiction in the United States. Sibling cluster: `rental_security_deposit_interest` (NY/CA interest regimes вҖ” different statutory thresholds), `rental_security_deposit_return_notice` (parallel state return-notice rules), `rental_just_cause_eviction` (iter 573 вҖ” different tenant-protection vector), `rental_application_denial_disclosure` (state-specific application-stage regimes), `rental_late_fee_cap`, `rental_pet_deposit_separate_security` (pet-deposit carve-out distinct from В§ 15B-covered security deposits).

`traderview-expense::rental_dc_topa_tenant_opportunity_purchase` is the **D.C. Tenant Opportunity to Purchase Act (TOPA) compliance module** (iter 591) вҖ” DC-specific companion to `rental_foreclosure_tenant_protection_ptfa` (iter 571 federal PTFA foreclosure tenant protection вҖ” adjacent right-of-first-refusal regime in different transaction context), `rental_just_cause_eviction` (iter 573 multi-state just-cause eviction вҖ” different tenant-protection vector), `rental_application_denial_disclosure` (state-specific written denial). TOPA was enacted in 1980 (D.C. Law 3-86) as part of the Rental Housing Conversion and Sale Act, codified at **D.C. Code В§ 42-3401.01 et seq.** with the operative tenant-purchase provisions at **В§ 42-3404.02** et seq. and the civil-action provisions at **В§ 42-3405.03**. TOPA is the most aggressive municipal tenant-purchase right in the United States: when a landlord sells residential rental property, tenants get a statutory right of first refusal to purchase the property at the terms offered by a third-party buyer, with extended negotiation and financing windows. **Three coverage tiers** (post-2018 amendments): (1) **Single-family homes**: EXEMPT as of **July 3, 2018** under the TOPA Single-Family Home Exemption Amendment Act of 2017; single-family dwellings with an Accessory Dwelling Unit are also exempted. (2) **2-4 unit buildings**: covered IF owned by a corporate business OR an individual owner who owns multiple DC properties; otherwise exempt. **90-day negotiation period** after Statement of Interest; extension of 1 day for each day landlord fails to deliver required information; 15-day extension if landlord enters into third-party contract during negotiation. (3) **5+ unit buildings**: full TOPA applies. **45-day Cooling-Off Period** after offer of sale (or **30 days** for Statement of Interest if tenant association already exists); **120-day negotiation period** following Statement of Interest; **120-240 day financing window** depending on lender needs. **5+ unit tenant organization registration** under D.C. Code В§ 42-3404.11: tenant organization may NOT assign rights to a third party during first 45 days unless registration application has been submitted to DHCD AND tenant organization has completed certified TOPA-rights training. **Civil enforcement** under D.C. Code В§ 42-3405.03: any aggrieved owner, tenant, or tenant organization may seek civil enforcement in law or equity; prevailing party entitled to costs and reasonable attorney fees; В§ 42-3509.01 housing-code-violation civil penalties may apply separately. **RENTAL Act of 2025** (Rebalancing Expectations for Neighbors, Tenants, and Landlords Act of 2025): passed by D.C. Council September 17, 2025; effective December 31, 2025. Significantly amends TOPA but does not eliminate the core right of first refusal regime вҖ” primarily shortens windows and clarifies exemptions. **Eleven-mode severity ladder**: NotApplicable, ExemptSingleFamilyHomePost2018, Exempt2To4UnitNonCorporateSingleProperty, Compliant5PlusUnitOfferOfSaleProvidedFullProcess, Compliant2To4UnitNegotiationPeriodActive, CompliantRightOfFirstRefusalRespectedOnThirdPartyContract, ViolationFailedToProvideOfferOfSale, ViolationFailedToObserveCoolingOffPeriod45Days, ViolationFailedToHonorRightOfFirstRefusalOnThirdPartyContract, ViolationTenantOrganizationAssignedRightsWithoutDhcdRegistration, ViolationLandlordTimelineNotHonoredCivilCauseOfAction. **Three property types**: SingleFamilyHome, TwoToFourUnit, FivePlusUnit. Mounted at `POST /api/rental/rental-dc-topa-tenant-opportunity-purchase`. **Twenty-four tests pin**: **5+ unit full process compliant** (120-day negotiation + 30-day Statement of Interest with pre-existing association), **single-family home exempt post-2018**, **2-4 unit non-corporate single-property exempt**, **2-4 unit corporate owner covered with 90-day**, **failed to provide offer of sale violation** (per se В§ 42-3404.02), **third-party contract with refusal compliant**, **third-party contract without refusal violation**, **5+ existing association 30-day statement window**, **5+ no association 45-day cooling-off window**, **cooling-off period violation third-party contract within 45 days**, **tenant org assigned rights without DHCD registration violation** (В§ 42-3404.11), **citations pin TOPA DC Code subsections** (В§ 42-3401.01 + В§ 42-3404.02 + В§ 42-3404.11 + В§ 42-3405.03 + В§ 42-3509.01), **citations pin D.C. Law 3-86 + RENTAL Act of 2025 + Sep 17, 2025 passage date**, **citations pin single-family exemption July 3, 2018**, **constant pins 90-day 2-4 negotiation**, **constant pins 120-day 5+ negotiation**, **constant pins 45-day cooling-off**, **constant pins 30-day existing assoc statement window**, **constant pins 5+ unit threshold**, **constant pins 15-day third-party extension**, **constant pins 120-240 day financing window**, **constant pins single-family exemption 2018 year**, **constant pins RENTAL Act 2025 effective date** (Dec 31, 2025), **constant pins TOPA 1980 original enactment**. Trader-landlord critical because DC TOPA is the highest single-source operational drag on landlord investment in the District: the full TOPA process can last MORE THAN A YEAR (45-day cooling-off + 30-45 day Statement of Interest + 120-day negotiation + 120-240 day financing = 315-455 days), during which the landlord cannot close on a third-party sale; landlord-financed deal terms must match exactly what was offered to tenants; failure to provide the Offer of Sale or honor the right of first refusal creates statutory civil-action exposure with attorney fees + costs to prevailing tenants. The post-2018 single-family exemption and 2-4 unit non-corporate carve-out narrowed scope considerably, but every corporate or multi-property-owner 2+ unit acquisition in DC carries TOPA closing-process risk. Sibling cluster: `rental_foreclosure_tenant_protection_ptfa` (iter 571 вҖ” federal 90-day vacate floor in foreclosure context, parallel right-of-first-refusal-adjacent regime), `rental_just_cause_eviction` (iter 573 вҖ” different tenant-protection vector on lease termination side), `rental_eviction_record_sealing_screening` (iter 579 вҖ” different tenant-screening regime), `rental_multilingual_lease_translation` (iter 577 вҖ” different state-specific tenant-protection regime).

`traderview-expense::rental_cooling_tower_inspection_local_law_77` is the **NYC Local Law 77 of 2015 cooling tower registration, inspection, and Legionella testing compliance module** (iter 589) вҖ” direct NYC building-systems-safety companion to `rental_facade_inspection_fisp_local_law_11` (iter 583 NYC LL 11 facade), `rental_gas_piping_inspection_local_law_152` (iter 585 NYC LL 152 gas piping), `rental_climate_mobilization_act_ll97_emissions` (iter 587 NYC LL 97 emissions), `rental_natural_gas_leak_response`. Enacted in **August 2015** in response to the **South Bronx Legionnaires' disease outbreak** (July-August 2015) that killed **8 people** and infected **138** вҖ” traced to contaminated cooling-tower water at the Opera House Hotel and several other buildings in the Mott Haven / Hunts Point area. Local Law 77 is the most aggressive municipal cooling-tower regulation in the United States and the first to require automatic biocide treatment plus quarterly Legionella culture testing for ALL registered cooling towers. **Statutory chain**: **NYC Admin Code В§ 17-194.1** (statutory basis); **Chapter 8 of the Rules of the City of New York** (operative detail вҖ” registration, MMP, sampling, certification); **NYS 10 NYCRR Subpart 4-1** (parallel state cooling-tower regulation, also enacted post-2015 outbreak). **Six operative obligations**: (1) **Register** cooling tower with NYC DOHMH within 30 days of installation; (2) **Develop and implement** a written Maintenance Management Plan (MMP) by a Qualified Person; (3) **Inspect tower every 90 days** while in operation; (4) Bacteriological sampling for **Legionella pneumophila culture + heterotrophic plate count** quarterly (March, June, September, December); (5) **Submit lab results to DOHMH within 5 days** of receipt; (6) **File annual certification** with DOHMH by November 1 of each year. **Qualified Person definition** (Chapter 8 В§ 8-04): (a) New York State licensed PE or RA; (b) certified water technologist with MPP-development training; (c) environmental consultant with at least 2 years operational experience in water-management planning. **Penalty schedule** (NYC Admin Code В§ 17-194.1(g)): **first violation** up to **$2,000**; **second or subsequent** up to **$5,000**; **violation accompanied by or resulting in fatality or serious injury** up to **$10,000**; **no MPP at all** $1,000; **incomplete MPP or not on-site** ~$500; **late or missing annual certification** up to $10,000. **NYC Local Law 159 of 2024 upcoming change**: effective May 7, 2026, **monthly Legionella testing** replaces the quarterly 90-day cycle вҖ” a 3Г— tightening of testing frequency that materially increases laboratory costs ($150-$400 per sampling event Г— 12 months = $1,800-$4,800/year per tower). **Twelve-mode severity ladder**: NotApplicableNoCoolingTower, CompliantInspectionAndCertificationOnTime, ViolationCoolingTowerNotRegistered, ViolationMmpAbsentNoPlanAtAll, ViolationMmpIncompleteOrNotOnSite, ViolationQuarterlyInspectionMissed, ViolationLegionellaTestingMissed, ViolationLabResultsNotSubmittedWithin5Days, ViolationAnnualCertificationLate, ViolationLl159MonthlyTestingRequirementMissedPostMay2026, ViolationQualifiedPersonNotEligible, AggravatedViolationFatalityOrSeriousInjury. Mounted at `POST /api/rental/rental-cooling-tower-inspection-local-law-77`. **Twenty-six tests pin**: **fully compliant baseline**, **no cooling tower not applicable**, **fatality or serious injury aggravated $10,000**, **not registered first violation $2,000 penalty**, **not registered subsequent violation $5,000 penalty** (history-tracked penalty escalation), **qualified person not eligible violation**, **no MMP at all $1,000 penalty**, **incomplete MMP $500 penalty**, **quarterly inspection missed 91 days violation**, **quarterly inspection exactly 90 days compliant** (statutory вүӨ reading), **Legionella testing missed 91 days violation pre-LL 159**, **LL 159 monthly testing post-2026 30-day violation** (35 days > 30-day boundary), **LL 159 2025 still quarterly testing 60 days compliant** (effective-date boundary), **lab results not submitted within 5 days violation**, **lab results at exactly 5 days compliant**, **annual certification late $10,000 penalty**, **citations pin LL 77 + Admin Code В§ 17-194.1 + Chapter 8 + 10 NYCRR Subpart 4-1**, **citations pin LL 159 monthly testing 2026 effective date**, **citations pin South Bronx 2015 outbreak (8 deaths, 138 cases)**, **constant pins 90-day inspection**, **constant pins 5-day lab result submission**, **constant pins $2,000 first violation**, **constant pins $5,000 subsequent violation**, **constant pins $10,000 fatality serious injury**, **constant pins LL 159 2026 effective year**, **constant pins South Bronx 8 deaths 138 cases**. Trader-landlord critical because cooling towers are concentrated in COMMERCIAL real estate (office, hotel, hospital, industrial вҖ” Class B and C office buildings particularly) plus large multifamily towers (Class A high-rises typically have rooftop cooling towers for shared-system HVAC). A trader-landlord acquiring NYC commercial property with cooling towers takes on quarterly inspection costs ($800-$2,000 per tower per quarter), MMP-development costs ($2,000-$10,000 upfront), Legionella testing costs ($150-$400 per sampling event), and the LL 159 monthly testing transition adds $1,800-$4,800/year per tower starting May 2026. The fatality-or-serious-injury aggravated penalty branch is the most serious вҖ” the South Bronx outbreak produced wrongful-death civil judgments of $1M-$5M+ per fatality victim against building owners, plus criminal manslaughter referrals against contractors in the Opera House Hotel case. Sibling cluster: `rental_facade_inspection_fisp_local_law_11` (iter 583 вҖ” NYC LL 11 facade, structurally parallel periodic compliance regime), `rental_gas_piping_inspection_local_law_152` (iter 585 вҖ” NYC LL 152 gas piping, complementary safety compliance), `rental_climate_mobilization_act_ll97_emissions` (iter 587 вҖ” NYC LL 97 emissions, energy-system parallel regime), `rental_natural_gas_leak_response` (NYC tenant gas-leak response вҖ” adjacent safety reporting), `rental_carbon_monoxide_detector` (combustion-safety device parallel), `rental_emergency_action_plan_high_rise` (NYC LL 26 high-rise EAP вҖ” cooling-tower contamination can trigger building-evacuation protocols).

`traderview-expense::rental_climate_mobilization_act_ll97_emissions` is the **NYC Local Law 97 of 2019 (Climate Mobilization Act) building greenhouse-gas emissions cap compliance module** (iter 587) вҖ” direct NYC-specific companion to `rental_gas_piping_inspection_local_law_152` (iter 585 вҖ” NYC LL 152 gas inspection), `rental_facade_inspection_fisp_local_law_11` (iter 583 вҖ” NYC LL 11 facade), `rental_energy_benchmarking` (NYC LL 84 predicate energy reporting that drives LL 97 emissions calculation), `rental_gas_appliance_ban` (all-electric mandate as compliance pathway). Enacted in 2019 as part of the NYC Climate Mobilization Act and codified at NYC Admin Code В§ 28-320 et seq. Emissions caps phase in starting **2024** with progressive tightening through **2050** to deliver a 40% citywide building-emissions reduction by 2030 and 80% by 2050. Buildings are responsible for ~two-thirds of NYC's total greenhouse-gas footprint, making LL 97 the most aggressive municipal building-emissions regulation in the United States. **Applicability**: buildings with gross floor area GREATER THAN **25,000 square feet** (with limited exceptions for industrial buildings, NYCHA, and rent-regulated portions covered separately under Article 321). **Compliance periods** with emissions intensity limits (kgCO2e per square foot) by occupancy group: **R-2 multifamily 2024-2029**: 6.75 kgCO2e/sqft; **R-2 multifamily 2030-2034**: 4.07 kgCO2e/sqft (40% reduction at period turn); **B business/office 2024-2029**: 8.46 kgCO2e/sqft; **B business/office 2030-2034**: 4.53 kgCO2e/sqft (47% reduction at period turn). **Penalty**: **$268 per metric ton CO2e** annual emissions over the building's applicable intensity-limit allowance (NYC Admin Code В§ 28-320.6 + 1 RCNY В§ 103-14). **Article 321 alternative compliance pathway**: buildings with more than **35% rent-regulated units** may implement **13 prescriptive energy conservation measures** (ECMs) instead of meeting emissions limits directly. Measures include lighting retrofits, heating-system upgrades, insulation, window seals, controls, and similar prescriptive items. Article 321 compliance must be certified by a registered design professional. **Adjustments**: financial hardship adjustment under В§ 28-320.7; critical facility adjustment under В§ 28-320.8 (hospitals, energy infrastructure, critical-services properties); special-circumstance adjustment under DOB rulemaking. Approval is discretionary; denials produce full emissions-cap exposure. **Nine-mode severity ladder**: NotApplicable, ExemptBuildingUnder25000Sqft, ExemptOccupancyNotCovered, CompliantEmissionsWithinIntensityLimit, CompliantArticle321PrescriptiveEcmsRentRegulated, CompliantApprovedHardshipAdjustment, CompliantApprovedCriticalFacilityAdjustment, ViolationEmissionsExceedLimitPenaltyAccrues, ViolationArticle321ClaimedButRentRegulatedThresholdNotMet. **Five occupancy groups**: R2Multifamily, BBusinessOffice, MMercantile, OtherCovered, R3OneOrTwoFamilyOrExempt. Mounted at `POST /api/rental/rental-climate-mobilization-act-ll97-emissions`. **Twenty-six tests pin**: **R-2 50,000 sqft 2026 within 6.75 intensity compliant** (300,000 kgCO2e вүӨ 337,500 allowance), **R-2 50,000 sqft 2026 above 6.75 intensity violation with penalty** (500,000 kgCO2e вҶ’ 162 metric tons over вҶ’ $43,416 annual penalty), **R-2 50,000 sqft 2030 tightens to 4.07 intensity** (period turn), **R-2 2030 with 2024-compliant emissions now violation** (96 metric tons over due to tightening), **B business/office 2024 8.46 intensity higher than R-2**, **B business/office 2030 drops to 4.53 intensity**, **building under 25,000 sqft exempt** (boundary), **building at 25,001 sqft just over threshold not exempt** (statutory > reading), **R-3 one- or two-family exempt**, **Article 321 with 50% rent-regulated compliant** (above 35% threshold), **Article 321 with exactly 35% rent-regulated NOT eligible** (boundary), **Article 321 with 36% rent-regulated eligible**, **approved hardship adjustment overrides emissions limit**, **approved critical facility adjustment overrides emissions limit**, **citations pin LL 97 + Admin Code В§ 28-320.6/.7/.8**, **citations pin Article 321 + 1 RCNY В§ 103-14**, **constant pins 25,000 sqft threshold**, **constant pins $268/ton penalty**, **constant pins R-2 first period 6.75**, **constant pins R-2 second period 4.07**, **constant pins B business first period 8.46**, **constant pins Article 321 35% threshold**, **constant pins Article 321 13 ECMs count**, **constant pins 2050 final target year**, **constant pins 80% final reduction**, **very large excess emissions saturating no overflow** (u64::MAX defense). Trader-landlord critical because NYC LL 97 affects EVERY trader-landlord with NYC commercial or multifamily inventory above the 25,000 sqft threshold вҖ” and the period-turn from 2024-2029 to 2030-2034 in 2030 represents a single-day 40-47% tightening that will push an estimated 80% of covered buildings into emissions-cap violation without efficiency upgrades. The financial exposure scales linearly with metric tons over limit вҖ” a 100,000 sqft office building 50% over its 2030 cap faces an annual penalty of roughly $268 Г— (100,000 Г— 4.53 / 100 Г— 0.5 / 1000) Г— 1000 вүҲ $60,700 per year вҖ” and these penalties stack annually, capitalizing into 7-figure 5-year exposure for unimproved properties. Compliance pathways are dominated by heat pump conversions (eliminating natural gas combustion which produces most building emissions), solar PV installation, building-envelope upgrades, and electric induction cooking вҖ” capital expenditures that range $50K-$5M+ per building. Sibling cluster: `rental_energy_benchmarking` (NYC LL 84 вҖ” annual energy and water use reporting that drives the LL 97 emissions calculation вҖ” every building subject to LL 97 must first benchmark under LL 84), `rental_gas_appliance_ban` (all-electric mandate вҖ” LL 97 incentivizes gas-to-electric conversion that the gas-appliance-ban formalizes), `rental_facade_inspection_fisp_local_law_11` (iter 583 вҖ” NYC LL 11 facade, structurally parallel periodic compliance regime), `rental_gas_piping_inspection_local_law_152` (iter 585 вҖ” NYC LL 152 gas piping, complementary safety compliance), `rental_carbon_monoxide_detector` (combustion-safety regime that interacts with LL 97 fuel-switching), `rental_solar_panel_disclosure` (solar PV installations are a primary LL 97 compliance pathway).

`traderview-expense::rental_gas_piping_inspection_local_law_152` is the **NYC Local Law 152 of 2016 periodic gas piping inspection compliance module** (iter 585) вҖ” direct NYC-specific companion to `rental_facade_inspection_fisp_local_law_11` (iter 583 вҖ” NYC LL 11 facade 5-year cycle, parallel building-safety regime), `rental_natural_gas_leak_response` (NYC tenant gas-leak response protocol), `rental_carbon_monoxide_detector` (combustion safety), `rental_gas_appliance_ban` (all-electric mandate that stacks atop LL 152 inspection requirement). Enacted in response to the **March 12, 2014 East Harlem gas explosion** at 1644-1646 Park Avenue that killed 8 people and collapsed two buildings due to undetected gas-piping deterioration вҖ” the precipitating incident behind LL 152's mandatory inspection regime. **Operative rule** (NYC Admin Code В§ 28-318 + 1 RCNY В§ 103-10): exposed gas piping systems in ALL NYC buildings вҖ” EXCEPT one- and two-family homes (Occupancy Group R-3) вҖ” must be inspected every **4 years** by a Licensed Master Plumber (LMP) or qualified individual working for an LMP. **Community-district rotating schedule**: Districts 1, 3, 10 вҶ’ 2024 (then 2028, 2032 ...); Districts 2, 5, 7, 13, 18 вҶ’ 2025; Districts 4, 6, 8, 9, 16 вҶ’ 2026; Districts 11, 12, 14, 15, 17 вҶ’ 2027. **Filing workflow**: LMP delivers Gas Piping System Periodic Inspection Report (GPS1) to building owner within **30 days** of inspection; owner submits Gas Piping System Periodic Inspection Certification (GPS2) to DOB within **60 days** of inspection. If conditions need correction: follow-up certification within **120 days** of inspection confirming all conditions corrected, OR within **180 days** with DOB-approved extension. **Unsafe condition workflow**: if LMP identifies any unsafe or hazardous condition, LMP must IMMEDIATELY (1) call 911, (2) notify building owner, (3) notify utility providing gas service, (4) notify NYC DOB. For immediately hazardous conditions, LMP may advise gas shutoff to section or entire building as a priority for life safety. **Penalties** (1 RCNY В§ 102-01): missed GPS2 filing вҖ” **$10,000** initial fine + **$10,000 additional per year** of continuing noncompliance; Cycle 2 and beyond non-certification вҖ” **$1,500** for 1-3 family residential (multifamily R-1/R-2 buildings) / **$5,000** for all other buildings (commercial/mixed-use). **Eleven-mode severity ladder**: NotApplicable, ExemptOneOrTwoFamilyR3, CompliantInspectionFiledOnTimeNoUnsafeConditions, CompliantInspectionFiledWithCorrectionsCertifiedWithin120Days, CompliantInspectionFiledWithCorrectionExtension180Days, ViolationLmpNotLicensedMasterPlumber, ViolationGps1NotDeliveredWithin30Days, ViolationGps2NotFiledWithin60Days, ViolationCorrectionsNotCertifiedWithin120Or180Days, ViolationUnsafeConditionGasNotShutOff, ViolationCommunityDistrictDeadlineMissed. **Four occupancy groups**: R3OneOrTwoFamily, R1Multifamily, R2Multifamily, OtherCommercial. Mounted at `POST /api/rental/rental-gas-piping-inspection-local-law-152`. **Twenty-eight tests pin**: **District 4 compliant inspection no unsafe** (baseline), **R-3 one- or two-family exempt**, **District 1 assigned year 2024**, **District 2 assigned year 2025**, **District 11 assigned year 2027** (full district-mapping verification across all four schedule groups), **missed district deadline no inspection penalty** ($10K initial + $10K Г— 2 years = $30K), **non-LMP inspector violation**, **unsafe condition no gas shutoff violation** (life-safety branch), **unsafe condition WITH shutoff advised proceeds** (compliant despite hazard if proper LMP-shutoff protocol followed), **GPS1 not delivered within 30 days violation**, **GPS2 not filed within 60 days violation** ($10K initial), **GPS2 not filed 2 years penalty $30,000** ($10K initial + $10K Г— 2), **corrections within 120 days compliant**, **corrections at exactly 120 days compliant** (statutory вүӨ reading), **corrections 121 days without extension violation** (boundary), **corrections 180 days with extension compliant**, **corrections 181 days with extension violation** (extension boundary), **Cycle 2 residential uncorrected penalty $1,500** (R-1 multifamily), **Cycle 2 commercial uncorrected penalty $5,000** (OtherCommercial), **citations pin LL 152 + NYC Admin Code В§ 28-318 + 1 RCNY В§ 103-10 + В§ 102-01**, **citations pin East Harlem explosion 2014 + 8 fatalities** (precipitating incident attribution), **constant pins LL 152 4-year cycle**, **constant pins GPS1 30-day delivery**, **constant pins GPS2 60-day filing**, **constant pins 120-day correction**, **constant pins 180-day correction with extension**, **constant pins $10,000 initial penalty**, **constant pins East Harlem 8 fatalities**. Trader-landlord critical because NYC LL 152 affects EVERY trader-landlord with NYC multifamily or commercial inventory outside of single-family stock вҖ” and the community-district rotation means trader-landlords across all five boroughs face inspection-deadline math that depends on the specific district of each property. Older NYC building stock (pre-1968 buildings particularly) often has corroded cast-iron and threaded-steel gas piping that fails LL 152 inspection, triggering expensive remediation work ($15K-$50K+ per building for piping replacement). The unsafe-condition branch is the most consequential вҖ” gas-shutoff protocol failures can produce immediate criminal referral if a fatality results (as happened in the East Harlem 2014 case where pre-LL 152 inspection lapses led to manslaughter charges against the contractor). Sibling cluster: `rental_facade_inspection_fisp_local_law_11` (iter 583 вҖ” NYC LL 11 facade 5-year cycle, structurally parallel), `rental_natural_gas_leak_response` (NYC tenant gas-leak response protocol вҖ” invoked when LL 152 inspection reveals leaks), `rental_carbon_monoxide_detector` (combustion-product detection upstream of LL 152 piping safety), `rental_gas_appliance_ban` (all-electric mandate вҖ” LL 152 inspection becomes moot for fully-decommissioned gas systems but applies until the gas service is terminated), `rental_boiler_inspection_compliance` (parallel boiler-inspection regime that interacts with gas-supply inspection), `rental_emergency_action_plan_high_rise` (NYC LL 26 high-rise EAP вҖ” gas emergencies invoke EAP protocols).

`traderview-expense::rental_facade_inspection_fisp_local_law_11` is the **NYC Facade Inspection Safety Program (FISP) / Local Law 11 of 1998 compliance module** (iter 583) вҖ” direct NYC-specific companion to `rental_soft_story_seismic_retrofit` (iter 581 CA wood-frame retrofit вҖ” different building-envelope regime in different jurisdiction), `rental_emergency_action_plan_high_rise` (NYC LL 26 FDNY EAP), `rental_balcony_inspection_seismic_safety` (CA SB 721 EEE inspection вҖ” distinct building-element regime), `rental_elevator_safety_inspection`. **Statutory chain**: **Local Law 10 of 1980** was the original NYC facade inspection law enacted after the May 16, 1979 facade-collapse fatality at 115 Madison Avenue (Barnard College freshman Grace Gold killed by falling masonry вҖ” the precipitating incident that established NYC facade-inspection regime); **Local Law 11 of 1998** is the current Facade Inspection Safety Program, codified at **1 RCNY 103-04**, expanding scope to include appurtenances and parapets; **Local Law 38 of 2007** instituted the 5-year inspection cycle (formerly variable subcycles). **Operative rule** (1 RCNY 103-04): exterior walls of ALL buildings GREATER THAN 6 stories (i.e., 7+ stories) must be inspected by a **Qualified Exterior Wall Inspector (QEWI)** every 5 years and a technical facade report filed with NYC Department of Buildings (DOB). Single-family homes and buildings 6 stories or fewer are exempt. **QEWI qualification** under 1 RCNY В§ 103-04(c)(1): must be a New York State licensed Professional Engineer (PE) or Registered Architect (RA) with at least **7 years** of relevant experience specifically with facades over 6 stories, AND must complete a separate DOB approval process before filing FISP reports. **Three reporting classifications**: **SAFE** (no work required; clean compliance); **SWARMP** (Safe With a Repair and Maintenance Program вҖ” facade safe at time of inspection but requires repairs/maintenance within the next 5-year cycle to prevent deterioration into an unsafe condition); **UNSAFE** (must be repaired within **90 days**; QEWI must notify owner and recommend sidewalk shed or other public-protection measures; owner must IMMEDIATELY erect sidewalk shed pending repair). **Current cycle**: **Cycle 10** commenced February 21, 2025 вҖ” runs through February 21, 2030 with subcycles 10A/10B/10C (each covering 2-year filing windows). **Penalty schedule** (DOB administrative tribunal): **$1,000 per month** late initial report filing; **$5,000 per year** failure to file; **$2,000** per uncorrected SWARMP condition not corrected before next cycle; additional civil penalties under NYC Admin Code В§ 28-301.1.1 + potential criminal referral if a fatality results. Enforcement intensified dramatically after the **December 17, 2019 Erica Tishman fatality** at 729 7th Avenue, where Tishman was killed by falling masonry from an unsafe facade. **Ten-mode severity ladder**: NotApplicable, ExemptBuildingSixStoriesOrFewer, CompliantSafeFiledOnTime, CompliantSwarmpFiledOnTimeRepairsInProgress, ViolationSwarmpRepairsNotMadeByNextCycle, ViolationUnsafeNotRepairedWithin90Days, ViolationUnsafeNoSidewalkShedPublicSafetyExposure, ViolationLateInitialReportFiling, ViolationFailureToFile, ViolationQewiNotQualified. **Four report classifications**: Safe, Swarmp, Unsafe, NotFiled. Mounted at `POST /api/rental/rental-facade-inspection-fisp-local-law-11`. **Twenty-eight tests pin**: **10-story SAFE filed on time compliant** (baseline), **6-story building exempt at threshold** (statutory вүӨ 6 reading), **7-story building NOT exempt** (boundary), **SWARMP with repairs in progress compliant**, **SWARMP repairs not completed violation** ($2K penalty per uncorrected condition), **UNSAFE no sidewalk shed immediate violation** (the most serious enforcement branch), **UNSAFE with shed within 90 days conditional compliance**, **UNSAFE with shed past 90 days violation**, **UNSAFE 90-day boundary at exactly 90 compliant** (вүӨ reading), **UNSAFE 91 days just over threshold violation**, **late initial report 3 months penalty $3,000** ($1K Г— 3 = $300,000 cents), **failure to file 2 years penalty $10,000** ($5K Г— 2), **NotFiled classification per se violation**, **QEWI not PE or RA violation**, **QEWI under 7-year experience violation**, **QEWI exactly 7 years qualifies** (statutory вүҘ reading), **QEWI no DOB approval violation**, **citations pin LL 11/1998 + LL 10/1980 + LL 38/2007**, **citations pin 1 RCNY В§ 103-04 + NYC Admin Code В§ 28-301.1.1**, **citations pin Grace Gold + Erica Tishman incidents** (the precipitating events behind enactment and enforcement intensification), **constant pins 6-story threshold**, **constant pins 5-year cycle**, **constant pins 90-day unsafe repair window**, **constant pins QEWI 7-year experience**, **constant pins $1,000/month late filing**, **constant pins $5,000/year failure to file**, **constant pins $2,000 SWARMP uncorrected**, **very large months late saturating no overflow** (u32::MAX Г— $1,000 Г— 100 cents defense). Trader-landlord critical because NYC FISP non-compliance triggers a four-pronged exposure: (1) DOB civil penalties accruing monthly at $1,000+/month, (2) DOB unsafe-building violation with potential vacate order and Buildings Bureau scaffold-erection contractor referral at $50K+/month, (3) tenant habitability claims with rent escrow + Cal. Civ. Code В§ 1942.4-equivalent rent-withholding rights, (4) liability exposure on facade-failure injury claims вҖ” the Erica Tishman fatality produced an $86M wrongful-death settlement against the building owner. The 7-story threshold is the single most consequential building-class boundary in the NYC trader-landlord operational landscape: every 7+-story building requires permanent QEWI engagement, $25K-$100K per inspection cycle for the inspection itself + facade-repair costs that can run $500K-$5M+ for major repairs. Sibling cluster: `rental_soft_story_seismic_retrofit` (iter 581 вҖ” different jurisdiction + different building-envelope regime), `rental_emergency_action_plan_high_rise` (NYC LL 26 вҖ” adjacent NYC high-rise compliance regime), `rental_balcony_inspection_seismic_safety` (CA SB 721 EEE inspection вҖ” different building-element regime), `rental_elevator_safety_inspection` (parallel NYC LL 196/2017 elevator inspection cycle).

`traderview-expense::rental_soft_story_seismic_retrofit` is the **California wood-frame soft-story mandatory seismic retrofit compliance module** (iter 581) вҖ” sibling to `rental_balcony_inspection_seismic_safety` (SB 721 EEE inspection regime вҖ” different seismic compliance vector covering balconies/decks/walkways), `rental_emergency_action_plan_high_rise` (NYC LL 26 / FDNY EAP), `rental_elevator_safety_inspection`. Two major California jurisdictions pinned with statutory citations and operative deadlines + property-class triggers. **Los Angeles вҖ” Ordinance 183893 (2015)** "Earthquake Hazard Reduction in Existing Wood-Frame Buildings with Soft, Weak, or Open-Front Walls" вҖ” the most ambitious mandatory seismic retrofit program in the United States, covering an estimated **13,500 soft-story buildings** citywide. **Priority 1 buildings** (3+ stories with ground-floor commercial occupancy вҖ” highest risk because combining structural vulnerability with high-foot-traffic ground floor) had a compliance deadline of **April 2024**. **Priority 2 buildings** (remaining smaller wood-frame structures under 16 units, typically 2-3 stories with ground-floor parking / tuck-under garages вҖ” the classic LA "dingbat" apartment building) have a deadline of **April 2026**. The ordinance requires property owners to submit retrofit plans, obtain building permits, complete construction, AND pass final inspection by the deadline вҖ” filing an application alone is NOT sufficient compliance. **San Francisco вҖ” Chapter 34B + Chapter 4D + Chapter 5E** of the SF Building Code (signed April 18, 2013; operative June 17, 2013): covers wood-frame buildings of 3+ stories (or 2 stories over basement), containing 5+ dwelling units, built before January 1, 1978 (pre-1978 cutoff because modern California seismic code adoption post-dates 1978). **Tier 1** (highest risk): Sept 15, 2017. **Tier 2**: Sept 15, 2018. **Tier 3**: Sept 15, 2019. **Tier 4**: Sept 15, 2020. As of September 15, 2021, ALL deadlines for ALL tiers have PASSED вҖ” non-compliance generates ongoing daily civil-penalty exposure with NO further extension available. **Ten-mode severity ladder**: NotApplicable, ExemptBuildingNotWoodFrame (steel/concrete/masonry construction outside scope), ExemptStoryCountBelowJurisdictionThreshold, ExemptUnitCountBelowJurisdictionThreshold, ExemptPostThresholdYearConstruction (SF post-1978 carve-out), CompliantRetrofitCompleteAndCertified, CompliantRetrofitInProgressBeforeDeadline, ViolationDeadlinePastedNoRetrofitCommenced, ViolationDeadlinePastedRetrofitIncomplete, DefaultJurisdictionNoMandatoryRegime. **Five retrofit-status states**: NotStarted, PlansFiled, PermitsObtained, ConstructionUnderway, CompleteAndCertified. Mounted at `POST /api/rental/rental-soft-story-seismic-retrofit`. **Twenty-five tests pin**: **LA Priority 2 complete certified compliant** (12-unit pre-1978 wood-frame), **LA Priority 1 ground-floor commercial 3+ stories deadline 2024** (highest-risk class), **LA Priority 1 deadline 2024 not started in 2026 is violation** (2 years past), **LA Priority 2 deadline 2026 in progress in 2025 compliant**, **LA Priority 2 in 2027 not started violation**, **LA Priority 2 in 2027 incomplete violation** (permits-obtained-only insufficient), **LA not wood-frame exempt**, **LA single-story below threshold exempt**, **SF Chapter 34B pre-1978 wood 3 stories 5 units complete compliant** (full SF triggering combination), **SF post-1978 building exempt** (1990 build), **SF below 5 units exempt** (4 units), **SF below 3 stories exempt** (2 stories), **SF not started in 2026 violation 5 years past deadline** (2021 final вҶ’ 2026 = 5 years past), **SF incomplete in 2026 violation**, **default jurisdiction no mandatory regime**, **citations pin LA Ordinance 183893 + SF Chapter 34B**, **citations pin LADBS + ESIP programs**, **constant pins LA ordinance 183893**, **constant pins LA Priority 1 deadline 2024**, **constant pins LA Priority 2 deadline 2026**, **constant pins LA 13,500 buildings estimate**, **constant pins SF Chapter 34B operative 2013**, **constant pins SF pre-1978 threshold**, **constant pins SF 5-unit minimum**, **constant pins SF 2021 final deadline**. Trader-landlord critical because California's soft-story retrofit costs run $80,000-$250,000+ per building (concentrated in the LA "dingbat" apartment inventory and SF Victorian/Edwardian multifamily stock), insurance non-renewal is common for non-retrofitted buildings, LADBS / SF DBI enforcement is active with referral to City Attorney for prosecution, and TENANTS in non-retrofitted buildings have civil-action standing under habitability warranty + Cal. Civ. Code В§ 1942.4 to compel retrofit at landlord expense. The post-deadline daily penalty exposure compounds rapidly вҖ” a $100/day Priority 2 violation that ran from April 2026 to April 2028 produces $73,000 in civil penalty alone before any tenant or insurance liability. Sibling cluster: `rental_balcony_inspection_seismic_safety` (SB 721 + SB 326 EEE inspection вҖ” distinct seismic compliance domain), `rental_property_registration` (most jurisdictions require new owner registration within 30 days), `rental_just_cause_eviction` (iter 573 вҖ” LA RSO + SF rent ordinance overlay tenant protections that LIMIT landlord's ability to vacate units for retrofit construction), `rental_tenant_relocation_assistance` (CA AB 1110 + LA RSO + SF Rent Ordinance impose tenant-relocation-payment obligations during retrofit construction).

`traderview-expense::rental_eviction_record_sealing_screening` is the **multi-jurisdiction eviction-record sealing and tenant-screening compliance module** (iter 579) вҖ” direct companion to `adverse_action_notice` (FCRA В§ 615 federal adverse-action notice when relying on consumer report), `rental_application_denial_disclosure` (state-specific written-denial regimes), `rental_tenant_criminal_background_screening` (parallel criminal-record sealing regime that overlays the eviction-record analysis for screening). Five major state regimes pinned with statutory citations and the operative sealing or lookback-period rule. (1) **CA AB 2819 (Chiu, 2016) вҖ” Cal. Code Civ. Proc. В§ 1161.2** (effective January 1, 2017): limited-jurisdiction (under $35,000) unlawful-detainer court records are automatically masked from public access for 60 days after complaint filing; records become **PERMANENTLY sealed unless the landlord prevails within 60 days** of filing the eviction complaint. Tenant-screening reports cannot include sealed records. (2) **WA SB 5160 (2021) вҖ” RCW 59.18.367**: 3-year reporting lookback limit вҖ” tenant-screening companies cannot report eviction records older than 3 years from filing date. (3) **NY RPAPL В§ 745(2)(c)(iv)**: state-of-art tenant-protection floor; tenant-screening services cannot consider housing-court records older than 5 years for adverse decisions. (4) **IL HB 1561 (2022) вҖ” 735 ILCS 5/9-121.5**: Cook County pilot court-order sealing with defendant-friendly presumption (subject to renewal). (5) **MN Minn. Stat. В§ 484.014**: automatic 3-year expungement when landlord did not prevail; discretionary court-order expungement in other cases. **Federal floor**: FCRA 15 U.S.C. В§ 1681c imposes a 7-year reporting cap nationally for civil judgments вҖ” applies as default in jurisdictions without their own sealing regime. **Seven-mode severity ladder**: NotApplicable, CompliantTenantScreeningExcludesSealedEvictionRecord, CompliantMaskedRecordNotReportedAsLandlordPrevailedWithin60Days, ViolationReliedOnSealedEvictionRecordForDenial, ViolationReliedOnEvictionRecordBeyondJurisdictionLookback, ViolationReliedOnAutomaticallyExpungedRecord (MN-specific), DefaultJurisdictionNoSealingRegime. **Five eviction-outcome states**: LandlordPrevailedWithin60Days, LandlordPrevailedAfter60Days, TenantPrevailed, SettledOrDismissed, PendingNoOutcomeYet. Mounted at `POST /api/rental/rental-eviction-record-sealing-screening`. **Twenty-three tests pin**: **CA tenant prevailed record permanently sealed**, **CA landlord prevailed within 60 days record unmasked** (the critical CA timing branch), **CA landlord prevailed after 60 days record permanently sealed**, **CA settled or dismissed record permanently sealed**, **CA pending within 60 days record still masked**, **CA pending past 60 days record now sealed** (61-day boundary), **CA landlord relied on sealed record is violation**, **WA record within 3-year lookback reportable** (2 years OK), **WA record beyond 3-year lookback violation** (4 years), **WA 3-year boundary at exactly 3 years is beyond** (statutory вүҘ reading), **NY 5-year lookback record within window reportable** (4 years), **NY 5-year lookback record beyond 5 years violation** (6 years), **MN 3-year automatic expungement when landlord did not prevail** (settled-or-dismissed branch вҖ” MN-specific severity), **MN 3-year no expungement when landlord prevailed**, **default jurisdiction no regime falls through**, **citations pin all five state regimes** (AB 2819 + RCW 59.18.367 + RPAPL В§ 745 + 735 ILCS 5/9-121.5 + В§ 484.014), **citations pin FCRA federal floor** (В§ 1681c), **constant pins CA 60-day masking window**, **constant pins WA 3-year lookback**, **constant pins NY 5-year lookback**, **constant pins MN 3-year automatic expungement**, **constant pins CA AB 2819 effective 2017**, **CA landlord did not rely on sealed record compliant**. Trader-landlord critical because tenant-screening violations attach at TWO distinct moments: (1) when the CRA reports a sealed record (CRA primary liability under В§ 1681c) and (2) when the landlord ACTS on a sealed record (landlord secondary liability under state statute + FCRA В§ 1681m). A trader-landlord receiving a tenant-screening report that mistakenly includes a sealed eviction must NOT consider it for adverse action even if it appears on the report вҖ” the burden falls on the landlord to verify sealing status, and reliance on improperly-disclosed sealed records produces statutory damages of $1,000-$25,000 per violation plus attorneys' fees + injunctive relief under state UDAP and federal FCRA private rights of action. Sibling cluster: `adverse_action_notice` (FCRA В§ 615 adverse-action notice вҖ” required even when landlord properly relies on a non-sealed record), `rental_application_denial_disclosure` (CA Civ. Code В§ 1950.6 + В§ 1786.40 ICRAA state regime), `rental_tenant_criminal_background_screening` (criminal-record sealing parallel that produces independent screening-compliance analysis), `rental_source_of_income_discrimination` (FEHA + parallel state protection that can convert eviction-record-based denial into source-of-income discrimination if used as pretext).

`traderview-expense::rental_multilingual_lease_translation` is the **California Civil Code В§ 1632 multilingual lease translation compliance module** (iter 577) вҖ” direct California-specific companion to `rental_application_denial_disclosure` (state-specific written-denial regimes), `adverse_action_notice` (FCRA adverse action federal regime), `rental_source_of_income_discrimination` (related fair-housing regime). Operative rule under **CA Civ. Code В§ 1632(b)**: a person engaged in a trade or business who NEGOTIATES PRIMARILY in **Spanish, Chinese, Tagalog, Vietnamese, or Korean** вҖ” orally or in writing вҖ” must deliver to the other party, BEFORE execution of the lease, a translation of the contract in the negotiated language that includes a translation of every term and condition. **Five covered languages** (statutory bright line вҖ” other languages NOT covered): Spanish, Chinese, Tagalog, Vietnamese, Korean. **Residential coverage** under В§ 1632(b)(1)(A): residential leases or rental agreements LONGER THAN ONE MONTH.
