`traderview-expense::rental_natural_gas_leak_response` is the **multi-jurisdictional rental property natural gas leak detection, response, and landlord-duty compliance framework module** вҖ” when a tenant reports a natural-gas odor (rotten egg / sulfur smell from mercaptan odorant) or a methane-leak event occurs in or around the rental unit, what immediate response duties attach (evacuate + 911 + utility + do not ignite), what utility-side and landlord-side obligations interact under PHMSA 49 C.F.R. Part 192, and what failure-mode liabilities expose landlord after an explosion, fire, asphyxiation, or carbon-monoxide event? Distinct from siblings `rental_propane_tank_lease_disclosure` (iter 475 вҖ” LP-gas tank), `rental_carbon_monoxide_detector` (CO sensor), `rental_hardwired_smoke_alarm_responsibility` (iter 481 вҖ” smoke detection), `rental_chimney_fireplace_inspection_disclosure` (iter 471), `rental_fire_extinguisher_requirement` (iter 473), `rental_gas_appliance_ban` (electrification policy), `tenant_emotional_distress_damages` (IIED). Trader-landlord critical because (1) natural-gas explosions are among the highest-stakes premises-liability claims вҖ” Merrimack Valley 2018 aggregate settlements exceeded $1B; (2) PHMSA Final Rule (effective January 17, 2025) introduced mandatory ALDS (advanced leak detection) performance standards and graded repair timelines; (3) wrong response (operating switches, attempting to shut off gas) is the leading cause of post-leak explosion вҖ” landlord training of property managers is the single most important compliance task; (4) methane detectors cost $25-$100 per unit vs explosion liability exposure exceeding $10M+; (5) Massachusetts Merrimack Valley framework is the leading model for anticipated state-level reforms.

**Four-jurisdiction framework**:

| Jurisdiction | Source | Standard |
|--------------|--------|----------|
| **Federal/PHMSA (universal floor)** | 49 C.F.R. Part 192; В§ 192.625 (odorization); В§ 192.706 (leakage surveys); PHMSA Final Rule January 17, 2025 | Mandatory mercaptan odorization + 15-month leakage surveys + advanced leak detection performance standards + graded repair timelines |
| **Massachusetts** | 220 C.M.R. 100.00; 220 C.M.R. 101.00; M.G.L. c. 164 В§ 105A (post-Merrimack Valley 2018) | Post-Merrimack Valley gas safety regime; engage Eversource / National Grid / Columbia Gas emergency line immediately |
| **California** | CPUC G.O. 112-F (gas pipeline safety); G.O. 58-A (natural gas service standards); Cal. Pub. Util. Code В§ 451; Cal. Civ. Code В§ 1941.1 | Safe-and-adequate-service mandate + implied warranty of habitability per Green v. Superior Court, 10 Cal. 3d 616 (1974); engage PG&E / SoCalGas / SDG&E |
| **Default** | Hilder v. St. Peter, 478 A.2d 202 (Vt. 1984); state PUC consumer-protection rules | Common-law habitability + tort negligence + premises liability |

**Universal landlord IMMEDIATE-RESPONSE six-step protocol** on tenant gas-odor complaint:
1. **EVACUATE** all occupants from unit and building IMMEDIATELY
2. **Call 911** to dispatch emergency services
3. **Call utility emergency line** FROM SAFE LOCATION outside building вҖ” every major utility maintains 24/7 gas-leak hotline
4. **DO NOT operate** light switches, electronics, vehicle ignition, or cell phones inside building (any spark can ignite gas-air mixture)
5. **DO NOT enter** unit to investigate or shut off appliances
6. **Wait for utility + fire department** to clear scene before any re-entry

**Post-incident landlord obligations**:
1. Engage licensed plumber to inspect all gas appliances + connections before service restoration
2. Document utility's emergency-response report
3. Notify all tenants of incident + cause + remediation
4. Update lease addenda with gas-emergency procedures
5. Consider methane detector installation ($25-$100 per unit; emerging best practice, not yet mandatory in most states)

**Five universal failure-mode liabilities**:
1. **Failed to respond to tenant gas-odor complaint** вҶ’ tort negligence + wrongful death + IIED (`tenant_emotional_distress_damages` iter 453)
2. **Operated appliances after odor detected** (ignition source) вҶ’ explosion liability + criminal negligence
3. **Failed to evacuate tenants** вҶ’ bodily injury exposure + wrongful death + premises liability
4. **Failed to inspect gas appliances post-restoration** вҶ’ CO + leak recurrence + tenant injury
5. **Inherited unit with disabled methane detector or non-functioning alarms** вҶ’ emerging best-practice violation (mandatory detector requirements expected in MA + CA over next 3-5 years post-Merrimack-Valley)

**Merrimack Valley 2018 incident context**: explosion across Andover + Lawrence + North Andover killed 1 + injured 25 + displaced thousands; aggregate settlements exceeded $1B; led to M.G.L. c. 164 В§ 105A + nationwide PHMSA reform momentum culminating in January 17, 2025 Final Rule on advanced leak detection and repair.

`traderview-expense::rental_oil_tank_replacement_disclosure` is the **rental home heating oil tank disclosure and replacement liability module** (iter 493) вҖ” when must a Northeast trader-landlord disclose tank age/location/material to incoming tenants, replace bare-steel tanks crossing the 30-year presumptive failure threshold, and reserve for CERCLA strict-liability exposure on legacy 1940-1980s underground storage tanks? Trader-landlord operational concern: NJDEP UHOT (Unregulated Heating Oil Tank) Program governs residential tanks вүӨ2000 gal per N.J.A.C. 7:14B-1.6(a)(3); Massachusetts 527 CMR 9.00 + 310 CMR 80.00 (MassDEP eff. 2015-01-02); Maine 38 M.R.S. В§ 568-A requires written rental disclosure of age + location + capacity + last inspection. Sibling of `rental_propane_tank_lease_disclosure` (LP-gas analog), `rental_lead_pipe_disclosure` (legacy infrastructure), `rental_pesticide_application_notification` (chemical-exposure pattern).

**Ten-jurisdiction framework** вҖ” New Jersey (UHOT) vs Massachusetts (527 CMR + 310 CMR + M.G.L. ch. 21E) vs New York (6 NYCRR 613 + Navigation Law Art. 12) vs Connecticut (22a-449(d)-1) vs Rhode Island (250-RICR-140-25-1) vs Maine (38 M.R.S. В§ 568-A) vs New Hampshire (Env-Or 411) vs Vermont (10 V.S.A. ch. 159 + В§ 6615) vs Pennsylvania (25 Pa. Code ch. 245 + USTIF) vs Default (40 C.F.R. Part 280 + CERCLA petroleum exclusion).

**Five-mode severity ladder**: NotApplicable (no oil tank), CompliantDisclosed (FRP/cathodic-protected, all disclosures pinned), DisclosureRequiredNotProvided (missing age/location/material at lease signing вҖ” 50% annual rent at risk), ReplacementRequiredAgeFailure (bare-steel вүҘ30 years presumptive failure вҖ” 50% annual rent at risk + 90-day replacement window), ReplacementRequiredLeakDetected (visible corrosion or oil odor вҖ” full annual rent + 24-hour NJDEP/MassDEP notification), CerclaStrictLiabilityExposure (UST with known prior release вҖ” full annual rent + $50K-$100K reserve + Final RAW/Soil RAO disclosure), RescissionRiskFraudInInducement (known release undisclosed вҖ” Restatement (Second) of Contracts В§ 164(1) + full annual rent recovery).

`traderview-expense::rental_organic_waste_collection_disclosure` is the **rental property organic waste collection disclosure compliance module** вҖ” when must a trader-landlord provide organics collection bins, tenant education, and move-in disclosure before claiming compliance with state organic waste diversion mandates? Trader-landlord operational concern: SB 1383-covered multifamily owners face per-incident contamination fines + per-tenant education-failure penalties + state-jurisdiction enforcement. Distinct from siblings `rental_energy_benchmarking` (energy/GHG), `rental_water_submetering_disclosure` (water billing), `rental_gas_appliance_ban` (electrification).

**Four regimes** вҖ” California SB 1383 vs Vermont Universal Recycling Law vs Seattle SMC 21.36.082 vs Default:

**California вҖ” SB 1383 of 2016 (Short-Lived Climate Pollutant Reduction Strategy, eff. January 1, 2022) + 14 CCR В§В§ 18984-18984.13** вҖ” multifamily properties with **5+ units** must provide organics collection containers + **annual tenant education** + **new tenant information within 14 days** of occupancy. Containers must be properly labeled/color-coded (green for organics under В§ 18984.7). 75% organic waste diversion target by 2025; 20% surplus food edible-recovery target. CalRecycle imposes per-incident contamination fines via local jurisdictions.

**Vermont вҖ” Universal Recycling Law (Act 148 of 2012, fully eff. July 1, 2020; 10 V.S.A. В§ 6605k)** вҖ” **bans food scraps from landfill statewide**; landlords must provide opportunity for tenants to recycle/compost food waste; **applies to ALL properties regardless of unit count** (unlike CA + Seattle 5+ unit thresholds); civil penalties **$200 to $1,000 for first offense** under 10 V.S.A. В§ 8007; most stringent statewide ban among comparators.

**Seattle вҖ” Seattle Municipal Code Ch. 21.36.082 (eff. January 1, 2015)** вҖ” multifamily **5+ units** required to participate in compost service; **$50 contamination fine per pickup** when recyclables or food waste contaminate landfill bin; 1% maximum contamination threshold; fines compound per incident; enforced by Seattle Solid Waste Director through Seattle Public Utilities.

**Default вҖ” common-law warranty of habitability + state solid waste regulations + RCRA Subtitle D (42 USC В§ 6941 et seq.)** вҖ” no specific statewide organic waste mandate; local municipal ordinances may impose (NYC LL97 organic waste rules, Boston organic waste pilot, Portland composting requirements); verify local jurisdiction.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/rental-organic-waste-collection-disclosure`. Thirty-four tests pin: **CA 5-unit in scope compliant** (boundary); **CA 4-unit out of scope** (below 5+ unit threshold); **CA no bins violation** (SB 1383 + В§ 18984); **CA unlabeled containers violation** (В§ 18984.7 color-coded); **CA no annual education violation**; **CA 14-day new tenant boundary compliant**; **CA 15-day new tenant violation**; **VT clean compliant**; **VT no recycling opportunity violation** (В§ 6605k landfill ban); **VT penalty at minimum in range** ($200); **VT penalty at maximum in range** ($1,000); **VT penalty below minimum violation** ($199.99 fails); **VT penalty above maximum violation** ($1,000.01 fails); **VT applies to all properties regardless of unit count** (1-unit no-opportunity violation); **Seattle 5-unit in scope compliant**; **Seattle no bins violation** (SMC 21.36.082); **Seattle contamination fine per incident $50**; **Seattle contamination fine multiple incidents** (5 incidents = $250); **Seattle defensive contamination overflow saturates**; **default no violations regardless of inputs**; **citation pins CA / VT / Seattle / default authorities** (all four); **note pins CA January 1, 2022 effective date**; **note pins CA 75% diversion target by 2025**; **note pins VT Act 148 July 1, 2020 effective date**; **note pins Seattle $50 contamination fine**; **note pins default RCRA Subtitle D** (42 USC В§ 6941); **VT uniquely no unit threshold invariant** (CA 1-unit OK, VT 1-unit violation); **CA uniquely requires annual education invariant** (VT + Seattle no annual education); **Seattle uniquely per-pickup fine invariant** (CA + VT no per-pickup fine); **CA unit threshold truth table** (6-cell sweep вҖ” 1/3/4 out, 5/10/100 in); **multiple CA violations stack** (3 violations).

`traderview-expense::rental_propane_tank_lease_disclosure` is the **multi-jurisdictional rental property PROPANE (Liquefied Petroleum Gas / LP-Gas) TANK disclosure and compliance framework module** вҖ” when a landlord rents a property served by a propane tank (either leased from supplier or owned outright), what disclosure must be given to tenant, what NFPA 58 installation/clearance/venting standards apply, and what failure-mode liabilities expose landlord after a leak, fire, or carbon monoxide event? Distinct from siblings `rental_gas_appliance_ban` (electrification policy), `rental_chimney_fireplace_inspection_disclosure` (iter 471), `rental_carbon_monoxide_detector`, `rental_fire_extinguisher_requirement` (iter 473), `rental_underground_storage_tank_disclosure` (UST/LUST petroleum), `mid_tenancy_temporary_relocation`, `tenant_emotional_distress_damages` (IIED). Trader-landlord critical because (1) trader inheriting rural property often inherits leased-tank arrangement locked to specific supplier вҖ” disclosure to tenant of supplier identity and lease status is essential at lease execution; (2) NFPA 58 clearance violations from later-built additions to home create insurer-denial risk + tort negligence exposure; (3) trader cannot unilaterally switch suppliers on leased tank without paying removal/relocation fees ($500-$1500 typical); (4) tenant-paid fuel vs landlord-paid fuel allocation must be specified in lease; (5) cathodic protection failure on buried tank is leading cause of late-life leak claims; (6) propane stove + furnace + water heater combination concentrates CO/fire risk in single rural-property tenancy.

**Three-jurisdiction framework**:

| Jurisdiction | Source | Standard |
|--------------|--------|----------|
| **Massachusetts (MOST PRESCRIPTIVE)** | 248 C.M.R. 8.00 (amendments to NFPA 58 by Mass. Board of Fire Prevention Regulations); M.G.L. c. 148 В§ 9 (gas-fitter licensing); M.G.L. c. 142 В§ 1 (plumbing/gas authority) | State amendments tighten clearances, venting, and installer-licensing beyond NFPA 58 minimums |
| **New York** | 19 NYCRR (Department of State Uniform Code adopting NFPA 58 by reference) | NY courts hold property owners and service providers responsible for personal injury + property damage + environmental harm; insurance compliance condition of coverage |
| **Default** | NFPA 58 (Liquefied Petroleum Gas Code, 2024 edition); DOT 49 C.F.R. Part 173 (transport); 49 C.F.R. Part 192 (pipeline integrity); 13 VAC 5-52-580 (Virginia IFC Chapter 61); Hilder v. St. Peter, 478 A.2d 202 (Vt. 1984); Cal. Civ. Code В§ 1941.1 | Universal NFPA 58 floor + common-law habitability |

**Two ownership models**:
1. **LEASED TANK** (most common residential) вҖ” tank owned by propane supplier (Suburban Propane, AmeriGas, Ferrellgas, etc.); customer pays lease fee plus fuel; supplier retains right of refill access; **CUSTOMER MAY BE LOCKED TO ORIGINAL SUPPLIER** and prohibited from switching without paying tank removal/relocation fees; supplier typically responsible for NFPA 58 compliance + inspection
2. **OWNED TANK** вҖ” customer purchased tank outright; full freedom to switch suppliers + comparison shop fuel; OWNER (landlord or tenant per lease) responsible for NFPA 58 compliance + inspection + maintenance + replacement (typical tank lifespan 20-30 years)

**NFPA 58 (2024 edition) clearance requirements** (above-ground horizontal tanks):

| Tank Capacity | Building Clearance | Property-Line Clearance | Ignition-Source Clearance |
|---------------|--------------------|--------------------------|----------------------------|
| 0-125 gallon residential | **0 ft** (if ASME-spec) | n/a | **10 ft** from window/door/ignition |
| 125-500 gallon | **10 ft** | **25 ft** | 10 ft |
| 500-2000 gallon | 10 ft | **50 ft** | 10 ft |
| Underground | 10 ft + cathodic protection required | n/a | n/a |

**Five universal failure-mode liabilities**:
1. **Tank leak** вҶ’ fire/explosion + tort negligence + tenant emergency relocation duty
2. **Improper venting/back-draft** вҶ’ CO poisoning (cross-reference `rental_carbon_monoxide_detector`) + IIED (`tenant_emotional_distress_damages` iter 453)
3. **Frost-heave / settling dislocation** вҶ’ corrosion + leak risk; cathodic protection failure on buried tanks
4. **Tenant refill obstruction** вҶ’ supplier service interruption + tenant relocation duty
5. **Unauthorized supplier switch on leased tank** вҶ’ contract breach + tank removal costs ($500-$1500)

**Trader-landlord critical fact patterns**: (1) Inherited rural property with 320-gallon leased Suburban Propane tank вҖ” disclose supplier identity + lease terms + switching restrictions to tenant at lease execution; (2) NFPA 58 ignition-source clearance violated by tenant-installed AC condenser 6 ft from tank вҖ” landlord duty to remediate; (3) Massachusetts trader installing tank via unlicensed handyman вҖ” M.G.L. c. 148 В§ 9 violation + insurance denial; (4) New York trader-landlord with non-compliant tank вҖ” 19 NYCRR personal injury liability; (5) underground tank without cathodic protection installed in 1995 вҖ” corrosion-induced leak claim emerging 25+ years later; (6) leased-tank locked customer вҖ” tenant cannot comparison-shop suppliers, leading to tenant rent disputes over fuel-price markup; (7) leak event during tenancy вҖ” emergency relocation + tort negligence + wrongful death exposure.

**Six covering states modeled**: `Massachusetts`, `NewYork`, `Default` for jurisdiction; `LeasedFromSupplier`, `OwnedByLandlord`, `OwnedByTenant`, `None` for ownership; `AboveGround`, `Underground` for location.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/rental-propane-tank-lease-disclosure`. Thirty-three tests pin: **no tank not applicable**; **MA compliant baseline**; **leak event top severity** (tenant_emotional_distress_damages); **CO event top severity**; **building clearance violation under 10 feet** (tank > 125 gal); **building clearance exactly 10 feet compliant**; **small tank under 125 no building clearance violation** (ASME-spec 0-foot); **ignition clearance violation under 10 feet** (window/door); **property-line violation mid-tank under 25 feet** (125-500 gal); **property-line violation large tank under 50 feet** (500-2000 gal); **underground tank no cathodic protection violation**; **underground tank with cathodic protection compliant**; **supplier inspection overdue over 12 months** (12-month NFPA 58 interval); **supplier inspection exactly 12 months compliant**; **leased tank supplier not disclosed disclosure required** ($500-$1500 lease terms); **leased tank terms not disclosed disclosure required**; **owned tank no supplier disclosure needed**; **fuel payment not disclosed disclosure required**; **MA jurisdiction cites 248 C.M.R. 8.00 + M.G.L. c. 148 В§ 9 + c. 142 В§ 1**; **NY jurisdiction cites 19 NYCRR + personal injury + insurance compliance**; **Default jurisdiction common-law habitability** (DOT 49 C.F.R. Part 173 + 13 VAC 5-52-580 + Hilder + В§ 1941.1); **severity priority event > clearance > inspection > disclosure**; **severity clearance > inspection > disclosure**; **severity inspection > disclosure**; **citation pins all authorities** (NFPA 58 + DOT 49 C.F.R. Part 173 + 49 C.F.R. Part 192 + 248 C.M.R. 8.00 + M.G.L. c. 148 В§ 9 + c. 142 В§ 1 + 19 NYCRR + 13 VAC 5-52-580 + Hilder v. St. Peter + 478 A.2d 202 + В§ 1941.1); **note pins three-jurisdiction framework**; **note pins two ownership models** (Suburban/AmeriGas/Ferrellgas + 20-30 years); **note pins NFPA 58 clearance table** (0-125 / 125-500 / 500-2000 + cathodic); **note pins five failure modes**; **note pins companion modules**; **3-cell jurisdiction truth table**; **4-cell tank-ownership truth table**; **multiple clearance violations stack** (building + ignition + property line).

`traderview-expense::rental_property_registration` is the **mandatory landlord rental property registration with state / municipal agency module** вҖ” distinct from siblings `owner_identification` (disclosure of owner identity to tenant), `landlord_identification_disclosure` (party-identification at lease execution), and `tenant_rights_statement_disclosure` (annual statement distribution). This module addresses the AFFIRMATIVE REGISTRATION OBLIGATION the landlord owes to the STATE / MUNICIPAL agency before lawfully renting. Trader-landlord operational concern вҖ” failure to register may bar rent collection in court, bar eviction, expose to municipal fines, and trigger state Consumer Fraud Act treble damages.

**Three regimes**:

| Regime | Filing agency | Scoping rule | Consequence of non-filing |
|--------|---------------|----------------|----------------------------|
| New Jersey В§ 46:8-28 (3+ unit) | NJ DCA Bureau of Housing Inspection | Multiple dwellings (3+ units) | Cannot collect rent / evict + CFA treble damages |
| New Jersey В§ 46:8-28 (1-2 unit) | Municipal clerk | 1-unit OR 2-unit non-owner-occupied | Same as above |
| District of Columbia В§ 47-2851.03 | DC Basic Business License | All rental property | DC v. Hayes equitable bar вҖ” barred from collecting rent |
| Default | Varies | No statewide mandate | Municipal ordinances may impose |

**New Jersey вҖ” N.J.S.A. В§ 46:8-28 + В§ 46:8-28.5**. Most explicit statewide framework. Two-tier filing structure:
- **1-unit OR 2-unit non-owner-occupied premises** вҶ’ file with MUNICIPAL CLERK.
- **3+ unit "multiple dwellings"** (under Hotel and Multiple Dwelling Law) вҶ’ file with **Bureau of Housing Inspection, NJ Department of Community Affairs**.

Certificate contents under В§ 46:8-28: name + address of record owner; name + address of record owner of rental business if different; authorized agent for service of process; person designated to receive rental notices; bank holding security deposits. **Amended certificate within 20 DAYS** of any change. В§ 46:8-28.5 вҖ” owner-occupied 2-family premises may be exempt.

**Owner-occupancy exception scoping** вҖ” applies only to 1-2 unit buildings. Owner-occupied 3+ unit buildings remain in scope (still required to file with NJ DCA). Pinned by `nj_scoping_truth_table` (6-cell sweep across unit count Г— owner-occupancy) and `nj_3_unit_owner_occupied_still_in_scope`.

**District of Columbia вҖ” D.C. Code В§ 47-2851.03**. Basic Business License with Rental Housing endorsement REQUIRED before renting residential property. **DC v. Hayes equitable bar** вҖ” landlord without Basic Business License is BARRED from collecting rent in court. DC Rental Housing Commission supervises filing + renewal + civil penalties. Pinned by `dc_no_license_violates` and `dc_hayes_equitable_bar_note`.

**Default вҖ” varies; mostly municipal**. Most states have NO statewide rental-property-registration mandate. Municipal ordinances (Chicago RLTO, NYC MDL В§ 325 for buildings of 3+ units, MA Mass. Gen. Laws ch. 111 В§ 197A) may impose registration via municipal authority. Federal default вҖ” no obligation.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/rental-property-registration`. Twenty-eight tests pin: **NJ 3-unit routes to DCA**; **NJ 1-unit routes to municipal clerk**; **NJ 2-unit non-owner-occupied routes to municipal clerk**; **NJ 2-unit owner-occupied exempt** (В§ 46:8-28.5); **NJ 1-unit owner-occupied exempt**; **NJ 3-unit owner-occupied still in scope** (owner-occupancy exception only for 1-2 units); **NJ no registration filed violates** (В§ 46:8-28); **NJ 1-unit no filing routes violation to municipal clerk** (agency-specific error message); **NJ amended certificate within 20 days compliant**; **NJ amended certificate not filed within 20 days violates** (20-DAY amendment rule); **NJ no recent change no amendment violation**; **NJ certificate contents note present** (authorized agent for service of process); **NJ consequences note describes treble damages and eviction bar** (cannot enforce rent collection + CFA В§ 56:8-1); **NJ multiple dwelling law note describes 3+ unit**; **NJ citation pins В§В§ 46:8-28 + 46:8-28.5 + Hotel and Multiple Dwelling Law**; **DC compliant with Basic Business License**; **DC no license violates** (В§ 47-2851.03); **DC v. `traderview-expense::rental_property_tax_pass_through_disclosure` is the **rental property tax + MCI + IAI pass-through disclosure framework module** (iter 519) вҖ” covers when trader-landlord may pass through property-tax increases (reassessment + special assessments + Major Capital Improvement (MCI) recoveries + local-improvement-district fees) to tenants under state and local rent-control / rent-stabilization regimes. Distinct from siblings `rental_property_registration` (registration framework вҖ” distinct compliance pathway), `rental_hoa_disclosure_at_lease` (HOA fees framework), `rental_solar_panel_disclosure` (iter 495 PPA pass-through analog), `rental_in_unit_laundry_appliance_provision` (iter 501 appliance maintenance вҖ” distinct), `tenant_late_fee_cap` (late-fee framework вҖ” distinct charge), `rental_junk_fee_transparency` (junk-fee disclosure framework вҖ” distinct), `mid_tenancy_temporary_relocation` (when MCI work displaces tenant), `rental_balcony_inspection_seismic_safety` (iter 511 вҖ” EEE inspection cost may qualify as MCI for NYC rent-stabilized units). Trader-landlord critical because (1) **California Proposition 13** (1978, Cal. Const. Art. XIII A В§ 1) caps annual property tax increases at 2% UNTIL change-of-ownership or new construction triggers full reassessment to market value вҖ” non-commercial residential leases generally do NOT pass through Prop-13 reassessment to tenants absent express lease clause; (2) **California Costa-Hawkins Rental Housing Act** (Cal. Civ. Code В§ 1954.50-.535) exempts post-1995 construction + single-family / condominium units from local rent control; (3) **NYC Rent Stabilization Major Capital Improvement (MCI)** under 9 NYCRR В§ 2522.4 permits owner pass-through of qualified capital improvement amortized over 12 years; **HSTPA 2019 capped MCI annual pass-through at 2% of base rent + reduced amortization + 30-year removal from rent calendar**; (4) **NYC Individual Apartment Improvement (IAI)** capped at $15,000 total over 15-year amortization (HSTPA cap); (5) **San Francisco Rent Ordinance В§ 37** permits limited operating-cost pass-through after Rent Board petition; В§ 37.10B unauthorized impositions void; (6) Boston rent control was REPEALED by Massachusetts ballot initiative in 1994 (Mass. Gen. Laws ch. 40P) вҖ” common-law contract freedom permits any lease-authorized pass-through.

**Six unit classes**: Pre1995MultifamilyRentControlled, Post1995CostaHawkinsExempt (Costa-Hawkins exempt + AB 1482 capped), SingleFamilyOrCondo (Costa-Hawkins exempt), NycRentStabilized (MCI + IAI framework applies), NycMarketRate, NonRentControlled.

**Six pass-through types**: PropertyTaxReassessment, MajorCapitalImprovementMci (NYC + 12-year amortization), IndividualApartmentImprovementIai (NYC + $15K cap + 15-year amortization), SpecialAssessmentLid, OperatingCostIncreasePetition, NoPassThrough.

**Five-jurisdiction framework** вҖ” California (Cal. Const. Art. XIII A В§ 1 Proposition 13 enacted 1978 + Cal. Civ. Code В§ 1954.50-.535 Costa-Hawkins Rental Housing Act + AB 1482 statewide 5% + CPI cap for non-Costa-Hawkins units + local rent boards preempt landlord-tenant contract on reassessment pass-through for rent-controlled units) vs NYC (9 NYCRR В§ 2522.4 MCI + 9 NYCRR В§ 2526.1 overcharge refund + HSTPA 2019 2% MCI annual cap + 12-year amortization + 30-year removal + $15K IAI cap + 15-year IAI amortization + NYS DHCR enforcement + Emergency Tenant Protection Act В§ 11 treble damages) vs SF (SF Rent Ordinance В§ 37 + В§ 37.7 capital improvement requires Rent Board approval + В§ 37.10B unauthorized impositions void + SF Rent Board petition framework) vs Boston (Mass. Gen. Laws ch. 40P rent control REPEALED 1994 + common-law contract freedom + Mayor Wu rent-stabilization proposal pending state enabling legislation + M.G.L. ch. 186 В§ 14 quiet enjoyment for punitive pass-throughs) vs Default (state-specific rent control вҖ” Oregon SB 608 statewide cap + Maine + Minneapolis ballot rent control + NJ municipal rent control).

**Eight-mode severity ladder**: NotApplicable, CompliantPassThroughAuthorized, UnauthorizedPassThroughLeaseClauseMissing (50% rent at risk вҖ” common-law contract violation), MciExceeds2PctAnnualCapViolation (50% rent at risk вҖ” HSTPA 2% cap), IaiExceeds15KOver15YearCapViolation (50% rent at risk вҖ” $15K HSTPA cap), PropertyTaxPassThroughVoidNonCommercial (100% rent at risk вҖ” Cal. Civ. Code В§ 1947.13 statutory penalty), MciNotRegisteredWithRentBoardViolation (100% rent at risk вҖ” DHCR treble damages + attorney's fees), ChangeOfOwnershipReassessmentImproperlyPassed (100% rent at risk вҖ” Costa-Hawkins / local rent board preempt).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/rental-property-tax-pass-through-disclosure`. Twenty-six tests pin: **no pass-through not applicable**; **compliant MCI within 2% cap** ($480 within $720 cap on $36K rent); **MCI exceeds 2% cap violation** (HSTPA 2019); **MCI not registered violation** (DHCR + treble damages); **CA property tax pass-through without lease clause void** (В§ 1947.13); **CA pre-1995 rent-controlled change-of-ownership improper** (Costa-Hawkins + Berkeley + LA RSO + SF + Oakland); **IAI exceeds $15K cap violation**; **IAI within $15K cap compliant**; **no lease clause unauthorized pass-through**; **CA jurisdiction pins Prop 13 + Costa-Hawkins + AB 1482 + 1978 + 1995**; **NYC jurisdiction pins HSTPA 2019 + DHCR + 9 NYCRR В§ 2522.4 + 30-year removal**; **SF jurisdiction pins Rent Ordinance В§ 37 + В§ 37.7**; **Boston jurisdiction pins ch. 40P REPEALED 1994 + Mayor Wu**; **default jurisdiction pins Oregon SB 608 + Minneapolis**; **coordination note references rental_property_registration + rental_hoa_disclosure_at_lease + tenant_late_fee_cap + rental_junk_fee_transparency + rental_balcony_inspection_seismic_safety siblings**; **NYC MCI HSTPA annual cap pins 2%**; **NYC MCI amortization pins 12 years**; **NYC MCI removal post-HSTPA pins 30 years**; **NYC IAI max amortized pins $15,000**; **NYC IAI amortization pins 15 years**; **CA Prop 13 annual cap pins 2%**; **CA Prop 13 enacted year pins 1978**; **Costa-Hawkins year pins 1995**; **NYC HSTPA year pins 2019**; **citation branch for each jurisdiction** (5-cell sweep); **zero rent zero risk does not panic**.

`traderview-expense::rental_radiator_steam_heat_safety` is the **rental radiator and steam heat safety framework module** (iter 517) вҖ” covers NYC Int 1489-2017 / Local Law 79 of 2018 radiator cover installation right (90-day window for households with children twelve or younger upon WRITTEN tenant request) plus NYC Int 0925-2024 "Ben Z's Law" biennial radiator inspection requirement in households where children under 6 reside (named after infant Bencel "Ben Z" Yancanay who died December 2020 from steam radiator burns) plus state-specific habitability frameworks. Distinct from siblings `rental_chimney_fireplace_inspection_disclosure` (solid-fuel heating framework), `rental_pellet_stove_disclosure` (iter 499 pellet-fuel appliance), `rental_natural_gas_leak_response` (gas-system leak framework), `rental_oil_tank_replacement_disclosure` (iter 493 oil-heat fuel storage), `tenant_smart_thermostat_install_right` (iter 497 thermostatic control upgrade pathway), `heat_requirements` (general heat-temperature habitability), `mid_tenancy_temporary_relocation` (when unit unsafe pending cover installation), `tenant_emotional_distress_damages` (IIED claim for severe burn injury). Trader-landlord critical because (1) NYC steam radiators reach **250В°F at peak heating** per ASHRAE Handbook; severe contact burns occur in less than 2 seconds at this temperature per Mayo Clinic burn classification; (2) NYC Admin Code В§ 27-2076 amendment requires landlord installation within 90 days of WRITTEN tenant request вҖ” failure classified as HAZARD triggering tenant rent withholding plus repair-and-deduct plus constructive eviction; (3) HPD civil penalty up to **$500 per violation** per HPD penalty schedule; (4) NYC steam radiator burn injury litigation routinely exceeds **$500K-$2M settlement exposure** for permanent scarring or disfigurement of pediatric or elderly tenant; (5) Ben Z's Law biennial inspection requirement applies to apartments AND common areas where children under 6 reside; (6) cover must completely enclose top + sides + front with grill openings sized to prevent child finger insertion per ASTM F963-23 toy safety standard (< 1/2 inch).

**Five heating-system types**: SteamRadiator (250В°F peak вҖ” highest burn risk), HotWaterRadiator (~180В°F peak), ElectricBaseboard, ForcedAirCentral (no radiator вҖ” out of scope), NoRadiator.

**Four tenant compositions**: HouseholdWithChildUnder12 (Int 1489-2017 cover-request right attaches), HouseholdWithChildUnder6 (Ben Z's Law biennial inspection requirement attaches), HouseholdWithElderlyOrImpaired (heightened common-law negligence duty), StandardAdult (base requirements only).

**Four-jurisdiction framework** вҖ” NYC (Int 1489-2017 / Local Law 79 of 2018 amending NYC Admin Code В§ 27-2076 + Int 0925-2024 Ben Z's Law biennial inspection + HPD $500 penalty + Housing Maintenance Code hazard classification) vs Boston (M.G.L. ch. 186 В§ 14 quiet enjoyment + 105 CMR 410.180 State Sanitary Code radiator-temperature provisions + 527 CMR 1.00 fire prevention + common-law negligence per se for steam burn injury) vs Chicago (Chicago Municipal Code В§ 5-12-110 RLTO habitability + В§ 13-196-300 building maintenance + Department of Public Health Section 11-4-1700 child injury reporting) vs Default (common-law habitability + premises-liability negligence + ASTM F2779-19 portable radiator safety standard manufacturer baseline applied by analogy).

**Seven-mode severity ladder**: NotApplicable, CompliantCoverInstalledOrNoRequest, LandlordCoverInstallationOverdue90Day (100% rent at risk вҖ” Int 1489-2017 HAZARD classification), BenZLawBiennialInspectionOverdue (100% rent at risk вҖ” Int 0925-2024 violation), CoverMissingGrillOpeningChildSafetySpec (100% rent at risk вҖ” ASTM F963-23 1/2-inch finger-trap spec), SteamBurnInjuryHabitabilityBreach (100% rent at risk + $500K-$2M settlement exposure), UnregulatedRadiatorWithoutThermostatHazard (50% rent at risk вҖ” TRV upgrade $50-$150 per radiator with Honeywell HR92 or Drayton Wiser).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/rental-radiator-steam-heat-safety`. Twenty-six tests pin: **no radiator not applicable**; **forced air central not applicable**; **compliant cover installed baseline**; **NYC cover overdue after 90 days violation full rent at risk** (Int 1489-2017 + В§ 27-2076); **NYC cover request within 90 days compliant** (boundary); **Ben Z's Law biennial inspection overdue child under 6** (Int 0925-2024); **Ben Z's Law child under 12 not under 6 no inspection violation** (age threshold); **cover missing grill opening spec violation** (ASTM F963-23); **steam burn injury habitability breach full rent** (250В°F + Mayo Clinic + $500K-$2M); **steam radiator no thermostat hazard half rent** (TRV + Honeywell HR92 + smart thermostat cross-reference); **NYC jurisdiction pins Int 1489-2017 + Local Law 79 + Ben Z + Bencel + 2020-12 + В§ 27-2076**; **Boston jurisdiction pins M.G.L. ch. 186 В§ 14 + 105 CMR 410.180 + 527 CMR 1.00**; **Chicago jurisdiction pins Chicago Municipal Code В§ 5-12-110 + В§ 13-196-300**; **default jurisdiction pins ASTM F2779-19**; **coordination note references heat_requirements + rental_chimney_fireplace_inspection_disclosure + rental_pellet_stove_disclosure + tenant_smart_thermostat_install_right siblings**; **coordination note pinned in all 4 jurisdictions**; **NYC radiator cover install days pins 90**; **NYC biennial inspection pins 24 months**; **NYC HPD penalty pins $500**; **steam radiator peak temp pins 250В°F**; **Int 1489 local law year pins 2018**; **Ben Z death date pins 2020-12**; **citation branch for each jurisdiction** (4-cell sweep); **severity priority burn injury overrides cover status**; **zero rent zero risk does not panic**; **hot water radiator compliant no steam burn hazard** (180В°F peak вҖ” lower risk than steam).

`traderview-expense::rental_radon_mitigation_disclosure` is the **multi-jurisdictional rental property RADON DISCLOSURE and MITIGATION compliance framework module** вҖ” when a landlord rents a residential property, what radon-hazard disclosure must be provided to tenant at lease signing, what tenant testing rights attach, what mitigation obligations follow elevated test results, and what failure-mode liabilities expose landlord after a tenant develops lung cancer with multi-decade latency? **Radon is the #2 LEADING CAUSE OF LUNG CANCER in the United States per EPA**, killing approximately 21,000 Americans annually. Distinct from siblings `rental_lead_pipe_disclosure` (lead service lines), `rental_basement_water_intrusion_disclosure`, `rental_chimney_fireplace_inspection_disclosure` (iter 471 вҖ” chimney safety), `rental_carbon_monoxide_detector`, `rental_natural_gas_leak_response` (iter 485 вҖ” methane leak), `rental_underground_storage_tank_disclosure` (UST/LUST), `tenant_emotional_distress_damages`. Trader-landlord critical because (1) Illinois 765 ILCS 90 effective January 1, 2024 is the leading state-level radon disclosure regime; (2) tenant 90-day testing right combined with lease-termination right creates significant turnover risk for landlords who decline to mitigate; (3) Illinois Zone 1 counties (Cook, DuPage, Kane, Lake, McHenry, Will, Winnebago, and many central + southern IL counties) average ABOVE 4 pCi/L; (4) AARST-NRPP certified mitigation cost $800-$1,500 is trivial relative to $1M-$5M+ lung cancer litigation exposure; (5) Maine, Florida, New Jersey, Iowa, Minnesota have growing state-level radon disclosure regimes.

**Three-jurisdiction framework**:

| Jurisdiction | Source | Standard |
|--------------|--------|----------|
| **Illinois (MOST STRINGENT for tenants)** | Illinois Tenants Radon Protection Act, **765 ILCS 90/** (effective January 1, 2024) | Pre-lease IEMA-OHS radon pamphlet + known-hazard records + tenant 90-day testing window + 4 pCi/L action level + **tenant LEASE TERMINATION right** if landlord declines to mitigate |
| **Maine** | 10 M.R.S. В§ 1494-A et seq. | Landlord-rented residential properties must **test every 10 years** + provide written disclosure of results |
| **Default** | US EPA, A Citizen's Guide to Radon (EPA 402/K-09/001); EPA Map of Radon Zones; Hilder v. St. Peter, 478 A.2d 202 (Vt. 1984); Cal. Civ. Code В§ 1941.1 | EPA 4 pCi/L action level + Zone 1/2/3 classification + common-law habitability + state implied warranty |

**Radon hazard data per US EPA**:
1. Radon is the **#2 LEADING CAUSE OF LUNG CANCER** (after smoking); approximately **21,000 deaths annually**
2. **1 in 15 homes** nationally have elevated radon levels > 4 pCi/L
3. EPA recommends fixing homes at **4 pCi/L** or higher and considering fixes between **2-4 pCi/L**
4. Elevated radon levels can be reduced to < 4 pCi/L **95% of the time** via mitigation
5. Mitigation cost typically **$800-$1,500** for active soil depressurization (ASD) system

**Mitigation methodology** вҖ” AARST-NRPP certified contractor required for warranty coverage:
1. **Active Soil Depressurization (ASD)** вҖ” most common; PVC pipe with inline fan extracts radon-laden soil gas; $800-$1,500 typical install
2. **Sub-Slab Depressurization (SSD)** вҖ” variant for basements with poured concrete floor
3. **Sub-Membrane Depressurization** вҖ” for crawl spaces with polyethylene membrane
4. Sealing cracks in foundation slab + sump cover + pipe penetrations (passive only; rarely sufficient alone)
5. **Post-mitigation verification testing** required to confirm < 4 pCi/L after system operation

**EPA Map of Radon Zones**: Zone 1 (HIGHEST, predicted indoor radon average > 4 pCi/L вҖ” including Cook, DuPage, Kane, Lake, McHenry, Will, Winnebago and many central + southern IL counties); Zone 2 (moderate 2-4 pCi/L); Zone 3 (lowest < 2 pCi/L). Maine + Iowa + Minnesota + parts of MA + PA + WI + WV are predominantly Zone 1.

**Five universal failure-mode liabilities**:
1. **Failure to provide radon pamphlet at lease signing** вҶ’ Illinois 765 ILCS 90 statutory violation + tenant rescission claim + civil penalty
2. **Failure to disclose known elevated radon levels** вҶ’ common-law fraud + tort negligence + state consumer-protection statute + punitive damages exposure
3. **Decline to mitigate after tenant test > 4 pCi/L** вҶ’ Illinois 765 ILCS 90 tenant 90-day LEASE TERMINATION right + tenant relocation cost recovery
4. **Failure to engage AARST-NRPP certified contractor** вҶ’ ineffective remediation + recurring liability + insurance denial
5. **Lung cancer claim** (multi-decade latency) вҶ’ tort negligence + wrongful death + punitive damages + IIED (`tenant_emotional_distress_damages` iter 453); **settlements routinely exceed $1M-$5M**

**Illinois Tenants Radon Protection Act mechanics** (765 ILCS 90/): (1) Pre-lease: landlord must provide tenant the state IEMA-OHS approved Radon Pamphlet PLUS any records indicating a radon hazard in the dwelling. (2) Tenant 90-day testing window: tenant may conduct radon test at own expense within 90 days of lease commencement. (3) If tenant test result EXCEEDS 4 pCi/L IEMA-OHS recommended action level AND landlord elects NOT TO MITIGATE, tenant may terminate the lease. (4) Statute does NOT obligate landlord to conduct testing or mitigation at landlord cost вҖ” only to disclose pre-lease and respond to tenant test results.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/rental-radon-mitigation-disclosure`. Twenty-nine tests pin: **IL compliant baseline**; **lung cancer claim top severity** ($1M-$5M+); **IL pamphlet missing severity** (765 ILCS 90 + BEFORE lease is signed); **IL known hazard undisclosed severity** (fraud + tort negligence); **tenant test over 4 pCi/L landlord declines tenant termination right** (765 ILCS 90 + 90-day); **tenant test exactly 4 pCi/L no termination right** (statute requires "exceeds"); **tenant test over 4 pCi/L landlord elects mitigation with certified contractor**; **uncertified contractor severity** (AARST-NRPP + warranty void); **mitigation complete post-verification missing warning**; **Maine test missing severity** (10 M.R.S. В§ 1494-A + every 10 years); **Maine test completed but not disclosed**; **Maine test and disclosure complete compliant**; **Default jurisdiction common-law habitability** (Hilder + В§ 1941.1); **EPA Zone 1 high-risk action warning**; **EPA Zone 3 low-risk no special warning**; **severity priority lung cancer > uncertified > termination**; **severity uncertified > termination right**; **severity termination > known hazard**; **severity known hazard > pamphlet**; **citation pins all authorities** (765 ILCS 90/ + January 1, 2024 + 10 M.R.S. В§ 1494-A + 4 pCi/L + EPA Map of Radon Zones + Hilder + 478 A.2d 202 + Green v. Superior Court + 10 Cal. 3d 616 + В§ 1941.1 + AARST-NRPP); **note pins three-jurisdiction framework**; **note pins EPA radon data** (#2 lung cancer + 21,000 + 1 in 15 + 95%); **note pins mitigation methodology** (ASD + SSD + sub-membrane + $800-$1,500 + AARST-NRPP); **note pins five failure modes**; **note pins EPA zone classification** (Cook + DuPage + Lake + Maine/Iowa/Minnesota); **note pins companion modules**; **3-cell jurisdiction truth table**; **IL uniquely 765 ILCS 90 invariant**; **multiple violations stack in actions**.

`traderview-expense::rental_satellite_dish_installation_right` is the **tenant satellite dish / OTARD antenna installation right compliance module** вҖ” when may a trader-landlord restrict tenant installation of a satellite dish or over-the-air antenna for video programming reception? Trader-landlord operational concern: federal **FCC OTARD Rule (47 CFR В§ 1.4000, eff. October 1996)** PREEMPTS state law, local ordinances, building codes, HOA covenants, AND lease provisions that impair installation on areas under tenant's EXCLUSIVE USE OR CONTROL; per-violation FCC Petition for Declaratory Ruling exposure + tenant injunctive relief in federal or state court. Distinct from siblings `rental_broadband_mte_rules` (cable/broadband building access), `rental_carbon_monoxide_detector`, `tenant_data_privacy`.

**Federal preemption framework** вҖ” В§ 1.4000(d) preempts state statutes, local zoning codes, building codes, HOA covenants, condominium declarations, AND lease provisions; preemption applies retroactively to existing restrictions. Statutory authority: Telecommunications Act of 1996 В§ 207 + 47 USC В§ 303.

**В§ 1.4000(a)(1) Covered antennas** вҖ” three classes: (i) **Direct broadcast satellite (DBS) dish antennas 1 meter or less** in diameter (or **any size in Alaska**); (ii) **Broadband radio service (BRS, formerly MMDS) antennas 1 meter or less**; (iii) **TV broadcast antennas** (no size limit).

**В§ 1.4000(a)(2) Exclusive use or control gate** вҖ” preemption ONLY applies to installation on property within tenant's **EXCLUSIVE USE OR CONTROL** (balcony + patio + exclusive-use yard assigned to tenant). Landlord MAY prohibit installation on **COMMON or RESTRICTED areas** вҖ” exterior walls, roof, common-area parking, shared corridors. Critical doctrinal line between protected and unprotected installation locations.

**В§ 1.4000(a)(3) Permissible restrictions** вҖ” narrowly tailored restrictions permitted ONLY if NECESSARY to (1) accomplish a **clearly defined, legitimate safety objective** OR (2) **preserve a historic district** designated under federal, state, or local law; AND no more burdensome than necessary; AND landlord shows **reasonable factual basis** for restriction. Aesthetic preference + landlord exclusive-marketing/revenue-share agreements are NOT permissible (anti-competitive bundling preempted).

**В§ 1.4000(f) Cost-impairment doctrine** вҖ” restriction impairs installation if it (1) **unreasonably delays or prevents** installation, maintenance, or use; (2) **unreasonably increases cost**; OR (3) **precludes acceptable quality reception**.

**Tenant enforcement** вҖ” tenant may file **FCC Petition for Declaratory Ruling** challenging restriction OR pursue private action in federal or state court of competent jurisdiction. FCC declaratory ruling has res judicata effect on landlord.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/rental-satellite-dish-installation-right`. Thirty-two tests pin: **DBS dish balcony no restriction protected**; **BRS antenna balcony covered**; **TV broadcast antenna balcony covered**; **DBS dish over 1 meter outside Alaska NOT covered**; **DBS dish over 1 meter in Alaska covered**; **non-OTARD antenna not covered**; **installation on exterior wall not protected**; **installation on roof not protected**; **installation on common area not protected**; **tenant patio exclusive use protected**; **aesthetic preference restriction not permissible** (В§ 1.4000(d)); **landlord exclusive-marketing agreement not permissible** (anti-competitive bundling); **safety objective restriction with narrow tailoring permissible**; **safety objective restriction without narrow tailoring violation** (В§ 1.4000(a)(3)); **historic district preservation with narrow tailoring permissible**; **unreasonable delay engages impairment violation** (В§ 1.4000(f)); **unreasonable cost increase engages impairment**; **precludes acceptable quality engages impairment**; **citation pins all authorities** (В§ 1.4000(a)-(f) + OTARD + October 1996 + DA 96-1755 + В§ 207 + 47 USC В§ 303); **note pins three covered antenna classes** (DBS + BRS + TV broadcast); **note pins exclusive use or control doctrine** (balcony + common areas); **note pins two permissible restriction grounds** (safety + historic district); **note pins federal preemption scope** (state statutes + HOA covenants + lease provisions + retroactive); **note pins three-prong impairment test**; **note pins tenant enforcement Petition and court** (res judicata); **note pins statutory authority 1996 Telecom Act**; **antenna type truth table 6 cells** (3 covered + Alaska-size variant + 2 uncovered); **installation location truth table 6 cells** (3 exclusive + 3 common); **restriction basis truth table 6 cells** (None + safety with/without narrowness + historic + aesthetic + exclusive-marketing); **impairment only violation when preemption engaged invariant**; **DBS dish size threshold invariant Alaska vs other states**; **multiple violations stack** (2 violations).

`traderview-expense::rental_video_surveillance_retention` is the **multi-jurisdictional rental video surveillance footage retention period framework module** вҖ” when landlord installs video surveillance in common areas, how long may landlord retain footage, what disclosure rules apply, and what biometric/facial-recognition restrictions stack on top? Distinct from sibling `security_camera_disclosure` (disclosure to tenants), `tenant_smart_lock_biometric_consent` (smart-lock biometrics specifically), `tenant_data_privacy` (broader data-handling framework), `landlord_master_key_retention` (iter 459 master-key access disclosure). Trader-landlord critical because video surveillance + biometric facial-recognition entry systems are increasingly deployed in multi-unit buildings but expose landlord to (1) BIPA $1,000-$5,000 per-violation statutory damages in Illinois; (2) Tex. Bus. & Com. Code В§ 503.001 capture-without-consent claims; (3) CCPA notice-and-deletion requirements in California; (4) common-law invasion-of-privacy and trespass claims universally.

**Four-jurisdiction framework**:

| Jurisdiction | Source | Penalty Structure |
|--------------|--------|--------------------|
| **Illinois (BIPA)** | 740 ILCS 14/ вҖ” most stringent | **Private right of action under 740 ILCS 14/20: $1,000 per negligent + $5,000 per intentional/reckless violation + attorney fees**; Rosenbach v. Six Flags Entm't Corp., 129 N.E.3d 1197 (Ill. 2019) no-injury-in-fact rule |
| **Texas (CUBI)** | Tex. Bus. & Com. Code В§ 503.001 | Texas Attorney General enforcement only; **up to $25,000 per violation**; Texas SB 9 of 2024 strengthened for minors |
| **California (CCPA + CPRA)** | Cal. Civ. Code В§ 1798.100 et seq.; CPRA / Prop. 24 (2020) | Notice at collection + biometric SPI В§ 1798.140(c)(1) + deletion В§ 1798.105 + В§ 1798.150 breach private right of action $100-$750 per consumer + Cal. Civ. Code В§ 1708.5 intrusion |
| **Default** | Restatement (Second) of Torts В§ 652B intrusion upon seclusion | Universal common-law overlay |

**Illinois BIPA (740 ILCS 14/) most stringent regime вҖ” 8 elements**:
1. **В§ 15(a)** вҖ” publicly-available written policy with retention period and erasure procedures
2. **В§ 15(b)** вҖ” WRITTEN CONSENT before collection of biometric identifiers
3. **В§ 15(c)** вҖ” no sale, lease, trade, or profit from biometric data
4. **В§ 15(d)** вҖ” no disclosure without consent or court order
5. **В§ 15(e)** вҖ” reasonable storage standard
6. **3-year retention cap** вҖ” biometric data MUST be destroyed when original purpose met OR within 3 YEARS after person's last contact (whichever earlier)
7. **В§ 20 private right of action** вҖ” $1,000 per negligent + $5,000 per intentional/reckless + attorney fees
8. **Rosenbach v. Six Flags** вҖ” no injury-in-fact requirement; class-action exposure

**Universally prohibited surveillance areas**:
1. Restrooms or any high-privacy expectation area
2. Tenant unit interiors
3. Pool changing areas
4. Tenant-specific storage units
5. **HIDDEN CAMERAS** (camera not disclosed to tenant) вҖ” universal prohibition
6. **AUDIO RECORDING** without all-party consent вҖ” federal Wiretap Act 18 U.S.C. В§ 2510 + state two-party-consent statutes (Cal. Penal Code В§ 632 + Illinois Eavesdropping Act 720 ILCS 5/14-2 + MD Cts. & Jud. Proc. В§ 10-402)

**Video surveillance retention period best-practice framework (6 elements)**:
1. **COMMON AREA** surveillance permitted with NOTICE; retention 30-90 days industry standard
2. **UNIT EXTERIOR** surveillance permitted with notice; retention 30-90 days
3. **UNIT INTERIOR** surveillance generally PROHIBITED absent express tenant consent + legitimate purpose
4. **BIOMETRIC FACIAL-RECOGNITION ENTRY SYSTEMS** require BIPA-style consent in Illinois + CUBI consent in Texas + CCPA notice + deletion right in California
5. **ACTIVE-MONITORING vs PASSIVE-RECORDING** вҖ” active monitoring carries higher liability
6. **THIRD-PARTY DISCLOSURE** strict limitations on law-enforcement / court-order / sale

**Trader-landlord critical fact patterns**: (1) Illinois trader installs facial-recognition entry in 50-unit building without BIPA written consent вҖ” 50 tenants Г— $5,000 intentional = **$250,000 statutory damages + attorney fees** per Rosenbach class action exposure; (2) Texas trader captures tenant biometric without consent вҖ” Texas AG enforcement up to **$25,000 per violation**; (3) California trader collects sensitive biometric without В§ 1798.100(b) notice вҖ” В§ 1798.150 breach $100-$750 per consumer + class-action multiplier; (4) trader retains 18 months footage without business purpose вҖ” violates BIPA 3-year cap + CCPA business-necessity + GDPR data minimization; (5) trader installs cameras in tenant unit interior вҖ” PROHIBITED universally + intrusion-upon-seclusion under Restatement В§ 652B + IIED under tenant_emotional_distress_damages iter 453; (6) audio recording in common areas вҖ” Wiretap Act + Cal. Penal Code В§ 632 + Illinois Eavesdropping + MD В§ 10-402 require consent from all parties.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/rental-video-surveillance-retention`. Twenty-eight tests pin: **IL common area compliant baseline**; **unit interior prohibited** (В§ 652B + iter 453 IIED); **high-privacy area prohibited**; **hidden camera universally prohibited**; **BIPA biometric without consent violation** (50 tenants Г— $1K = $50K negligent); **BIPA intentional violation $5,000 per** ($250K for 50); **CCPA no notice violation** ($75K Г— 100 tenants = $7.5K breach); **Texas biometric no consent violation**; **audio recording two-party consent required** (Wiretap Act + В§ 632 + 720 ILCS); **IL retention within 3-year cap compliant** (1,095 days); **IL retention over 3-year cap violation** (1,200 days); **CA retention over 90 days violation**; **TX retention over 90 days violation**; **BIPA sale of biometric prohibited** (В§ 15(c) + В§ 503.001); **statutory damages exposure summary** (Rosenbach); **jurisdiction truth table four cells**; **IL uniquely 3-year cap invariant**; **citation pins all authorities** (740 ILCS 14/ + В§ 15(a)/(b)/(c)/(d)/(e) + В§ 20 + Rosenbach + Tex. Bus. & Com. Code В§ 503.001 + Texas SB 9 of 2024 + Cal. Civ.
