**SB 721 unit-count threshold** вҖ” applies only to multifamily rental with **3 or more units**. Boundary tests pin 2 units вҶ’ no inspection required; 3 units вҶ’ inspection required. Pinned by `sb721_2_units_no_inspection_required`, `sb721_3_units_boundary_inspection_required`, and `sb721_no_eee_above_6_feet_no_inspection_required` (no EEE вҶ’ no inspection regardless of unit count).

**SB 326 uniquely excludes contractors** вҖ” SB 326 limits qualified inspectors to **licensed architect** or **licensed civil/structural engineer** ONLY. A/B/C-5 contractors and certified building inspectors are NOT qualified under SB 326 (they ARE qualified under SB 721). Pinned by `sb326_contractor_disqualified`, `sb326_certified_inspector_disqualified`, `sb326_uniquely_excludes_contractors_invariant` (same A/B/C-5 contractor qualifies in SB 721 but not SB 326), `inspector_qualification_truth_table_sb721` (7-qualification sweep: 4 qualify), and `inspector_qualification_truth_table_sb326` (7-qualification sweep: 2 qualify).

**Inspector independence requirement (both regimes)** вҖ” inspector CANNOT be a local government employee AND CANNOT be the entity performing repairs. Pinned by `sb721_local_gov_employee_disqualified` and `sb721_entity_performing_repairs_disqualified`.

**SB 721 6-year vs SB 326 9-year recurring cycle invariant** вҖ” SB 721 multifamily rental recurring inspection is shorter (every 6 years vs SB 326's 9 years), reflecting higher renter-population safety priority vs condo-owner-occupancy. Pinned by `sb721_uniquely_6_year_sb326_uniquely_9_year_invariant` and `three_regimes_routed_correctly`.

**SB 326 applies to ALL Davis-Stirling condos** вҖ” no unit-count threshold (unlike SB 721's 3-unit minimum). Pinned by `sb326_no_unit_count_requirement_applies_to_all_condos` (unit_count=1 still triggers).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/balcony-inspection`. Thirty-four tests pin: **SB 721 compliant passes**; **SB 721 2 units no inspection required**; **SB 721 3 units boundary inspection required**; **SB 721 no EEE above 6 feet no inspection required**; **SB 721 missed deadline violates** (January 1, 2026 + AB 2579); **SB 721 unqualified inspector violates**; **SB 721 local gov employee disqualified**; **SB 721 entity performing repairs disqualified**; **SB 721 missing 15% sampling violates**; **SB 721 121-day repair window violation**; **SB 721 120-day repair window compliant** (boundary); **SB 721 contractor A/B/C-5 qualifies**; **SB 721 certified inspector qualifies**; **SB 721 civil engineer qualifies**; **SB 721 citation pins authorities**; **SB 326 compliant passes**; **SB 326 contractor disqualified** (В§ 5551(b) ONLY); **SB 326 no unit count requirement applies to all condos**; **SB 326 civil engineer qualifies**; **SB 326 certified inspector disqualified**; **SB 326 missed deadline violates** (January 1, 2025); **SB 326 citation pins Civ. Code**; **default no inspection required**; **default citation pins premises liability**; **three regimes routed correctly**; **SB 721 uniquely 6-year SB 326 uniquely 9-year invariant**; **SB 326 uniquely excludes contractors invariant**; **inspector qualification truth table SB 721** (7-qualification sweep: 4 qualify); **inspector qualification truth table SB 326** (7-qualification sweep: 2 qualify); **SB 721 note pins January 2026 deadline and AB 2579**; **SB 721 note pins 15% and 120-day repair**; **SB 326 note pins January 2025 deadline and 9-year cycle**; **SB 721 stacks 4 violations when all 4 failures**; **SB 721 repairs completed short-circuits 120-day violation**.

`traderview-expense::bedbug_disclosure` is the **state bedbug disclosure + inspection-duty compliance table** вҖ” sibling to `heat_requirements`, `foreclosure_tenant_rights`, `lead_disclosure`, `detector_requirements`, `soi_protection`, `just_cause_eviction`, `dv_termination`, `lockout_penalties`, `application_fees`, `entry_notice`, `retaliation_windows`, `eviction_notices`, `late_fee_caps`, `deposit_interest`, `deposit_return_windows`, `lease_disclosures`, `habitability_remedies`, `rent_control`, `military_termination`, `security_deposit_caps`, and `contractor_1099`. Recent legislative wave (2009-2024) hit dense urban housing hardest after the post-2000 bedbug resurgence.

**Five regimes** across 51 jurisdictions:

| Regime                              | States                                                                  |
|-------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| **Pre-lease history disclosure**    | CA Civ. Code В§ 1954.603 (2017) / NJ N.J.A.C. В§ 5:10-3                  |
| **Informational pamphlet only**     | AZ В§ 33-1319 (SFH exempt) / AL / FL / GA / IL Chicago / MI / MN / NE / NV / OH / OR / PA / RI / SD / TX / WI / WV |
| **Post-discovery adjacent-unit notice** | NY RPL В§ 235-j (2010, amended 2024 вҖ” 72 hours)                      |
| **Inspection duty on tenant report** | ME 14 M.R.S. В§ 6021-A (5d) / KS K.S.A. В§ 58-2576a (5d) / IA Iowa Code В§ 562A.15 (7d) / NH RSA В§ 540-A (7d) |
| **No statewide statute**            | 24 other states вҖ” implied habitability + local ordinances              |

**NY's 72-hour adjacent-unit notice is unique** post-discovery requirement. Once landlord learns of an infestation, written notice to tenants in immediately adjacent units (above/below/side) is required within 72 hours per RPL В§ 235-j (amended 2024 вҖ” previous version required 24 hours to all tenants). Pinned by `ny_is_only_state_with_adjacent_notice_hours` (sweep verifying every other state has `adjacent_notice_hours = None`).

**NY 72-hour boundary is bright-line at exact 72**: complies AT 72h; fails at 73h. Pinned by `ny_72_hour_window_complies_at_exactly_72_hours` + `ny_72_hour_adjacent_notice_violation_at_73_hours`. The clock only starts when landlord LEARNS of infestation вҖ” `ny_no_violation_if_no_infestation_learned_of` (1000h without infestation = no violation).

**AZ В§ 33-1319 single-family home exemption is load-bearing.** AZ explicitly excludes single-family rentals from the bedbug pamphlet regime. CA does NOT have a similar exemption вҖ” pre-lease history disclosure required even for single-family. Pinned by `az_single_family_home_exempted` (SFH + missing pamphlet = complies) + `ca_sfh_not_exempted_from_disclosure` (CA SFH + missing disclosure = violation).

**ME 14 M.R.S. В§ 6021-A inspection duty 5-day boundary.** Tenant report triggers 5-day inspection clock. 6 days without inspection = violation; 5 days exact = within window. Pinned by `me_5_day_inspection_duty_violation_at_6_days` + `me_5_day_window_complies_at_exactly_5_days` + `me_complies_when_inspection_done_even_past_deadline` (30 days post-report but inspection done = complies).

**IA / NH 7-day inspection windows** are the next tier; KS matches ME at 5 days. Pinned by `inspection_duty_states_pinned` (4-state regime sweep with day count assertion).

**Multiple simultaneous violations stack.** CA missing both pre-lease history AND info pamphlet вҶ’ 2 distinct violation entries. Pinned by `multiple_simultaneous_violations_stack`.

**No-statewide-statute states (24 jurisdictions) always comply** at the state level вҖ” implied habitability covenant and local ordinances govern instead. Pinned by `no_statute_states_always_comply` (24-state sweep with all flags missing вҶ’ still complies).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/bedbug-disclosure-check`. Twenty-five tests pin: 51-row coverage; CA pre-lease history violation; AZ pamphlet required + **single-family exemption** (load-bearing carve-out); CA SFH not exempted (distinguishes from AZ); **NY 72h boundary** (complies at 72h, fails at 73h, no violation pre-learn, complies if notified); ME 5-day boundary (complies at 5d, fails at 6d, complies if completed); KS 5-day mirror; IA 7-day boundary; NJ pre-lease required; no-statute 24-state sweep always complies; unknown state errors; case-insensitive; sorted all_states; non-empty citations; pre-lease history states (CA/NJ) pinned; **NY-only adjacent-notice sweep** (every other state has None); inspection-duty 4-state sweep (ME/KS = 5d, IA/NH = 7d); multiple violations stack.

`traderview-expense::commercial_lease_cam_charge_disclosure` is the **commercial lease CAM (Common Area Maintenance) charge disclosure and tenant audit rights framework module** вҖ” trader-landlord critical because commercial leases routinely pass through CAM/operating-expense escalations that can total 20-40% of base rent over time; ill-disclosed or improperly calculated CAM exposes landlord to TENANT AUDIT CLAIMS and POTENTIAL REFUNDS averaging 5-15% of contested charges per industry-standard BOMA practice. Companion to `commercial_lease_personal_guaranty_enforceability`, `tenant_estoppel_certificate`, `lease_disclosures`, `rental_property_registration`.

**Three-jurisdiction framework**:

| Jurisdiction | Source | Key Rules |
|--------------|--------|-----------|
| **California** | Cal. Civ. Code В§ 1938 (CASp accessibility disclosure); Garrett v. Coast and Southern, 9 Cal. 4th 1 (1995) | Good-faith reconciliation; annual budget + year-end reconciliation industry standard |
| **New York** | Common-law contract interpretation; N.Y. CPLR В§ 4519 Dead Man's Statute | No specific commercial CAM statute; BOMA Operating Expense Guide governs |
| **Default** | BOMA Operating Expense Guide 2024; Restatement (Second) of Contracts В§ 200 | Ambiguity-favoring-tenant; UCC Article 2A INAPPLICABLE to real property |

**BOMA Operating Expense Guide categories of CAM (13 categories)**:
1. Utilities (electric/gas/water/sewer)
2. Janitorial and cleaning services
3. Property management fees
4. Security and access control
5. Landscaping and grounds maintenance
6. Snow removal (seasonal)
7. Trash removal
8. HVAC maintenance contracts
9. Elevator maintenance contracts
10. Common-area repairs
11. Insurance premiums
12. Real estate taxes (separately or within CAM)
13. Capital expenditures AMORTIZED over useful life (typically 10-15 years)

**Standard EXCLUSIONS from CAM (12 categories)**: capital improvements (vs. amortized capital repairs); marketing and leasing costs (broker commissions); tenant-specific build-outs; landlord's debt service / mortgage interest; depreciation; income taxes; ground rent paid by landlord; reserves for future contingencies; legal fees for tenant disputes; penalties for landlord's own legal violations; insurance proceeds offsetting expenses; above-market affiliated-party vendor services.

**GROSS-UP PROVISION** вҖ” when building less than fully occupied, landlord may "gross-up" VARIABLE expenses (utilities, janitorial, trash) to what they would have been at 95-100% occupancy; tenant pays pro-rata share of grossed-up amount; gross-up FLOORS occupancy at 95% or 100% per lease terms; FIXED expenses (real estate taxes, insurance) are NOT grossed up.

**BASE-YEAR ESCALATION** вҖ” tenant pays only INCREASE over base-year amount:
```
Tenant CAM = (Current Year Total - Base Year Total) Г— Tenant Pro-Rata Share
```

**TENANT AUDIT RIGHTS standard provisions (7 elements)**:
1. Annual right to audit landlord's books
2. Notice period 90-180 days after reconciliation statement
3. Scope limitation to most recent fiscal year
4. Confidentiality obligations
5. Cost-shifting clause (tenant pays unless discrepancy exceeds 3-5% threshold)
6. Refund obligation for overcharges
7. Right to inspect underlying invoices and contracts

**BOMA-survey CAM dispute statistics**: 1 in 4 (25%) tenants experience billing discrepancies in CAM reconciliations; average overcharge recovery 5-15% of contested amount; most common errors: incorrect gross-up calculation + capital-expenditure misclassification + management-fee duplication.

**Trader-landlord critical fact patterns**: (1) office tower 65% occupied with 95% gross-up floor вҖ” variable expenses inflated 1.46Г—; (2) HVAC system replacement ($500K) passed as operating вҖ” should be amortized over 15-year useful life ($33K annual); (3) affiliated management company 5% gross rent (market 3-4%) вҖ” above-market affiliated-party excludable; (4) reconciliation late beyond 90 days fiscal year-end вҖ” tenant claims WAIVER of true-up; (5) artificially deflated base year вҖ” tenant audit may rectify.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/commercial-lease-cam-charge-disclosure`. Twenty-eight tests pin: **fully compliant passes**; **full pass-through tenant owes 10%** (10% pro-rata of $1M = $100K); **base-year escalation only excess** (10% of $200K = $20K); **fixed stop full amount**; **no annual budget violation** (Garrett v. Coast and Southern); **no year-end reconciliation violation** (WAIVER); **reconciliation past 180 days late** (200 days exceeds window); **gross-up above 95% improper**; **gross-up at 95% boundary proper**; **gross-up under 95% proper**; **capital improvement passed violation** (AMORTIZED 10-15 years); **affiliated party above-market violation** (ARMS-LENGTH market rate); **leasing costs in CAM violation** (BOMA exclusion); **no audit right violation** (BOMA standard); **cap exceeded without explanation violation** (5% cap); **escalation structure truth table three cells**; **citation pins all authorities** (Cal. Civ. Code В§ 1938 + Garrett v. Coast and Southern + BOMA International Operating Expense Guide (2024) + ANSI/BOMA Z65.1-2017 + Restatement (Second) of Contracts В§ 200 + N.Y. CPLR В§ 4519 + UCC Article 2A inapplicable); **note pins three-jurisdiction framework**; **note pins 13 CAM categories**; **note pins 12 exclusions**; **note pins gross-up provision**; **note pins base-year escalation formula**; **note pins audit seven elements**; **note pins BOMA survey statistics** (1 in 4, 5-15%); **note pins CA В§ 1938 CASp**; **note pins trader fact patterns five**; **note pins companion modules**; **multiple failures stack**.

`traderview-expense::commercial_lease_personal_guaranty_enforceability` is the **multi-jurisdictional commercial lease personal guaranty enforceability framework module** вҖ” trader-landlord critical because commercial leases routinely require personal guaranty of corporate or LLC tenant obligations as condition of rental. Recent NYC COVID-era legislation plus Second Circuit constitutional rulings significantly altered the enforceability landscape; "Good Guy Guaranty" industry-standard limitations cap guarantor exposure to surrender-date arrears; common-law Statute of Frauds and material-modification rules continue to constrain enforceability. Distinct from sibling `tenant_lease_guarantor_disclosure` (residential вҖ” NY HSTPA 2019 one-month-aggregate cap + FCRA + Restatement (Third) of Suretyship).

**Four-jurisdiction framework**:

| Jurisdiction | Source | Key Rule |
|--------------|--------|----------|
| **NYC** | NYC Admin. Code В§ 22-1005 + Melendez v. City of New York three decisions | UNENFORCEABLE personal guaranty for commercial lease obligations arising March 7, 2020 through June 30, 2021 for three covered tenant categories (food/beverage on-premises consumption, non-essential retail, EO-required closure) |
| **NY State** | NY GOL В§ 5-701(a)(1) plus В§ 5-701(a)(2) | Statute of Frauds requires WRITTEN guaranty for > 12-month leases AND ALL promises to answer for debt of another; "Good Guy Guaranty" industry standard limits to surrender-date arrears |
| **California** | Cal. Civ. Code В§ 2787-2856 + В§ 2819 + В§ 1670.5 + В§ 2799 | Material modification (В§ 2819) DISCHARGES; unconscionability (В§ 1670.5); continuing-guaranty revocation (В§ 2799) |
| **Default** | Restatement (Third) of Suretyship and Guaranty (1996) В§ 41 + В§ 39 | Material modification (В§ 41) and novation (В§ 39) EXTINGUISH; strict construction against creditor |

**NYC Admin. Code В§ 22-1005 (enacted May 26, 2020)** вҖ” UNENFORCEABLE personal guaranty for commercial lease obligations arising **March 7, 2020 through June 30, 2021** for three covered tenant categories: (1) food/beverage on-premises consumption ceased; (2) non-essential retail; (3) required to close under executive orders.

**Melendez v. City of New York constitutional history (three decisions)**:
1. **Melendez v. City of New York, 16 F.4th 992 (2d Cir. 2021)** вҖ” initial Second Circuit panel decision
2. **Melendez v. City of New York, 27 F.4th 119 (2d Cir. 2022)** вҖ” panel rehearing VACATED dismissal and remanded; Second Circuit held law substantially impaired contracts and served legitimate public purpose BUT was not appropriate and reasonable means
3. **Melendez v. City of New York, 668 F. Supp. 3d 184 (S.D.N.Y. Mar. 31, 2023)** вҖ” SDNY Judge Abrams held law **VIOLATES Contracts Clause (U.S. Const. art. I В§ 10)** because PERMANENTLY extinguishes guaranties rather than deferring enforcement; pending Supreme Court review

**Second Circuit constitutional concerns**: (1) law effected PERMANENT (not temporary) unenforceability; (2) not limited to circumstances where guarantors owned or intended to reopen the businesses; (3) allocated economic risk to landlords rather than guarantors; (4) not conditioned on demonstrated financial need; (5) provided landlords with no alternative remedial avenues.

**"Good Guy Guaranty" (GGG) industry-standard NYC commercial term (5 elements)**:
1. Guarantor liability LIMITED to rent through DATE OF SURRENDER
2. Surrender requires (a) tenant out of premises; (b) keys delivered; (c) advance notice typically 30-90 days
3. Guarantor NOT LIABLE for (a) future rent post-surrender; (b) future damages from re-tenanting; (c) attorney's fees post-surrender
4. Effectively converts full-recourse guaranty into surrender-date-arrears guaranty
5. Strong tenant incentive to vacate cleanly rather than abandon

**NY GOL В§ 5-701(a) Statute of Frauds**:
- В§ 5-701(a)(1) вҖ” agreement not to be performed within one year (covers > 12-month leases)
- В§ 5-701(a)(2) вҖ” promise to answer for debt of another (covers ALL guaranties)
- Both require WRITING SIGNED by guarantor or authorized agent

**Trader-landlord critical fact patterns**: (1) NYC retail LLC default March 2020 вҖ” В§ 22-1005 bars enforcement for blackout-window arrears (non-essential retail); arrears outside enforceable; (2) NY 36-month lease oral guaranty вҖ” В§ 5-701(a)(1) UNENFORCEABLE; (3) Good Guy Guaranty вҖ” tenant LLC vacates with 30-day notice plus keys at month 18 of 60-month lease вҖ” guarantor liable only for month-18 arrears, not remaining 42 months; (4) CA 15% rent increase without guarantor consent вҖ” В§ 2819 MATERIAL MODIFICATION discharges; (5) landlord novation with new tenant LLC вҖ” Restatement В§ 39 NOVATION extinguishes.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/commercial-lease-personal-guaranty-enforceability`. Twenty-seven tests pin: **NY 60-month written enforceable**; **NY 36-month oral unenforceable**; **NY 12-month oral enforceable**; **NYC В§ 22-1005 blackout unenforceable**; **NYC В§ 22-1005 engages only for covered business**; **NYC В§ 22-1005 engages only within blackout**; **Melendez constitutional history disclosed**; **Good Guy Guaranty with surrender limits liability**; **Good Guy Guaranty without surrender no limitation**; **CA material modification discharges**; **default material modification Restatement В§ 41**; **novation extinguishes**; **enforceable guaranty message displayed**; **jurisdiction truth table four cells**; **NYC uniquely engages В§ 22-1005 invariant**; **citation pins all authorities** (NYC Admin. Code В§ 22-1005 + Melendez 16 F.4th 992 + Melendez 27 F.4th 119 + Melendez 668 F. Supp. 3d 184 + NY GOL В§ 5-701(a)(1) + NY GOL В§ 5-701(a)(2) + Cal. Civ. Code В§ 2787-2856 + Cal. Civ. Code В§ 2819 + Cal. Civ. Code В§ 1670.5 + Cal. Civ. Code В§ 2799 + Restatement (Third) of Suretyship and Guaranty + В§ 41 + В§ 39 + U.S. Const. art. I В§ 10); **note pins four-jurisdiction framework**; **note pins NYC В§ 22-1005 three categories**; **note pins Melendez three decisions**; **note pins Second Circuit five concerns**; **note pins Good Guy Guaranty five elements**; **note pins NY GOL В§ 5-701 two subsections**; **note pins CA suretyship framework**; **note pins Restatement (Third) Suretyship 1996**; **note pins trader fact patterns five**; **note pins companion modules**; **multiple failures stack**.

`traderview-expense::cooling_requirements` is the **state + municipal landlord cooling / maximum-indoor-temperature / AC-installation compliance check** вҖ” companion to `heat_requirements` (winter minimum) addressing the SUMMER maximum-temperature axis and the emerging AC-installation mandates that have surged in 2024вҖ“2026 driven by extreme-heat events. Habitability law now extends beyond the winter-heating canon.

**Six regimes** diverge along five axes:

| Regime | Maximum-temperature standard | Cure period | Effective season | Coverage | Install mandate |
|--------|------------------------------|-------------|------------------|----------|------------------|
| `ArizonaPhoenix` | **82В°F** habitable (refrigerated) / **86В°F** (evaporative cooler) | 5 days | Year-round | All habitable rooms inc. bathrooms | No |
| `ArizonaTucson` | 82В°F habitable (refrigerated) / 86В°F (evaporative) | 5 days | Year-round | All habitable rooms | No |
| `Dallas` | **Indoor вүӨ outdoor вҲ’ 20В°F** (relative threshold) | 7 days | **April 1 to November 1** | All rentals | No |
| `NYCCoolHomes` | **78В°F bedrooms when outdoor > 82В°F** | None | **June 15 to September 15** | **Bedrooms only** | **YES** вҖ” 60-day landlord response window |
| `California` | Implied warranty (no statutory cap) | "Reasonable time" | Year-round | Existing cooling only | No |
| `Default` | None | None | n/a | n/a | No |

**NYC Cool Homes for All (Int 0994-2024) is the regime-defining 2024 expansion.** It's the only AC INSTALLATION mandate in the matrix вҖ” tenant request begins 2028-03-01; enforcement begins 2030-01-01; landlord has 60 days to fulfill the request. Coverage extends to both market-rate AND rent-stabilized units. Pinned by `nyc_install_mandate_request_within_60_days_not_overdue`, `nyc_install_mandate_request_past_60_days_overdue`, `nyc_install_at_60_day_boundary_not_overdue`, and the 6-regime invariant `only_nyc_imposes_install_mandate_6_regime_invariant`.

**Dallas alone uses a relative-to-outdoor threshold.** The Dallas City Code Chapter 27 refrigerated-air rule requires indoor temperatures be 20В°F below outdoor (so 109В°F outside вҶ’ 89В°F indoor cap). All other regimes use absolute thresholds. Pinned by `dallas_109_outdoor_89_indoor_at_boundary_compliant`, `dallas_109_outdoor_91_indoor_violation`, and the invariant `only_dallas_uses_relative_to_outdoor_threshold_invariant` (which confirms Phoenix threshold is constant across outdoor temperatures while Dallas threshold varies).

**Arizona regimes have a unique evaporative-cooler carve-out.** Phoenix + Tucson allow a higher 86В°F maximum when the unit uses an evaporative ("swamp") cooler rather than refrigerated AC вҖ” recognizing the lower cooling capacity of evaporative systems in low-humidity climates. Pinned by `phoenix_evaporative_at_86_boundary_compliant` and the 6-regime invariant `only_arizona_regimes_have_evaporative_cooler_carve_out_invariant` (which confirms the evap flag is a no-op for Dallas + NYC + CA + Default).

**Effective-season windows differ by regime.** Phoenix: year-round (driven by extreme-heat risk months beyond summer). Dallas: April 1 to November 1 (DOY 91вҖ“305). NYC: June 15 to September 15 (DOY 166вҖ“258). Outside the season, no statutory maximum applies. Pinned by `dallas_outside_season_no_requirement`, `dallas_at_april_1_season_start_boundary_in_season`, `dallas_at_november_1_season_end_boundary_in_season`, `nyc_outside_season_no_requirement`.

**NYC's outdoor trigger uses STRICT greater-than (> 82В°F, not вүҘ).** When outdoor is exactly 82В°F, the bedroom-maximum cap does NOT engage. Pinned by `nyc_in_season_outdoor_at_82_does_not_trigger_max`.

**Cure-period distinctions are statutorily fixed.** Arizona: 5 days after written notice (A.R.S. В§ 33-1364). Texas/Dallas: 7 days (Tex. Prop. Code В§ 92.052). NYC, CA, Default: no statutory cure window (NYC has its own 60-day install window for the install-mandate path; CA uses "reasonable time" under В§ 1941.1). Pinned by `phoenix_5_day_cure_period_violation_on_day_6`, `phoenix_at_5_day_cure_boundary_not_yet_expired`, `dallas_7_day_cure_period`, and `cure_period_only_arizona_5_dallas_7_invariant`.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/cooling-requirements`. Twenty-eight tests pin: **Phoenix refrigerated at 82В°F boundary compliant**; **Phoenix refrigerated above 82В°F violation**; **Phoenix evaporative at 86В°F boundary compliant**; **Phoenix evaporative above 86В°F violation**; **Phoenix 5-day cure violation on day 6**; **Phoenix at 5-day boundary not yet expired**; **Tucson mirrors Phoenix 82В°F max**; **Dallas 109В°F outdoor + 89В°F indoor at boundary compliant**; **Dallas 109В°F outdoor + 91В°F indoor violation**; **Dallas outside season no requirement**; **Dallas 7-day cure period**; **Dallas at April 1 season-start boundary in-season**; **Dallas at November 1 season-end boundary in-season**; **NYC in-season + outdoor > 82В°F + bedroom max 78В°F**; **NYC in-season + outdoor at 82В°F does NOT trigger max** (regression вҖ” strict > not вүҘ); **NYC outside season no requirement**; **NYC install request within 60 days not overdue**; **NYC install request past 60 days overdue**; **NYC install at 60-day boundary not overdue**; **CA no statutory max returns None**; **Default no statutory requirement**; **only-NYC-imposes-install-mandate 6-regime invariant**; **only-Dallas-uses-relative-to-outdoor-threshold invariant**; **only-Arizona-regimes-have-evaporative-cooler-carve-out invariant**; **cure-period only-Arizona-5-Dallas-7 invariant**; **citation pins authority per regime** (A.R.S. В§ 33-1364 for AZ; Dallas City Code Chapter 27 for Dallas; Int 0994-2024 for NYC; В§ 1941.1 for CA); **sibling-module note across all 6 regimes** (UX-text regression for heat_requirements pairing); **no cooling in unit no repair duty outside install mandate** (regression вҖ” non-install regimes don't impose violation on uninstalled units).

`traderview-expense::heat_requirements` is the **state heat minimum temperature compliance table** вҖ” winter habitability obligation. Sibling to `foreclosure_tenant_rights`, `lead_disclosure`, `detector_requirements`, `soi_protection`, `just_cause_eviction`, `dv_termination`, `lockout_penalties`, `application_fees`, `entry_notice`, `retaliation_windows`, `eviction_notices`, `late_fee_caps`, `deposit_interest`, `deposit_return_windows`, `lease_disclosures`, `habitability_remedies`, `rent_control`, `military_termination`, `security_deposit_caps`, and `contractor_1099`. Failure to provide adequate heat is one of the most common habitability violations вҖ” leading to rent withholding, code enforcement, and M.G.L. c. 186 В§ 14 criminal felony exposure for willful interruption.

**Three regimes** across 51 jurisdictions:

| Regime                              | States                                                       |
|-------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------|
| **Specific statute / code**         | NY / MA / IL / MN / CT / NJ / OR / DC / RI / MD / VA / WI / CA (13 with concrete temperatures) |
| **Implied habitability covenant**   | TX / FL / WA / OH / PA / NM / MT / NV / NH / IN / ND / NE / MI / DE / KS / KY / ME / NC / NH / SC / TN / UT / VT / WV / AZ / AK / CO / IA / OK / SD (~28 with no specific temp; caller applies local code) |
| **No statewide statute**            | AL / AR / GA / HI / ID / LA / MO / MS / SD / WY (10 states; HI listed because warm climate makes no heat needed) |

**Verified specific-statute rules:**

| State    | Day temp | Day window     | Night temp | Night window    | Heat season       | Outside trigger | Citation                              |
|----------|----------|----------------|------------|-----------------|--------------------|-----------------|---------------------------------------|
| NY (NYC) | 68В°F     | 6am-10pm       | 62В°F       | 10pm-6am        | Oct 1 - May 31    | **< 55В°F**      | NYC Admin. Code В§ 27-2029             |
| MA       | 68В°F     | 7am-11pm       | 64В°F       | 11pm-7am        | Sept 16 - Jun 14  | none            | M.G.L. c. 105 В§ 410.201               |
| IL       | 68В°F     | 8:30am-10:30pm | 66В°F       | 10:30pm-8:30am  | Sept 15 - Jun 1   | none            | Chicago Mun. Code В§ 13-196-410        |
| MN       | 68В°F     | 24/7 (no split) | 68В°F      | n/a             | Oct 1 - Apr 30    | none            | Minn. Stat. В§ 504B.161                |
| CT       | 65В°F     | 24/7           | 65В°F       | n/a             | Oct 1 - May 31    | none            | Conn. Gen. Stat. В§ 47a-7              |
| NJ       | 68В°F     | 6am-11pm       | 65В°F       | 11pm-6am        | Oct 1 - May 1     | none            | N.J.A.C. В§ 5:10-14.3                  |
| OR       | 68В°F     | 6am-10pm       | 60В°F       | 10pm-6am        | Oct 1 - May 1     | none            | ORS В§ 90.320                          |
| DC       | 68В°F     | 6am-11pm       | 65В°F       | 11pm-6am        | Oct 1 - May 1     | none            | 14 DCMR В§ 503                         |
| RI       | 68В°F     | 6am-11pm       | 64В°F       | 11pm-6am        | Oct 1 - May 1     | none            | R.I. Gen. Laws В§ 45-24.3-8            |
| MD       | 68В°F     | 7am-11pm       | 65В°F       | 11pm-7am        | Oct 1 - May 1     | none            | Md. Code Real Prop. В§ 8-211           |
| VA       | 68В°F     | 6am-11pm       | 65В°F       | 11pm-6am        | Oct 15 - May 1    | none            | Va. Code В§ 36-105                     |
| WI       | 67В°F     | 24/7           | 67В°F       | n/a             | Oct 1 - Apr 30    | none            | Wis. Admin. Code В§ ATCP 134.04        |
| CA       | 70В°F     | 24/7           | 70В°F       | n/a             | Nov 1 - May 31    | none            | Cal. Code Regs. tit. 25 В§ 34          |

**NY's 55В°F outside-temperature trigger is load-bearing and unique to NY.** The NYC Heat Law's daytime 68В°F requirement only fires when outside temp drops below 55В°F. Every other state requires continuous compliance during heat season regardless of outside temperature. Pinned by `ny_is_only_state_with_outside_trigger` (sweep verifying every other state has `outside_temp_trigger_f = None`) + `ny_day_outside_above_55_disengages_requirement` (60В°F outside = no requirement, complies even at 60В°F indoor) + `ma_no_outside_temp_trigger_continuous_requirement` (MA fails at 67В°F even with 70В°F outside).

**Night requirement is unconditional**. NY's nighttime 62В°F applies regardless of outside temp вҖ” the 55В°F trigger gates only the daytime window. Pinned by `night_window_outside_trigger_does_not_apply` (2am, indoor 60В°F, outside 80В°F вҶ’ still fails the 62В°F night requirement).

**Heat season wrap-around math is load-bearing.** Most heat seasons span the calendar boundary (Oct 1 - May 31 wraps past Jan 1). The `in_heat_season` helper handles `start_month > end_month` correctly. Pinned by `ny_heat_season_wraps_past_jan_1` (Jan 15 is in NY's Oct-May season) + `ny_heat_season_boundary_oct_1_in_season` + `ny_heat_season_boundary_sep_30_out_of_season` (one-day boundaries) + MA September boundary tests.

**Day vs night boundary** is a half-open interval `[day_start, night_start)`. NY at hour 22 (10pm) exact = night (start of night window); hour 21 (9pm) = day. Pinned by `ny_day_night_boundary_at_22_hour`.

**Hawaii is listed as no-statewide-statute deliberately** вҖ” warm climate makes heat moot; the no_statute classification is correct rather than a coverage gap. Pinned by `hawaii_no_statewide_statute`.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/heat-requirements-check`. Twenty-six tests pin: 51-row coverage; **NY day 68В°F when outside < 55В°F** + **NY outside вүҘ 55В°F disengages** + **NY night 62В°F regardless of outside**; NY heat-season boundary (Oct 1 in season, Sept 30 out of season, Jan 15 wraps); MA day 68 / night 64 with 7am-11pm split + MA continuous requirement (no outside trigger); MN single temp 68 no day/night split; CT 65В°F with 24/7 application; **habitability-only states (7-state sweep) return complies=true with habitability_only flag**; **no-statute states (9-state sweep) return complies=true with no_statute flag**; Hawaii no-statute deliberately; MA Sept 16/15 season boundary; unknown state handled; case-insensitive; sorted all_states; non-empty citations; specific-statute states pinned (13-state regime sweep); **NY-only outside-trigger pin** (sweep verifying NoTrigger on all other 50 states); shortfall math correct; **NY day-night boundary at hour 22** (half-open interval pin); night window unconditional vs outside trigger.

`traderview-expense::foreclosure_tenant_rights` is the **federal PTFA + state foreclosure tenant rights compliance table** вҖ” sibling to `lead_disclosure`, `detector_requirements`, `soi_protection`, `just_cause_eviction`, `dv_termination`, `lockout_penalties`, `application_fees`, `entry_notice`, `retaliation_windows`, `eviction_notices`, `late_fee_caps`, `deposit_interest`, `deposit_return_windows`, `lease_disclosures`, `habitability_remedies`, `rent_control`, `military_termination`, `security_deposit_caps`, and `contractor_1099`.

**Federal floor (universal)**: Protecting Tenants at Foreclosure Act (PTFA) вҖ” enacted 2009 as Title VII of the Helping Families Save Their Homes Act, sunset in 2014, then **permanently reinstated in 2018** by В§ 304 of the Economic Growth, Regulatory Relief, and Consumer Protection Act (EGRRCPA). Two core protections:

1. **90-day notice minimum** вҖ” all bona fide tenants must receive вүҘ 90 days written notice before vacating after foreclosure
2. **Lease honor through expiration** вҖ” tenant may stay through bona fide lease end if the lease has > 90 days remaining AND was entered into before notice of foreclosure

**Bona fide tenancy three-prong test** вҖ” ALL three required:

| Prong | Test                                                                          | Field                            |
|-------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------|----------------------------------|
| (a)   | Tenant is NOT the mortgagor or spouse/parent/child of mortgagor               | `bona_fide_prong_not_mortgagor_family` |
| (b)   | Lease/tenancy was the result of an arm's length transaction                   | `bona_fide_prong_arm_length`     |
| (c)   | Rent is at or above FMR OR is reduced/subsidized by federal/state/local subsidy | `bona_fide_prong_fair_market_rent` |

Failing ANY prong вҶ’ PTFA protections do NOT apply. Pinned by `all_three_bona_fide_prongs_required` (combinatorial 2Г—2Г—2 sweep вҖ” only the all-true cell returns bona_fide_tenant=true).

**Subsidized below-FMR rent satisfies prong (c).** Section 8 voucher tenants with below-market rent still qualify because subsidy explicitly counts under the statute. Pinned by `subsidized_rent_satisfies_prong_c`.

**State additions** layered atop the federal floor (federal is a floor, not a ceiling):

| Regime                            | States                                                       |
|-----------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------|
| **Federal floor only**            | 44 states (most jurisdictions; standard PTFA 90 days)        |
| **Extended notice period**        | DC (120 days В§ 42-3505.01a) / CT / IL / MD / NY / OR         |
| **Right of first refusal**        | CA (SB 1079 + AB 1837) вҖ” included in Comprehensive regime    |
| **Comprehensive state protections** | CA / MA c. 186A / NJ В§ 2A:50-69 вҖ” extended notice + ROFR + non-honor of owner-occupant exception |

**DC is the only state with a longer notice period than 90 days** at 120 days under В§ 42-3505.01a. Pinned by `dc_120_day_notice_is_the_only_extended_period_state` (sweep verifying every other state is 90 days).

**Purchaser-owner-occupant exception** is federal: if the foreclosure purchaser will occupy the unit as a primary residence, the 90-day notice replaces the lease-completion right. However, **MA and NJ do NOT honor this exception** вҖ” even with a primary-residence purchaser, the tenant may still complete the lease in those two pro-tenant comprehensive-regime states. Pinned by `massachusetts_does_not_honor_owner_occupant_exception` + `new_jersey_does_not_honor_owner_occupant_exception`. Most states honor the exception (federal default).

**California's right of first refusal under SB 1079 / AB 1837** lets a bona fide tenant purchase the property at the trustee's sale within 45 days post-auction. Pinned by `california_right_of_first_refusal_available` + `texas_no_right_of_first_refusal` (negative case).

**Tenant-may-complete-lease logic**: lease must extend beyond the notice vacate deadline AND no owner-occupant override. Lease already expired before notice вҶ’ no completion path. Pinned by `tenant_may_complete_lease_when_extends_beyond_notice` + `lease_already_expired_no_completion_path`.

**Bright-line 90-day boundary** for federal floor: 90 days exact = complies; 89 days = fails. Pinned by `federal_floor_90_day_notice_complies_at_exact_boundary` + `federal_floor_89_day_notice_fails`.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/foreclosure-tenant-check`. Twenty-five tests pin: 51-row coverage; **90-day exact boundary** (federal); 89-day federal short; **DC 120-day extended period** (exact + short); **CA right of first refusal**; TX no ROFR (negative); each prong's failure mode individually flagged; subsidized rent satisfies prong (c) [Section 8 case]; tenant-may-complete-lease when lease extends; owner-occupant exception overrides lease completion (federal default); **MA + NJ don't honor owner-occupant exception** (comprehensive-regime pin); lease already expired no completion path; unknown state вҶ’ federal floor; case-insensitive; sorted all_states; non-empty citations; comprehensive-states 3-state regime sweep; **DC-only 120-day sweep** (every other state is 90); **all-three-prongs combinatorial sweep** (8 combinations, only all-true qualifies); note describes "MAY complete lease" path; note states shortfall when noncompliant.

`traderview-expense::lead_disclosure` is the **federal Title X + state lead-based paint compliance table** вҖ” sibling to `detector_requirements`, `soi_protection`, `just_cause_eviction`, `dv_termination`, `lockout_penalties`, `application_fees`, `entry_notice`, `retaliation_windows`, `eviction_notices`, `late_fee_caps`, `deposit_interest`, `deposit_return_windows`, `lease_disclosures`, `habitability_remedies`, `rent_control`, `military_termination`, `security_deposit_caps`, and `contractor_1099`.

**Federal floor (universal)** вҖ” Section 1018 of Title X of the Residential Lead-Based Paint Hazard Reduction Act of 1992 (40 CFR Part 745 / 24 CFR Part 35 Subpart A) applies to **all pre-1978 private rentals nationwide**. Four mandatory landlord disclosure elements:

1. EPA pamphlet "Protect Your Family From Lead in Your Home"
2. Federal Lead Warning Statement in the lease (specific federal language)
3. Disclosure of any known lead-based paint records / prior inspections
4. 10-day risk-assessment window for tenant before lease binds

**Federal penalty**: $10,000 per violation under 40 CFR В§ 745.118(c), plus tenant may sue for **treble damages**.

**Five state regime types** layered atop the federal floor:

| Regime                              | States                                                          | Trigger                              |
|-------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------|
| **Federal floor only**              | 41 states (AK / AL / AR / AZ / CA / CO / DE / FL / GA / HI / IA / ID / IN / KS / KY / LA / ME / MI / MO / MS / MT / NC / ND / NE / NH / NM / NV / OH / OK / OR / PA / SC / SD / TN / TX / UT / VA / WA / WV / WY) | No state additions |
| **Child-based deleading**           | MA / CT / MN / WI                                               | Child under 6 in household           |
| **Periodic inspection**             | NJ (2022) / MD / DC                                             | On 3-year or annual cycle            |
| **Inspection at occupancy change**  | IL / RI / VT                                                    | Each new tenant                      |
| **Comprehensive (combined)**        | NY (Pub. Health Law + NYC LL1)                                  | Child + periodic + investigation     |

**MA Lead Law (M.G.L. c. 111 В§ 197, 1971) is the strictest in the country.** When a child under 6 occupies a pre-1978 rental, the landlord must "delead" вҖ” remove or permanently cover all lead-paint hazards вҖ” regardless of blood-lead level. Federal compliance alone is NOT sufficient. Pinned by `ma_strict_law_requires_deleading_with_child_under_6` (federal-compliant + child + no deleading = NOT compliant) + `ma_no_child_under_6_no_state_violation` (no child = MA state rules don't trigger, federal floor alone suffices).

**NJ Lead-Safe Law (P.L. 2021, c.182, effective July 2022)** requires periodic inspections of ALL pre-1978 rentals on a 3-year cycle, regardless of whether a child occupies. Initial inspection deadline was July 22, 2024 (or first tenant turnover if earlier). Pinned by `nj_lead_safe_law_periodic_inspection_required` (no child needed; missing inspection alone is a violation).

**RI / VT inspection-at-occupancy-change** is the third major model. Each new tenant triggers a new inspection requirement; landlord cannot stack inspections by spacing turnovers. Pinned by `ri_inspection_at_change_of_occupancy_required`.

**NY (NYC Local Law 1 of 2004) is the comprehensive case.** Multiple regime flags fire simultaneously (child-trigger + periodic). When both miss, two distinct violations emit. Pinned by `ny_comprehensive_requires_both_periodic_and_child_action` (2 simultaneous violations).

**1978 cutoff is a hard `<` boundary, not `вүӨ`**. Property built in 1978 exactly is post-1978 and gets no federal obligation. Property built 1977 gets the full Title X treatment. Pinned by `property_built_1978_exact_is_post_1978_no_obligation` + `property_built_1977_triggers_federal_floor`.

**Federal-floor-only states do not stack state-side violations.** When state regime = `FederalFloorOnly` (TX / FL / CO / AZ etc., 41 jurisdictions), even missing state-side inputs don't produce violations because the state has no statutory requirement. Pinned by `federal_floor_only_states_no_state_additions` (8-state sweep).

**Post-1978 property skips all state rules too.** Even in MA, a 1980-built rental is exempt from the Massachusetts Lead Law because the law applies only to pre-1978 housing. Pinned by `post_1978_property_state_lead_rules_dont_apply`.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/lead-disclosure-check`. Twenty-three tests pin: 51-row coverage; **1977/1978/1980 cutoff boundary** (regulatory bright line); each of 4 federal disclosure elements individually triggers a violation; **MA child-under-6 deleading** trigger + no-child case; NJ periodic-inspection regime; RI occupancy-change regime; **NY comprehensive (two simultaneous violations)** when both rules fail; 8-state federal-floor-only sweep; unknown state handled; case-insensitive; sorted `all_states()`; non-empty citations; **child-based-deleading 9-state sweep** (CT/MA/MN/WI/NY/IL/RI/VT/DC); **periodic-inspection 4-state sweep** (NJ/MD/NY/DC); **occupancy-change 4-state sweep** (IL/RI/VT/MD); post-1978 in MA also skips state rules; federal penalty constant matches 40 CFR В§ 745.118 ($10k/violation); treble-damages flag pinned across 10 states; multiple violations stack (NJ regime: 2 federal + 2 NJ inspections = 4); note describes post-1978 no-obligation case.

`traderview-expense::lead_in_drinking_water_disclosure` is the **mandatory landlord-paid disclosure of LEAD IN DRINKING WATER to tenant module** вҖ” when a public water system notifies a landlord that drinking water serving the building exceeds the EPA lead action level (15 Вөg/L), what disclosure obligation does the landlord owe to tenants? Distinct from siblings `lead_disclosure` (federal Title X lead-based PAINT disclosure), `flood_disclosure`, `radon_disclosure`, `asbestos_disclosure`, and `mold_disclosure`. Trader-landlord operational concern in NJ, MI (Flint legacy), and other states with explicit lead-in-drinking-water statutes.

**Three regimes**:

| Regime | Statutory mandate | Action level | Private right of action |
|--------|---------------------|---------------|---------------------------|
| New Jersey N.J.S.A. 58:12A-12.4 et seq. | 3 business days distribution + prominent posting | Federal 15 Вөg/L | YES (P.L. 2021 c. 82 + c. 183) |
| Michigan Mich. Comp. Laws В§ 325.1001 et seq. | Lead Action Level Exceedance Notice distribution | **State 12 Вөg/L (below federal)** | YES |
| Default | NO statutory landlord distribution mandate | Federal 15 Вөg/L | NO |

**New Jersey вҖ” N.J.S.A. 58:12A-12.4 et seq. (Lead in Drinking Water Notification Act)**. Most explicit statutory framework. Public water system MUST notify landlords when sampling shows lead exceeding action level (15 Вөg/L). Landlord then MUST (1) distribute the notice to EVERY TENANT within **THREE BUSINESS DAYS** of receipt AND (2) **POST the notice in a PROMINENT LOCATION** accessible to tenants. **P.L. 2021, c. 82** and **P.L. 2021, c. 183** amended the framework to include private right of action + civil penalties. NJ Department of Environmental Protection (NJ DEP) enforces.

**Michigan вҖ” Mich. Comp. Laws В§ 325.1001 et seq. (Safe Drinking Water Act post-Flint amendments)**. Post-Flint framework. Michigan Lead and Copper Rule (effective 2018, MAC R 325.10101 et seq.) sets **STATE action level at 12 Вөg/L** вҖ” BELOW the federal 15 Вөg/L action level. Landlord must distribute Lead Action Level Exceedance Notice when public water supply reports lead above action level. State enforcement + private right of action.

**Default вҖ” federal SDWA + EPA Lead and Copper Rule**. 42 U.S.C. В§ 300f et seq. + 40 CFR Part 141.85 federal floor only. Public water system MUST deliver Consumer Confidence Report (CCR) annually + issue Lead Public Education Statements when action level exceeded. **NO statutory landlord-tenant distribution mandate at federal level** вҖ” landlord's obligation governed by state-specific lead disclosure / habitability framework. State UDAP may reach landlord failure to disclose known lead in drinking water. Common-law negligence per se for pediatric lead poisoning claims.

**Michigan unique lower action level**. Michigan state action level (12 Вөg/L) is BELOW federal action level (15 Вөg/L), creating a wider zone of mandatory disclosure than other states. Pinned by `michigan_uniquely_lower_action_level_invariant` and boundary truth tables (`federal_action_level_boundary_truth_table` 5-cell sweep at 13/14/15/16/17 Вөg/L + `michigan_action_level_boundary_truth_table` 5-cell sweep at 11/12/13/14/15 Вөg/L).

**NJ unique three-business-day requirement**. Only NJ specifies the strict 3-business-day distribution deadline. MI uses general "must distribute" without specific timing. Default imposes no distribution mandate. Pinned by `nj_three_business_day_unique_invariant` (3-regime sweep).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/lead-in-drinking-water-disclosure`. Thirty-two tests pin: **NJ clean compliance passes**; **NJ missing distribution violates** (В§ 58:12A-12.4 THREE BUSINESS DAYS); **NJ missing posting violates** (В§ 58:12A-12.4 PROMINENT LOCATION); **NJ private water no disclosure obligation** (only public water system triggers); **NJ no lead notice no obligation**; **NJ federal action level 15 Вөg/L exceeded** (>15 triggers); **NJ at 15 boundary not exceeded** (strict greater-than); **NJ P.L. 2021 amendments note present** (c. 82 + c. 183); **NJ private right of action available**; **NJ citation pins NJSA + amendments + N.J.A.C. 7:10**; **MI clean compliance passes**; **MI action level 12 below federal note**; **MI at 13 state exceeded federal not**; **MI at 12 boundary not exceeded**; **MI at 16 both exceeded**; **MI missing both distribution and posting violates**; **MI citation pins state Lead and Copper Rule**; **default compliant always** (no landlord distribution mandate); **default no landlord distribution mandate note**; **default CCR note when missing CCR** (40 CFR Part 141 Subpart O system-side violation); **default state UDAP and negligence note**; **default no private right of action**; **default citation references federal SDWA + LCR**; **three regimes routed correctly**; **NJ three-business-day unique invariant**; **NJ unique private right of action invariant**; **Michigan uniquely lower action level invariant** (state 12 vs federal 15); **NJ both violations simultaneous**; **federal action level boundary truth table** (5-cell sweep 13/14/15/16/17); **Michigan action level boundary truth table** (5-cell sweep 11/12/13/14/15); **NJ clean no violations**; **MI clean no violations**.

`traderview-expense::lead_renovation_repair_painting` is the **EPA Lead Renovation, Repair, and Painting (RRP) Rule compliance check** вҖ” 40 CFR Part 745, Subpart E. Critical for any trader-landlord renovating older multifamily property вҖ” TSCA В§ 16(a) penalties are PER-DAY-PER-VIOLATION and have triggered EPA's largest-ever lead-paint enforcement actions. Distinct from `lead_disclosure` (TSCA В§ 1018 / 40 CFR Part 745 Subpart F вҖ” initial lead-based paint disclosure upon SALE or LEASE), `asbestos_disclosure`, and `bedbug_extermination_cost` вҖ” this module addresses ONLY the RENOVATION WORK PRACTICES pathway when work disturbs lead-based paint in pre-1978 housing or a child-occupied facility.

**Two jurisdictional regimes**:

| Regime | Authority | States | Firm registration |
|--------|-----------|--------|---------------------|
| **EPA Federal** | 40 CFR Part 745 Subpart E + TSCA В§ 16(a) | ~35 states + DC | EPA online application |
| **State-Authorized** | TSCA В§ 404 (15 USC В§ 2684) delegation | 15 EPA-delegated states: WI, IA, NC, MS, KS, RI, UT, OR, MA, AL, WA, GA, OK, DE, VT | State lead program |

State-authorized programs MUST be AT LEAST AS PROTECTIVE as the federal floor вҖ” the 8 compliance elements are universal regardless of jurisdiction.

**Eight compliance elements** (all required when rule applies):

| # | Element | Authority |
|---|---------|-----------|
| 1 | Target housing (pre-1978) OR child-occupied facility | В§ 745.83 definitions |
| 2 | Work disturbs paint above de minimis (>6 sq ft interior / >20 sq ft exterior / window replacement / demolition) | В§ 745.83 minor-repair-and-maintenance exemption |
| 3 | Firm EPA or state-authorized certified | В§В§ 745.89, 745.90 |
| 4 | Individual renovator trained (EPA-accredited 8-hour course; 5-year validity; 4-hour refresher) | В§ 745.90 |
| 5 | "Renovate Right" pamphlet provided to owner + occupant + signed acknowledgment or certificate of mailing retained | В§ 745.84 |
| 6 | Containment used (plastic sheeting + signage) + prohibited work practices avoided (no open-flame burning, no machine sanding/grinding without HEPA, no heat guns above 1100В°F) | В§ 745.85(a)(1), (a)(3) |
| 7 | Cleanup verification completed (visual inspection + dust-cleanup verification cards or clearance testing) | В§ 745.85(b) |
| 8 | Records retained 3 years post-renovation | В§ 745.86 |

**Two-prong applicability gate kills RRP at the gate.** If the building is NOT target housing (pre-1978) OR child-occupied facility, the rule doesn't apply вҖ” no compliance required regardless of work scope. If work falls within the В§ 745.83 minor-repair-and-maintenance threshold (вүӨ 6 sq ft interior / вүӨ 20 sq ft exterior / no window replacement / no demolition), the rule also doesn't apply. Pinned by `post_1978_housing_rule_does_not_apply`, `minor_repair_below_de_minimis_rule_does_not_apply`, `neither_target_housing_nor_above_de_minimis_rule_does_not_apply`, and `rule_applies_when_target_housing_and_above_de_minimis`.

**TSCA В§ 16(a) civil penalty up to $37,500 per day per violation** at statutory base (15 USC В§ 2615(a)(1)). Inflation-adjusted maximum under 40 CFR В§ 19.4 is materially higher (mid-$40K range as of 2025; 2026 adjustment canceled by OMB so 2025 figure persists). Criminal penalties under TSCA В§ 16(b) available for knowing or willful violations вҖ” up to $25,000/day plus imprisonment. The penalty note surfaces in every applicable-rule result. Pinned by `note_pins_tsca_section_16_penalty`.

**8-element invariant вҖ” any single gap defeats compliance.** Pinned by `all_eight_elements_must_be_satisfied_invariant` (8-iteration sweep flipping each element to false confirms each individually breaks compliance) and `multiple_violations_accumulate_all_listed` (3 simultaneous gaps surface 3+ violations).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/lead-renovation-repair-painting`. Twenty-two tests pin: **full compliance federal passes**; **full compliance state-authorized passes**; **post-1978 housing rule does not apply** (target housing gate); **minor repair below de minimis rule does not apply** (work-scope gate); **missing firm certification violation** (В§В§ 745.89, 745.90); **missing individual training violation** (В§ 745.90 8-hour); **missing pamphlet distribution violation** (В§ 745.84 Renovate Right); **missing pamphlet acknowledgment violation**; **pamphlet not provided skips acknowledgment check** (cascade); **missing containment violation** (В§ 745.85(a)(1) plastic sheeting); **prohibited work practices violation** (В§ 745.85(a)(3) open-flame burning); **missing cleanup verification violation** (В§ 745.85(b) clearance testing); **missing records retention violation** (В§ 745.86 3 years); **note pins TSCA В§ 16(a) $37,500/day penalty**; **federal jurisdiction note describes EPA application**; **state-authorized note lists 15 states** (WI, IA, NC, MS, KS, RI, UT, OR, MA, AL, WA, GA, OK, DE, VT); **citation federal pins Part 745 Subpart E + TSCA В§ 16(a) + 40 CFR В§ 19.4**; **citation state-authorized pins TSCA В§ 404 + 15 USC В§ 2684 + federal floor**; **all 8 elements must be satisfied invariant** (8-iteration sweep); **multiple violations accumulate all listed**; **rule applies when target housing and above de minimis**; **neither target housing nor above de minimis rule does not apply**.

`traderview-expense::detector_requirements` is the **state-by-state smoke + carbon monoxide detector compliance table** вҖ” life-safety obligation with massive liability exposure (tenant death from non-functional CO detector = wrongful death suit + criminal exposure in some states). Sibling to `soi_protection`, `just_cause_eviction`, `dv_termination`, `lockout_penalties`, `application_fees`, `entry_notice`, `retaliation_windows`, `eviction_notices`, `late_fee_caps`, `deposit_interest`, `deposit_return_windows`, `lease_disclosures`, `habitability_remedies`, `rent_control`, `military_termination`, `security_deposit_caps`, and `contractor_1099`.

**All 50 states require smoke detectors in residential rental units.** The variation lives in placement rules (every bedroom vs outside sleeping areas vs every level), power source (10-year sealed battery vs replaceable battery), and landlord-install obligations at occupancy.

**Three CO detector regimes:**

| Regime                              | States                                                                  |
|-------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| **All units (universal)**           | CO / CT / DC / IL / MA / MD / ME / MN / NH / NJ / NY / OR / RI / VT / WA (15 jurisdictions) |
| **Only with fuel source or garage** | CA / AZ / DE / FL / GA / HI / IA / KS / KY / LA / MI / MT / NC / NE / NM / NV / OH / PA / TN / TX / UT / VA / WI / AK + ~10 others |
| **No statewide CO (smoke only)**    | AL / AR / ID / IN / MO / MS / ND / OK / SC / SD / WV / WY                |

**CA model carve-out is load-bearing.** The CA rule under Health & Safety Code В§ 13113.7 / SB 745 requires CO detectors only when the property has a fossil-fuel-burning appliance OR an attached garage. Property without either (e.g., all-electric, detached garage) doesn't trigger the CO requirement at all. Pinned by `california_co_only_with_fuel_or_garage` (no fuel + no garage = no CO required, complies) + `california_co_required_with_garage_only` (garage alone triggers) + `california_co_required_with_fuel_only` (fuel alone triggers). The "OR" between the two conditions is the load-bearing logic.

**10-year sealed battery requirement** is the modern wave вҖ” CA SB 745 (2014, replacement triggers), NY NYC Local Law 111, MD, OR. Sealed unit prevents the "battery removed at 3am because it was chirping" failure mode that drives most tenant deaths from CO poisoning and fires. Pinned by `ten_year_battery_states_pinned` (4-state sweep) + the per-state violation tests for CA and NY.

**Smoke in every bedroom is rarer than outside-sleeping-area placement.** Only CA, TX, and WI require smoke in every bedroom in the table. All states require smoke outside each sleeping area. Pinned by `texas_smoke_in_every_bedroom_required` (TX-specific) + `smoke_outside_sleeping_area_universal_violation_check` (8-state sweep).

**Universal smoke + landlord-install** вҖ” every row has `smoke_in_every_bedroom_installed` or `outside_each_sleeping_area` checked AND `landlord_install_at_occupancy = true`. The landlord-install obligation means the landlord must verify detectors are present and working at occupancy; tenant takes over routine battery replacement after that.

**Violations list distinguishes missing-detector from wrong-placement.** Multiple violations stack вҖ” pinned by `multiple_violations_listed_in_result` (3 simultaneous violations all listed individually). Note text reports the count.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/detector-check`. Twenty-four tests pin: 51-row coverage; CA fully compliant; **CA OnlyWithFuelOrGarage carve-out** (no fuel + no garage вҶ’ CO not required); CA CO triggered by garage alone; CA CO triggered by fuel alone; NY AllUnits regime (CO always required regardless of fuel); MA AllUnits same; IL every-level-with-sleeping rule (CO on each level violation); TX every-bedroom smoke rule (TX-specific); **CA 10-year sealed battery violation**; **NY NYC Local Law 111 sealed battery** violation; MO smoke-only no-CO regime (NoStatewide); smoke-outside-sleeping universal sweep; landlord-install-at-occupancy violation; CO model with no-fuel/no-garage produces no violation; multiple violations stack in list; unknown state errors; case-insensitive lookup; sorted `all_states()`; non-empty citations; **CO-only states pinned** (8-state regime sweep); **CO AllUnits states pinned** (15-state regime sweep); **NoStatewide states pinned** (12-state sweep); 10-year sealed battery states pinned.

`traderview-expense::snow_removal_responsibility` is the **state snow/ice removal landlord responsibility compliance check** вҖ” addresses one of the most heavily litigated premises-liability topics in cold-weather states. Three jurisdictions have meaningfully different rules with sharply different liability postures: Massachusetts abolished the natural-vs-unnatural accumulation distinction and imposes reasonable-care duty; Illinois grants statutory IMMUNITY to residential owners who voluntarily clear public sidewalks; New York City mandates a 4-hour removal window by municipal ordinance.

**Four regimes**:

| Regime | Authority | Landlord duty | Timeline | Tenant delegation valid? | Immunity? |
|--------|-----------|----------------|----------|----------------------------|-----------|
| `Massachusetts` | Papadopoulos v. Target Corp. 457 Mass. 368 (2010) + 105 CMR 410.452 | **Reasonable care** + primary means-of-egress duty | None statutory (reasonable) | **Only if** single-family + tenant has independent private entrance | No |
| `Illinois` | 745 ILCS 75/ Snow and Ice Removal Act (1979) | **None statutory** | None | Per lease | **YES** on public sidewalk (residential, voluntary, non-willful) |
| `NewYorkCity` | NYC Admin Code В§ 16-123 | Mandatory removal | **4 hours** after snow stops (except 9 PMвҖ“7 AM) | Only single-family; owner still gets ticket | No |
| `Default` | вҖ” | Common-law habitability | None | Per lease | No |

**Three regression-critical distinguishers**:
- **Only IL has voluntary-clearing immunity** вҖ” IL Snow Removal Act 745 ILCS 75/ shields landlords who voluntarily clear public sidewalks from liability unless willful/wanton. MA + NYC + Default impose liability for negligent clearing.
- **Only NYC has 4-hour timeline** вҖ” В§ 16-123 sets a concrete statutory clock; MA + IL + Default have no statutory timeline.
- **Only MA requires independent-entrance for valid delegation** вҖ” MA Sanitary Code 105 CMR 410.452 bars landlord delegation of means-of-egress duty unless tenant has independent private entrance. NYC permits single-family delegation regardless of entrance (but owner still gets ticket).

**Massachusetts Papadopoulos invariant**: pre-2010, MA distinguished "natural" snow (no landlord duty) from "unnatural" accumulation (duty). The 2010 SJC decision ELIMINATED this distinction вҖ” landlords now owe reasonable care to all lawful visitors regardless of natural vs unnatural accumulation.

**Illinois immunity carve-outs**: immunity does NOT extend to (a) private property (driveway, walkway to front door, garage entrance), (b) willful or wanton conduct, or (c) failures to correct dangerous conditions that cause accumulation on the public sidewalk.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/snow-removal-responsibility`. Twenty-one tests pin: **MA means-of-egress landlord duty + uncleared violation** + Papadopoulos citation; **MA natural-accumulation-distinction-abolished** regression (12-hour-uncleared still violates regardless of natural); **MA lease delegation invalid in multi-unit** + delegation_to_tenant_valid = false; **MA lease delegation VALID single-family with independent private entrance**; **IL public-sidewalk immunity applies** + 745 ILCS 75/ citation (regression-critical вҖ” 12-hour-uncleared no violation under IL Act); **IL willful/wanton overrides immunity** + WillfulOrWantonOverrideImmunity violation; **IL private property no immunity** + "does NOT extend to private property" citation; **IL private property cleared compliant**; **NYC at 4-hour boundary compliant** (> strict required); **NYC at 5 hours violation** + В§ 16-123 + "4 hours" citation; **NYC multi-unit invalid delegation** + violation; **NYC single-family delegation valid**; **default no-specific-obligation** + "common-law habitability" citation; **jurisdiction routing MA/IL/NYC (NY-Buffalo вҶ’ Default) + case-insensitive**; **only-IL-has-voluntary-clearing-immunity** 4-regime invariant; **only-NYC-has-4-hour-timeline** 3-regime invariant; **only-MA-requires-independent-entrance-for-delegation** (regression-critical вҖ” MA single-family-without-independent-entrance bars delegation but NYC permits); **citations pin authorities** (Papadopoulos + 745 ILCS 75/ + В§ 16-123).

`traderview-expense::soft_story_seismic_retrofit` is the **mandatory soft-story seismic retrofit ordinance compliance module** вҖ” when must a trader-landlord owning a wood-frame multifamily building with a soft/weak first story (open ground-floor parking, retail, or similar large openings lacking lateral bracing) commission a mandatory structural retrofit? Trader-landlord critical for CA multifamily owners: non-compliance exposes owner to ordinance penalties + uninhabitable-building findings + insurance non-renewal. Distinct from siblings `balcony_inspection` (SB 721 / SB 326 Exterior Elevated Elements visual inspection), `water_heater_earthquake_strap` (CA В§ 19211 individual appliance seismic anchoring), and `fire_sprinkler_disclosure` (fire-suppression system disclosure).

**Four regimes** вҖ” three CA cities with distinct frameworks plus default:

| Regime | Authority | Unit threshold | Compliance timeline | Penalty |
|--------|-----------|----------------|---------------------|---------|
| San Francisco | Building Code Chapter 34B (Ord. 66-13, operative June 17, 2013) | **5+ residential units** + 2+ stories over soft story | All deadlines PASSED September 15, 2021 | ~$840/day + SF В§ 102A.16 UNSAFE designation |
| Los Angeles | Ordinance 183893 (Nov 22, 2015) | None (any wood-frame soft story) | **3-phase staged** (2yr / 3.5yr / 7yr) | LAMC В§ 91.6314 Order to Comply + civil + criminal misdemeanor + recordation |
| Berkeley | BMC Chapter 19.39 (eff. 2015) | **3+ residential units** + soft-story first floor | Ongoing certification | BMC enforcement |
| Default | Common-law premises liability + local | N/A | N/A | N/A statutory |

**San Francisco 5+ unit + 2+ stories threshold** вҖ” wood-frame structures with 5 or more residential units AND 2 or more stories OVER a soft/weak story. All tier compliance deadlines passed as of September 15, 2021; non-compliance triggers SF Building Code В§ 102A.16 UNSAFE designation and ~$840/day penalty. Pinned by `sf_compliant_passes`, `sf_4_units_out_of_scope`, `sf_5_units_in_scope_boundary` (exactly 5), `sf_not_wood_frame_out_of_scope`, `sf_no_soft_story_out_of_scope`, `sf_in_scope_not_retrofit_complete_violates`, `sf_uniquely_requires_5_units_invariant` (SF requires 5+; Berkeley requires only 3+), `sf_citation_pins_ordinance` (Chapter 34B + Ord. 66-13 + June 17, 2013), `sf_note_pins_5_unit_threshold`, and `sf_note_pins_840_per_day_penalty` (В§ 102A.16).

**Los Angeles 3-phase staged compliance** вҖ” Ordinance 183893 (Nov 22, 2015) covers ~13,500 wood-frame soft-story buildings with staged deadlines:
- **2 years**: structural report + retrofit/demolition plans
- **3.5 years**: secure all required permits
- **7 years**: complete retrofit or demolition + close all permits

Priority 2 deadline: **April 2026**. Pinned by `la_compliant_at_year_8_retrofit_complete`, `la_year_1_structural_report_compliant`, `la_year_1_not_started_violates`, `la_year_2_boundary_structural_report_engages`, `la_year_3_permits_required`, `la_year_3_only_structural_report_violates`, `la_year_4_retrofit_complete_required`, `la_year_8_retrofit_complete_required`, `la_year_8_permits_secured_violates`, `la_no_unit_threshold_3_units_in_scope` (LA has no unit minimum), `la_citation_pins_ordinance_183893` (LAMC В§ 91.6314), `la_note_pins_april_2026_priority_2_deadline`, `la_note_pins_3_phase_timeline`, and `la_uniquely_has_staged_compliance_invariant` (SF + Berkeley = single completion stage; LA = 3-phase).

**Berkeley 3+ unit lower threshold** вҖ” BMC Chapter 19.39 (eff. 2015) applies to wood-frame multifamily structures with 3 or more units (lower than SF's 5+ threshold) AND soft-story first floor; ongoing certification requirement. Pinned by `berkeley_3_unit_boundary_in_scope` (exactly 3), `berkeley_2_units_out_of_scope`, `berkeley_not_complete_violates`, `berkeley_citation_pins_bmc`, and `berkeley_uniquely_lower_3_unit_threshold_invariant` (Berkeley catches 3-unit buildings that SF doesn't).

**Default fallback** вҖ” no statutory soft-story retrofit requirement; common-law premises liability + state building code (where adopted) + local ordinances may apply. Analogous programs exist in Oakland + San Jose + Pasadena + West Hollywood. Pinned by `default_no_violation`, `default_citation_pins_local_ordinances`, and `default_note_pins_other_ca_cities` (Oakland + San Jose + Pasadena + West Hollywood).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/soft-story-seismic-retrofit`. Thirty-three tests pin: **SF compliant passes**; **SF 4 units out of scope**; **SF 5 units in scope boundary**; **SF not wood frame out of scope**; **SF no soft story out of scope**; **SF in scope not retrofit complete violates** (В§ 102A.16); **SF citation pins ordinance** (Chapter 34B + Ord. 66-13 + June 17, 2013); **SF note pins 5-unit threshold**; **SF note pins $840 per day penalty** (В§ 102A.16); **LA compliant at year 8 retrofit complete**; **LA year 1 structural report compliant**; **LA year 1 not started violates**; **LA year 3 permits required**; **LA year 3 only structural report violates**; **LA year 8 retrofit complete required**; **LA year 8 permits secured violates**; **LA no unit threshold 3 units in scope**; **LA citation pins Ordinance 183893** (LAMC В§ 91.6314); **LA note pins April 2026 Priority 2 deadline**; **LA note pins 3-phase timeline** (2yr / 3.5yr / 7yr); **Berkeley 3-unit boundary in scope**; **Berkeley 2 units out of scope**; **Berkeley not complete violates** (Chapter 19.39); **Berkeley citation pins BMC**; **default no violation**; **default citation pins local ordinances**; **default note pins other CA cities**; **four regimes routed correctly**; **SF uniquely requires 5 units invariant**; **LA uniquely has staged compliance invariant**; **Berkeley uniquely lower 3-unit threshold invariant**; **LA year 2 boundary structural report engages**; **LA year 4 retrofit complete required**.

`traderview-expense::soi_protection` is the **state-by-state source-of-income (SOI) discrimination protection table** вҖ” sibling to `just_cause_eviction`, `dv_termination`, `lockout_penalties`, `application_fees`, `entry_notice`, `retaliation_windows`, `eviction_notices`, `late_fee_caps`, `deposit_interest`, `deposit_return_windows`, `lease_disclosures`, `habitability_remedies`, `rent_control`, `military_termination`, `security_deposit_caps`, and `contractor_1099`. The **federal Fair Housing Act does NOT cover source of income** вҖ” landlords may refuse Section 8 voucher holders nationwide unless a state or local statute provides protection. ~18-20 states + DC + many cities have enacted SOI protection since CT (1989) became the first.

**Three regimes:**

| Regime                              | States                                                                       |
|-------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| **Statewide full protection**       | CA / CO / CT / DC / DE / IL / MA / MD / ME / MN / NJ / NM / OR / RI / VA / VT / WA (17 jurisdictions) |
| **Statewide challenged / partial**  | NY (HSTPA 2019 struck down at appellate level March 2026 вҖ” NYC local survives) / UT (veterans only) |
| **No statewide (federal floor only)** | 33 other states; local ordinances in GA (Atlanta), PA (Philadelphia/Pittsburgh), TN (Memphis), TX (Austin), WI (Madison/Dane County) |

**Historical waves:**

| Wave            | States                                  |
|-----------------|-----------------------------------------|
| Pre-2000 pioneers | DC (1977), CT/MA/VT (1989), RI (1996) |
| 2002-2014       | NJ (2002), ME (2009), OR (2014)         |
| 2018 wave       | WA (2018)                               |
| 2019 wave       | CA SB 329 (2019), NY HSTPA (2019)       |
| 2020 wave       | CO / DE / MD / VA вҖ” racial-equity / fair-housing push |
| 2023 wave       | IL / MN / NM вҖ” most recent batch        |

**`verify_current_status_needed` flag** is load-bearing. NY's SOI provision was struck down at the appellate level in March 2026 вҖ” statewide enforcement is contested but NYC Human Rights Law still applies. UT В§ 13-21-302 covers veterans only, not full voucher population. Both flag `verify_current_status_needed: true` to tell the UI to surface "check current court status + locality" rather than reporting a definitive answer. Pinned by `new_york_challenged_regime_verify_needed` and `utah_partial_only_veterans` + the `challenged_states_set_verify_needed_flag` sweep.

**Voucher type doesn't change the answer.** Section 8 HCV, HUD-VASH, FUP, and other federal/state subsidy vouchers all get the same state-law treatment вҖ” the SOI statute classifies "source of income" without distinguishing voucher programs. The `voucher_type` enum is captured on input for future-proofing if a state someday differentiates. Pinned by `voucher_type_does_not_change_state_law_outcome` (CA returns identical results across all four voucher types).

**Local-only ordinances surface in the citation.** GA, PA, TN, TX, WI all have `NoProtection` regime statewide but mention local ordinances (Atlanta / Philadelphia / Memphis / Austin / Madison) in the citation field. Caller must check municipal law for these states. Pinned by `local_only_states_flagged_in_citation`.

**Unknown state defaults to federal floor (landlord may refuse).** Safest assumption вҖ” if we don't have the state in the table, federal FHA doesn't cover SOI so the landlord can refuse. Pinned by `unknown_state_defaults_to_federal_floor`.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/soi-protection-check`. Eighteen tests pin: 51-row coverage; CA full protection (landlord may NOT refuse); TX no statewide (landlord may refuse, federal floor only); CT 1989 oldest statewide; OR 2014 first post-2010; **NY challenged regime sets verify flag**; UT partial-only-veterans sets verify flag; **17-state full-protection sweep** (catches regime-flag regression); voucher_type doesn't change outcome (all four types same); unknown state defaults to federal floor; case-insensitive lookup; sorted `all_states()`; non-empty citations; **2023 wave** (IL/MN/NM all enacted 2023); **2020 wave** (CO/DE/MD/VA); pre-2000 pioneers (CT/MA/VT/RI/DC); local-only-states flagged in citation (GA/PA/TN/TX/WI); `verify_current_status_needed` flag set for both NY and UT.

`traderview-expense::immigration_status_protection` is the **state Immigrant Tenant Protection landlord compliance check** вҖ” two states (California AB 291 2017 + Illinois Immigrant Tenant Protection Act 2019) have enacted statutes prohibiting landlords from weaponizing tenants' immigration status. The federal Fair Housing Act bars national-origin discrimination but does NOT specifically protect against immigration-status-related threats вҖ” these state laws fill that gap.

**Three regimes**:

| Regime | Statute | Prohibited acts | Application-inquiry prong | Warrant carve-out | Criminal prosecution | $/violation |
|--------|---------|-----------------|-----------------------------|---------------------|-----------------------|-------------|
| `CaliforniaAb291` | Cal. Civ. Code В§В§ 1940.05, 1940.2, 1940.3, 1942.5 | Disclosure + threat + eviction + retaliation + **application inquiry** | **Yes** (В§ 1940.3, CA-only) | **Yes** (В§ 1940.2 вҖ” judge-signed warrant/subpoena in criminal investigation) | **Yes** (AG/DA) | $2,000 |
| `Illinois` | 765 ILCS 755/ (eff. 2019-08-23) | Disclosure + threat + eviction + retaliation | No | No | No | $2,000 |
| `Default` | вҖ” | None statutorily | вҖ” | вҖ” | вҖ” | $0 |

**Three CA-only prongs distinguish CA from IL**: В§ 1940.3 application-inquiry prohibition; В§ 1940.2 judicial-warrant carve-out (only CA allows disclosure under judge-signed warrant/subpoena); criminal prosecution by AG/DA. All three are CA-specific; IL has none of them.

**Both states share $2,000 per-violation civil penalty + actual damages + attorney fees**. CA additionally permits criminal charges; IL adds equitable relief.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/immigration-status-protection`. Twenty-three tests pin: **CA disclosure to ICE violation** + В§ 1940.2 + $2,000 penalty regression; **CA judicial-warrant carve-out excuses disclosure** + "judicial warrant" citation; **CA threat to disclose violation** + AB 291 citation; **CA threat NOT excused by warrant** (regression вҖ” warrant only excuses disclosure, not threats); **CA eviction-based-on-status violation** + В§ 1942.5; **CA retaliation violation**; **CA application-inquiry violation** + В§ 1940.3 + "CA-only" citation regression; **CA criminal prosecution available**; **IL disclosure violation** + 765 ILCS 755/ citation; **IL threat violation**; **IL eviction violation** + В§ 5 citation; **IL application-inquiry NOT prohibited** (regression вҖ” IL lacks the В§ 1940.3-equivalent application prong); **IL no judicial-warrant carve-out** (regression вҖ” disclosure violates even with warrant); **IL no criminal prosecution**; **IL $2,000 civil penalty**; **default no obligation** + FHA citation; **state routing CA/IL/Default**; **case-insensitive lookup**; **only CA has application-inquiry prong** (single-test 3-regime invariant); **only CA has judicial-warrant carve-out** (single-test 3-regime invariant вҖ” same warrant input only excuses CA disclosure); **only CA has criminal prosecution** (single-test 3-regime invariant); **CA and IL both $2,000 civil penalty** (invariant); **citations pin authorities** (В§ 1940.2 + В§ 1940.3 + В§ 1942.5 + 765 ILCS 755/).

`traderview-expense::reasonable_accommodation_modification` is the **Federal FHA + state reasonable-accommodation / reasonable-modification compliance check for tenants with disabilities** вҖ” implements the two-prong duty under 42 U.S.C. В§ 3604(f)(3) and the state-level overlays that expand or harden it. Distinct from `service_animal` (which is one specific accommodation flavor) and from `senior_disabled_protection` (which is age-based / income-based eviction protection, not the FHA disability framework).

**Two statutory prongs (federal floor, applies across all four regimes)**:

| Prong | Statute | Cost borne by | Denial threshold |
|-------|---------|---------------|------------------|
| **Modification** вҖ” physical change to unit/common area | 42 U.S.C. В§ 3604(f)(3)(A) | **Tenant** | Restoration agreement permissible **only** if modification would materially impact the next occupant (per 2008 HUD Joint Statement on Reasonable Modifications) |
| **Accommodation** вҖ” change to rule, policy, practice, or service | 42 U.S.C. В§ 3604(f)(3)(B) | **Landlord** | Undue financial/administrative burden **OR** fundamental alteration of the program |

**Four-regime overlay**:

| Regime | Statute | Unique requirement |
|--------|---------|--------------------|
| `Federal` | 42 U.S.C. В§ 3604(f)(3)(A)вҖ“(B) | Baseline; HUD Joint Statements (2004 accommodation + 2008 modification) |
| `California` | Cal. Civ. Code В§ 54.1(b)(3)(A)вҖ“(B) + В§ 54(b) | **Escrow capped at reasonable estimate of restoration cost**; no additional security may be required; В§ 54(b) sensory-disability coverage **broader than ADA** (blind, visually-impaired, deaf, hearing-impaired) |
| `NYC` | N.Y.C. Admin. Code В§ 8-107(15)(c) + В§ 8-102 | **MANDATORY cooperative-dialogue requirement** вҖ” landlord MUST engage in documented written/oral dialogue; failure to engage is itself a discriminatory practice independent of substantive outcome |
| `Washington` | RCW 49.60.222(2)(b) | Mirrors FHA cost allocation (modification = tenant pays; accommodation = landlord pays) |

**The NYC cooperative-dialogue mandate is the unique strict requirement.** N.Y.C. В§ 8-107(15)(c) is the only regime among the four that imposes an affirmative duty on the landlord to engage in a documented dialogue. The dialogue must occur within a reasonable time after the request is received (audit threshold pinned at `NYC_DIALOGUE_REASONABLE_TIME_DAYS = 30`). Failure to engage is the discriminatory act itself вҖ” even if the underlying request would have been denied on undue-burden grounds. Pinned by `nyc_failure_to_engage_dialogue_is_violation`, `nyc_dialogue_beyond_reasonable_time_is_violation`, `nyc_dialogue_at_reasonable_time_boundary_compliant`, `nyc_dialogue_failure_independent_of_substantive_grant`, and the multi-regime invariant `only_nyc_imposes_cooperative_dialogue_mandate_4_regime_invariant`.

**The California escrow cap is the unique state expansion.** Cal. Civ. Code В§ 54.1(b)(3)(B) prohibits the landlord from imposing additional security on the disabled tenant and caps any escrow agreement at the reasonable-estimate-of-restoration-cost benchmark.
