Pinned by `california_modification_escrow_within_cap_compliant`, `california_modification_escrow_at_cap_boundary_compliant`, `california_modification_escrow_exceeds_cap_violation`, and the multi-regime invariant `only_california_caps_escrow_at_reasonable_estimate_4_regime_invariant`.

**Restoration agreement is permissible only where the modification materially impacts the next occupant.** Under HUD's 2008 Joint Statement on Reasonable Modifications, restoration is reasonable for modifications that would not be useful or could be hazardous to future tenants (e.g., narrowed doorways, lowered countertops), but NOT for modifications a typical occupant would prefer (e.g., grab bars in the bathroom, ramps at the entrance). Pinned by `federal_modification_restoration_permissible_only_when_materially_impacts_next` and `federal_modification_restoration_not_permissible_when_no_material_impact`.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/reasonable-accommodation-modification`. Twenty-seven tests pin: **federal modification grantable + tenant pays**; **federal modification restoration permissible iff materially impacts next**; **federal accommodation grantable + landlord pays**; **federal accommodation undue burden defeats duty**; **federal accommodation fundamental alteration defeats duty**; **no qualifying disability no duty**; **no nexus no duty**; **CA modification escrow within cap**; **CA modification escrow at cap boundary compliant** (вүӨ); **CA modification escrow exceeds cap violation**; **CA В§ 54(b) sensory disability note** (UX-text regression); **NYC cooperative dialogue required flag set**; **NYC failure to engage = violation**; **NYC dialogue beyond reasonable time = violation**; **NYC dialogue at 30-day boundary compliant**; **NYC failure independent of substantive grant**; **WA modification grantable + tenant pays**; **WA accommodation grantable + landlord pays**; **only-NYC-imposes-cooperative-dialogue 4-regime invariant**; **only-CA-caps-escrow-at-reasonable-estimate 4-regime invariant**; **modification always at tenant expense across all 4 regimes invariant**; **accommodation always at landlord expense across all 4 regimes invariant**; **nexus universal across all 4 regimes invariant**; **undue burden defeats accommodation across all 4 regimes invariant**; **citation pins subsection per regime + request type** (В§ 3604(f)(3)(A) for fed mod, В§ 3604(f)(3)(B) for fed acc, В§ 54.1(b)(3) for CA mod, В§ 8-107(15)(c) + В§ 8-102 for NYC, RCW 49.60.222(2)(b) for WA); **notes always include accommodation-vs-modification distinction** (UX-text regression across all 8 regime/request combinations).

`traderview-expense::mid_tenancy_ownership_change` is the **state landlord mid-tenancy ownership-change notice + security-deposit-transfer compliance check** вҖ” operational concern for any landlord SELLING or transferring ownership of a tenant-occupied property mid-tenancy. Distinct from `landlord_identification_disclosure` (initial identity disclosure at tenancy start) and `foreclosure_tenant_rights` (foreclosure-driven ownership change with PTFA protections). This module addresses the VOLUNTARY mid-tenancy sale path: successor must notify tenant + transfer security deposit to successor or refund to tenant.

**Six regimes**:

| Regime | Authority | Statutory deadline | Joint+several liability | Unique feature |
|--------|-----------|----------------------|--------------------------|------------------|
| `California` | Cal. Civ. Code В§ 1962(c) + В§ 1950.5(g) | **15 days** | **YES** | В§ 1962(c) BARS nonpayment eviction during noncompliance |
| `Massachusetts` | Mass. G.L. c. 186 В§ 15B(2)(b) | **45 days** | **YES** | Deposit transfer plus accrued interest |
| `Florida` | Fla. Stat. В§ 83.49(5) | Upon sale (immediate) | **YES** | Simultaneous tenant notice required |
| `Washington` | RCW 59.18.060(2) + RCW 59.18.270 | Reasonable time | **YES** | Updated landlord identification required |
| `NewYork` | N.Y. GOL В§ 7-105 | **5 days** (strictest) | **YES** | Transferor personal liability |
| `Default` | None | None | No | Common-law successor-liability principles |

**New York В§ 7-105 has the strictest deadline in the matrix.** Five days from transfer for the prior landlord to transfer the security deposit to the new owner + provide written notice to tenant. Failure creates personal liability of the transferor. Pinned by `new_york_within_5_days_compliant`, `new_york_at_5_day_boundary_compliant`, `new_york_past_5_day_deadline_violation`, and the 5-cell boundary invariant `five_day_boundary_truth_table_new_york_invariant`.

**California В§ 1962(c) is the only regime that BARS nonpayment eviction during successor noncompliance.** If the successor owner has not provided В§ 1962 identity disclosure within 15 days, the successor (or transferor) CANNOT serve an eviction notice for nonpayment of rent that accrued during the period of noncompliance. This is a uniquely strong tenant remedy. Pinned by `california_nonpayment_eviction_during_noncompliance_barred` and the 6-regime invariant `nonpayment_eviction_barred_only_in_california_invariant` (CA vs 5 other regimes confirmed do not bar).

**Deposit disposition compliance has two alternative paths**: (1) transfer to successor with tenant notice, OR (2) refund to tenant. Either path satisfies the statute; failing both is a violation. Pinned by `california_refund_to_tenant_alternative_path_compliant`, `california_neither_transfer_nor_refund_violation`, `california_transfer_without_tenant_notice_violation`, and the 4-cell truth-table invariant `deposit_disposition_compliant_iff_transfer_or_refund_invariant`.

**Joint and several liability applies in 5 of 6 regimes.** California, Massachusetts, Florida, Washington, and New York all impose joint and several liability of successor and prior landlord for deposit repayment if successor fails to comply. Only the Default fallback regime lacks the statutory joint-and-several attachment. Pinned by `joint_and_several_liability_only_in_5_regulated_regimes_invariant`.

**Massachusetts has the longest transfer window at 45 days, but requires accrued interest.** The MA c. 186 В§ 15B(2)(b) framework gives transferor more time but adds the interest-transfer obligation. Pinned by `massachusetts_within_45_days_compliant`, `massachusetts_at_45_day_boundary_compliant`, `massachusetts_past_45_day_deadline_violation`.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/mid-tenancy-ownership-change`. Twenty-five tests pin: **CA within 15 days with disclosure compliant + past 15-day deadline no disclosure violation + at 15-day boundary compliant + nonpayment eviction during noncompliance BARRED + refund to tenant alternative path compliant + neither transfer nor refund violation + transfer without tenant notice violation**; **MA within 45 days + at boundary + past deadline violation**; **FL upon sale with transfer compliant + neither transfer nor refund violation**; **WA reasonable time compliant**; **NY within 5 days + at boundary + past 5-day deadline violation**; **Default no specific deadline but deposit disposition required + neither transfer nor refund violation**; **deposit disposition compliant iff transfer or refund 4-cell truth-table invariant**; **statutory deadline per regime invariant** (CA 15 + MA 45 + FL 0 + WA 0 + NY 5 + Default 0); **joint+several liability only in 5 regulated regimes invariant**; **nonpayment eviction barred only in California 6-regime invariant**; **citation pins authority per regime** (В§ 1962(c) + В§ 1950.5(g) for CA; c. 186 В§ 15B for MA; В§ 83.49(5) for FL; RCW 59.18.060 for WA; В§ 7-105 for NY); **sibling-module note across all 6 regimes** (UX-text regression for landlord_identification_disclosure + foreclosure_tenant_rights + VOLUNTARY scope); **5-day boundary truth-table NY invariant** (5 cells: 0/4/5/6/30 days).

`traderview-expense::mid_tenancy_security_deposit_increase` is the **mandatory landlord-paid prohibition on mid-tenancy security deposit increase module** вҖ” when may a landlord demand additional security deposit during the tenancy after initial collection? Distinct from siblings `security_deposit_caps` (initial cap), `damage_deduction_itemization`, `deposit_interest`, `security_deposit_bank_disclosure`, and `deposit_return_windows`. Trader-landlord operational concern when annual rent increase or pet addition triggers desire for higher deposit.

**Four regimes**:

| Regime | Authority | Cap | Bad-faith damages |
|--------|-----------|-----|---------------------|
| California Cal. Civ. Code В§ 1950.5(c) + AB 12 | 1 mo unfurnished / 2 mo furnished (eff. July 1, 2024) | Standard remedies |
| New Jersey N.J.S.A. В§В§ 46:8-21.2 + 46:8-19 | 1.5 mo | DOUBLE DAMAGES + attorney fees |
| New York N.Y. Gen. Oblig. Law В§ 7-108(1-a)(a) HSTPA 2019 | 1 mo statewide | HSTPA remedies |
| Default | Lease + state caps | State-specific UDAP |

**California вҖ” Cal. Civ. Code В§ 1950.5(c) + AB 12**. Mid-tenancy security deposit increase generally NOT permitted unless (a) lease modification basis (new pet, additional occupant) AND (b) total deposit remains within В§ 1950.5(c) statutory cap (one month's rent for unfurnished; two months for furnished, per AB 12 effective July 1, 2024) AND (c) tenant written consent. Pinned by `ca_no_basis_violates`, `ca_cap_exceeded_violates`, `ca_no_consent_violates`, `ca_multiple_violations_simultaneous`.

**New Jersey вҖ” N.J.S.A. В§В§ 46:8-21.2 + 46:8-19**. Mid-tenancy increase prohibited absent lease modification basis OR proportional annual rent increase basis. Total deposit cap is 1.5 months' rent (В§ 46:8-19). **Bad-faith mid-tenancy increases recoverable with DOUBLE DAMAGES + attorney fees + court costs** (uniquely punitive among the four regimes). Pinned by `nj_no_basis_violates`, `nj_cap_exceeded_violates`, `nj_double_damages_note_present`, `nj_unique_double_damages_invariant`.

**New York вҖ” N.Y. Gen. Oblig. Law В§ 7-108(1-a)(a) (HSTPA 2019)**. Security deposit capped at ONE MONTH's rent statewide. Mid-tenancy increase permitted only when proportional to rent increase AND total remains within one-month cap.

**Default вҖ” lease controls + common-law contract modification + good-faith doctrine**. Most states allow mid-tenancy increase if lease permits OR by mutual written modification. State UDAP statutes may reach abusive mid-tenancy increases.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/mid-tenancy-security-deposit-increase`. Twenty-three tests pin: **CA clean increase authorized**; **CA no basis violates** (lease modification basis required); **CA cap exceeded violates** (AB 12 cap); **CA no consent violates** (tenant written consent required); **CA citation pins AB 12**; **NJ clean increase authorized**; **NJ no basis violates** (В§ 46:8-21.2 + proportional rent or lease modification); **NJ cap exceeded violates** (В§ 46:8-19 1.5 months); **NJ double damages note present** (uniquely punitive); **NY clean increase authorized**; **NY cap exceeded violates** (В§ 7-108(1-a)(a) one-month HSTPA); **NY HSTPA 2019 note present**; **NY no basis violates**; **default compliant always**; **default authorized with consent or basis**; **default no basis no consent not authorized but compliant**; **no increase attempted always compliant** (4-regime sweep); **four regimes routed correctly**; **CA multiple violations simultaneous** (3 violations); **NJ unique double damages invariant**; **CA + NJ + NY clean no violations**.

`traderview-expense::mid_tenancy_term_modification` is the **mid-tenancy modification of NON-RENT lease terms** landlord compliance check вҖ” when may a landlord unilaterally change pet policy, parking rules, smoking policy, late-fee structure, utility allocation, or other non-rent terms during an existing tenancy? Distinct from `rent_increase_notice_period` (rent-amount changes for CA/WA/OR), `lease_termination_notice` (ending tenancy or non-renewal across NY/CA/OR/WA/NJ + default), and `lease_waiver_enforceability` (enforceability of lease waivers of statutory tenant rights). This module addresses ONLY mid-tenancy modification of non-rent terms during an ongoing tenancy.

**Four regimes**:

| Regime | Authority | Periodic-tenancy unilateral modification | Fixed-term | Mail extension |
|--------|-----------|-------------------------------------------|------------|----------------|
| **California** | Cal. Civ. Code В§ 827(a); CCP В§В§ 1162, 1013(a) | YES вҖ” 30 days written notice for monthly; 7-day floor for shorter periodic (agreement may reduce to вүҘ 7 days) | bilateral consent required | +5 days for mail within CA |
| **New York** | RPL В§ 226-c (HSTPA 2019) + common law | NO вҖ” no statute authorizes unilateral non-rent term modification; В§ 226-c covers rent + non-renewal only | bilateral consent required | n/a |
| **Texas** | Tex. Prop. Code В§В§ 91.001, 92.006 | NO absent lease clause; lease-granted right enforceable with written notice + prescribed advance period | bilateral consent required absent lease clause | n/a |
| **Default** | Restatement (Second) of Contracts В§ 149 + common law | NO вҖ” bilateral consent required; В§ 149 statute of frauds applies to material modifications of 1-year-plus leases | bilateral consent required | n/a |

**California В§ 827(a) is the only US statute permitting unilateral mid-tenancy modification of non-rent terms in periodic tenancies.** Outside CA, even week-to-week and month-to-month landlords must obtain tenant signed consent for non-rent term changes mid-tenancy вҖ” or terminate the tenancy per state termination-notice statute and re-let on new terms. Pinned by `ca_monthly_thirty_day_personal_service_valid`, `ny_monthly_unilateral_modification_void`, `tx_unilateral_without_lease_clause_void`, `default_unilateral_modification_void`.

**California's CCP В§ 1013(a) 5-day mail extension is silent but load-bearing.** A landlord who mails a В§ 827(a) modification notice 30 days before the effective date has under-noticed by 5 days вҖ” the modification is unenforceable. Service by personal delivery under CCP В§ 1162 carries no extension. Pinned by `ca_monthly_mail_service_requires_extra_five_days`, `ca_monthly_mail_service_thirty_five_days_valid`, `ca_thirty_day_with_mail_one_day_short_invalid`.

**The 7-day floor under В§ 827(a) cannot be contracted below.** A rental agreement clause specifying 3-day modification notice is void as against the statutory floor; the floor (7 days) is the minimum enforceable notice. Pinned by `ca_agreement_reduces_below_seven_void_falls_to_floor` (3 days specified вҶ’ 7 days required). Agreement may reduce monthly periodic 30-day notice to as low as 7 days вҖ” pinned by `ca_monthly_agreement_reduces_to_ten_days_valid` (10 days valid).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/mid-tenancy-term-modification`. Twenty-six tests pin: **CA monthly 30-day personal service valid baseline**; **CA monthly short notice violation** (25 < 30); **CA monthly mail service requires extra 5 days** (30 short of 35 mail-extended); **CA monthly mail service 35 days valid**; **CA monthly agreement reduces to 10 days valid**; **CA agreement reduces below 7 days void вҶ’ falls to 7-day floor**; **CA week-to-week 7 days valid baseline**; **CA fixed-term requires bilateral consent** (В§ 827(a) does not reach fixed-term); **CA oral notice invalid for periodic** (В§ 827(a) requires writing); **CA fixed-term tenant consent satisfies modification** (bilateral path bypasses В§ 827(a) analysis); **NY monthly unilateral modification void** (no statute authorizes); **NY fixed-term unilateral modification void**; **NY tenant consent satisfies bilateral requirement**; **NY notes termination alternative** (В§ 226-c 30/60/90-day termination path); **TX unilateral without lease clause void**; **TX lease clause with written notice valid** (lease-granted modification right); **TX lease clause oral notice invalid**; **TX lease clause zero days notice invalid**; **default unilateral modification void**; **default consent satisfies modification**; **default notes statute of frauds** (Restatement (Second) of Contracts В§ 149); **citation CA pins В§ 827(a) and В§В§ 1162, 1013(a)** (service statutes); **citation NY pins В§ 226-c HSTPA 2019**; **citation TX pins В§В§ 91.001, 92.006**; **CA 7-day floor boundary with mail extension** (W2W + 5 = 12 days; 11 days insufficient); **CA 30-day with mail 1-day-short invalid** (34 < 35 effective).

`traderview-expense::mid_tenancy_temporary_relocation` is the **mid-tenancy temporary relocation rights** module вҖ” when landlord requires tenant to TEMPORARILY vacate for substantial repairs / renovation / abatement work with tenant retaining the right to return. Distinct from `tenant_relocation_assistance` (no-fault permanent eviction dollar amounts), `lease_termination_catastrophic_damage` (fire/flood termination), and `demolition_tenant_notice` (permanent demolition).

**Four regimes**:

| Regime | Authority | Threshold | Relocation requirement |
|--------|-----------|-----------|--------------------------|
| **California** | Cal. Civ. Code В§В§ 1946.2(d)(2), 1946.2(d)(3), 1942.5(b); SF Rent Ord. В§ 37.9(a)(11); Long Beach SRTD | Substantial remodel requires vacancy вүҘ 30 days | One month rent OR alternative housing + tenant right to return |
| **New Jersey** | N.J.S.A. В§В§ 2A:18-61.1(g), 2A:18-61.11 | Anti-Eviction Act renovation removal | Alternative housing OR relocation expenses |
| **Washington** | RCW 59.18.085; Seattle SMC 22.210; Bellingham Tenant Protections Ord. | Displacement for repair / maintenance / abatement | Relocation assistance + written notice |
| **Default** | common-law habitability + municipal overlays (SF / Long Beach / Seattle / Bellingham) | lease + common law | varies by municipality |

**Universal cosmetic-improvement carve-out.** Painting, decorating, and minor repairs do NOT qualify as substantial remodel under Cal. Civ. Code В§ 1946.2(d)(2) and trigger no temporary relocation requirement in any regime. Pinned by `cosmetic_improvement_no_relocation_required_universal` (4-regime sweep) and `cosmetic_improvement_path_bypasses_all_other_compliance_checks`.

**CA В§ 1946.2(d)(2) 30-day vacancy threshold uniquely defines substantial remodel.** Cal. Civ. Code requires the work to require tenant vacancy for AT LEAST 30 DAYS to qualify as substantial remodel just-cause. Work not meeting the 30-day threshold cannot serve as substantial remodel grounds. Pinned by `ca_substantial_remodel_without_30_day_vacancy_violation`, `ca_substantial_remodel_30_day_threshold_invariant` (boundary check), `ca_30_day_threshold_unique_to_california` (4-regime sweep confirms only CA imposes the 30-day threshold).

**CA dual-path compliance: relocation payment OR alternative housing.** В§ 1946.2(d)(3) вҖ” landlord may EITHER pay one-month rent relocation assistance OR provide alternative housing. Either path satisfies. Pinned by `ca_missing_relocation_or_housing_violation` (both missing вҶ’ violation) and `ca_alternative_housing_satisfies_compliance` (housing alone satisfies).

**CA В§ 1942.5(b) tenant right to return preserved.** Temporary relocation must preserve tenant's right to return at completion of work. Permanent displacement without proper В§ 1946.2 just-cause termination is unlawful. Pinned by `ca_no_right_to_return_violation`.

**NJ Anti-Eviction Act В§ 2A:18-61.1(g) renovation removal + В§ 2A:18-61.11 alternative housing or relocation expenses.** Same dual-path compliance as CA вҖ” alternative housing OR relocation expenses satisfy. Pinned by `nj_full_compliance_passes`, `nj_missing_written_notice_violation`, `nj_missing_both_housing_and_relocation_violation`, `nj_relocation_payment_satisfies_compliance`, `nj_unique_anti_eviction_act_invariant` (4-regime sweep confirms only NJ engages В§ 2A:18-61.1(g)).

**Local municipal overlay** notes engaged when `local_jurisdiction_overlay_applies` true. SF Rent Ordinance В§ 37.9(a)(11), Long Beach SRTD Ordinance, Seattle SMC 22.210, and Bellingham Tenant Protections Ordinance all impose additional requirements beyond state law. Pinned by `ca_local_jurisdiction_overlay_note_engaged`, `wa_local_jurisdiction_overlay_note_engaged`, `default_municipal_overlay_note_always_present`.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/mid-tenancy-temporary-relocation`. Twenty-seven tests pin: **cosmetic improvement no relocation required universal** (4-regime sweep); **CA substantial remodel full compliance passes**; **CA substantial remodel without 30-day vacancy violation** (В§ 1946.2(d)(2)); **CA missing written notice violation**; **CA missing relocation or housing violation** (В§ 1946.2(d)(3)); **CA alternative housing satisfies compliance** (dual-path); **CA no right to return violation** (В§ 1942.5(b)); **CA local jurisdiction overlay note engaged** (SF / Long Beach); **NJ full compliance passes**; **NJ missing written notice violation** (В§ 2A:18-61.1(g)); **NJ missing both housing and relocation violation** (В§ 2A:18-61.11); **NJ relocation payment satisfies compliance**; **WA full compliance passes**; **WA missing written notice violation** (RCW 59.18.085); **WA missing relocation assistance violation**; **WA local jurisdiction overlay note engaged** (Seattle / Bellingham); **default compliant with lease + common law**; **default municipal overlay note always present**; **citation CA pins subsections + municipal overlay**; **citation NJ pins В§ 2A:18-61.1(g) + В§ 2A:18-61.11**; **citation WA pins RCW 59.18.085 + local overlays**; **CA substantial remodel 30-day threshold invariant** (with/without check); **cosmetic improvement path bypasses all other compliance checks** (universal carve-out); **NJ no return right note engaged**; **CA multiple violations accumulate**; **NJ unique Anti-Eviction Act invariant** (4-regime sweep); **CA 30-day threshold unique to California** (4-regime sweep).

`traderview-expense::landlord_identification_disclosure` is the **state landlord-identification / emergency-contact-information disclosure compliance check** вҖ” addresses the upfront requirement that a landlord disclose who they are, where they can be served, and how the tenant reaches an emergency contact. Sibling to `tenant_data_privacy` (data handling) вҖ” this module addresses the inverse direction (LANDLORD's identification disclosed TO tenant).

**Four regimes**:

| Regime | Statute | Deadline | Phone required | Emergency contact | Municipal registration | Unlawful detainer consequence |
|--------|---------|----------|----------------|---------------------|--------------------------|--------------------------------|
| `California` | Cal. Civ. Code В§ 1962 | 15 days | **Yes** (CA-only) | No | No | **JURISDICTIONAL PREREQUISITE** to UD; defective disclosure bars eviction |
| `NewJersey` | N.J.S.A. 46:8-27 through 46:8-37 (Landlord Identity Law) | 30 days | No | **Yes** (NJ-only) | **Yes** (NJ-only) вҖ” file with municipal clerk | No |
| `Washington` | RCW 59.18.060 | None | No | No | No | No |
| `Default` | вҖ” | None | вҖ” | вҖ” | вҖ” | No |

**California В§ 1962 is the strictest**: defective disclosure is JURISDICTIONALLY fatal to an unlawful detainer action. A landlord who attempts to evict for nonpayment without В§ 1962-compliant disclosure cannot maintain the action. Sets up name + telephone + street-address-for-personal-service + entity-for-rent-payments вҖ” all four required. 15-day deadline applies to oral agreement creation OR written agreement execution OR successor-owner succession.

**New Jersey's Landlord Identity Law has two-step process**: (a) file Certificate of Registration with municipal clerk within 30 days; (b) supply registration info to each tenant. NJ-unique emergency-contact requirement: registration must identify a representative of owner or managing agent who may be contacted in case of emergency. This is regression-critical вҖ” CA + WA do not require an emergency contact.

**Washington's RCW 59.18.060 is the simplest**: name + address of (a) person authorized to manage premises and (b) owner or authorized agent. No phone, no emergency contact, no deadline.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/landlord-identification-disclosure`. Twenty-five tests pin: **CA full compliance**; **CA missing written disclosure bars unlawful detainer** + JURISDICTIONAL citation; **CA missing phone violation** (regression вҖ” CA-only phone requirement); **CA missing street address violation**; **CA past 15-day deadline violation** + В§ 1962 citation; **CA at 15-day boundary compliant** (вүӨ strict); **CA unlawful-detainer flag only when attempting**; **NJ full compliance**; **NJ not-registered violation** + В§ 46:8-28 citation; **NJ missing emergency contact violation** + "emergency contact" citation (regression вҖ” NJ-only); **NJ past 30-day deadline violation**; **NJ at 30-day boundary compliant**; **WA full compliance**; **WA missing disclosure violation** + RCW 59.18.060 citation; **WA missing name violation**; **WA missing address violation**; **WA no phone or emergency-contact requirement** (regression вҖ” WA simpler than CA/NJ); **default no obligation**; **state routing CA/NJ/WA/Default + case-insensitive**; **only-CA-has-unlawful-detainer-jurisdictional-consequence** 3-regime invariant (regression-critical вҖ” CA defect bars UD; NJ + WA do not have parallel consequence); **only-NJ-has-emergency-contact-requirement** 3-regime invariant; **only-NJ-requires-municipal-clerk-registration** 2-regime invariant; **only-CA-requires-phone-disclosure** 3-regime invariant; **citations pin authorities** (В§ 1962 + 46:8 + RCW 59.18.060).

`traderview-expense::just_cause_eviction` is the **state-by-state just-cause eviction + relocation assistance table** вҖ” sibling to `dv_termination`, `lockout_penalties`, `application_fees`, `entry_notice`, `retaliation_windows`, `eviction_notices`, `late_fee_caps`, `deposit_interest`, `deposit_return_windows`, `lease_disclosures`, `habitability_remedies`, `rent_control`, `military_termination`, `security_deposit_caps`, and `contractor_1099`. Major statutory shift 2019-2021 вҖ” CA AB 1482 (Tenant Protection Act), OR SB 608, WA HB 1236 вҖ” changed whether a landlord can terminate at lease end at all, and required relocation assistance equal to one month's rent for no-fault grounds.

**Four regimes**:

| Regime                          | Coverage threshold | Relocation                          | States                                  |
|---------------------------------|--------------------|-------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------|
| **Statewide after N months**    | CA 12mo / OR 12mo  | 1 month current rent                | CA Civ. Code В§ 1946.2 (AB 1482) / OR ORS В§ 90.427 (SB 608) |
| **Statewide day-one**           | Immediate          | WA: 1 mo rent / DC: 0 / NJ: 0       | WA RCW В§ 59.18.650 (HB 1236) / DC В§ 42-3505.01 / NJ Anti-Eviction Act |
| **Partial by building**         | Rent-regulated / 4+ unit only | Statewide does NOT reach all rentals вҖ” caller checks rent-regulation status | NY HSTPA / IL local ordinances / ME 14 M.R.S. В§ 6002 |
| **No statewide regime**         | No coverage        | No relocation                       | 41 other states                         |

**OR small-landlord exemption is load-bearing.** SB 608 carves out landlords with вүӨ 4 units from the relocation obligation even though the just-cause requirement still applies вҖ” tenant still gets protection, landlord still needs grounds, but no $$$ on no-fault terminations. Pinned by `oregon_small_landlord_4_units_exempt_from_relocation` (4 units exempt, $0 relocation) and `oregon_5_unit_landlord_owes_relocation` (5 units owes full month's rent). CA has no equivalent small-landlord carve-out.

**At-fault vs no-fault distinction.** All grounds satisfy just-cause once it applies, but only no-fault grounds (owner move-in, substantial remodel, withdrawal from market) trigger relocation assistance. At-fault grounds (non-payment, lease violation, nuisance, criminal activity) require no payment regardless of state. Pinned by `california_at_fault_grounds_no_relocation` and `oregon_at_fault_grounds_no_relocation_regardless_of_unit_count` (the OR small-landlord exempt flag stays false on at-fault path because the exemption only matters for no-fault).

**Relocation is current-rent-anchored.** The figure is the displaced tenant's monthly rent as of the notice date вҖ” not market rent. AB 1482 makes this explicit; OR SB 608 mirrors. Deliberate pro-tenant choice: the displaced tenant is compensated for what THEY were paying.

**Boundary at 12-month threshold matters for both CA and OR**: 11 months вҶ’ no-cause termination still available; 12 months exactly вҶ’ just-cause kicks in. Pinned by `california_under_12_months_no_just_cause_required` and `california_at_12_months_just_cause_required` + the OR mirror in `oregon_at_12_months_boundary`.

**PartialByBuilding flag (`partial_by_building_check_rent_regulation`) tells the caller to look further.** NY HSTPA covers rent-stabilized buildings, IL has Chicago RLTO + Evanston + Mt. Prospect local ordinances, ME applies just-cause to 4+ unit buildings only. The compute returns `just_cause_required: false` for these (because the statewide regime doesn't reach the building by default) but sets the flag so the downstream caller queries the appropriate per-building or per-municipality table.

**NJ Anti-Eviction Act 1974 is the strongest pro-tenant in the US** but has no statutory relocation (regime carries `relocation_months_rent: 0`). The regime still flags `just_cause_required: true` and `no_cause_termination_available: false`. Pinned by `new_jersey_anti_eviction_act_day_one_no_relocation`.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/just-cause-check`. Twenty-five tests pin: 51-row coverage; **CA 12-month boundary** (under/at); at-fault grounds satisfy without relocation; no-fault owner move-in = 1 mo rent; **OR small-landlord 4-units exempt + 5-units owes** (load-bearing carve-out); OR 12mo boundary mirrors CA; WA day-one + 1mo relocation; NJ Anti-Eviction Act day-one no-relocation; DC day-one no-relocation; **NY/ME/IL partial-by-building flag set** (caller-must-check-further regression target); no-statewide states pass through; unknown state errors; case-insensitive lookup; sorted `all_states()`; non-empty citations on every row; substantial-remodel and withdrawal-from-market both trigger CA relocation; **OR at-fault grounds never trigger relocation** even at 100 units; zero rent вҶ’ zero relocation no overflow; **i64::MAX/2 rent no overflow** (saturating_mul regression target).

`traderview-expense::just_cause_termination_notice_content` is the **just-cause termination notice CONTENT requirements table** вҖ” what specific content and format must a landlord's WRITTEN termination notice contain to satisfy just-cause statutory requirements? Distinct from sibling `just_cause_eviction` (substantive just-cause grounds + relocation assistance) and `lease_termination_notice` (general termination notice periods). Trader-landlord operational concern in just-cause jurisdictions вҖ” even when the substantive ground is valid, a defective notice is VOIDABLE and forces restart. Notice content + format requirements are independent procedural traps from the substantive just-cause analysis.

**California вҖ” Cal. Civ. Code В§ 1946.2(c)**. Written notice MUST state cause from В§ 1946.2(b)(1) at-fault grounds (14 enumerated categories: nonpayment, breach of material term, nuisance, criminal activity, refusal of entry, etc.) OR В§ 1946.2(b)(2) no-fault grounds (owner move-in, withdrawal from market, demo, gov order, substantial remodel). For CURABLE at-fault violations (lease breach, unauthorized pets, unlawful subletting, property damage), 3-day cure opportunity required before notice to quit. For no-fault grounds, В§ 1946.2(d)(3) one-month-rent relocation assistance required. **В§ 1946.2(c) вҖ” failure to comply renders the written termination notice VOID** (most aggressive consequence of any state).

**Washington вҖ” RCW 59.18.650(2)**. Written notice MUST state specific cause AND specific facts and circumstances constituting the cause (stricter than CA written-reason requirement). В§ 59.18.650(2)(a)-(p) enumerates 16 just-cause categories.

**Oregon вҖ” ORS 90.427 (SB 608)**. Written notice MUST state reason. Tenancies < 1 year permit no-cause termination with 30-day notice; tenancies вүҘ 1 year require just-cause. Strict-compliance regime вҖ” defective notice is VOIDABLE.

**New Jersey вҖ” N.J.S.A. 2A:18-61.2**. Anti-Eviction Act notice MUST state specific cause from В§ 2A:18-61.1(a)-(r) 18 enumerated statutory grounds (nonpayment, disorderly conduct, substantial breach, illegal use, drug-related criminal activity, owner occupancy, conversion, demo, substantial conversion, etc.). Notice content must match the statutory ground EXACTLY вҖ” generic "breach" or "misconduct" does not satisfy.

**Default вҖ” limited content requirements**. Most non-just-cause states require minimal content (date, parties, termination date); lease + state-specific eviction statute control content + format.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/just-cause-termination-notice-content`. Thirty-one tests pin: **CA at-fault non-curable full compliance**; **CA missing specific cause voidable** (В§ 1946.2(c)); **CA oral notice voidable** (WRITTEN required); **CA at-fault curable without cure opportunity voidable** (3-day cure); **CA at-fault curable with cure opportunity compliant**; **CA no-fault without relocation assistance voidable** (В§ 1946.2(d)(3)); **CA no-fault with relocation assistance compliant**; **CA improper service voidable** (Code Civ. Proc. В§ 1162); **CA VOID consequence note always present**; **WA full compliance**; **WA missing specific facts voidable** (FACTS AND CIRCUMSTANCES); **WA unique facts/circumstances requirement invariant** (4-other-regime sweep вҖ” only WA requires); **WA 16 enumerated categories note** (В§ 59.18.650(2)(a)-(p)); **OR full compliance**; **OR missing specific cause voidable** (ORS 90.427); **OR under-one-year no-cause path note** (SB 608); **NJ full compliance**; **NJ missing specific cause voidable** (В§ 2A:18-61.2 + В§ 2A:18-61.1(a)-(r)); **NJ exact statutory ground match required note**; **default oral notice violation**; **default written notice compliant**; **citation California pins (b)(1)/(b)(2)/(c)/(d)(3) + Code Civ. Proc. В§ 1162**; **citation Washington pins В§ 59.18.650(2) + (a)-(p) + RCW 59.12.040**; **citation Oregon pins В§ 90.427 + SB 608 + ORS 90.155**; **citation New Jersey pins В§ 2A:18-61.1(a)-(r) + 2A:18-61.2**; **CA void consequence uniquely explicit invariant**; **CA at-fault grounds note lists 14 categories**; **CA no-fault grounds note lists substantial remodel category**; **NJ Anti-Eviction Act lists 18 grounds note**; **voidable flag engaged on any violation** (regression target); **five regimes routed correctly** (CA + WA + OR + NJ + Default sweep).

`traderview-expense::dv_termination` is the **state-specific domestic violence early lease termination table** вҖ” sibling to `lockout_penalties`, `application_fees`, `entry_notice`, `retaliation_windows`, `eviction_notices`, `late_fee_caps`, `deposit_interest`, `deposit_return_windows`, `lease_disclosures`, `habitability_remedies`, `rent_control`, `military_termination`, `security_deposit_caps`, and `contractor_1099`. Federal **VAWA** (34 U.S.C. В§ 12491) provides a floor for federally-assisted housing (HUD, Section 8, LIHTC, public housing). State laws extend the protection to the private market and provide affirmative early-termination rights with notice + documentation requirements.

Four notice-period bands + three special cases:

| Band                    | States                                                          |
|-------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------|
| **3 days**              | IL Safe Homes Act (765 ILCS 750/15) вҖ” strictest pro-tenant      |
| **7 days**              | ME / NM                                                          |
| **14 days**             | CA В§ 1946.7 / DC В§ 42-3505.07 / HI В§ 521-80 / OR В§ 90.453 / TN / VT |
| **28-30 days**          | AZ / CT / DE / IA / IN / LA / MD / MI / MT / NC / ND / NE / NH / NJ / NV / NY В§ 227-c / PA / RI / SD / TX В§ 92.0161 / UT / VA / WI |
| **End of current month**| WA В§ 59.18.575 (calendar-anchored)                              |
| **Immediate (0 days)**  | MA В§ 24 / MN В§ 504B.206 (immediate with documentation)          |
| **No fixed period**     | CO В§ 38-12-402 (statute exists, "reasonable" notice required)   |
| **No state statute**    | 15 states (VAWA floor only)                                     |

**Documentation requirements** are nearly uniform across states with statutes: at least ONE of (a) protective order, (b) police report, or (c) qualified-third-party statement (medical provider, mental health professional, victim service provider, clergy). Compute returns `documentation_sufficient: true` if any of the three flags is true.

**Documentation freshness window** is state-specific and varies 30 days (CT, IA, NE) вҶ’ 60 days (DC, IL, NJ, NV, WI) вҶ’ 90 days (HI, MA, NC, OR, WA) вҶ’ 180 days (CA, DE, RI) вҶ’ no requirement (most 30-day-notice states). Notice date - incident date must be within the window. Pinned by `california_180_day_freshness_window` (179 days inside / 181 days outside).

**Three immediate-termination triggers** can override the notice requirement entirely:
- **Co-tenant violence**: TX В§ 92.0161(b), AZ, HI, IL, MA, MN, OR allow immediate termination when the abuser is a co-tenant on the lease
- **Landlord/agent violence**: WA В§ 59.18.575, AZ, DC, HI, IL, MA, MN, OR allow immediate termination when the abuser is the landlord
- Compute returns `immediate_termination_available: true` whenever the state's per-trigger flag is set AND the corresponding input flag is true

States WITHOUT a carve-out don't override: setting `violence_by_co_tenant = true` in CA has no effect because CA В§ 1946.7 still requires the 14-day notice. Pinned by `co_tenant_violence_does_not_trigger_immediate_in_states_without_carveout` (7-day actual still shortfalls by 7 against the 14-day requirement).

**Washington's EndOfCurrentMonth regime** is calendar-anchored, not day-counted вҖ” required_notice_days returns None. Notice on Jan 15 вҶ’ must terminate вүҘ Jan 31 (end of month). The `end_of_month()` helper handles year rollover (Dec вҶ’ next Jan) and short-month February correctly. Pinned by `washington_short_month_february_handled` (Feb 5 notice вҶ’ Feb 28 end) and `washington_december_notice_wraps_to_dec_31` (Dec 15 notice вҶ’ Dec 31).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/dv-termination-check`. Twenty-four tests pin: 51-row coverage; CA 14-day exact boundary + 13-day shortfall; TX 30-day boundary; **IL 3-day strictest band**; WA end-of-month with year-rollover and short-Feb handling (calendar-anchored math regression targets); WA Jan-31 boundary; TX co-tenant violence waives notice; WA landlord violence waives notice; **CA without co-tenant carve-out doesn't waive** (7-day shortfall pinned); documentation sufficient with only protective order; documentation insufficient when all three false; CA 180-day freshness window inside/outside; no-freshness states accept any-age documentation; 15 no-statute states flagged correctly with VAWA-only note; unknown state errors; case-insensitive lookup; sorted `all_states()`; non-empty citations; notice-before-incident negative freshness window; end-of-month boundary same-day; shortfall zero when compliant; shortfall reported only for Days regime not EndOfCurrentMonth (note text carries the explanation instead).

`traderview-expense::lock_change_between_tenancies` is the **state landlord lock-change / rekey / security-device compliance check for new-tenant turnover** вҖ” operational concern for every landlord between tenancies. Tenant safety topic distinct from `lockout_penalties` (which addresses unlawful self-help lockouts during tenancy) and `dv_termination` (which addresses domestic-violence emergency lock change during tenancy). This module addresses the BETWEEN-TENANCIES lock change / security device installation duty.

**Six regimes** diverge along three axes:

| Regime | Authority | Mandatory rekey | Deadline | At landlord expense | Specific security device |
|--------|-----------|------------------|----------|----------------------|---------------------------|
| `Texas` | Tex. Prop. Code В§ 92.156(a) | **YES** | **7 days** from move-in | **YES** (В§ 92.156 requires landlord pays; tenant pays only В§ 92.156(b) additional rekey) | No |
| `California` | Cal. Civ. Code В§ 1941.3(a)(1)вҖ“(2) | No | n/a | n/a | **Deadbolt** (13/16 inch bolt extension into doorjamb) |
| `Illinois` | 765 ILCS 5/12 + Chicago RLTO | **YES** | **0 days** (same-day) | **YES** | No |
| `Virginia` | Va. Code В§ 55.1-1221 | No | n/a | n/a | **Locks AND peepholes** |
| `NewYork` | No statewide mandate | No | n/a | n/a | NYC Housing Maintenance Code В§ 27-2043 may impose peephole standards |
| `Default` | None | No | n/a | n/a | None |

**Illinois is stricter than Texas on timing.** Same-day rekey (0 days) vs Texas 7-day window. Pinned by `illinois_one_day_after_move_in_violation` (1 day post-move-in violates IL but not TX) and the comparative invariant `illinois_stricter_than_texas_timing_invariant`.

**Texas В§ 92.156(a) requires landlord to bear the rekeying expense.** Charging the tenant for the between-tenancy rekey violates the statute (although В§ 92.156(b) permits tenant-requested ADDITIONAL rekeying at tenant expense). Pinned by `texas_charged_tenant_expense_violation` and the 6-regime invariant `only_tx_il_require_at_landlord_expense_invariant` (TX + IL only; CA + VA + NY + Default not).

**California В§ 1941.3 covers DEADBOLT INSTALLATION + maintenance, not rekeying between tenancies.** В§ 1941.3(a)(1) requires an operable deadbolt on each main swinging entry door; В§ 1941.3(a)(2) specifies the bolt must extend at least 13/16 of an inch beyond the strike edge into the doorjamb. Pinned by `california_operable_deadbolt_with_spec_compliant`, `california_missing_deadbolt_violation`, and `california_deadbolt_extension_below_spec_violation`.

**Virginia В§ 55.1-1221 mandates BOTH locks AND peepholes.** Missing peephole is a standalone violation. Pinned by `virginia_missing_peephole_violation` and the 6-regime invariant `only_ca_and_va_require_specific_security_device_invariant` (CA + VA only).

**Texas 7-day boundary truth table:** day 0, 6, 7 = compliant; day 8 = violation. Pinned by `texas_boundary_7_versus_8_days_truth_table_invariant`.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/lock-change-between-tenancies`. Twenty-two tests pin: **TX within 7 days at landlord expense compliant + at 7-day boundary compliant + past 7-day deadline violation + landlord did not rekey violation + charged tenant expense violation**; **CA operable deadbolt with spec compliant + missing deadbolt violation + extension below spec violation** (В§ 1941.3(a)(2) 13/16 inch); **IL same-day rekey compliant + 1 day after move-in violation + did not rekey violation**; **VA locks and peephole compliant + missing peephole violation**; **NY no statewide mandate compliant** (even with no rekeying); **Default no mandate compliant regardless** (no deadbolt + no peephole + no rekey); **only-TX-and-IL-impose-mandatory-rekey 6-regime invariant**; **only-CA-and-VA-require-specific-security-device 6-regime invariant**; **Illinois stricter than Texas timing invariant** (1 day post-move-in violates IL but not TX); **only-TX-IL-require-at-landlord-expense 6-regime invariant**; **citation pins authority per regime**; **sibling-module note across all 6 regimes** (UX-text regression for lockout_penalties + dv_termination + BETWEEN-TENANCIES scope); **TX boundary 7 vs 8 days truth table 4-cell invariant** (0/6/7/8 days).

`traderview-expense::dv_survivor_lock_change` is the **DV / sexual assault / stalking survivor mid-tenancy lock change rights check** вҖ” landlord operational concern for tenant safety while preserving the landlord's property-control interest. Distinct from `dv_termination` (early termination of lease without penalty for DV survivors), `lock_change_between_tenancies` (mandatory rekeying when tenancy turns over), and `landlord_harassment` (anti-retaliation framework) вҖ” this module addresses ONLY the MID-TENANCY LOCK-CHANGE PATHWAY where the tenant remains in possession.

**Four regimes** with sharp cost-allocation divergence:

| Regime | Authority | Cost allocation | Timing | Documentation |
|--------|-----------|------------------|--------|----------------|
| **California** | Cal. Civ. Code В§В§ 1941.5, 1941.6; SB 1051 (2023); SB 782 (2010) | **LANDLORD pays** (uniquely) | landlord 24-hour deadline; tenant self-help if landlord inaction; 21-day reimbursement | restraining order / police report / qualified-third-party letter / **signed statement** (SB 1051) |
| **Texas** | Tex. Prop. Code В§В§ 92.156(d)/(e), 92.016 | **TENANT pays** (additional tenant-requested rekey under В§ 92.156(e)) | unlimited tenant requests; turnover at landlord expense under В§ 92.156(d) | per В§ 92.016 DV termination (separately covered) |
| **Washington** | RCW 59.18.575; RCW 59.18.570 | **TENANT pays** | tenant self-help permitted; 7-day notice to landlord required | protection order OR qualified-third-party record (signed statement alone NOT accepted) |
| **Default** | common-law lease + state-specific DV statute | TENANT pays (common-law) | varies sharply | varies |

**California uniquely shifts cost to the landlord.** Pinned by `california_uniquely_landlord_pays_for_dv_lock_change_invariant` (4-regime sweep: only CA = Landlord; TX/WA/Default = Tenant). California requires landlord action WITHIN 24 HOURS of receiving written documentation. If the landlord fails, the tenant may self-help and the landlord must reimburse within 21 days. Pinned by `ca_landlord_acts_within_24_hours_valid_landlord_pays`, `ca_landlord_delayed_25_hours_violation`, `ca_exactly_24_hours_valid` (boundary), `ca_self_help_with_late_reimbursement_violation`, `ca_self_help_at_21_days_exact_boundary_valid_22_days_invalid`.

**California В§ 1941.5 (perpetrator not co-tenant) vs В§ 1941.6 (perpetrator IS co-tenant).** Both provisions require landlord-pays. The distinction drives the statutory citation but not the cost outcome. Pinned by `ca_perpetrator_co_tenant_uses_section_1941_6`.

**SB 1051 (2023) expanded acceptable documentation in California** to include a signed statement by the tenant вҖ” California is the only regime in this module that accepts tenant-signed-statement-only as DV documentation. Pinned by `ca_signed_statement_documentation_accepted`. Washington REJECTS signed-statement-only documentation. Pinned by `wa_signed_statement_alone_not_accepted` and `wa_tenant_signed_statement_with_protection_order_invalid_path_still_fails` (the field is incompatible with WA regime regardless of other timing).

**Washington's 7-day notice rule is strict.** Tenant may change locks at TENANT's expense WITHOUT prior landlord authorization, but must deliver written notice to landlord within 7 DAYS of the lock change with valid protection order OR qualified-third-party record. Pinned by `wa_tenant_paid_within_7_day_notice_valid` (3 days valid), `wa_at_7_day_notice_boundary_valid` (7 exact), `wa_8_day_notice_violation` (8 days late), `wa_no_notice_provided_violation`.

**Washington's qualified third parties** include law enforcement officers, state court employees, healthcare professionals, licensed mental health providers, clergy, and crime-victim or witness-program advocates. Pinned by `wa_qualified_third_party_letter_accepted` and the qualified-third-parties note appearing in every WA result.

**Texas treats tenant-requested rekey (including DV survivor) under В§ 92.156(e) at TENANT expense.** Standard turnover rekey under В§ 92.156(d) is at LANDLORD expense (within 7 days of new tenancy). DV-specific lock-change relief in TX is provided through В§ 92.016 (DV early termination вҖ” covered separately by `dv_termination`). Pinned by `tx_tenant_requested_rekey_at_tenant_expense`, `tx_turnover_rekey_at_landlord_expense`, `tx_notes_section_92_016_dv_termination_separately_covered`.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/dv-survivor-lock-change`. Twenty-five tests pin: **CA landlord acts within 24 hours valid landlord-pays**; **CA landlord delayed 25 hours violation**; **CA exactly 24 hours valid** (boundary); **CA perpetrator co-tenant uses В§ 1941.6**; **CA self-help with timely 21-day reimbursement valid**; **CA self-help with late 22-day reimbursement violation**; **CA signed statement documentation accepted** (SB 1051); **CA self-help at 21-day boundary valid**; **TX tenant-requested rekey at tenant expense** (В§ 92.156(e)); **TX turnover rekey at landlord expense** (В§ 92.156(d)); **TX notes В§ 92.016 DV termination separately covered**; **WA tenant-paid within 7-day notice valid**; **WA at 7-day notice boundary valid**; **WA 8-day notice violation**; **WA no notice provided violation**; **WA signed statement alone NOT accepted** (rejects CA-only documentation type); **WA qualified third party letter accepted**; **default tenant expense with common-law caution note**; **citation CA pins В§В§ 1941.5, 1941.6 + SB 1051 + SB 782**; **citation TX pins В§В§ 92.156(d)/(e), 92.016**; **citation WA pins RCW 59.18.575 + RCW 59.18.570**; **California uniquely landlord-pays for DV lock change invariant** (4-regime sweep CA Landlord, TX/WA/Default Tenant); **CA landlord inaction violation engages 24-hour deadline specifically** (48 hours surfaces in message); **WA tenant signed statement with protection order invalid path still fails** (signed-statement incompatibility persists regardless of notice timing); **CA self-help at 21 days exact boundary valid 22 days invalid**.

`traderview-expense::lockout_penalties` is the **state-specific self-help eviction penalty table** вҖ” sibling to `application_fees`, `entry_notice`, `retaliation_windows`, `eviction_notices`, `late_fee_caps`, `deposit_interest`, `deposit_return_windows`, `lease_disclosures`, `habitability_remedies`, `rent_control`, `military_termination`, `security_deposit_caps`, and `contractor_1099`. Self-help eviction (lockout, utility shutoff, removal of tenant property without court order) is universal landlord exposure вҖ” every state prohibits it вҖ” but the dollar consequences vary by 10Г— across jurisdictions.

**Seven distinct penalty regimes** are present in the table:

| Regime                          | States                                  | Formula                                                              |
|---------------------------------|-----------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------|
| **Additive per-day**            | CA В§ 789.3                              | `max($100/day Г— days, $250 minimum) + actual + fees`                |
| **Additive rent + flat**        | TX В§ 92.0081                            | `1 month rent + $1,000 + actual + fees`                              |
| **Greater-of rent-mult or actual** | FL В§ 83.67 / WA В§ 59.18.290 / MA c.186 В§14 | `max(3Г— rent, actual) + fees`                                  |
| **Greater-of rent-mult or actual-mult** | AZ В§ 33-1367                    | `max(2Г— rent, 2Г— actual) + fees`                                     |
| **Greater-of rent-mult or flat, plus actual** | CO В§ 38-12-510            | `max(3Г— rent, $5,000) + actual + fees`                              |
| **Treble actual**               | NY RPL В§ 235 / DC В§ 42-3505.01 / NJ В§ 2A:39-1 | `3Г— actual + fees` (+ criminal exposure in NY/NJ)             |
| **Rent multiple + actual**      | IL Forcible Entry Act                   | `2Г— rent + actual + fees`                                            |
| **Actual damages only**         | ~35 other states                        | actual + fees (where statute provides)                               |
| **No statute**                  | AR / WV / WY / MS / SD / ID             | actual common-law trespass damages only                              |

**CO В§ 38-12-510 is the strictest state in the table** вҖ” the deliberate 2021 HB21-1121 reform set a `max(3Г— rent, $5,000)` floor PLUS actual damages, so even a tenant with zero actual damages on a $1,000/month unit recovers $5,000 (the flat floor wins over $3,000 = 3Г— rent). Pinned by `colorado_strictest_state_three_times_rent_or_5k_plus_actual` covering both low-rent floor-wins and high-rent multiplier-wins.

**Three states have statutory floors that survive all-zero input**: CA ($250 per-day minimum), TX ($1,000 flat), CO ($5,000 flat). Every other regime zeros out cleanly. Pinned by `zero_rent_zero_actual_zero_days_no_panic` which sweeps all three positive-floor states against the negative-control states (FL/WA/NY/AZ/IL/MA) вҖ” catches a future regression where someone moves the floor logic out of CA/TX/CO into a shared helper.

**Treble model has no statutory floor independent of actual damages**: 3Г— 0 = 0. Distinct from the per-day model. Pinned by `treble_with_zero_actual_yields_zero_total`. This matters at the UI layer вҖ” telling a landlord "you owe at least 3Г— actual" is misleading if actual is zero (e.g., immediate self-cure before any tenant outlay).

**Criminal exposure flag** is independent of civil regime. NY, NJ, MA, GA, MI, NH, CT, LA, MO, NV, PA all flag `criminal_exposure: true` вҖ” the landlord faces misdemeanor / criminal trespass charges separate from the civil suit. Pinned by `criminal_exposure_states_pinned`. The flag is a downstream UI signal, not used in the dollar compute.

**Arkansas is uniquely `NoStatute`** вҖ” the only US state with effectively no tenant protections on self-help. Pinned by `arkansas_uniquely_no_statute_landlord_friendly`.

**Integer cents + saturating_mul throughout** вҖ” large rent / large day count never overflow i64. Pinned by `large_rent_no_overflow_via_saturating_mul` with $10 billion rent against the CO 3Г— regime, which still clamps under i64::MAX.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/lockout-penalty-check`. Twenty-five tests pin: 51-row coverage; **CA per-day floor cross-over math** (1 day / 2 days / 3 days / 5 days вҖ” floor wins until day 3); CA zero-days still returns $250 minimum; TX additive 1mo rent + $1k + actual; FL greater-of with both directions (actual wins, statutory wins); WA mirrors FL with distinct citation noting separate utility-shutoff statute; AZ both-sides-multiplied; **CO strictest with floor-wins and multiplier-wins both pinned**; treble states pinned at 3Г— actual; treble Г— 0 = 0; IL 2Г— rent additive; MA mirrors FL but criminal flag set; actual-damages-only states pass through; no-statute states (AR/WV/WY/MS/SD/ID) yield actual only with AR uniquely flagged; unknown state errors; case-insensitive lookup; sorted `all_states()`; non-empty citations; attorney_fees_recoverable flag pinned across 9 strong fee-shifting states; criminal_exposure flag pinned across 11 states; arkansas no-statute uniquely; all-zero input no panic; **large-rent no overflow**; TX zero-actual still has $1k+rent floor; greater-of equal amounts no double-count.

`traderview-expense::application_fees` is the **state-specific rental application / tenant-screening fee cap table** вҖ” sibling to `entry_notice`, `retaliation_windows`, `eviction_notices`, `late_fee_caps`, `deposit_interest`, `deposit_return_windows`, `lease_disclosures`, `habitability_remedies`, `rent_control`, `military_termination`, `security_deposit_caps`, and `contractor_1099`. Application fees are the highest-frequency landlord-tenant transaction and increasingly regulated вҖ” NJ AG put landlords on notice in 2024, and CA's CPI-adjusted cap moves every December.

Six regulatory regimes plus no-statute:

| Regime                                | States                                                                  | Citation example                   |
|---------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------|------------------------------------|
| **Prohibited**                        | MA, VT                                                                  | M.G.L. c. 186 В§ 15B / 9 V.S.A. В§ 4456a |
| **Flat cap**                          | NY $20 / VA $50 / WI $20 / DC $50 / NJ $50                              | RPL В§ 238-a (HSTPA 2019)           |
| **CPI-adjusted cap + actual-cost limiter** | CA $65.86 (2026) вҖ” lesser-of cap-or-actual-cost                       | Cal. Civ. Code В§ 1950.6            |
| **Lesser-of-cap-or-actual-cost**      | MD $25 cap                                                              | Md. Code Real Prop. В§ 8-213        |
| **Greater-of-flat-or-pct-of-rent**    | DE: greater of $50 or 10% of monthly rent                               | 25 Del. C. В§ 5514(d)               |
| **Actual cost only (no markup)**      | WA / OR                                                                 | RCW В§ 59.18.257 / ORS В§ 90.297     |
| **No statute**                        | 38 other states                                                         | n/a                                |

**Integer cents arithmetic is load-bearing.** Money is `i64` cents throughout, not `Decimal` вҖ” CA's $65.86 is exactly `6586_i64` with no precision drift, and DE's `10% Г— monthly_rent_cents` uses `saturating_mul + 10_000` floor-divide. The `delaware_pct_rounds_down_at_uneven_rent` test pins this: rent of $1,234.56 (123_456Вў) вҶ’ 10% = 12,345.6Вў вҶ’ integer floor 12,345Вў. A future "fix" that converts to `Decimal` rounds halfway-banker's-round upward and would silently change the compute on uneven rents.

**Prohibited vs no-statute distinction matters.** MA tenant can sue for the prohibited fee; TX tenant cannot вҖ” landlord just charges what they want. Both come back as "non-compliant" if the proposed fee is nonzero, but `prohibited` flag and `no_statute` flag are mutually exclusive on the result. Downstream UI must distinguish them. Pinned by `massachusetts_prohibited_flag_distinct_from_no_statute`.

**California CPI cap moves annually.** The $65.86 value is the 2026 figure (per the California Apartment Association December 2025 announcement). Callers must refresh this annually вҖ” the citation field flags `cpi_adjusted: true` and the result note spells out "CPI-adjusted" so a stale value is visible at the UI layer.

**Actual-cost-only states require the actual cost.** WA and OR fail closed if `actual_screening_cost_cents` is `None` вҖ” compliance can't be determined without the third-party screening invoice. The `actual_cost_required_but_missing` flag on the result tells the caller to ask the user for the cost rather than silently approving the fee.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/application-fee-check`. Twenty-three tests pin: 51-row coverage; MA prohibits any fee ($0 OK, any Вў violates); VT prohibition with distinct citation; NY $20 exact-boundary + $20.01 over; **CA 2026 CPI cap with both actual-cost-provided and not-provided paths** (load-bearing dual-mode test); MD lesser-of $25-or-actual-cost; **DE greater-of $50-or-10%-rent at low rent / high rent / 1-cent-over boundary**; DE rounds-down at uneven rent (123_456Вў вҶ’ 12_345 cap, not 12_346); WA actual-cost-only with cost provided; WA `actual_cost_required_but_missing` flag set when missing; OR mirrors WA with distinct citation; no-statute states (TX/FL/CO/AZ/GA) accept any fee; unknown state returns no-statute with error note; case-insensitive lookup; sorted `all_states()`; non-empty citations on every row; **flat-cap states (NY/VA/WI/DC/NJ) pinned at exact boundary AND boundary+1** (catches future cap typos); MA prohibited vs TX no-statute flags mutually exclusive; CA without actual-cost uses CPI cap with informative note; DE 0Вў rent falls back to $50 minimum (concession-period regression target); $0 proposed fee always complies in every Cap-rule state; CA note spells out CPI + actual-cost-limiter when both apply; negative proposed fee clamps excess to 0 (input-sanity stability).

`traderview-expense::broker_fee_allocation` is the **broker fee allocation between landlord and tenant** module вҖ” who pays the rental broker's commission when a rental agreement is executed? Trader-landlord operational concern in markets where broker fees range from one month's rent to 15% of annual rent (NYC, Boston, San Francisco). Distinct from `application_fees` (pre-tenancy screening fee caps), `non_refundable_cleaning_fees` (move-in non-refundable charges), `pet_fees` (pet-specific charges), and `lease_disclosures` (general lease disclosures).

**Two regimes**:

| Regime | Authority | Default rule |
|--------|-----------|--------------|
| **NYC FARE Act** | NYC Local Law 119 (eff. June 11, 2025); NYC Admin. Code В§В§ 20-699.20, 20-699.21, 20-699.22 | Party-who-hired-pays вҖ” landlord-hired brokers paid by landlord; В§ 20-699.20 prohibits passing fee to tenant; В§ 20-699.21 disclosure of other fees in listing AND lease |
| **Default** | lease + market practice; Boston Ord. 17-2024 disclosure overlay | Lease controls; in tight rental markets landlords traditionally pass to tenants unless lease specifies |

**NYC FARE Act (effective June 11, 2025) uniquely prohibits pass-through to tenant.** The party who hires the broker is the party responsible for paying вҖ” in most NYC rentals, the landlord hires the listing broker, so the landlord pays. The same broker who publishes a listing with the landlord's permission cannot charge the tenant. Tenants retain the right to hire their own broker (and pay that broker's fee). Pinned by `nyc_landlord_hired_landlord_pays_compliant`, `nyc_landlord_hired_imposing_fee_on_tenant_violation` (В§ 20-699.20), `nyc_tenant_hired_own_broker_tenant_pays`, `nyc_uniquely_prohibits_pass_through_to_tenant_invariant` (2-regime sweep: NYC violates while Default permits), `nyc_landlord_hired_with_tenant_imposition_violates_party_who_hired_rule`.

**NYC В§ 20-699.21 disclosure of other rental-related fees required.** Landlord must disclose all rental-related fees in BOTH the listing AND the rental agreement. Disclosure failure is a separate violation from the imposition prohibition вҖ” both can stack. Pinned by `nyc_other_fees_not_disclosed_violation`, `nyc_disclosure_required_even_when_landlord_pays` (disclosure obligation persists when landlord-pays is correct), `nyc_only_imposes_disclosure_obligation_invariant`, `nyc_multiple_violations_accumulate` (imposition + non-disclosure = 2 violations).

**NYC В§ 20-699.22 enforcement via NYC DCWP** (Department of Consumer and Worker Protection) вҖ” civil penalty OR civil action. Pinned by `nyc_dcwp_enforcement_note_always_present`, `dcwp_enforcement_note_pins_section_20_699_22`, `nyc_civil_penalty_or_action_described_in_enforcement_note`.

**Tenant who hired their own broker pays their own broker regardless of regime.** Pinned by `nyc_tenant_hired_invariant_tenant_pays_in_both_regimes` (cross-regime tenant-self-hired outcome), `nyc_imposing_fee_on_tenant_but_tenant_self_hired_no_violation` (FARE Act protects tenant from landlord-hired broker fee, NOT from tenant's own broker fee).

**Default regime allows pass-through to tenant via lease.** When the landlord engages the broker and the lease provides for tenant to pay the fee, the lease term controls. Pinned by `default_landlord_hired_passes_to_tenant_via_lease_compliant`, `default_landlord_hired_no_lease_impose_landlord_pays`, `default_boston_disclosure_note_present` (Boston Ord. 17-2024 disclosure overlay surfaces in notes).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/broker-fee-allocation`. Twenty-six tests pin: **NYC landlord hired landlord pays compliant**; **NYC landlord hired imposing fee on tenant violation** (В§ 20-699.20); **NYC tenant hired own broker tenant pays**; **NYC both parties hire their own brokers**; **NYC no broker engaged no fee allocation**; **NYC other fees not disclosed violation** (В§ 20-699.21); **NYC DCWP enforcement note always present**; **default landlord hired landlord pays by default**; **default landlord hired passes to tenant via lease compliant**; **default tenant hired own broker tenant pays**; **default Boston disclosure note present** (Ord. 17-2024); **citation NYC FARE Act pins Local Law 119 + В§ 20-699.20/.21/.22**; **citation default pins Boston Ord. + market practice**; **NYC multiple violations accumulate** (2 simultaneous = 2 violations); **NYC uniquely prohibits pass-through to tenant invariant** (2-regime sweep); **NYC party-who-hired-pays rule landlord hired**; **NYC tenant hired invariant tenant pays in both regimes**; **NYC disclosure required even when landlord pays**; **NYC full compliance path**; **default no disclosure required compliant** (no statutory obligation); **NYC landlord hired with tenant imposition violates party-who-hired rule**; **default landlord hired no lease impose landlord pays**; **DCWP enforcement note pins В§ 20-699.22**; **NYC civil penalty or action described in enforcement note**; **NYC imposing fee on tenant but tenant self-hired no violation** (tenant's own broker may charge tenant); **NYC only imposes disclosure obligation invariant** (default lacks statutory disclosure requirement).

`traderview-expense::retaliation_windows` is the **state-specific anti-retaliation rebuttable-presumption-window table** вҖ” sibling to `eviction_notices`, `entry_notice`, `late_fee_caps`, `deposit_interest`, `deposit_return_windows`, `lease_disclosures`, `habitability_remedies`, `rent_control`, `military_termination`, `security_deposit_caps`, and `contractor_1099`. Pins the burden-shift window: when a landlord initiates an adverse action (eviction filing, rent increase, service reduction, refusal to renew) within the statutory window after a tenant exercises a protected right, a rebuttable presumption arises that the action is retaliatory and the burden shifts to the landlord to show a legitimate non-retaliatory reason.

The presumption windows cluster into three bands plus three special categories:

| Band                          | States                                                       |
|-------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------|
| **90 days** (URLTA short)     | AK / DE / MI / MN / VT / WA                                  |
| **6 months / 180 days**       | AL / AZ / CA / CO / CT / DC / KS / MA / MD / ME / MT / NE / NH / NM / NV / OR / PA / RI / SC / TN / TX / WI |
| **1 year**                    | HI / IA / IL / KY / NC / NY / VA                             |
| Statute, **no presumption period** (burden stays on tenant) | FL В§ 83.64 / OH В§ 5321.02 / OK 41 O.S. В§ 101 |
| Statute, **case-by-case window** (NJ Anti-Reprisal Act)     | NJ                                            |
| **No statute** at all         | AR / GA / ID / IN / LA / MO / MS / ND / SD / UT / WV / WY    |

**Days vs Months matters.** "180 days" is exact-day math; "6 months" is calendar-anchored and varies 181-184 days depending on which months the window spans. The `PresumptionPeriod::Days(n)` vs `Months(n)` distinction is load-bearing: e.g. CA uses `Days(180)` while TX uses `Months(6)`. For a complaint on 2026-08-31, CA's window ends 2027-02-27 (exact day 180) but TX's ends 2027-02-28 (calendar 6 months, using `chrono::Months::checked_add` for short-month rollover). A regression that "simplifies" `Months(6)` to `Days(180)` would silently misclassify the TX 8/31 вҶ’ 2/28 case as outside the window. The `months_path_uses_calendar_anchored_window_not_180_days` test pins this against future drift.

**Action-before-complaint short-circuits.** If the landlord's action precedes the tenant's complaint, no causal link exists and the presumption cannot apply regardless of state. The `landlord_action_before_complaint_cannot_be_retaliation` test pins this вҖ” `days_since_complaint` is negative and the result is non-presumption with a note explaining the missing causal link.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/retaliation-check`. Twenty tests pin: 51-row coverage; case-insensitive lookup and unknown-state handling; CA 180-day exact boundary inside / 181 outside; WA 90-day window cluster boundary; TX 6-month calendar-anchored window with 8/31 вҶ’ 2/28 short-month rollover (the load-bearing month-vs-day case); IL 12-month band 11-month inside / 13-month outside; FL statute-no-presumption-period burden-on-tenant flag; NJ case-by-case neither presumption nor burden-on-tenant; no-statute states (12 jurisdictions) all flagged correctly; landlord-action-before-complaint cannot be retaliation; day-zero same-day action within window (hostile-landlord scenario); unknown state code marked no-statute; **90-day band states uniformly reject day 91**; **6-month band states uniformly reject month 7**; **12-month band states uniformly hold at month 11** (three-way band consistency catches future cell-edit errors); `all_states()` returns sorted by code; every row has non-empty citation; calendar-anchored window math distinct from 180-day math; note describes burden-on-landlord vs days-past explicitly when inside vs outside window.

`traderview-expense::entry_notice` is the **state-specific landlord entry-notice hour-count table** вҖ” sibling to `eviction_notices`, `late_fee_caps`, `deposit_interest`, `deposit_return_windows`, `lease_disclosures`, `habitability_remedies`, `rent_control`, `military_termination`, `security_deposit_caps`, and `contractor_1099`. Closes the privacy-rights leg of the landlord-state-data set: every other module covers money or eviction, this one covers when the landlord is allowed in the door.

Each state's landlord-tenant code sets a minimum advance-notice period before non-emergency entry (repairs, inspection, showing to prospects). The hour count clusters into four bands:

| Band                   | Hours | States                                                       |
|------------------------|-------|--------------------------------------------------------------|
| URLTA-2015 default     | 24    | AK / CA / CO / IA / ME / MT / NE / NV / NM / OH / OK / OR / SC / SD / TN / UT / VA |
| URLTA-1974 default     | 48    | AL / AZ / DE / DC / HI / KY / RI / VT                        |
| Strictest (repairs)    | 48    | WA (with separate 24h column for showings to prospects)      |
| Aggressive carve-out   | 12    | FL (Fla. Stat. В§ 83.53) / WI (ATCP В§ 134.09)                |
| No statutory hours     | n/a   | AR / CT / GA / ID / IL / IN / KS / LA / MA / MD / MI / MN / MO / MS / NC / ND / NH / NJ / NY / PA / TX / WV / WY (common-law "reasonable" applies but is not measurable in hours) |

**The Washington split is load-bearing.** RCW 59.18.150 is the only statute in the table with a per-purpose carve-out: 48h required for repairs/inspection, 24h for showings to prospective buyers or replacement tenants. Every other state uses one column for all non-emergency purposes. The compute fn picks `showing_hours.or(standard_hours)` so the carve-out applies only when the showing column is explicitly set.

**Emergency, tenant-requested, and abandonment exceptions short-circuit to compliant in every state** вҖ” these are universal common-law exceptions, not per-state rules. Models them as `EntryPurpose` variants that bypass the hour-count check entirely with a labeled `exception` field on the result.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/entry-notice-check`. Twenty-two tests pin: 51-row coverage (50 states + DC); case-insensitive state lookup; unknown state errors; emergency entry compliant at 0h notice in every state including WA-48h; tenant-requested entry compliant at 0h; abandoned-unit entry compliant at 0h; CA 24h exactly compliant; CA 23h one hour short (exact shortfall reporting); FL 12h minimum + 11h one-hour-short; **WA 48h repairs but 24h showings split** (the only per-purpose carve-out); URLTA-1974 states (AL/AZ/KY/HI/RI/VT) all default to 48h; no-statute states report compliant at 0h (no measurable standard); "reasonable" states (CT/MN/KS) treated like no-statute; unknown-state error reported; `all_states()` returns sorted by code (first AK, last WY); every row has non-empty citation; showings fall back to standard column when no carve-out (CA showing = 24h); inspections use the standard column (OH 24h); 0h notice fails in every hour-count state; excess notice (96h in CA) still compliant with 0 shortfall; FL 12h is distinct from 24h default (18h compliant in FL/WI, not in AL); emergency short-circuits even for unknown state code (lookup-first ordering).

`traderview-expense::eviction_diversion_program` is the **municipal/state pre-filing eviction-diversion-program landlord compliance check** вҖ” addresses the affirmative duty for the landlord to ENROLL in a mediation/diversion program AND participate in good faith for a minimum period BEFORE filing an eviction in court. Distinct from `eviction_notices` (notice period landlord gives tenant) вҖ” this module is about the diversion-program enrollment + participation duty layered ON TOP of the notice period.

**Three regimes**:

| Regime | Statute | Min waiting period | Tenant notice | Imminent-harm carve-out | Tenant-default carve-out |
|--------|---------|---------------------|----------------|--------------------------|----------------------------|
| `Philadelphia` | Phila Code В§ 9-811 (Eviction Diversion Program; 2022 amendment expanded to all grounds) | **30 days** of good-faith EDP participation | Notice of diversion rights required | **Yes** вҖ” В§ 9-811(2)(b) exempts imminent-harm/harassment evictions | No |
| `NewJersey` | NJ DCA Eviction Diversion Program + Court Rule 6:4 | **45 days** from enrollment IF tenant schedules mediation | **14-day notice** of mediation right + copy to local dispute resolution center | No | **Yes** вҖ” if tenant doesn't schedule mediation in 14 days, landlord may proceed |
| `Default` | вҖ” | 0 | вҖ” | вҖ” | вҖ” |

**Philadelphia EDP enforcement**: tenant may assert noncompliance as a defense; court may sua sponte dismiss noncompliant filings; protections are **non-waivable** by lease.

**The two carve-outs distinguish the regimes**: only Philadelphia has the imminent-harm exemption (landlord may file directly without EDP enrollment); only NJ has the tenant-default carve-out (if tenant doesn't schedule mediation in 14 days, landlord may proceed without the 45-day wait).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/eviction-diversion-program`. Twenty-two tests pin: **PHL imminent harm carve-out skips EDP** + В§ 9-811(2)(b) citation; **PHL nonpayment not enrolled blocks filing**; **PHL enrolled no notice blocks**; **PHL 29 days insufficient**; **PHL at 30-day boundary compliant** (вүҘ strict); **PHL no good-faith blocks**; **PHL all requirements met compliant**; **PHL applies to lease breach post-2022 amendment** (regression вҖ” pre-2022 was nonpayment only); **PHL applies to term expiration post-2022**; **NJ not enrolled blocks** + "NJ DCA" citation; **NJ enrolled no notice blocks** + "14-day notice" citation; **NJ tenant did not schedule вҶ’ landlord may proceed** (regression вҖ” NJ-specific tenant-default carve-out); **NJ tenant scheduled 44 days insufficient**; **NJ at 45-day boundary compliant** (вүҘ strict); **NJ no good-faith blocks**; **default no diversion required**; **jurisdiction routing PA/Philadelphia + PA/Phila вҶ’ Philadelphia, PA/Pittsburgh вҶ’ Default, NJ вҶ’ NewJersey**; **case-insensitive lookup**; **only PHL has imminent-harm carve-out** (single-test invariant вҖ” same imminent-harm input only triggers PHL exemption); **only NJ has tenant-default carve-out** (single-test invariant вҖ” same tenant-didn't-schedule input only lets NJ landlord proceed early); **waiting period ordering PHL 30 < NJ 45**; **citations pin authorities** (Phila Code В§ 9-811 + NJ DCA).

`traderview-expense::eviction_notices` is the **state-specific eviction-notice period table** вҖ” sibling to `late_fee_caps` and `deposit_interest`. Each state's landlord-tenant statute sets a minimum notice period before the landlord can file for possession, varying dramatically by ground:

- **Pay or Quit** (nonpayment): TX 3 days, FL 3 days, CT 3 days vs MA 14 days, NY 14 days, WA 14 days (post-2019), CO 10 days (post-2021), DC 30 days, NJ вҖ” no pay-or-quit notice required (landlord files directly after 5 business days late).
- **Cure or Quit** (curable lease breach): typically 7-30 days; tenant has the right to fix the breach and avoid eviction.
- **Unconditional Quit** (non-curable, e.g. illegal activity): typically 3-14 days; no cure right.
- **No-Cause Termination** of month-to-month: many states **scale by tenancy length** вҖ” CA 30 days under 1 year / 60 days at 1+ year (CCP В§1946.1); NY 30/60/90 days under HSTPA; OR 30 days under 1 year / 90 days at 1+ year with just cause; PA 15/30 days. NJ has effectively **eliminated** no-cause for residential tenants under the Anti-Eviction Act; WA largely eliminated under the 2021 Just Cause Act.

25 jurisdictions modeled (AL/AZ/CA/CO/CT/DC/FL/GA/IL/MA/MD/MI/MN/NC/NJ/NY/OH/OR/PA/SC/TN/TX/VA/WA/WI), each with statute citation + URL + notes calling out the load-bearing carve-outs (CA AB1482 just cause for >12-month tenancies in multi-unit; CO HB21-1121 pay-or-quit raise from 3 to 10 days; NY HSTPA 14-day pay-or-quit and 90-day no-cause for вүҘ 2-year tenancies; WA 2021 just-cause statewide). Unknown states return `state_recognized: false` with a "consult state code directly" note rather than guessing.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/eviction-notice-check`. Eighteen tests pin: TX 3-day pay-or-quit; NY 14-day pay-or-quit post-HSTPA; NY no-cause scales to 90 days at 2 years; NY no-cause 30 days at 6 months; CA no-cause 30вҶ’60 at 1 year boundary; CA 3-day pay-or-quit with just-cause flag; CO post-2021 10-day pay-or-quit; WA post-2019 14-day pay-or-quit + just-cause flag; NJ no pay-or-quit notice required; NJ no-cause unavailable (Anti-Eviction); GA no pre-filing pay-or-quit; DC 30 days for all four grounds; unknown state returns not-recognized; case-insensitive lookup; just-cause jurisdictions flagged correctly (CA/DC/NJ/OR/WA); non-just-cause states not flagged (TX/FL/AZ/AL); citation correctness for CA/TX/NY; OR no-cause scales to 90 days at 18 months.

`traderview-expense::late_fee_caps` is the **state-specific late-fee-cap + grace-period table** вҖ” sibling to `deposit_interest`. The existing `rental_leases.late_fee_fixed` and `late_fee_pct` columns on iter 1's migration accepted any value with no statute check; this module fills the gap. 17 jurisdictions with statutory caps or grace requirements:

| State | Cap formula                | Cap value          | Grace |
|-------|----------------------------|--------------------|-------|
| CA    | Reasonable (case law)      | ~6% bound          | 0     |
| CO    | Greater of $50 or 5%       | $50 / 5%           | 7     |
| CT    | Reasonable                 | none               | 9     |
| DC    | 5% of monthly rent         | 5%                 | 5     |
| DE    | 5% of monthly rent         | 5%                 | 5     |
| MA    | Reasonable (30-day delay)  | none               | 30    |
| MD    | 5% of monthly rent         | 5%                 | 0     |
| ME    | 4% of monthly rent         | 4%                 | 15    |
| MN    | 8% of overdue rent         | 8%                 | 0     |
| NC    | Greater of $15 or 5%       | $15 / 5%           | 5     |
| NJ    | Reasonable                 | none               | 5     |
| NV    | 5% of monthly rent         | 5%                 | 0     |
| NY    | **Lesser** of $50 or 5%    | $50 / 5%           | 5     |
| OR    | Reasonable (~5%)           | ~5% bound          | 4     |
| TX    | 12% safe harbor (1-4 unit) | 12%                | 2     |
| VA    | 10% of past-due rent       | 10%                | 0     |
| WA    | Reasonable                 | none               | 5     |
